# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Performance Analysis Pipeline

The following flowchart shows the **complete logical flow** a student must follow when given any algorithm to analyze, as expected by KTU examiners.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given a code snippet] --> B[Identify the input size variable n]
    B --> C{Is it a single block of statements?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Count constant operations, result is O of 1]
    C -- No --> E{Is it a single loop?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Multiply body cost by number of iterations]
    E -- No --> G{Is it a nested loop?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply summation of inner loop, then outer]
    G -- No --> I{Is it recursive?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Write a recurrence relation T of n]
    J --> K[Expand recurrence or use Master Theorem]
    I -- No --> L[Check for break, continue, return mid loop]
    L --> M[Adjust best, average, worst case separately]
    F --> N[Drop constants and lower order terms]
    H --> N
    K --> N
    D --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Big-O, Theta, or Omega result]
    O --> P[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Graph — Asymptotic Notation Set Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Algorithm T of n] --> B{Find Lower Bound}
    A --> C{Find Upper Bound}
    B --> D[Big Omega g of n]
    C --> E[Big O g of n]
    D --> F{Do both bounds match?}
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Big Theta g of n - Tight Bound]
    F -- No --> H[Only upper or lower bound exists]
    H --> I[Use Big O or Big Omega separately]
    G --> J[Final Asymptotic Classification]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Complexity Class Growth Rates

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Constant O 1] --> L1[Logarithmic O log n]
    L1 --> L2[Linear O n]
    L2 --> L3[Log Linear O n log n]
    L3 --> L4[Quadratic O n squared]
    L4 --> L5[Cubic O n cubed]
    L5 --> L6[Exponential O 2 to the n]
    L6 --> L7[Factorial O n factorial]
    L0 -.fastest.-> L7
    L7 -.slowest.-> L0
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — The Big-O Proof Construction

This diagram shows the **conceptual procedure** to prove $T(n) = O(g(n))$ — a frequent KTU question.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Student
    participant T as Target T of n
    participant G as Candidate g of n
    participant C as Constant c
    participant N0 as Threshold n0
    U->>T: 1. Write T of n explicitly
    U->>G: 2. Choose a candidate g of n
    U->>C: 3. Find a constant c that works
    U->>N0: 4. Find n0 beyond which the inequality holds
    U->>U: 5. Verify T of n is less or equal to c times g of n for all n greater than n0
    U-->>U: 6. Conclude T of n is Big O of g of n
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Best, Worst, Average Case of Linear Search

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input array A of size n, key k] --> B{Is A of 0 equal to k?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Best Case: 1 comparison, Omega of 1]
    B -- No --> D{Is A of 1 equal to k?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Best Case for position 1, 2 comparisons]
    D -- No --> F[Continue checking all positions]
    F --> G{Is A of n minus 1 equal to k?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Worst Case Present, n comparisons, O of n]
    G -- No --> I[Worst Case Absent, n comparisons, O of n]
    F --> J[Average Case: n plus 1 over 2 comparisons, Theta of n]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]
**Define time complexity and space complexity of an algorithm. Why is asymptotic analysis preferred over exact measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Time Complexity** is a function $T(n)$ that gives the number of elementary operations performed by an algorithm on an input of size $n$. (1 mark)
- **Space Complexity** $S(n)$ is the total memory required, equal to auxiliary space plus the memory occupied by the inputs. (1 mark)
- Asymptotic analysis is preferred because it is **machine-independent**, **language-independent**, and predicts growth as $n \to \infty$ without depending on processor speed, RAM, or compiler optimizations, unlike exact measurement which varies with hardware. (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Distinguish between Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta notations with one-line definitions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $O(g(n))$ | Upper bound (worst case). $T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$. (1 mark) |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | Lower bound (best case). $T(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$. (1 mark) |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | Tight bound. $T(n)$ grows at the same rate as $g(n)$ both above and below. (1 mark) |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**a)** Explain the concept of asymptotic notations. Define Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta with suitable mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For the following code fragments, compute the time complexity $T(n)$ in Big-O notation and show the derivation. **(7 marks)**

```c
/* Fragment 1 */
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = i; j < n; j++) {
        printf("KTU");
    }
}

/* Fragment 2 */
int k = 1;
while (k < n) {
    printf("%d", k);
    k = k * 3;
}
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Asymptotic Notations Explained (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Asymptotic notations are mathematical symbols that describe the *order of growth* (rate of increase) of an algorithm's running time or space as the input size $n$ tends to infinity. They abstract away hardware and language details.

**Big-O — Upper Bound (2 marks):**
$T(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:

$$
0 \le T(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$

It represents the **worst-case** performance guarantee. Example: $3n^{2} + 5n = O(n^{2})$.

**Big-$\Omega$ — Lower Bound (1.5 marks):**
$T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:

$$
0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$

It represents the **best-case** lower limit.

**Big-$\Theta$ — Tight Bound (1.5 marks):**
$T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if $T(n) = O(g(n))$ AND $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$, i.e., $c_1 \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

### Part (b) — Complexity Derivations (7 marks)

**Fragment 1 — Triangular nested loop (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** For a fixed outer value $i$, the inner loop runs from $j = i$ to $j = n-1$, giving $(n - i)$ iterations.

**Step 2:** Total cost is the sum:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (n - i)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $k = n - i$, so when $i = 0$, $k = n$; when $i = n-1$, $k = 1$. The sum becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} k \\
     &= \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
     &= \frac{n^{2} + n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Drop the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ and the lower-order term $n$:

$$
T(n) = O(n^{2})
$$

**Fragment 2 — Loop with multiplying counter (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Values of $k$ are $1, 3, 9, 27, \ldots, 3^{m}$.

**Step 2:** The loop terminates when $3^{m} \ge n$, i.e., $m \ge \log_3 n$.

**Step 3:** Number of iterations $= \lceil \log_3 n \rceil$.

$$
T(n) = O(\log_3 n) = O(\log n)
$$

(since logarithms of different bases differ by only a constant factor).

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** What is performance analysis? Explain the differences between worst-case, best-case, and average-case analysis with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the time complexity of the following recursive function in Big-O notation. Show each step of the recurrence expansion. **(7 marks)**

```c
int mystery(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return mystery(n / 2) + mystery(n / 2) + 1;
}
```

Also, briefly explain why $O(\log n)$ is generally considered acceptable for large datasets while $O(n^{2})$ is not.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Performance Analysis & Case Analysis (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Performance analysis is the theoretical study of an algorithm's efficiency in terms of time $T(n)$ and space $S(n)$ consumed as a function of input size $n$, independent of hardware.

**Best Case (1.5 marks):** The minimum number of operations the algorithm performs for the most favorable input. Denoted using $\Omega$ (Big-Omega).
*Example:* In linear search, the best case is finding the element at the first position, taking 1 comparison.

**Worst Case (1.5 marks):** The maximum number of operations the algorithm performs for the most unfavorable input. Denoted using $O$ (Big-O). Used as a guaranteed upper limit.
*Example:* In linear search, the worst case is the element being at the last position (or absent), taking $n$ comparisons.

**Average Case (2 marks):** The expected (mean) number of operations over all possible inputs, computed as:

$$
T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} i \cdot P(\text{input leads to } i \text{ comparisons})
$$

*Example:* For linear search where the key is equally likely at any of the $n$ positions, the average number of comparisons is $\frac{n+1}{2}$, which is $\Theta(n)$.

### Part (b) — Recurrence Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Write the recurrence. Let $T(n)$ be the time for `mystery(n)`. The function does two recursive calls on half the input and one constant-time addition.

$$
T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + c
$$

**Step 2:** Expand level by level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2 T(n/2) + c \\
     &= 2 \big[ 2 T(n/4) + c \big] + c = 4 T(n/4) + 2c + c \\
     &= 4 \big[ 2 T(n/8) + c \big] + 3c = 8 T(n/8) + 4c + 3c \\
     &= 8 T(n/8) + 7c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the pattern. After $k$ levels of expansion:

$$
T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + (2^{k} - 1) c
$$

**Step 4:** The base case is reached when $n / 2^{k} = 1$, i.e., $2^{k} = n$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 5:** Substitute $k = \log_2 n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) c \\
     &= n \cdot T(1) + (n - 1) c \\
     &= n \cdot c_{1} + (n - 1) c_{2} \\
     &= (c_1 + c_2) n - c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Drop the constant factor and constant term:

$$
T(n) = O(n)
$$

**Acceptability Discussion (within the 7 marks, last 1 mark):** $O(\log n)$ grows extremely slowly — doubling $n$ adds only 1 extra step. Hence it is acceptable even for $n$ in the billions. In contrast, $O(n^{2})$ quadruples when $n$ doubles, making it infeasible beyond $n = 10^{6}$ in typical 1-second time limits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop constants** — Writing $T(n) = O(3n + 5)$ instead of $O(n)$ costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Big-O with Big-$\Theta$** — Big-O is an upper bound, NOT an equality. Saying "the algorithm takes exactly $O(n^2)$ time" is sloppy and loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the summation in nested loops** — Writing the answer as "$O(n^2)$" without showing $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} 1 = n^2$ loses **2–3 marks** out of 7 in Part (b).
> 4. **Misinterpreting logarithmic loops** — Writing $O(n)$ for `for(i=1; i<n; i*=2)` is a common error. Always write $k$ such that $2^{k} \ge n$ to derive $\log$.
> 5. **Forgetting auxiliary vs input space** — When asked for space complexity, never forget to add the input storage. Recursive stack depth is **auxiliary** space, not input.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Performance Analysis** = theoretical study of $T(n)$ and $S(n)$ using asymptotic notations, *not* measuring wall-clock time.
- **Asymptotic Notations** = $O$ (upper), $\Omega$ (lower), $\Theta$ (tight). They describe *growth rate*, not exact time.
- **Big-O is the most frequently used** in KTU exams and always refers to the **worst case** unless stated otherwise.
- **Rules of thumb:**
  - Single loop on $n$ → $O(n)$
  - Nested loop on $n \times n$ → $O(n^{2})$
  - Loop with halving/doubling counter → $O(\log n)$
  - Divide-and-conquer recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ → solved using Master Theorem.
- **Space Complexity = Input + Auxiliary**. Recursion depth contributes to auxiliary space.
- **Drop constants and lower-order terms** while simplifying. $5n^{2} + 100n + 17 \to O(n^{2})$.
- **Order of growth (fastest → slowest):** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^{2}) < O(n^{3}) < O(2^{n}) < O(n!)$.
- **Best, Worst, Average cases** correspond to most favorable, most unfavorable, and expected input data respectively.
- **Performance Analysis ≠ Performance Measurement** — analysis is theoretical; measurement is empirical (using `clock()`, `time.h`).
- **Why it matters:** Asymptotic analysis guides algorithm choice in OS schedulers, DB query planners, search engines, and compiler optimizations.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:** forgetting Big-O definitions, skipping summation steps, confusing $O$ with $\Theta$, and treating nested loops as $O(n)$ instead of $O(n^{2})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Space Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Space Complexity** of an algorithm is the amount of **memory space** required by the algorithm to solve a problem of size `n`, as a function of the input size `n`. It is formally expressed using the asymptotic notation **Big-O**, **Big-Theta (Θ)**, or **Big-Omega (Ω)**.

Mathematically, the space requirement `S(P)` of any program `P` is expressed as:

$$S(P) = c + S_p(I)$$

Where:
- `c` = the **Fixed Part** (constant space): independent of the input size. It includes instruction space, space for simple variables, fixed-size structured variables, and constants.
- `S_p(I)` = the **Variable Part** (instance-dependent space): depends on the particular instance `I` of the problem being solved. It includes the space for composite variables (arrays, structures), dynamically allocated memory, and recursion stack space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** *"Space complexity is a measure of the amount of working storage an algorithm needs. It quantifies the maximum auxiliary memory (excluding the space required to store the input) that the algorithm consumes during its execution."*

## 1.2 Auxiliary Space vs. Total Space Complexity

A subtle but **highly tested distinction** in KTU exams:

- **Total Space Complexity** = Input Space + Auxiliary Space
- **Auxiliary Space** = Extra space temporarily used by the algorithm (NOT counting the space taken by the input itself).

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU problems, the term *"Space Complexity"* is used synonymously with **Auxiliary Space**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. The phrase *"In-place algorithm"* refers to an algorithm whose auxiliary space is **O(1)** or **O(log n)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Blueprints of a House"

Imagine you are a civil engineer designing a house:

- The **plot of land** (the input) is already given. You cannot reduce its size.
- The **blueprint (algorithm)** tells you how much **scaffolding, cement bags, and worker sheds** (auxiliary memory) you will need during construction.
- A smart blueprint (e.g., pre-fabricated design) uses *constant* scaffolding — that is an **in-place** algorithm.
- A wasteful blueprint that needs an entire warehouse of materials per floor is **O(n)** auxiliary space.

**Space complexity simply asks:** *"How much extra memory does the algorithm borrow beyond the input itself?"*

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Memory is measured in bytes**, but algorithmically in **memory words** (one word ≈ 4 or 8 bytes depending on the architecture).
- The standard asymptotic classes for space complexity are:
  - **O(1)** — Constant space
  - **O(log n)** — Logarithmic space
  - **O(n)** — Linear space
  - **O(n²)** — Quadratic space
  - **O(2ⁿ)** — Exponential space (typically infeasible)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Space Complexity (The 4 Pillars)

Any algorithm's space usage can be decomposed into **four logical components**:

1. **Instruction Space (Fixed)**
   - The compiled binary code of the program stored in memory.
   - Depends on the program, *not* on `n`. Treated as part of the constant `c`.

2. **Data Space (Variable)**
   - Space for storing:
     - **Simple variables** and **constants** → `O(1)` contribution.
     - **Composite variables** (arrays, matrices, structures, linked list nodes) → `O(n)`, `O(n²)`, etc.
     - **Dynamically allocated memory** (heap allocations via `malloc`/`new`).

3. **Environment Stack Space (Variable)**
   - Activation records of function calls.
   - Becomes significant in **recursive algorithms**.
   - Depth of recursion = `O(n)` in linear recursion, `O(log n)` in divide-and-conquer recursion.

4. **Pointer / Reference Space (Variable)**
   - Space for storing addresses/pointers.
   - Common in linked data structures (trees, graphs, linked lists).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** In exam answers, *always classify* the space into "Fixed Part" and "Variable Part" before computing the total.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Algorithm / Construct | Auxiliary Space | Total Space Complexity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|----------------------|-----------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort | O(1) | O(n) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Heap Sort | O(1) | O(n) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Quick Sort (in-place partition) | O(log n) stack | O(n) | ★★★★ |
| 4 | Merge Sort | O(n) auxiliary array | O(n) | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Linear Search | O(1) | O(n) | ★★★ |
| 6 | Binary Search (iterative) | O(1) | O(n) | ★★★★ |
| 7 | Binary Search (recursive) | O(log n) stack | O(n) | ★★★★ |
| 8 | Fibonacci (iterative) | O(1) | O(1) | ★★★★★ |
| 9 | Fibonacci (naive recursive) | O(n) stack | O(n) | ★★★★★ |
| 10 | Factorial (recursive) | O(n) stack | O(n) | ★★★★ |
| 11 | BFS / DFS (adjacency list) | O(V) | O(V + E) | ★★★★ |
| 12 | Dijkstra's Algorithm | O(V) | O(V²) or O(V+E) | ★★★ |
| 13 | Strassen's Matrix Multiplication | O(n²) | O(n²) | ★★★ |
| 14 | Tower of Hanoi (recursive) | O(n) | O(n) | ★★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the `\` character has been intentionally avoided inside cell values to preserve **KTU 2024 Scheme markdown rendering** integrity. The symbol `n` is the input size, `V` is the number of vertices, and `E` is the number of edges.

## 2.3 Recursion: The Hidden Space Killer

Recursion is the **most common source** of space-complexity questions in KTU. Every recursive call pushes a new **stack frame** containing:
- Return address
- Parameters passed by value
- Local variables
- Saved registers

Therefore:
- **Depth of recursion tree = Maximum stack space consumed.**
- Naive recursive Fibonacci (computing `fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)`) uses `O(n)` stack space due to linear depth.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Why Space Complexity Matters |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Embedded Systems** (IoT, microcontrollers) | RAM is in KBs — `O(n²)` algorithms crash the device. |
| **Mobile Computing** | Battery and RAM are constrained — in-place algorithms preferred. |
| **Big Data / Distributed Systems** | Memory pressure determines scalability across clusters. |
| **Compilers** | Symbol tables and parse trees are analyzed for space-efficient representations. |
| **GPU Programming** | GPU memory is precious — kernel design targets minimal auxiliary space. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Iterative Sum of an Array

**Problem:** Compute the sum of `n` integers stored in an array `A[0..n-1]`.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
Algorithm: ArraySum(A, n)
Input:  An array A of n integers
Output: The sum of all n integers

1. sum ← 0          // 1 integer variable
2. for i ← 0 to n-1 do
3.     sum ← sum + A[i]
4. end for
5. return sum
```

**Space Analysis (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1:** Variable `sum` occupies 1 integer word → **1 unit of memory**.
- **Step 2:** Loop counter `i` occupies 1 integer word → **1 unit of memory**.
- **Step 3:** The input array `A` (already provided) occupies `n` units → this is **input space, NOT auxiliary space**.
- **Step 4:** The instruction space for the program is a constant `c`.
- **Step 5:** No dynamic allocation, no recursion.

**Total Space Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total}(n) &= c \quad \text{(instruction space)} \\
&\quad + 1 \quad \text{(variable 'sum')} \\
&\quad + 1 \quad \text{(variable 'i')} \\
&\quad + n \quad \text{(input array A — counted in total space)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{total}(n) = n + c + 2
$$

**Asymptotic (Big-O) form:**

$$
S_{total}(n) = O(n)
$$

**Auxiliary space only:**

$$
S_{aux}(n) = c + 2 = O(1) \quad \text{(Constant — In-Place Algorithm)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm is **in-place** because it modifies/uses constant extra memory regardless of `n`. The `O(n)` in the total space is purely from the input itself.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Recursive Factorial

**Problem:** Compute `n!` using recursion.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
Algorithm: Factorial(n)
1. if n = 0 then
2.     return 1
3. else
4.     return n × Factorial(n - 1)
5. end if
```

**Space Analysis (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1:** Each call to `Factorial` creates a stack frame.
- **Step 2:** Each stack frame stores:
  - The parameter `n` → 1 word.
  - The return address → 1 word.
- **Step 3:** The recursion depth is exactly `n + 1` (calls: `Factorial(n)`, `Factorial(n-1)`, ..., `Factorial(0)`).
- **Step 4:** Therefore, the stack space grows linearly with `n`.

**Total Space Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total}(n) &= c \quad \text{(instruction space)} \\
&\quad + (n+1) \times 2 \quad \text{(n+1 stack frames, each 2 words)} \\
&\quad + \text{any constant local variables}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{total}(n) = 2n + 2 + c
$$

**Asymptotic form:**

$$
S_{total}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(Linear space due to recursion stack)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though the **time complexity** of this recursive factorial is also `O(n)`, the space complexity is `O(n)` *only because of the recursion stack*. An iterative version using a `for` loop would have `O(1)` auxiliary space.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Naive Recursive Fibonacci (The Classic Trap)

**Problem:** Compute `Fib(n) = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2)` with base cases `Fib(0) = 0, Fib(1) = 1`.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
Algorithm: Fib(n)
1. if n ≤ 1 then
2.     return n
3. else
4.     return Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2)
5. end if
```

**Space Analysis — Common Mistake Trap:**

Many students wrongly state that the space complexity is `O(2ⁿ)` (matching the time complexity). **This is incorrect.**

**Correct Reasoning:**
- At any point in time, the **maximum depth of the call stack** is the longest path from root to leaf in the recursion tree.
- The recursion tree has depth `n`, NOT `2ⁿ` nodes-deep at any one moment.
- After `Fib(n-1)` finishes, its stack frames are **popped** before `Fib(n-2)` begins.

**Stack Depth Derivation:**

$$
\text{Maximum Stack Depth} = n + 1
$$

**Each frame holds:** parameter `n`, return address, and the partial result.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total}(n) &= c + (n+1) \times k \quad \text{(where k is a constant frame size)} \\
&= c + k(n+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{total}(n) = O(n)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Writing `O(2ⁿ)` for the space of recursive Fibonacci. The TIME is `O(2ⁿ)`, but the SPACE is only `O(n)`. The call stack is *unwound* before the second subtree is explored.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Time vs. Space Trade-off

| Strategy | Time | Space | Trade-off Type |
|----------|------|-------|----------------|
| Recursive Fibonacci | O(2ⁿ) | O(n) | **Time-heavy**, memory efficient |
| DP / Memoized Fibonacci | O(n) | O(n) | Time saved by using extra array |
| Iterative Fibonacci (loop) | O(n) | O(1) | **Best** — full optimization |
| Recursive Merge Sort | O(n log n) | O(n) | Time optimal, uses auxiliary array |
| Iterative Merge Sort | O(n log n) | O(n) or O(1)* | Slightly more code, same space |

*O(1) variant uses in-place merging but is complex and slower in practice.

---

## 3.5 The Master Theorem of Space (Recurrence)

For divide-and-conquer algorithms of the form:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

The **space recurrence** is different from the time recurrence:

$$
S(n) = S\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + O(1)
$$

This is because **only one recursive call is on the stack at a time** (assuming sequential execution). The solution is:

$$
S(n) = O(\log_b n)
$$

**Example:** Merge Sort → `S(n) = O(log n)` for the *recursion stack*, but the *auxiliary array* is `O(n)`. The dominant term is `O(n)`.

$$
S_{MergeSort}(n) = \max\{O(\log n), O(n)\} = O(n)
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of Space Complexity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Space Complexity S of Algorithm P] --> B[Fixed Part c]
    A --> C[Variable Part Sp of I]
    A --> D[Input Space]

    B --> B1[Instruction Space]
    B --> B2[Simple Variables]
    B --> B3[Constants]

    C --> C1[Composite Variables Arrays Structures]
    C --> C2[Dynamic Memory Heap]
    C --> C3[Recursion Stack]
    C --> C4[Pointers and References]

    D --> D1[Size of Input n words]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The total space an algorithm consumes is the **sum of three regions** — the fixed compile-time space, the instance-dependent variable space, and the input itself. For **auxiliary space**, the `D` block is excluded.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow: How Recursion Consumes Stack Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Algorithm Starts]) --> call1[Call fib n]
    call1 --> call2[Call fib n-1]
    call2 --> call3[Call fib n-2]
    call3 --> call4[Call fib n-3]
    call4 --> base[Base Case fib 0 reached]
    base --> ret4[Return value unwind]
    ret4 --> ret3[Return value unwind]
    ret3 --> ret2[Return value unwind]
    ret2 --> ret1[Return value unwind]
    ret1 --> end1([Algorithm Ends])
```

**Insight:** At the deepest point, the stack holds `n+1` active frames. As unwinding happens, frames are popped one by one. **Peak stack depth = n+1 = O(n).**

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Memory Layout During Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW[Low Memory Address]
        CODE[Instruction Space Compiled Binary]
        CONST[Constants and Simple Variables]
    end

    subgraph MID[Middle Memory Region]
        INPUT[Input Array of Size n]
        AUX[Auxiliary Space Temporary Variables]
    end

    subgraph HIGH[High Memory Address - Grows Downward]
        STACK[Recursion Stack Frames]
        HEAP[Dynamic Heap Allocations]
    end

    CODE --> CONST --> INPUT --> AUX --> HEAP --> STACK
```

**Engineering Note:** The stack typically grows from **high memory toward low memory**, while the heap grows from low toward high. Stack overflow occurs when recursion depth exceeds the allocated stack size — a direct consequence of poor space-complexity design.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: In-place vs. Out-of-place Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does algorithm use only constant extra memory?}
    Q1 -- YES --> IP[In-Place Algorithm O of 1 auxiliary]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Does it use log n extra memory?}
    Q2 -- YES --> IPL[In-Place Logarithmic O log n]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Does it use linear memory?}
    Q3 -- YES --> OP[Out of Place Linear O of n]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Does it use polynomial memory?}
    Q4 -- YES --> OOP[Out of Place Polynomial O of n to the k]
    Q4 -- NO --> EXP[Exponential O of 2 to the n]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always conclude a space-complexity analysis with a one-liner classification: *"This is an in-place algorithm with O(1) auxiliary space"* or *"This is an out-of-place algorithm with O(n) auxiliary space."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define space complexity. Distinguish between auxiliary space and total space complexity. [3 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Space complexity** is a measure of the amount of memory an algorithm requires as a function of the input size `n`. It includes the memory needed to store the input, the auxiliary data structures, and the runtime stack.
>
> **Auxiliary Space** is the *extra* memory used by the algorithm **excluding the space occupied by the input**. For example, in Bubble Sort, the input array occupies `O(n)` space, but the algorithm only uses a constant number of temporary variables (`i`, `j`, `temp`, `flag`), so the auxiliary space is `O(1)`.
>
> **Total Space Complexity** = **Input Space + Auxiliary Space**.
>
> Formally, `S(P) = c + S_p(I)`, where `c` is the constant fixed part and `S_p(I)` is the variable part depending on the input instance.

---

### Question A2: What is an in-place algorithm? Give two examples with their auxiliary space. [3 Marks]

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> An **in-place algorithm** is one that transforms the input using **constant extra space** (`O(1)` auxiliary), or in a relaxed sense, logarithmic extra space `O(log n)`. The input is typically overwritten by the output.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Bubble Sort** — Auxiliary space `O(1)`. Only uses loop counters and a `temp` variable for swapping.
> 2. **Selection Sort** — Auxiliary space `O(1)`. Only uses a `min_index` variable and a `temp` variable.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Insertion Sort O(1), Heap Sort O(1), Quicksort O(log n) — partial in-place.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** Derive the space complexity of the following recursive algorithm. State whether it is an in-place algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

```
Algorithm: Power(base, exp)
1. if exp = 0 then
2.     return 1
3. else
4.     return base × Power(base, exp - 1)
5. end if
```

**(b)** An iterative algorithm `SumArray` computes the sum of `n` integers using one loop counter, one accumulator, and the input array. Compute and justify its **total space complexity** and **auxiliary space complexity**. Mention whether the algorithm qualifies as in-place. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Space Complexity of Recursive `Power`

**Step 1: Identify the components contributing to space.**

- The function uses **one parameter** `base` and one parameter `exp` per call.
- The function uses **no local variables** (other than the return value and return address managed by the compiler).
- The function is **recursive** with depth `exp + 1`.

**Step 2: Determine the recursion depth.**

- The base case is reached when `exp = 0`.
- The chain of calls is: `Power(b, exp) → Power(b, exp-1) → ... → Power(b, 0)`.
- Total number of stack frames created = `exp + 1`.

**Step 3: Compute the space per frame.**

Each stack frame contains:
- 1 word for `base`
- 1 word for `exp`
- 1 word for the return address
- Space for any compiler-saved registers (treated as a constant `k`)

**Step 4: Total space derivation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total}(n) &= c \quad \text{(instruction space)} \\
&\quad + (n+1) \times (2 + k) \quad \text{(n+1 frames of constant size)} \\
&\quad + \text{no other variables}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{total}(n) = c + (n+1)(k+2) = O(n)
$$

**Step 5: Auxiliary space.**

$$
S_{aux}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(dominated by the recursion stack)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The algorithm is **NOT an in-place algorithm** because the auxiliary space is `O(n)`, not `O(1)`.

---

### Part (b) — Space Complexity of Iterative `SumArray`

**Step 1: Identify all variables.**

- `i` — loop counter (1 word)
- `sum` — accumulator (1 word)
- `A[]` — input array of size `n` (n words)
- `n` — size of input (1 word, often passed as parameter)

**Step 2: Fixed and variable parts.**

- **Fixed part** `c` = instruction space + space for `sum` + space for `i` = `c + 2`.
- **Variable part** `S_p(I)` = space for array `A` of size `n` = `n` words.

**Step 3: Total space computation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total}(n) &= c + 2 + n \\
&= n + c + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{total}(n) = O(n)
$$

**Step 4: Auxiliary space computation.**

The auxiliary space excludes the input `A`:

$$
S_{aux}(n) = c + 2 = O(1)
$$

**Step 5: Classification.**

Since the auxiliary space is `O(1)`, the algorithm **is an in-place algorithm**.

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total Space Complexity | `O(n)` |
| Auxiliary Space Complexity | `O(1)` |
| In-place? | **Yes** |
| Reason | No recursion, no dynamic allocation, only constant extra variables |

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**

**(a)** Compare the space complexity of **Recursive Fibonacci** and **Iterative Fibonacci** algorithms. Justify why the naive recursive Fibonacci has `O(n)` space and not `O(2ⁿ)`. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the **Merge Sort** algorithm on an input of size `n`, compute the **auxiliary space complexity**. State which part of the algorithm contributes the dominant term. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Recursive vs. Iterative Fibonacci

**Recursive Fibonacci:**

The recursion is `Fib(n) = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2)`.

- The recursion tree has **exponential number of nodes** (`O(2ⁿ)` total calls).
- **However**, after `Fib(n-1)` returns, its stack frames are **popped** before `Fib(n-2)` is invoked.
- At any point in time, the **maximum active stack depth** is `n + 1` (the height of the recursion tree).

**Space derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{rec}(n) &= c \quad \text{(instruction space)} \\
&\quad + (n+1) \times k \quad \text{(maximum n+1 active frames of size k)} \\
&= c + k(n+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{rec}(n) = O(n)
$$

> **Justification for O(n) and not O(2ⁿ):** The call stack is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** structure. The second recursive call (`Fib(n-2)`) only begins *after* the first one (`Fib(n-1)`) completes and its stack frames are released. Hence, the peak memory is the **height of the recursion tree**, not the total number of nodes.

**Iterative Fibonacci:**

```
Algorithm: IterativeFib(n)
1. a ← 0, b ← 1
2. for i ← 2 to n do
3.     c ← a + b
4.     a ← b
5.     b ← c
6. end for
7. return b
```

- Uses only 4 variables: `a`, `b`, `c`, `i` — each 1 word.
- **No recursion**, **no dynamic allocation**.

$$
S_{iter}(n) = c + 4 = O(1)
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Recursive Fibonacci | Iterative Fibonacci |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Total Space | `O(n)` | `O(1)` |
| Auxiliary Space | `O(n)` | `O(1)` |
| In-place? | No | **Yes** |
| Time Complexity | `O(2ⁿ)` | `O(n)` |
| Preferred? | No | **Yes** (in production) |

---

### Part (b) — Auxiliary Space of Merge Sort

**Algorithm Outline:**

```
Algorithm: MergeSort(A, low, high)
1. if low < high then
2.     mid ← (low + high) / 2
3.     MergeSort(A, low, mid)
4.     MergeSort(A, mid+1, high)
5.     Merge(A, low, mid, high)
6. end if
```

**Step 1: Identify space contributions.**

- **Input array `A`** → `O(n)` (not part of auxiliary space).
- **Recursion stack** → depth = `O(log n)` (because the array is divided in half at each level).
- **Auxiliary array** in `Merge()` step → `O(n)` to hold the temporary merged result.

**Step 2: Analyze each component.**

- **Recursion stack space:**

$$
S_{stack}(n) = S_{stack}\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1)
$$

By the Master Theorem, this solves to:

$$
S_{stack}(n) = O(\log n)
$$

- **Auxiliary array space (in Merge step):**

A temporary array of size `n` is allocated to hold the merged output, regardless of the recursion level. This is `O(n)`.

**Step 3: Total auxiliary space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{aux}(n) &= S_{stack}(n) + S_{merge}(n) \\
&= O(\log n) + O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{aux}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(since } O(n) \text{ dominates } O(\log n))
$$

**Conclusion:** The **dominant term** in the auxiliary space of Merge Sort is the **`O(n)` auxiliary array used during the `Merge()` step**, not the recursion stack.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write the total space complexity of Merge Sort as `O(n log n)`. That is the **time** complexity. The space is `O(n)`.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse the recursion stack of `O(log n)` with the auxiliary array of `O(n)`. The latter dominates.
> 3. **Do NOT** claim Merge Sort is in-place. It is **not** in-place because the auxiliary space is `O(n)`, not `O(1)`.
> 4. **Always mention** the reason for each `O(...)` term explicitly in your answer to secure full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Space Complexity:**

- **Definition:** Space complexity is the total memory an algorithm uses as a function of input size `n`. Expressed as `S(P) = c + S_p(I)`.
- **Total Space = Input Space + Auxiliary Space.** Auxiliary space excludes the input.
- **In-place algorithm** → Auxiliary space is `O(1)` (or `O(log n)` in relaxed definitions). Examples: Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Heap Sort.
- **Out-of-place algorithm** → Auxiliary space is `O(n)` or higher. Examples: Merge Sort (O(n)), naive recursive Fibonacci (O(n)).
- **Recursion increases space** by adding stack frames. The maximum space is the **depth of the recursion tree**, NOT the total number of calls.
- **Naive recursive Fibonacci:** TIME = `O(2ⁿ)`, but SPACE = `O(n)`. *This is the #1 most-mistaken question in KTU exams.*
- **Iterative algorithms** generally have `O(1)` auxiliary space.
- **Merge Sort** space = `O(n)` due to auxiliary array, NOT `O(n log n)`.
- **Quicksort** space = `O(log n)` on average (recursion stack), worst case `O(n)` on a sorted input.
- **DP / Memoization** trades **space for time** — increases auxiliary space to reduce time.
- **Always end** your exam answer with: *"Therefore, the auxiliary space complexity is `O(...)` and the algorithm is [in-place / out-of-place]."*

> **Final Note from the Examiner:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers, the most frequently awarded marks (3-mark Part A questions) test the **distinction between total and auxiliary space**. Always write *both* in your answer for full credit. A flawless answer to a "compute space complexity" problem has **three parts**: (1) identify components, (2) derive the formula, (3) classify as in-place or out-of-place.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Complexity** is a computational complexity metric that describes the **quantitative amount of computational time** taken by an algorithm to run, expressed as a function of the **length of the input (n)**. It is formally defined using **Asymptotic Notations** which characterize the upper, lower, and tight bounds of an algorithm's running time as the input size approaches **infinity**.

Mathematically, the time complexity function is denoted as $T(n)$, where $n$ is the input size and $T(n)$ returns the number of primitive operations (additions, comparisons, assignments, etc.) executed by the algorithm.

$$T : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$$

The three fundamental cases evaluated in time complexity analysis are:
- **Best Case Time Complexity** $\rightarrow$ $T_{\text{best}}(n)$ — minimum operations on any input of size $n$.
- **Average Case Time Complexity** $\rightarrow$ $T_{\text{avg}}(n)$ — expected number of operations over all possible inputs of size $n$.
- **Worst Case Time Complexity** $\rightarrow$ $T_{\text{worst}}(n)$ — maximum operations on any input of size $n$.

In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, unless specified otherwise, students are expected to compute the **worst-case time complexity** as it provides a guaranteed upper bound on the algorithm's running time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Search" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine searching for a specific book in a library.
> - If the book is on the **first shelf** you check $\rightarrow$ **Best Case** (lucky scenario).
> - If the librarian searches **every shelf systematically** $\rightarrow$ **Average Case** (expected work).
> - If the book is on the **very last shelf** checked $\rightarrow$ **Worst Case** (guaranteed upper bound).
>
> Time complexity tells us the **maximum number of "checks"** an algorithm will need as the library (input) grows from 10 books to 10 million books.

A more precise mathematical analogy: Consider a sprinter running a **100-metre dash**.
- The **track length is fixed** (constant time $O(1)$).
- Running **$n$ laps** takes time proportional to $n$ (linear time $O(n)$).
- Running a **tournament with $n$ participants in round-robin format** takes time proportional to $n^2$ (quadratic time $O(n^2)$).

## 1.3 Why Time Complexity Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Role of Time Complexity |
|---|---|
| **Database Systems** | Query optimization relies on asymptotic analysis of B-Tree/Hash operations. |
| **Networking** | Routing algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) are chosen based on $O(E \log V)$ vs. $O(VE)$. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Hard real-time deadlines demand provable worst-case bounds ($\leq$ **1 ms**). |
| **Machine Learning** | Training algorithms must scale sub-linearly or linearly to handle Big Data. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must master the distinction between **$O$, $\Theta$, and $\Omega$** notations and be able to derive recurrence relations for recursive algorithms, applying the **Master's Theorem** for divide-and-conquer recurrences.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Asymptotic Notation

> [!NOTE]
> Asymptotic notations describe the **growth rate** of functions as $n \to \infty$. Constants and lower-order terms are ignored because they become negligible for large inputs.

### 2.1.1 Big-O Notation — Upper Bound ($O$)

$f(n) = O(g(n))$ means there exist **positive constants $c$ and $n_0$** such that:

$$0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

This says: $f(n)$ grows **no faster than** $g(n)$.

### 2.1.2 Big-Omega Notation — Lower Bound ($\Omega$)

$f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ means there exist **positive constants $c$ and $n_0$** such that:

$$0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

This says: $f(n)$ grows **at least as fast as** $g(n)$.

### 2.1.3 Big-Theta Notation — Tight Bound ($\Theta$)

$f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if both $O$ and $\Omega$ conditions hold simultaneously:

$$c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

This says: $f(n)$ grows **at the same rate as** $g(n)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

| Notation | Mathematical Definition | Intuitive Meaning | Common Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $O(1)$ | Constant time | Array index access | $a[i]$ in array |
| $O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic | Halving the problem | Binary search |
| $O(n)$ | Linear | Single loop over input | Linear search |
| $O(n \log n)$ | Linearithmic | Divide & conquer | Merge sort, Heap sort |
| $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic | Nested loops | Bubble, Selection sort |
| $O(n^3)$ | Cubic | Triple nested loops | Naive matrix multiplication |
| $O(2^n)$ | Exponential | Recursive subsets | Tower of Hanoi, Fibonacci |
| $O(n!)$ | Factorial | Permutations | Brute-force TSP |

| Rule | Formula / Identity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Sum of first $n$ integers | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$ | Arithmetic series |
| Sum of squares | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} = \Theta(n^3)$ | Quadratic series |
| Geometric series | $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} x^i = \dfrac{x^n - 1}{x - 1}$ for $x \neq 1$ | Powers of a base |
| Logarithm power rule | $\log_b(a^n) = n \cdot \log_b(a)$ | Exponent extraction |
| Change of base | $\log_a n = \dfrac{\log_b n}{\log_b a}$ | Base conversion |
| Master Theorem form | $T(n) = aT\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$ | Divide & conquer recurrence |
| Recursion tree sum | $T(n) = a^k T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^k}\right) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} a^j f\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^j}\right)$ | Total cost across levels |

## 2.3 Operational Rules for Asymptotic Analysis

- **Rule of Sum:** $O(f(n)) + O(g(n)) = O(\max(f(n), g(n)))$.
  *Intuition:* The slower-growing term is absorbed by the faster one.
- **Rule of Product:** $O(f(n)) \cdot O(g(n)) = O(f(n) \cdot g(n))$.
  *Intuition:* Sequential multiplications stack growth rates.
- **Rule of Composition:** If $T_1(n) = O(f(n))$ and $T_2(n) = O(g(n))$, then nested application gives $O(f(g(n)))$ — substitution rule.
- **Polynomial Rule:** For $f(n) = a_k n^k + a_{k-1} n^{k-1} + \dots + a_0$, we have $f(n) = \Theta(n^k)$. The leading term dominates.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Asymptotic Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table illustrates why asymptotic complexity determines **algorithm scalability** in practice, assuming each primitive operation takes **1 microsecond ($\mu s$)**:

| Complexity | $n = 10$ | $n = 10^3$ | $n = 10^6$ | $n = 10^9$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $O(\log n)$ | $<1 \mu s$ | $\approx 10 \mu s$ | $\approx 20 \mu s$ | $\approx 30 \mu s$ |
| $O(n)$ | $10 \mu s$ | $1$ ms | $1$ s | $\approx 17$ min |
| $O(n \log n)$ | $\approx 33 \mu s$ | $\approx 10$ ms | $\approx 20$ s | $\approx 8$ hours |
| $O(n^2)$ | $100 \mu s$ | $1$ s | $\approx 12$ days | $\approx 31$ years |
| $O(2^n)$ | $\approx 1$ ms | Astronomically large | Infeasible | Infeasible |

This table is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question** as it visually demonstrates why an $O(n^2)$ algorithm fails for large-scale data.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sum of First $n$ Natural Numbers

**Problem Statement:** Determine the asymptotic complexity of the following loop:

```text
sum = 0
for i = 1 to n:
    sum = sum + i
```

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis:**

The loop body executes a single addition operation. The loop runs for $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$, contributing exactly $n$ iterations.

**Step 1:** Identify the per-iteration cost. Each iteration performs one addition and one comparison (the loop condition). Both are $O(1)$ operations.

**Step 2:** Count total iterations. The loop runs from $i = 1$ to $i = n$, yielding $n$ iterations.

**Step 3:** Express the total cost. The total number of primitive operations is:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(1) = O(1) \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 = O(1) \cdot n = O(n)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the closed-form formula for arithmetic series. The value accumulated in `sum` equals:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{n^2 + n}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Asymptotic reduction. Since lower-order terms and constants are dropped:

$$T(n) = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} = \Theta(n^2) \text{ for the SUM value, but } \Theta(n) \text{ for the TIME complexity}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):** The *running time* of the algorithm is $O(n)$, but the *numerical sum* it computes is $\Theta(n^2)$. Students often confuse the value with the cost.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nested Loop Analysis (Quadratic Time)

**Problem Statement:** Analyze the time complexity of the following nested loop:

```text
for i = 1 to n:
    for j = 1 to n:
        print(i, j)
```

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis:**

**Step 1:** Outer loop iterates $n$ times (from $i = 1$ to $i = n$).

**Step 2:** For each fixed value of $i$, the inner loop iterates $n$ times (from $j = 1$ to $j = n$), and the inner body is a single $O(1)$ `print` statement.

**Step 3:** Compute total operations using a double summation:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} O(1)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the inner sum first (innermost summation is constant with respect to $j$):

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(n) = O(n) \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the outer sum:

$$T(n) = O(n) \cdot n = O(n^2)$$

**Step 6:** Verifying with a closed-form sum:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n = n \cdot n = n^2 = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recursive Algorithm: Tower of Hanoi (Exponential Time)

**Problem Statement:** The Tower of Hanoi problem with $n$ disks has recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$, with base case $T(1) = 1$. Prove that $T(n) = 2^n - 1 = \Theta(2^n)$.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Proof by Induction:**

**Step 1:** Expand the recurrence by unrolling it. Substituting $T(n-1) = 2T(n-2) + 1$ into the original:

$$T(n) = 2[2T(n-2) + 1] + 1 = 4T(n-2) + 2 + 1$$

**Step 2:** Continue unrolling one more level. Substituting $T(n-2) = 2T(n-3) + 1$:

$$T(n) = 4[2T(n-3) + 1] + 3 = 8T(n-3) + 4 + 3$$

**Step 3:** Identify the pattern. After $k$ unrolling steps:

$$T(n) = 2^k \cdot T(n - k) + (2^k - 1)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the base case. Set $n - k = 1$, so $k = n - 1$. Then $T(1) = 1$:

$$T(n) = 2^{n-1} \cdot T(1) + (2^{n-1} - 1)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $T(1) = 1$:

$$T(n) = 2^{n-1} \cdot 1 + 2^{n-1} - 1 = 2 \cdot 2^{n-1} - 1 = 2^n - 1$$

**Step 6:** Asymptotic conclusion:

$$T(n) = 2^n - 1 = \Theta(2^n)$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Master Theorem Application (Merge Sort)

**Problem Statement:** The Merge Sort recurrence is $T(n) = 2T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)$. Determine the asymptotic complexity.

The Master Theorem has three cases for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$:

- **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.
- **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ and regularity holds, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2:** Compute the critical exponent: $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$.

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n) = n$ with $n^{\log_b a} = n$. We have $f(n) = \Theta(n)$, which matches the case-2 form with $k = 0$.

**Step 4:** Apply Case 2 of the Master Theorem:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^1 \cdot \log^1 n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Step 5 (Verification via recursion tree):** The recursion tree for Merge Sort has $\log_2 n$ levels. Each level performs $\Theta(n)$ total work for the merge step. Total work:

$$T(n) = \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n = n \cdot \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Binary Search Time Complexity

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the worst-case time complexity of binary search is $\Theta(\log n)$.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis:**

**Step 1:** The recurrence for binary search is:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1), \quad T(1) = O(1)$$

**Step 2:** Unroll the recurrence. Each recursive call halves the input size and performs a constant-time comparison:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c + c = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2c$$

**Step 3:** Generalizing for $k$ levels of unrolling:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot c$$

**Step 4:** Termination condition. The recursion stops when $\dfrac{n}{2^k} = 1$, which gives $2^k = n$, so $k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the equation:

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \cdot \log_2 n = d + c \cdot \log_2 n$$

**Step 6:** Asymptotic conclusion:

$$T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to write $T(n) = \log n$ without specifying the base. The base does **not** affect asymptotic notation, but you should write $\log_2 n$ for full marks in derivations.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Standard Algorithms

| Algorithm | Recurrence Relation | Final Time Complexity | Master Theorem Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binary Search | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Case 1 |
| Merge Sort | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Case 2 |
| Quick Sort (avg) | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Case 2 |
| Quick Sort (worst) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Not applicable |
| Strassen's Multiplication | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ | $\Theta(n^{2.81})$ | Case 1 |
| Karatsuba Multiplication | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$ | Case 1 |
| Linear Search | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Linear recurrence |
| Tower of Hanoi | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ | Exponential recurrence |
| Fibonacci (naive) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\phi^n)$, $\phi \approx 1.618$ | Linear recurrence |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Algorithm Analysis Pipeline

The following diagram illustrates the standard procedure a KTU student should follow when analyzing the time complexity of an algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Algorithm Pseudocode] --> stepB[Identify Input Size n]
    stepB --> stepC[Identify Primitive Operations]
    stepC --> stepD{Loop or Recursion?}
    stepD -->|Loops| stepE[Sum the per-iteration cost]
    stepD -->|Recursion| stepF[Formulate Recurrence Relation]
    stepE --> stepG[Apply Algebraic Identities]
    stepF --> stepH{Recurrence Form}
    stepH -->|Divide and Conquer| stepI[Apply Master Theorem]
    stepH -->|Linear Recurrence| stepJ[Unroll and Solve]
    stepH -->|Substitution| stepK[Trial Solution Method]
    stepG --> stepL[Drop Constants and Lower-Order Terms]
    stepI --> stepL
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM[Final Asymptotic Notation]
    stepM --> stepN[Report O, Theta, or Omega Bound]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    classA[O log n] --> classB[O n]
    classB --> classC[O n log n]
    classC --> classD[O n squared]
    classD --> classE[O n cubed]
    classE --> classF[O two to n]
    classF --> classG[O n factorial]
    style classA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style classB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each arrow $A \rightarrow B$ means the complexity class of $A$ is asymptotically **smaller (faster)** than that of $B$. Reading left-to-right shows the **slowest to fastest growth** progression. The color coding follows a traffic-light convention: green is highly efficient, yellow is moderate, and red is intractable for large $n$.

## 4.3 Recursion Tree Schematic for Merge Sort

```mermaid
graph TD
    level0["Level 0: Problem size n, cost n"]
    level1a["Level 1 left: n/2, cost n/2"]
    level1b["Level 1 right: n/2, cost n/2"]
    level2a["Level 2: n/4, cost n/4"]
    level2b["Level 2: n/4, cost n/4"]
    level2c["Level 2: n/4, cost n/4"]
    level2d["Level 2: n/4, cost n/4"]
    level3["Level log n: size 1, cost 1"]
    level0 --> level1a
    level0 --> level1b
    level1a --> level2a
    level1a --> level2b
    level1b --> level2c
    level1b --> level2d
    level2a --> level3
    level2b --> level3
    level2c --> level3
    level2d --> level3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Recursion Tree:** The recursion tree has $\log_2 n$ levels. At each level, the **total work is exactly $\Theta(n)$** because the merge step processes all elements. The total cost is therefore $\Theta(n) \times \log n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Choosing the Correct Asymptotic Notation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[Given Functions f n and g n] --> q2{Is f n bounded above by some constant times g n for large n?}
    q2 -->|Yes| q3[Then f n = O of g n]
    q2 -->|No| q4{Is f n bounded below by some constant times g n for large n?}
    q4 -->|Yes| q5[Then f n = Omega of g n]
    q4 -->|No| q6{Is f n bounded both above and below by constant multiples of g n?}
    q6 -->|Yes| q7[Then f n = Theta of g n]
    q6 -->|No| q8[None of the above may apply directly]
    q3 --> endA[Result: Upper Bound]
    q5 --> endB[Result: Lower Bound]
    q7 --> endC[Result: Tight Bound]
    q8 --> endD[Result: Use Little o or Little omega]
```
