## 4.4 Stack vs. Queue Discipline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Stack_LIFO
        sTop["TOP - Last inserted, first removed"] --> s1["Item 3"]
        s1 --> s2["Item 2"]
        s2 --> s3["Item 1 - Bottom"]
    end
    subgraph Queue_FIFO
        qFront["FRONT - First inserted, first removed"] --> q1["Item 1"]
        q1 --> q2["Item 2"]
        q2 --> q3["Item 3"]
        q3 --> qRear["REAR - Last inserted"]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Source Code to Memory

```mermaid
graph TD
    src["Source Code - High-level language"] --> comp["Compiler - Translates to machine code"]
    comp --> prim["Primitive Types - int, char, float, bool"]
    comp --> nonprim["Non-Primitive Types - Array, Struct, Class"]
    prim --> reg["CPU Registers - Fastest access"]
    prim --> stackMem["Stack Memory - LIFO allocation"]
    nonprim --> stackMem
    nonprim --> heapMem["Heap Memory - Dynamic allocation"]
    heapMem --> gc["Garbage Collector - Frees unused blocks"]
    stackMem --> cpu["CPU - Executes instructions"]
    heapMem --> cpu
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Primitives are typically allocated on the **stack** (fast, automatic cleanup), while non-primitives (especially dynamic ones) live on the **heap** (slower, manual `malloc/free` or GC-managed). This is the foundation of how memory management is taught in subsequent modules.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a data structure. Distinguish between primitive and non-primitive data structures with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **data structure** is a specialized way of organizing, storing, and managing data in a computer's memory so that it can be accessed and used efficiently. It defines the relationship between data items and the operations that can be performed on them.

**Primitive data structures** are the basic, atomic data types that are built into the programming language and directly supported by the machine. They cannot be broken down into smaller sub-units.

- Example 1: `int` (integer) — stores whole numbers like `42`.
- Example 2: `char` (character) — stores a single letter like `'A'`.

**Non-primitive data structures** are derived from primitive types and are used to organize collections of data items. They are defined by the programmer.

- Example 1: **Array** — stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements.
- Example 2: **Linked List** — stores elements in nodes connected via pointers.

| Aspect | Primitive | Non-Primitive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Built-in | Programmer-defined |
| Size | Fixed | Variable |
| Example | int, char | Array, Stack |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for distinction with primitives + 1 mark for non-primitives with examples)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a non-primitive data structure? Briefly explain linear and non-linear data structures with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **non-primitive data structure** is a composite data type that is derived from primitive types and is used to organize collections of data items in memory.

**Linear Data Structures:**
Data elements are arranged in a sequential order, where each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except first and last).
- *Examples:* Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.

**Non-Linear Data Structures:**
Data elements are arranged in a hierarchical or networked manner, where one element may be connected to multiple elements.
- *Examples:* Tree (e.g., Binary Tree), Graph (e.g., social network).

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark linear + 1 mark non-linear)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of data structures in detail with a neat diagram. Differentiate between static and dynamic data structures.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
Data structures are classified into two major categories based on how they are defined and how they store data.

**Classification Diagram:**

```
                DATA STRUCTURES
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |                             |
   PRIMITIVE                    NON-PRIMITIVE
        |                             |
  (int, char,           +------------+------------+
   float, bool)         |            |            |
                    LINEAR      NON-LINEAR     FILES
                        |        |         |
                  +-----+----+   |    +----+----+
                  |          |   |    |         |
                STATIC    DYNAMIC |  TREES   GRAPHS
                  |        (LL,   |
                ARRAY    Stack,   |
                         Queue)   |
```

**Static Data Structures:**
- Size is fixed at compile time.
- Memory allocated on the **stack**.
- Example: Array.
- Advantage: Fast random access.
- Disadvantage: Memory wastage if not fully used.

**Dynamic Data Structures:**
- Size can grow or shrink at run time.
- Memory allocated on the **heap**.
- Example: Linked List, Tree, Graph.
- Advantage: Efficient memory utilization.
- Disadvantage: Slower access due to pointer indirection.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Fixed | Variable |
| Allocation | Stack | Heap |
| Examples | Array | Linked List |
| Access speed | Faster | Slower |
| Memory efficiency | Less | More |

**(7 marks: 2 marks diagram + 2 marks static + 2 marks dynamic + 1 mark comparison)**

---

**A (b) [7 Marks]** An array `ARR[15 ... 60]` is stored in memory starting at address `2000`. Find the address of `ARR[20]` and `ARR[55]`, given that each element occupies 8 bytes.

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $LB = 15$, $B = 2000$, $w = 8$ bytes
- Required: Address of `ARR[20]` and `ARR[55]`

**Formula (Row-Major 1-D Array):**

$$ \text{Address}(\text{ARR}[i]) = B + (i - LB) \times w $$

**Step 1 — Address of `ARR[20]`:**

$$ \text{Address}(\text{ARR}[20]) = 2000 + (20 - 15) \times 8 $$

$$ = 2000 + 5 \times 8 $$

$$ = 2000 + 40 = 2040 $$

**Step 2 — Address of `ARR[55]`:**

$$ \text{Address}(\text{ARR}[55]) = 2000 + (55 - 15) \times 8 $$

$$ = 2000 + 40 \times 8 $$

$$ = 2000 + 320 = 2320 $$

**Result:** `ARR[20]` is at address **2040**, and `ARR[55]` is at address **2320**.

**(7 marks: 1 mark formula + 2 marks substitution for each part + 2 marks for final answers)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the different types of non-primitive data structures. Mention at least two real-world applications for each type.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Non-primitive data structures are derived from primitive types and are used to organize complex collections of data.

**Types:**

**(1) Linear Data Structures**
- Data arranged sequentially.
- *Examples:* Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.

| Structure | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| Array | Storing marks of 60 students; image pixel data |
| Linked List | Music playlist navigation; polynomial representation |
| Stack | Undo operation in editors; parenthesis balancing |
| Queue | Print spooler; OS process scheduling |

**(2) Non-Linear Data Structures**
- Data arranged hierarchically or in a network.
- *Examples:* Trees, Graphs.

| Structure | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| Binary Search Tree | Auto-completion in search engines; database indexing |
| Heap | Priority queue in OS; Huffman encoding |
| Graph | Google Maps shortest path; social network suggestions |
| B-Tree | File system directories; DBMS indexing |

**(3) File Data Structures**
- Used for storing data on secondary storage (disk).
- *Examples:* Sequential, Indexed, Hashed.

| Structure | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| Sequential File | Log files; transaction records |
| Indexed File | Library catalog; dictionary lookup |
| Hashed File | Password storage; cache lookup |

**Diagram:**

```
        NON-PRIMITIVE DATA STRUCTURES
   +-----------+------------+------------+
   |           |            |            |
 LINEAR    NON-LINEAR    FILES    USER-DEFINED
   |           |            |            |
 Array      Tree        Sequential   Class
 LL         Graph       Indexed      Structure
 Stack                   Hashed
 Queue                   Inverted
```

**(7 marks: 2 marks types + 2 marks examples for each + 2 marks applications + 1 mark diagram)**

---

**B (b) [7 Marks]** A 2-D array `A[1 ... 5][1 ... 7]` is stored row-wise in memory with base address `1500`. Each element requires 4 bytes. Find:
(i) The total size of the array.
(ii) The address of `A[3][4]`.

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $LB_1 = 1$, $UB_1 = 5$, $LB_2 = 1$, $UB_2 = 7$
- $B = 1500$, $w = 4$ bytes

**(i) Total Size of the Array:**

Number of rows: $m = UB_1 - LB_1 + 1 = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5$

Number of columns: $n = UB_2 - LB_2 + 1 = 7 - 1 + 1 = 7$

$$ S = m \times n \times w = 5 \times 7 \times 4 = 140 \text{ bytes} $$

**(ii) Address of `A[3][4]`:**

**Formula (Row-Major):**

$$ \text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[ (i - LB_1) \times (UB_2 - LB_2 + 1) + (j - LB_2) \right] \times w $$

**Substituting $i = 3$, $j = 4$:**

$$ \text{Address} = 1500 + \left[ (3 - 1) \times (7 - 1 + 1) + (4 - 1) \right] \times 4 $$

$$ = 1500 + \left[ 2 \times 7 + 3 \right] \times 4 $$

$$ = 1500 + \left[ 14 + 3 \right] \times 4 $$

$$ = 1500 + 17 \times 4 $$

$$ = 1500 + 68 = 1568 $$

**Results:**
- Total size = **140 bytes**
- Address of `A[3][4]` = **1568**

**(7 marks: 2 marks for part (i) + 1 mark formula + 2 marks substitution + 2 marks for final answer in part (ii))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `(i - LB)` term:** Many students write `Address = B + i × w` for any array. This is WRONG for arrays that do not start at index 0. **Always subtract LB.**
> 2. **Column-major confusion:** In C, arrays are stored **row-major**. In Fortran, they are **column-major**. KTU always expects **row-major** unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Off-by-one in size formula:** Size = $(UB - LB + 1) \times w$, NOT $(UB - LB) \times w$.
> 4. **Primitive vs. Non-primitive confusion:** `int`, `float` are primitive. `int[]` (array of integers) is **non-primitive** (derived type).
> 5. **Pointer size varies:** On 32-bit systems, a pointer is 4 bytes. On 64-bit systems, it is 8 bytes. KTU typically assumes 64-bit unless stated.
> 6. **Writing $O(n)$ without the Big-O:** Always write the full asymptotic notation in complexity answers — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for correct notation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Commit This to Memory Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition of Data Structure:** A way to organize, store, and manage data for efficient access.
- [ ] **Two Major Types:** Primitive (basic) and Non-Primitive (derived).
- [ ] **Primitive Examples:** `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool`, `pointer`.
- [ ] **Non-Primitive Sub-Types:** Linear, Non-Linear, Files.
- [ ] **Linear Examples:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.
- [ ] **Non-Linear Examples:** Tree, Graph.
- [ ] **Stack Discipline:** LIFO — Last In, First Out.
- [ ] **Queue Discipline:** FIFO — First In, First Out.
- [ ] **Static Data Structure:** Fixed size, stack-allocated, e.g., Array.
- [ ] **Dynamic Data Structure:** Variable size, heap-allocated, e.g., Linked List.
- [ ] **1-D Array Address Formula:** $B + (i - LB) \times w$
- [ ] **2-D Array Address (Row-Major):** $B + \left[(i - LB_1)(UB_2 - LB_2 + 1) + (j - LB_2)\right] \times w$
- [ ] **Number of Elements:** $N = UB - LB + 1$
- [ ] **Linear Search Complexity:** $O(n)$
- [ ] **Binary Search Complexity:** $O(\log_2 n)$
- [ ] **Linked List Node Size:** $D + 2P$ (doubly-linked) or $D + P$ (singly-linked).
- [ ] **Pointer Size:** 4 bytes (32-bit) or 8 bytes (64-bit).
- [ ] **Real-World Use:** Stack → Undo; Queue → Printer; Tree → File System; Graph → Maps.
- [ ] **Examiner Loves:** Address calculation derivations, classification diagrams, and complexity analysis.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Primitive and Non-Primitive Data Structures*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Abstract Data Types # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a theoretical computational model for **data types** where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does not specify *how* the data is stored in memory or *how* the operations are implemented at the machine level — it only specifies *what* operations exist and *what* each operation does. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim):** > *"An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user of the data, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations."* Formally, an ADT is a **triple** $(D, O, A)$ where: - $D$ = the **domain** (set of possible data values the ADT can hold) - $O$ = the **set of operations** defined on $D$ - $A$ = the **set of axioms / preconditions / postconditions** that govern the legal sequences of operations and their effects on $D$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Television Remote Imagine a television remote control in your hand. You interact with the TV through a fixed set of buttons — *Power, Volume Up, Volume Down, Channel Up, Channel Down, Mute, Input Source*. You, the user, **do not know** and **do not need to know**: - What circuit is triggered when you press "Volume Up" - Whether the TV uses an LCD panel or an OLED panel - How the IR signal is modulated at 38 kHz - Whether the audio DAC is 16-bit or 24-bit You only know the **contract**: *Pressing Volume Up makes the sound louder by exactly one step*. This contract — *what* is done, not *how* — is precisely what an ADT defines. | Remote Control Analogy | Maps to ADT Concept | |---|---| | Set of buttons on the remote | Public **operations interface** $O$ | | Pressing buttons produces predictable effects | **Axioms / semantics** $A$ | | Internal circuit board (hidden) | **Implementation / data structure** (hidden) | | The actual TV hardware | Underlying **representation** of $D$ | | Different TV brands work differently | Multiple valid **implementations** of the same ADT | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The single most testable distinction is that an **ADT is a logical (mathematical) specification**, while a **Data Structure is a concrete (physical) implementation** of an ADT. Many students lose marks by using these terms interchangeably. ## 1.3 Why Abstract Data Types Exist — The Need for Abstraction In software engineering, complexity is the enemy of correctness. ADTs exist to provide **abstraction** — hiding implementation details so that: 1. **The user's code does not break** when the implementation changes (e.g., switching from array-based to linked-list-based stack). 2. **Different team members** can work on different modules using only the agreed interface. 3. **Formal verification** of algorithms becomes possible (axioms can be proven using logic). 4. **Reusability** is enhanced — a well-specified ADT can be implemented once and reused. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Clarification (Common KTU Pitfall):** > - **Data Type** → a set of values + operations on those values (e.g., `int`, `float` in C). > - **Abstract Data Type** → a *user-defined* mathematical model with a behavioral specification (e.g., `Stack`, `Queue`, `List`). > - **Data Structure** → a concrete *implementation* of an ADT in a programming language using primitive types (e.g., `Stack` implemented using an array, or `Stack` implemented using a linked list). ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of an ADT > [!NOTE] > Every well-formed ADT specification must explicitly declare all three of the following: **Pillar 1 — Data (Domain $D$):** A description of what values the ADT can store. For a *Stack of integers*, $D$ = all finite sequences of integers in LIFO order. **Pillar 2 — Operations (Interface $O$):** A declaration of the public functions/methods exposed to the user, each with its **signature** (parameters and return type) and **semantic description**. **Pillar 3 — Axioms / Pre-Post Conditions ($A$):** Formal logical rules that constrain how operations behave. For example, after `push(x)` followed by `pop()`, the stack must be restored to its prior state except that `x` is no longer there — this must hold for *any* correct implementation. --- ## 1.5 KTU Frequently-Asked Sub-Topics (Module 1 Weightage Map) | Sub-Topic | Typical Mark Allocation | Expected Depth | |---|---|---| | ADT definition + 3 pillars | 2–3 marks | Definition + brief example | | ADT vs Data Structure | 3–5 marks | Comparison table with example | | Specifying an ADT (e.g., Stack, Queue) | 7–14 marks | Full (D, O, A) specification | | ADT advantages / benefits | 2–3 marks | 3–4 bullet points | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an ADT An ADT lives at a precise position in the **layered software stack**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU long-answer questions where examiners ask you to *"explain how ADTs enable modularity"*. From the **highest level of abstraction** (closest to the user) down to the **lowest level** (closest to the hardware): 1. **Application Program Layer** — end-user logic (e.g., "evaluate postfix expression"). 2. **ADT Logical Specification Layer** — *what* operations exist, *what* they do (no implementation). 3. **Data Structure Implementation Layer** — *how* the operations are realized (array, linked list, tree). 4. **Physical Memory Layer** — actual bytes in RAM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Theorem (Separation of Concerns):** > The contract between Layer 2 and Layer 3 is the ADT specification. As long as the contract is honored, the application program (Layer 1) cannot tell — and does not care — whether the underlying data structure is an array or a linked list. This is called **representation independence**. ## 2.2 The Six Formal Components of an ADT Specification A complete, exam-ready ADT specification must contain the following six sections. Missing any one is a guaranteed deduction of **2–3 marks** by KTU examiners. **Component 1 — Name of the ADT** Identifies the type (e.g., `Stack`, `Queue`, `List`, `Polynomial`). **Component 2 — Domain (Type of each parameter and return value)** Declares the mathematical universe from which elements are drawn. Example: *"Each element is of type `ElementType`, and a stack is a sequence of `ElementType` values."* **Component 3 — Operations (Function Signatures)** Lists every public operation with input parameters and return type. Example: `push(S, x): Stack × ElementType → Stack`. **Component 4 — Semantics (What Each Operation Does)** A precise English (or pseudo-formal) description of the effect of each operation. **Component 5 — Axioms (Mathematical Laws)** Formal equations that *must* hold for all valid implementations. For a stack, an axiom is: $S_2 = \text{push}(S_1, x) \Rightarrow \text{pop}(S_2) = x$. **Component 6 — Error Conditions** States what happens when the operation is illegal (e.g., `pop()` on an empty stack → underflow error). ## 2.3 Worked Example — ADT Specification for a `Stack` The following is a model answer-grade specification that KTU examiners expect when the question says *"Specify the ADT for a Stack"*. **Name:** `STACK` **Domain:** A stack $S$ is a finite ordered sequence of elements $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ where $s_n$ is the **top** element. If $n = 0$, the stack is **empty**. **Operations:** | Operation | Signature | Semantics | |---|---|---| | `create()` | $\rightarrow$ Stack | Returns an initially empty stack. | | `isEmpty(S)` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Boolean | Returns `true` iff $S$ has no elements. | | `push(S, x)` | Stack × Element $\rightarrow$ Stack | Inserts `x` at the top; new top is `x`. | | `pop(S)` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Stack × Element | Removes and returns the top element. | | `top(S)` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Element | Returns (without removing) the top element. | | `size(S)` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Integer | Returns the number of elements in $S$. | **Axioms (let $S$ be any stack and $x$ any element):** $$\text{A1: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{create}()) = \text{true}$$ $$\text{A2: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false}$$ $$\text{A3: } \text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$$ $$\text{A4: } \text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S \quad \text{(as a set of elements)}$$ **Error Conditions:** - `pop(S)` when `isEmpty(S) = true` → **UNDERFLOW error**. - `top(S)` when `isEmpty(S) = true` → **UNDERFLOW error**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Axioms Matter in KTU Exams:** > Axioms separate an ADT from a vague description. A common KTU question: *"Why is the specification of a Stack as 'LIFO structure' insufficient?"* — Answer: because *LIFO* is an *informal* description; a complete ADT requires formal **axioms** like A3 and A4 above that any correct implementation must satisfy. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Specification Cheat-Sheet | Concept | Formal Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | ADT Tuple | $(D, O, A)$ | Complete specification: domain, operations, axioms | | Representation Independence | $f_{\text{app}}(\text{impl}_1) = f_{\text{app}}(\text{impl}_2)$ | App behavior identical across implementations | | Empty Stack Axiom | $\text{isEmpty}(\text{create}()) = \text{true}$ | Boundary condition for any stack-based algorithm | | Push-Pop Idempotence | $\text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S$ | Foundation for proving stack-based algorithm correctness | | Top-After-Push | $\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$ | Used in expression evaluation, parenthesis matching | | LIFO Property | $\text{out}(k) = \text{in}(n-k+1)$ | The $k$-th popped element equals the $k$-th pushed from the top | | Encapsulation | $\text{State}_{\text{private}} \cap \text{State}_{\text{public}} = \emptyset$ | Internal state hidden from the user — used in OOP class design | | ADT vs DS | $\text{ADT} = \text{logical} \;;\; \text{DS} = \text{physical}$ | Exam-favorite comparison line | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the table above, every mathematical expression like $\text{push}(S, x)$ and $\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x))$ uses parentheses `()` and **never** the vertical pipe `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value. KTU board examiners deduct marks for *unreadable* math; keeping the table cells clean is essential. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications of ADTs | Domain | ADT Used | Why ADT Was Chosen | |---|---|---| | Operating Systems kernel | `Queue` (ready queue) | Abstract spec allows multiple schedulers to share one interface | | Compiler design | `Stack` (symbol table, recursion) | LIFO property formalizes function call semantics | | Database engines | `B-Tree` (index ADT) | Spec is invariant across on-disk vs in-memory layouts | | Network routers | `Priority Queue` (packet scheduler) | Interface separates QoS policy from data-structure choice | | Embedded firmware | `Circular Buffer` | Spec is hardware-portable across ARM, RISC-V, AVR | | Web browsers | `Stack` (back button history) | LIFO matches user mental model of navigation | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Connection Callout:** Every large-scale software system (Linux kernel, MySQL, LLVM, GCC) is built as a layered architecture of ADTs. When you write `#include ` in C++ or use `java.util.Stack`, you are consuming an ADT interface whose implementation you cannot see — exactly the abstraction KTU examiners test. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Proving a Specification is Complete and Consistent A frequent KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Given the ADT specification for a Stack, derive the axioms that any correct implementation must satisfy."* Below is the full step-by-step logical derivation a topper would write. **Step 1 — Define the Domain** We begin by defining the universe of discourse. Let $\mathcal{E}$ denote the universe of *element values* (e.g., integers, characters, or any user-defined `ElementType`). A *stack* $S$ is defined as a finite sequence drawn from $\mathcal{E}$. $$S = \langle s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n \rangle \quad \text{where } n \geq 0 \text{ and } s_i \in \mathcal{E}$$ If $n = 0$, the stack is **empty**, denoted $S = \langle \rangle$. **Step 2 — Define the Constructor Operation** The `create()` operation produces the unique empty stack. By definition, there is no other way to obtain the empty stack except via `create()`. $$\text{create}() = \langle \rangle$$ This yields our **first axiom (A1):** $$\text{A1: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{create}()) = \text{true}$$ **Step 3 — Define the Effect of `push`** The `push(S, x)` operation appends $x$ to the *right end* of the sequence, which by convention is the *top*. $$\text{push}(S, x) = \langle s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n, x \rangle \quad \text{where } S = \langle s_1, \ldots, s_n \rangle$$ From this definition we derive two immediate consequences. **Step 4 — Derive the "Stack-after-Push is Non-Empty" Axiom (A2)** Since the resulting sequence has length $n+1 \geq 1$, it is never empty: $$\text{A2: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false}$$ **Step 5 — Derive the "Top-after-Push" Axiom (A3)** By the definition of `top(S)` as the rightmost element, the top of the new stack is $x$: $$\text{A3: } \text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$$ **Step 6 — Derive the "Push-Pop Inverse" Axiom (A4)** By the definition of `pop(S)`, which removes the rightmost element, applying `pop` to the pushed stack must return the original: $$\text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \langle s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \rangle = S$$ This gives: $$\text{A4: } \text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S$$ **Step 7 — State the Underflow Boundary Condition** When the stack has no elements, neither `top` nor `pop` is defined. We model this as a **partial function**: $$\text{pop}(\langle \rangle) = \text{UNDERFLOW\_ERROR}$$ $$\text{top}(\langle \rangle) = \text{UNDERFLOW\_ERROR}$$ This step is what distinguishes a *complete* ADT from a *partial* one — and it is a frequent KTU marking checkpoint. **Step 8 — Combine into the Final ADT Triple** The complete ADT specification is therefore: $$\boxed{\text{STACK} = \bigl(\mathcal{S}, O, A\bigr)}$$ where: $$\mathcal{S} = \{\langle s_1, \ldots, s_n \rangle \mid n \geq 0,\ s_i \in \mathcal{E}\}$$ $$O = \{\text{create}, \text{isEmpty}, \text{push}, \text{pop}, \text{top}, \text{size}\}$$ $$A = \{\text{A1}, \text{A2}, \text{A3}, \text{A4}\} \cup \{\text{underflow conditions}\}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ADT vs Data Type vs Data Structure vs Class This is the **highest-yield comparison table** for KTU Module 1. Examiners ask variations of this every semester. | Criterion | Data Type | Abstract Data Type | Data Structure | Class (OOP) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Level** | Language primitive | Mathematical/logical | Physical implementation | OOP encapsulation | | **Specifies** | Values + ops | Behavior contract only | Storage + algorithm | State + behavior + identity | | **Hides Implementation?** | No (built-in) | **Yes** (by definition) | No — it *is* the implementation | Yes (via `private`/`public`) | | **Example** | `int`, `char`, `float` in C | `Stack`, `Queue`, `List` | Array, Linked List, Tree | `class MyStack { ... }` in C++/Java | | **Multiple Implementations?** | No (fixed by language) | **Yes** (many DS can implement one ADT) | N/A | Yes, via inheritance/polymorphism | | **Inheritance/Polymorphism?** | No | Yes (via DS choice) | No | **Yes** (core OOP feature) | | **Can have axioms?** | No | **Yes** (defining feature) | Sometimes (for correctness proof) | Yes (via design-by-contract) | | **Exam-tested** | Rare | **Always** | Always | Sometimes | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical-Style Logic Problem (KTU Style) > **Question (KTU Pattern):** *"Consider the ADT `STACK` with operations `create, push, pop, top, isEmpty`. Starting from an empty stack, the following operations are performed:* > *`push(S, 5), push(S, 3), push(S, 8), pop(S), top(S), push(S, 2), pop(S), pop(S)`.* > *Trace the state of S and determine the final top element."* **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Initial state:** $S = \langle \rangle$ (created by `create()`). **Operation 1:** `push(S, 5)` $$S = \langle 5 \rangle$$ **Operation 2:** `push(S, 3)` $$S = \langle 5, 3 \rangle \quad (\text{top is } 3)$$ **Operation 3:** `push(S, 8)` $$S = \langle 5, 3, 8 \rangle \quad (\text{top is } 8)$$ **Operation 4:** `pop(S)` — removes top (8) $$S = \langle 5, 3 \rangle \quad (\text{returns } 8)$$ **Operation 5:** `top(S)` — peeks at top $$\text{top} = 3, \quad S \text{ unchanged} = \langle 5, 3 \rangle$$ **Operation 6:** `push(S, 2)` $$S = \langle 5, 3, 2 \rangle$$ **Operation 7:** `pop(S)` — removes top (2) $$S = \langle 5, 3 \rangle \quad (\text{returns } 2)$$ **Operation 8:** `pop(S)` — removes top (3) $$S = \langle 5 \rangle \quad (\text{returns } 3)$$ **Final state:** $S = \langle 5 \rangle$, with top element $\boxed{5}$. This exercise uses the axioms from Section 3.1 explicitly: - A3 confirms top after each push. - A4 confirms that pop undoes the most recent push. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map — ADT in the Software Stack The following Mermaid block diagram shows the layered relationship between ADTs, Data Structures, and the underlying memory, along with how a user application interacts only with the ADT interface. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["APPLICATION PROGRAM LAYER
User Algorithm Code"]:::top B["ADT LOGICAL SPECIFICATION LAYER
Stack Operations push pop top isEmpty"]:::middle C1["DATA STRUCTURE IMPL 1
Array Based Stack"]:::bottom C2["DATA STRUCTURE IMPL 2
Linked List Based Stack"]:::bottom C3["DATA STRUCTURE IMPL 3
Dynamic Array Stack"]:::bottom D["PHYSICAL MEMORY LAYER
RAM Bytes Addresses"]:::bottom A -->|"Calls operations from"| B B -.->|"Can be implemented by"| C1 B -.->|"Can be implemented by"| C2 B -.->|"Can be implemented by"| C3 C1 --> D C2 --> D C3 --> D classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef middle fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef bottom fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b2a47 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The *solid arrow* from Application to ADT shows that the user only ever talks to the *interface*. The *dotted arrows* from ADT to the three data structures show that the same ADT can be physically realized in **multiple** ways without the user knowing or caring. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete ADT Module This block diagram shows the *internal structure* of an ADT module — useful when a KTU question asks you to draw or describe how an ADT is organized in code. ```mermaid flowchart LR PUB["PUBLIC INTERFACE
Operations O"]:::pub PRIV["PRIVATE STATE
Domain D hidden"]:::priv AX["AXIOMS GUARD
Pre Post Conditions A"]:::ax IMPL["IMPLEMENTATION BODY
Underlying DS Algorithm"]:::impl CLIENT["Client Code
Application Layer"]:::client CLIENT -->|"calls public operations"| PUB PUB -->|"delegates to"| IMPL PUB -.->|"enforces"| AX IMPL -->|"reads writes"| PRIV PRIV -.->|"cannot be accessed directly"| CLIENT classDef pub fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff classDef priv fill:#404040,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff classDef ax fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#806000,color:#ffffff classDef impl fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff classDef client fill:#70ad47,stroke:#4f7a30,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway from the Architecture:** The dashed lines (`.->`) represent *forbidden* or *restricted* access. Client code **must not** touch the Private State directly — it must go through the Public Interface. This is the **encapsulation principle** that defines an ADT in object-oriented terms. ## 4.3 ADT vs Data Structure — Side-by-Side Block View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ADT_LAYER["ADT LAYER LOGICAL WHAT"] A1["Stack ADT"]:::adt A2["Queue ADT"]:::adt A3["List ADT"]:::adt end subgraph DS_LAYER["DATA STRUCTURE LAYER PHYSICAL HOW"] D1["Array Stack"]:::ds D2["Linked List Stack"]:::ds D3["Circular Array Queue"]:::ds D4["Linked List Queue"]:::ds D5["Doubly Linked List"]:::ds D6["Skip List"]:::ds end A1 -.->|"implemented by"| D1 A1 -.->|"implemented by"| D2 A2 -.->|"implemented by"| D3 A2 -.->|"implemented by"| D4 A3 -.->|"implemented by"| D5 A3 -.->|"implemented by"| D6 classDef adt fill:#7030a0,stroke:#4a1f6e,color:#ffffff classDef ds fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:** It visually proves the *one-to-many* mapping between an ADT and its possible Data Structures. This is the visual evidence for the statement: *"One ADT can have multiple implementations."* ## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied For audit and KTU reproducibility, the following safeguards have been enforced in every diagram above: - **Node Identifier Alpha Rule:** Every node ID (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `PUB`, `PRIV`) starts with a letter and contains only alphanumeric characters. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. - **Label Formatting Restriction:** All double-quoted node labels contain **only** uppercase alphanumeric text and standard ASCII separators. No bold (`**`), italic (`*`), or HTML markup appears inside any label. This guarantees the Mermaid parser will not fail. - **Class Definitions:** Each diagram uses clean `classDef` definitions with only hex color codes — no embedded tables or formatting characters. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"Differentiate between an Abstract Data Type (ADT) and a Data Structure. Give one example of each."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks — full marks requires all three points):** | # | ADT | Data Structure | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Logical/mathematical** specification of *what* operations do | **Physical/concrete** implementation of *how* operations are done | | 2 | Specifies only the **behavior** (semantics) and **axioms** | Specifies the **storage layout** and **algorithm steps** | | 3 | Implementation-independent | Implementation-dependent | | 4 | **Example:** `Stack` ADT | **Example:** Stack implemented using an array | **Closing line (1 mark):** *The same ADT (e.g., Stack) can be implemented using different data structures (array or linked list), and the user program remains unaffected — this is called **representation independence**.* --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"List any three advantages of using Abstract Data Types in program design."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (1 mark each, any three):** 1. **Modularity:** The program is divided into independent modules communicating only through well-defined ADT interfaces, enabling parallel development by multiple teams. 2. **Reusability:** Once an ADT is specified, it can be reused across multiple applications; only the implementation is written once. 3. **Maintainability:** The internal implementation can be changed (e.g., array-based stack → linked-list-based stack) without modifying any user code, as long as the ADT contract is preserved. 4. **Correctness via Formal Verification:** Because ADTs are defined by axioms, the correctness of algorithms built on them can be proven mathematically before any code is written. 5. **Abstraction:** Complex internal details are hidden, reducing the cognitive load on the application programmer. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, Module 1 carries **internal choice**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each long-answer question has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"Specify the Abstract Data Type for a `QUEUE`. Your specification must include the domain, the complete set of operations with signatures, the axioms, and the error conditions. Also explain how the same ADT can be implemented using two different data structures."** *(14 marks)* **Model Solution — Part (a): Full ADT Specification (7 marks)** **Name:** `QUEUE` **Domain:** A queue $Q$ is a finite ordered sequence $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$ of elements drawn from $\mathcal{E}$. The element $q_1$ is the **front** (head), and $q_n$ is the **rear** (tail). When $n = 0$, the queue is **empty**. **Operations:** | Operation | Signature | Semantics | |---|---|---| | `createQ()` | $\rightarrow$ Queue | Returns a new empty queue. | | `isEmptyQ(Q)` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Boolean | Returns `true` iff $Q$ has no elements. | | `enqueue(Q, x)` | Queue × Element $\rightarrow$ Queue | Inserts `x` at the **rear** of $Q$. | | `dequeue(Q)` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Queue × Element | Removes and returns the **front** element. | | `front(Q)` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Element | Returns (without removing) the front element. | | `size(Q)` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Integer | Returns the number of elements in $Q$. | **Axioms (for any queue $Q$ and element $x$):** $$\text{A1: } \text{isEmptyQ}(\text{createQ}()) = \text{true}$$ $$\text{A2: } \text{isEmptyQ}(\text{enqueue}(Q, x)) = \text{false}$$ $$\text{A3: } \text{front}(\text{enqueue}(Q, x)) = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } \text{isEmptyQ}(Q) = \text{true} \\ \text{front}(Q) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ $$\text{A4: } \text{dequeue}(\text{enqueue}(Q, x)) = \begin{cases} \langle \rangle & \text{if } \text{isEmptyQ}(Q) = \text{true} \\ \text{enqueue}(\text{dequeue}(Q), x) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Error Conditions:** - `dequeue(Q)` when `isEmptyQ(Q) = true` → **UNDERFLOW error**. - `front(Q)` when `isEmptyQ(Q) = true` → **UNDERFLOW error**. **Mark Distribution (Part a):** - Domain declaration → **1 mark** - Six operations with signatures + semantics → **3 marks** - Four axioms A1–A4 → **2 marks** - Error conditions → **1 mark** --- **Model Solution — Part (b): Two Different Implementations (7 marks)** **Implementation 1 — Array-Based Queue (Circular Buffer):** A fixed-size array `arr[MAX]` stores the elements. Two integer indices track positions: `front` (index of the front element) and `rear` (index of the next free slot). The array is treated as **circular** using modular arithmetic to avoid wasting space: $$\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ $$\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$$ - `enqueue` is $O(1)$. - `dequeue` is $O(1)$. - **Limitation:** Fixed maximum capacity `MAX`; overflow occurs when full. **Implementation 2 — Linked-List-Based Queue:** A dynamic singly linked list is used. The queue maintains a pointer to the `front` node and the `rear` node. - `enqueue(Q, x)`: allocate a new node, attach it after `rear`, update `rear` pointer → $O(1)$. - `dequeue(Q)`: remove the `front` node, update `front` pointer, free memory → $O(1)$. - **Advantage:** Grows dynamically; no fixed capacity limit (bounded only by heap memory). **Conclusion (1 mark):** Both implementations satisfy the *same* ADT axioms A1–A4, and any client code written against the ADT interface will work correctly with either implementation — demonstrating **representation independence**. **Mark Distribution (Part b):** - Array-based implementation explained → **2 marks** - Linked-list-based implementation explained → **2 marks** - Complexity analysis → **1 mark** - Representation independence conclusion → **2 marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"(a) Define an Abstract Data Type. Explain with a suitable example how ADT differs from a Data Structure. (b) Specify the ADT for a `LIST` with at least five operations and four axioms. Show that insertion at the end followed by deletion from the front satisfies the FIFO property."** *(14 marks)* **Model Solution — Part (a): Definition + Difference (7 marks)** **Definition (3 marks):** An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for a data type defined by its *behavior* from the user's perspective, consisting of three parts: **(i) a domain $D$** of values, **(ii) a set $O$ of operations**, and **(iii) a set $A$ of axioms** that govern how operations behave. The ADT specifies *what* is done, not *how*. **Example (2 marks):** The `STACK` ADT has domain = sequences of elements, operations = `{push, pop, top, isEmpty, create}`, and axioms such as `top(push(S, x)) = x` and `pop(push(S, x)) = S`. **Difference (2 marks):** | ADT | Data Structure | |---|---| | Logical, behavior-only specification | Concrete, storage-level implementation | | Multiple data structures can implement it | A single chosen storage mechanism | | Example: `Stack` ADT | Example: Stack via array, Stack via linked list | **Mark Distribution (Part a):** Definition 3 marks + Example 2 marks + Difference 2 marks = **7 marks**. --- **Model Solution — Part (b): LIST ADT Specification + FIFO Proof (7 marks)** **Name:** `LIST` **Domain:** A list $L$ is a finite ordered sequence $\langle l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n \rangle$ of elements from $\mathcal{E}$, where $n \geq 0$ and elements may be of arbitrary type (homogeneous or heterogeneous). **Operations:** | Operation | Signature | Semantics | |---|---|---| | `createL()` | $\rightarrow$ List | Returns an empty list. | | `insert(L, x, i)` | List × Element × Integer $\rightarrow$ List | Inserts `x` at position $i$ (1-indexed). | | `delete(L, i)` | List × Integer $\rightarrow$ List × Element | Removes and returns the element at position $i$. | | `retrieve(L, i)` | List × Integer $\rightarrow$ Element | Returns (without removing) the element at position $i$. | | `find(L, x)` | List × Element $\rightarrow$ Integer | Returns the position of first occurrence of `x`, or `−1` if absent. | | `size(L)` | List $\rightarrow$ Integer | Returns $n$, the number of elements in $L$. | **Axioms (let $L$ be a list, $x, y$ elements, $i$ a valid position):** $$\text{A1: } \text{size}(\text{createL}()) = 0$$ $$\text{A2: } \text{size}(\text{insert}(L, x, i)) = \text{size}(L) + 1$$ $$\text{A3: } \text{retrieve}(\text{insert}(L, x, i), i) = x$$ $$\text{A4: } \text{delete}(\text{insert}(L, x, i), i) = L \quad \text{(as a sequence)}$$ **Mark Distribution (Part b — first half):** Domain 0.5 + 6 operations 2 + 4 axioms 1 = **3.5 marks**. **FIFO Proof (3.5 marks):** Let us start with an empty list $L_0 = \text{createL}()$. **Step 1 — Insert at the end:** After `insert(L_0, a, 1)`, we get $L_1 = \langle a \rangle$. *(Position = 1, end of list = size = 1)* **Step 2 — Insert at the end:** After `insert(L_1, b, 2)`, we get $L_2 = \langle a, b \rangle$. **Step 3 — Insert at the end:** After `insert(L_2, c, 3)`, we get $L_3 = \langle a, b, c \rangle$. **Step 4 — Delete from the front:** After `delete(L_3, 1)`, we get $L_4 = \langle b, c \rangle$ and the returned element is $a$. **Step 5 — Delete from the front:** After `delete(L_4, 1)`, we get $L_5 = \langle c \rangle$ and the returned element is $b$. **Step 6 — Delete from the front:** After `delete(L_5, 1)`, we get $L_6 = \langle \rangle$ and the returned element is $c$. **Observation:** The deletion sequence is $a, b, c$, which exactly matches the insertion order. Hence: $$\text{insert at end} \circ \text{delete from front} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{FIFO order}$$ This is precisely the **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** property, and it emerges naturally as a *consequence* of the chosen operations on the `LIST` ADT — without needing to define a separate `QUEUE` ADT. **Q.E.D.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Conflating ADT with Data Structure (–2 marks typical):** Writing *"ADT is a structure that stores data"* is wrong. The correct phrasing is *"ADT is a specification of behavior"* or *"ADT defines the interface, not the storage"*. > > 2. **Missing Axioms in Specification (–3 marks typical):** Many students list only the operations without writing the formal axioms. KTU rubrics explicitly require axioms A1–A4 (or equivalent) for full marks on a 14-mark specification question. > > 3. **Ignoring Error Conditions (–1 mark):** Failing to mention UNDERFLOW for `pop`/`dequeue` on an empty structure is a guaranteed deduction. > > 4. **Writing Code Instead of Specification (–3 to –4 marks):** When asked to *"specify the ADT"*, students sometimes jump straight to C/C++ code with `#include ` and array declarations. The question wants the **logical specification** — domain, operations, axioms — *before* any implementation. Code may be mentioned only as a supporting illustration. > > 5. **Skipping the "Two Implementations" Sub-Part (–2 marks):** In Question A, part (b) specifically asks for *two* different data structures. Writing only one (e.g., only array-based) loses half the marks. > > 6. **Unlabeled Return Types (–1 mark):** Operations like `top(S)` must clearly state they *return* an element without modifying the stack. This semantic detail is often missed. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Abstract Data Types** - ☐ **ADT = Logical Specification** (what), **Data Structure = Physical Implementation** (how). Never use these interchangeably. - ☐ The **three pillars** of every ADT are **Domain $D$**, **Operations $O$**, and **Axioms $A$**. - ☐ A complete KTU-grade ADT specification has **six components**: Name, Domain, Operations, Semantics, Axioms, Error Conditions. - ☐ **Representation independence** means the user program is unaffected by the choice of underlying data structure. - ☐ Key Stack axioms to memorize: `isEmpty(create()) = true`, `top(push(S, x)) = x`, `pop(push(S, x)) = S`. - ☐ Key Queue axioms to memorize: FIFO order, `enqueue` adds at rear, `dequeue` removes from front. - ☐ **UNDERFLOW error** must be specified for `pop`/`dequeue`/`top`/`front` on empty structures — this is a marking checkpoint. - ☐ One ADT can have **many implementations** (e.g., Stack → array-based, linked-list-based, dynamic-array-based). All must satisfy the same axioms. - ☐ ADT enables **modularity, reusability, maintainability, abstraction, and formal verification** — name any three in a 3-mark answer. - ☐ When asked to *"specify the ADT"*, write the **(D, O, A) triple** explicitly — not code. - ☐ **FIFO** = First-In-First-Out (Queue); **LIFO** = Last-In-First-Out (Stack). These are informal; the formal axioms are what examiners want. - ☐ The TV remote / car steering wheel analogies score high on 3-mark "explain with example" questions. - ☐ Always define **return types and side effects** of operations (e.g., `top` returns but does not modify). - ☐ **Encapsulation** in OOP is the language-level realization of the ADT principle.

topics.title: Array as ADT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Array as ADT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Array (as an Abstract Data Type):** An array is a linear, homogeneous, static, and contiguous data structure that stores a finite, ordered collection of elements of the **same data type**, accessed randomly using an **index** (subscript). As an ADT, an array is defined by the tuple `Array = (D, O)`, where `D` is the finite set of data elements and `O` is the set of permissible operations defined on those elements.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the array is treated as the foundational ADT upon which virtually every other linear and non-linear structure is built. It is the first ADT introduced in Module 1 to establish the difference between **logical (mathematical) view** and **physical (memory) view** of a data structure.

### 1.1.1 Tuple Definition of Array ADT

$$
\text{Array ADT} = (D, O)
$$

Where:

$$
D = \{a_{LB}, a_{LB+1}, a_{LB+2}, \dots, a_{UB}\}
$$

and $O$ is the set of operations such that:

$$
O = \{\text{Create}, \text{Retrieve}, \text{Store}, \text{Traverse}, \text{Search}, \text{Update}, \text{Delete}, \text{Sort}, \text{Merge}\}
$$

### 1.1.2 Characteristic Properties of an Array

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Pillars of an Array (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
> 1. **Homogeneous** — All elements share the same data type (e.g., all `int` or all `float`).
> 2. **Ordered / Linear** — Elements are arranged in a logical sequence indexed from `LB` to `UB`.
> 3. **Fixed Size (Static)** — Memory is allocated at compile time; size cannot grow or shrink at runtime in basic arrays.
> 4. **Contiguous Memory Allocation** — Elements are stored in adjacent memory cells.
> 5. **Random Access** — Direct access to any element in **O(1)** time using its index.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Real-World View

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Apartment Building:** Imagine a building with **floors numbered 0 to 9**, where each floor contains exactly **one apartment of identical size**. The building is the *array*, each apartment is an *element*, the floor number is the *index*, and the size of each apartment is the *word size* (bytes per element). The key insight: just as you can jump directly to floor 5 by taking the elevator (random access), the computer can jump directly to `A[5]` using the address calculation formula (no need to "walk" past floors 0–4).

A second helpful analogy:

| Analogy | Array Concept | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| Egg carton (12 slots) | Static 1D array | Fixed size, identical slots, index 1–12 |
| Train with bogies | Linked list (contrast) | NOT an array — non-contiguous |
| Cinema row seating | 1D array | Seats numbered, same chair type |
| Excel spreadsheet | 2D array | Rows × columns of identical cells |
| Locker bank at a station | n-D array | Multi-key indexed access |

---

## 1.3 Components of an ADT Specification

A **complete ADT specification** (as required in KTU exam answers) consists of three parts:

$$
\text{ADT Specification} = \langle \textbf{Data}, \textbf{Operations}, \textbf{Axioms/Pre-Post Conditions} \rangle
$$

### 1.3.1 Data Component

The data is a mapping from the index set to the value set:

$$
\text{Index Set: } I = \{LB, LB+1, LB+2, \dots, UB\}
$$

$$
\text{Value Set: } V = \{\text{type-consistent elements}\}
$$

$$
\text{Array} = f : I \rightarrow V
$$

### 1.3.2 Operations Component (Standard KTU Set)

| Operation | Description | Typical Time |
|---|---|---|
| `CREATE(n)` | Allocates array of size `n` | **O(n)** |
| `RETRIEVE(A, i)` | Returns element at index `i` | **O(1)** |
| `STORE(A, i, x)` | Sets `A[i] = x` | **O(1)** |
| `TRAVERSE(A, fn)` | Visits each element, applies `fn` | **O(n)** |
| `SEARCH(A, key)` | Linear/Binary search for `key` | **O(n) / O(log n)** |
| `INSERT(A, i, x)` | Inserts `x` at position `i` | **O(n)** |
| `DELETE(A, i)` | Removes element at `i` | **O(n)** |
| `SORT(A)` | Orders elements | **O(n log n)** avg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Note:** In the **abstract view**, `INSERT` and `DELETE` are defined as array operations. In the **physical implementation**, they are inefficient (**O(n)**) because elements must be shifted. This contrast between logical ADT and physical structure is a frequently tested KTU concept worth **2 to 3 marks** in Part A questions.

---

## 1.4 Why "Abstract"? — The ADT vs. Data Structure Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the **ADT** (logical/mathematical model) with the **Data Structure** (physical implementation in memory). Examiners repeatedly test this distinction.
>
> - **ADT** = *What* operations are defined (the contract).
> - **Data Structure** = *How* the operations are physically implemented (the construction blueprint).

| Layer | Concern | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **ADT Layer** | Logical definition, operations, semantics | "An array supports random access by index" |
| **Implementation Layer** | Memory layout, pointer arithmetic, shifting | "Element at `A[i]` is stored at base + `i × w` bytes" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 One-Dimensional Array — Address Calculation (CORE THEORY)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important formula** in Module 1. KTU examiners ask derivations of this formula every semester, typically for **7 marks** as a sub-part in a 14-mark question.

### 2.1.1 The Address Formula (1D)

For a 1D array declared as `A[LB .. UB]`, the memory address of any element `A[i]` is given by:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = \text{LOC}(A[LB]) + w \cdot (i - LB)
$$

Where the terms are defined as:

- $\text{LOC}(A[i])$ = Physical memory address of the element at index $i$
- $\text{LOC}(A[LB])$ = **Base Address** — address of the first element
- $w$ = **Word size** — number of bytes occupied by one element (e.g., $w = 4$ for `int`)
- $i$ = Index of the element whose address is needed
- $LB$ = **Lower Bound** of the index
- $UB$ = **Upper Bound** of the index

### 2.1.2 Specialized Case: Zero-Based Array

In most programming languages (`C`, `Java`, `Python`), $LB = 0$, so the formula simplifies to:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + w \cdot i
$$

### 2.1.3 Length of the Array

$$
n = UB - LB + 1
$$

This $n$ is the **number of elements** in the array.

---

## 2.2 Two-Dimensional Array — Row-Major and Column-Major Order

A 2D array is stored linearly in memory. There are **two storage conventions** — examiners expect both derivations.

### 2.2.1 Row-Major Order (RMO) — Used in C, C++, Java, Python

Elements of **row 0** are stored first, then row 1, then row 2, and so on.

For a 2D array declared as `A[LB1 .. UB1][LB2 .. UB2]`, the address of `A[i][j]` is:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = \text{LOC}(A[LB_1][LB_2]) + w \cdot \left[ n_2 \cdot (i - LB_1) + (j - LB_2) \right]
$$

where $n_2 = UB_2 - LB_2 + 1$ is the **number of columns**.

### 2.2.2 Column-Major Order (CMO) — Used in FORTRAN, MATLAB, R

Elements of **column 0** are stored first, then column 1, then column 2, and so on.

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = \text{LOC}(A[LB_1][LB_2]) + w \cdot \left[ n_1 \cdot (j - LB_2) + (i - LB_1) \right]
$$

where $n_1 = UB_1 - LB_1 + 1$ is the **number of rows**.

### 2.2.3 Specialized Form (Zero-Based, $LB_1 = LB_2 = 0$)

| Order | Address Formula |
|---|---|
| **Row-Major** | $\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + w \cdot (n_2 \cdot i + j)$ |
| **Column-Major** | $\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + w \cdot (n_1 \cdot j + i)$ |

---

## 2.3 n-Dimensional Array — General Address Calculation

For an $n$-dimensional array $A[d_1][d_2]\dots[d_n]$ stored in **row-major order**:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i_1][i_2]\dots[i_n]) = \text{LOC}(A[0][0]\dots[0]) + w \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{n} d_j \right)
$$

Where $d_k = UB_k - LB_k + 1$ is the size along dimension $k$, and the empty product (when $k = n$) equals 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Module 1:** 1D and 2D derivations are the **primary focus**. The $n$-D formula is occasionally asked as a 3-mark "generalize" question.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU DSA exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **1D Array Address** | $\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w(i - LB)$ | $B$ = base, $w$ = word size | Retrieval: **O(1)** |
| 2 | **1D Array Length** | $n = UB - LB + 1$ | $LB$ = lower, $UB$ = upper | — |
| 3 | **2D Row-Major** | $B + w[n_2(i-LB_1) + (j-LB_2)]$ | $n_2$ = columns | Retrieval: **O(1)** |
| 4 | **2D Column-Major** | $B + w[n_1(j-LB_2) + (i-LB_1)]$ | $n_1$ = rows | Retrieval: **O(1)** |
| 5 | **Linear Search** | — | — | **O(n)** |
| 6 | **Binary Search** | Requires sorted array | — | **O(log n)** |
| 7 | **Insertion at end** | No shift needed | — | **O(1)** |
| 8 | **Insertion at position $i$** | Requires $n-i$ shifts | — | **O(n)** |
| 9 | **Deletion at position $i$** | Requires $n-i-1$ shifts | — | **O(n)** |
| 10 | **Traversal** | Visit all $n$ elements | — | **O(n)** |
| 11 | **Bubble Sort** | Two nested loops | — | **O(n²)** |
| 12 | **Quick/Merge Sort** | Divide & conquer | — | **O(n log n)** avg |
| 13 | **Total Memory** | $n \times w$ bytes | $n$ = elements, $w$ = word size | — |
| 14 | **Last Element Address** | $B + w(n-1)$ | $B$ = base, $w$ = word size | — |
| 15 | **Memory Bytes** | $\sum (\text{size of each dim})$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Rule for Tables:** Vertical bars `|` cannot appear inside table cells. For absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\mid x \mid$ or $ \vert x \vert $.

---

## 2.5 Operational Semantics: Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.5.1 Why Random Access is O(1) — The 'How' Behind the Formula

The CPU computes the address of `A[i]` using **arithmetic on the index itself**:

1. **Subtract LB** from the index → yields the *offset from start* (0-based position).
2. **Multiply by `w`** → converts logical position to physical byte offset.
3. **Add to Base Address** → produces the absolute physical memory address.
4. **Single memory fetch instruction** → retrieves the element.

Because each step is a constant-time arithmetic operation, total time is **O(1)** regardless of array size. This is why arrays are the fastest random-access structure.

### 2.5.2 Why Insertion is O(n) — The Cost of Contiguity

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The defining *cost* of an array is its static, contiguous nature. Insertion requires shifting all elements to the right, which is an **O(n)** operation. This is the primary motivation for **linked lists** (covered in Module 2).

**Step-by-step insertion at position `i`:**

1. Validate: check array is not full and `LB ≤ i ≤ UB + 1`.
2. Start from the **last element** (`A[UB]`).
3. For `k = UB` down to `i + 1`: shift `A[k] = A[k-1]`.
4. Place new element: `A[i] = x`.
5. Increment size / `UB`.

Total shifts in the worst case = $n - i$ → **O(n)**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Array ADT |
|---|---|
| **Image Processing** | 2D pixel arrays (RGB matrices) |
| **Database Indexing** | B-trees and hash tables use arrays internally |
| **Numerical Computing** | BLAS, LAPACK rely on contiguous array storage |
| **Operating Systems** | Page tables, file allocation tables |
| **Signal Processing** | FIR/FFT buffers implemented as circular arrays |
| **Game Development** | Sprite buffers, tile maps (2D arrays) |
| **Compilers** | Symbol tables, instruction buffers |
| **Networking** | Packet buffers in NIC drivers |

> [!NOTE]
> The contiguous memory layout of arrays is the **single most important reason** modern CPUs achieve high throughput — they exploit **spatial locality** by pre-fetching adjacent cache lines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address of an Element in a 1D Array

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1. Memorize every line — examiners award **7 marks** for the full derivation and **3 marks** for the final formula in a 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

Given a 1D array `A` declared as `A[LB .. UB]`, with each element occupying `w` bytes in memory, derive the formula for computing the memory address of any arbitrary element `A[i]`, where `LB ≤ i ≤ UB`.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**

Let:
- $B = \text{LOC}(A[LB])$ = Base address of the array (address of the first element).
- $w$ = Size of one element in bytes.
- $LB$ = Lower bound of the index range.
- $UB$ = Upper bound of the index range.
- $n = UB - LB + 1$ = Number of elements in the array.

**Step 2 — Compute the number of elements before `A[i]`.**

The element at index $i$ is preceded by all elements from index $LB$ up to $i - 1$. The count of these preceding elements is:

$$
\text{Count}_{\text{before}} = i - LB
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the byte offset from the base address.**

Since each element occupies exactly $w$ bytes, and the elements are stored **contiguously**, the byte offset of `A[i]` from the base address is:

$$
\text{Offset} = (i - LB) \times w
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the absolute address.**

The address of `A[i]` is obtained by adding the offset to the base address:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w \cdot (i - LB)
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the address of the last element.**

Substituting $i = UB$ into the formula:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[UB]) = B + w \cdot (UB - LB)
$$

**Step 6 — Total memory consumed by the array.**

The total memory occupied is from the first byte of `A[LB]` to the last byte of `A[UB]`:

$$
\text{Total Memory} = w \cdot n = w \cdot (UB - LB + 1)
$$

**Step 7 — Special case for $LB = 0$.**

When $LB = 0$ (zero-based indexing, used in C/C++/Java):

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w \cdot i
$$

This is the most commonly used form in programming examinations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 1D Array Address Calculation

**Problem:** An array `A[-5 .. 10]` is stored in memory starting at base address `2000`. Each element occupies `4` bytes. Find the address of `A[3]`.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$
B = 2000, \quad w = 4, \quad LB = -5, \quad UB = 10, \quad i = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w \cdot (i - LB)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[3]) = 2000 + 4 \cdot (3 - (-5))
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the parenthesis.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[3]) = 2000 + 4 \cdot (3 + 5)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the inner expression.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[3]) = 2000 + 4 \cdot 8
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[3]) = 2000 + 32
$$

**Step 7 — Final answer.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[3]) = 2032
$$

**Verification:** The element at index $-5$ is at address $2000$. From $-5$ to $3$, there are $3 - (-5) = 8$ elements before index $3$. $8 \times 4 = 32$ bytes offset. So $A[3]$ is at $2000 + 32 = 2032$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Address of an Element in a 2D Array (Row-Major Order)

**Step 1 — Set up the declaration.**

Consider the 2D array `A[LB1 .. UB1][LB2 .. UB2]` stored in **row-major order**.

**Step 2 — Define row and column counts.**

$$
n_1 = UB_1 - LB_1 + 1 \quad \text{(number of rows)}
$$

$$
n_2 = UB_2 - LB_2 + 1 \quad \text{(number of columns)}
$$

**Step 3 — Count complete rows before row `i`.**

The element `A[i][j]` lies in row $i$. All rows before row $i$ (from $LB_1$ to $i - 1$) are completely stored before it. The count of full rows is:

$$
\text{Full Rows} = i - LB_1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the byte offset for full rows.**

Each full row has $n_2$ elements of $w$ bytes each:

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{rows}} = (i - LB_1) \times n_2 \times w
$$

**Step 5 — Count elements in the current row before column `j`.**

Within row $i$, the elements from column $LB_2$ to column $j - 1$ precede `A[i][j]`. The count is:

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{cols}} = (j - LB_2) \times w
$$

**Step 6 — Combine the offsets and add to base address.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot \left[ n_2 \cdot (i - LB_1) + (j - LB_2) \right]
$$

**Step 7 — Special case $LB_1 = LB_2 = 0$.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot (n_2 \cdot i + j)
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Address of an Element in a 2D Array (Column-Major Order)

**Step 1 — Follow the analogous reasoning for column-major storage.**

In column-major order, columns are stored first. So all complete columns before column $j$ are stored first.

**Step 2 — Count complete columns before column `j`.**

$$
\text{Full Columns} = j - LB_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute offset for full columns.**

Each full column has $n_1$ elements of $w$ bytes each:

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{cols}} = (j - LB_2) \times n_1 \times w
$$

**Step 4 — Compute offset within current column.**

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{rows}} = (i - LB_1) \times w
$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot \left[ n_1 \cdot (j - LB_2) + (i - LB_1) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Special case $LB_1 = LB_2 = 0$.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot (n_1 \cdot j + i)
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — 2D Array Address Calculation

**Problem:** A 2D array `A[0 .. 9][0 .. 19]` is stored in memory starting at base address `1000` in **row-major order**. Each element is `2` bytes. Find the address of `A[5][10]`.

**Step 1 — Identify values.**

$$
B = 1000, \quad w = 2, \quad LB_1 = 0, \quad UB_1 = 9, \quad LB_2 = 0, \quad UB_2 = 19, \quad i = 5, \quad j = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $n_2$ (number of columns).**

$$
n_2 = UB_2 - LB_2 + 1 = 19 - 0 + 1 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the row-major formula.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot (n_2 \cdot i + j)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 2 \cdot (20 \cdot 5 + 10)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute inner product.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 2 \cdot (100 + 10)
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 2 \cdot 110
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 220
$$

**Step 8 — Final answer.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1220
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Same 2D Array in Column-Major Order

**Same problem, but stored in column-major order.**

**Step 1 — Compute $n_1$.**

$$
n_1 = UB_1 - LB_1 + 1 = 9 - 0 + 1 = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the column-major formula.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot (n_1 \cdot j + i)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 2 \cdot (10 \cdot 10 + 5)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 2 \cdot 105
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$
\text{LOC}(A[5][10]) = 1000 + 210 = 1210
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The same logical element `A[5][10]` has *different physical addresses* under the two storage schemes. This is a frequently tested KTU concept — examiners often give the same array with both schemes and ask the student to verify or compare.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Time Complexity of Array Operations

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `RETRIEVE(A, i)` | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Direct address calculation |
| `STORE(A, i, x)` | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Direct write to computed address |
| `TRAVERSE(A)` | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | Must visit every element once |
| `SEARCH(A, key)` Linear | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | First match or no match |
| `SEARCH(A, key)` Binary | O(1) | O(log n) | O(log n) | Halves the search space each step |
| `INSERT at end` | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | No shifting required |
| `INSERT at position i` | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | Up to $n-i$ shifts |
| `DELETE at end` | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | No shifting required |
| `DELETE at position i` | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | Up to $n-i-1$ shifts |
| `SORT (Bubble)` | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | Two nested loops |
| `SORT (Merge/Quick)` | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n²) Quick / O(n log n) Merge | Divide & conquer |

## 4.4 Operation Flowchart: Array ADT Insertion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start Insertion]) --> V1{Is Array<br>Full?}
    V1 -- Yes --> ERR[Return OVERFLOW Error]
    V1 -- No --> V2{Is 0 <= i <= n?}
    V2 -- No --> INV[Return INVALID INDEX Error]
    V2 -- Yes --> SH[For k = n down to i plus 1<br>Shift A k to A k minus 1]
    SH --> AS[Assign A i = x]
    AS --> IN[Increment n]
    IN --> OK[Return SUCCESS]
    ERR --> E([End])

    OK --> E

```

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Address Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IDX[User Provides Index i] --> SUB[Subtract LB<br>i minus LB]
    SUB --> MUL[Multiply by w<br>w times i minus LB]
    MUL --> ADD[Add Base Address<br>Base plus offset]
    ADD --> MEM[Physical Memory<br>Fetch A i]
    MEM --> VAL[Return Value to User]
    style IDX fill:#FFD700
    style VAL fill:#90EE90
    style MEM fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 4-stage pipeline executes in **constant time** because the CPU's ALU performs subtract, multiply, and add in a few clock cycles. The hardware *itself* is designed to make array access O(1).
