# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define an Array as an Abstract Data Type. List any four standard operations defined on an array ADT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** An Array is a linear, homogeneous, static, and contiguous Abstract Data Type that stores a finite, ordered collection of elements of the same data type, accessed randomly using an index ranging from a lower bound (LB) to an upper bound (UB). Formally, the array ADT is a pair `(D, O)` where `D` is the index-to-value mapping and `O` is the set of operations defined on it.

**Any four operations (1 mark — 0.25 each):**

1. **CREATE(n)** — Allocates an array of `n` elements.
2. **RETRIEVE(A, i)** — Returns the element at index `i`.
3. **STORE(A, i, x)** — Assigns the value `x` to index `i`.
4. **TRAVERSE(A, fn)** — Visits each element and applies function `fn`.
5. **SEARCH(A, key)** — Searches for `key` in the array.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between an Abstract Data Type (ADT) and a Data Structure? Illustrate with reference to the Array ADT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ADT | Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | *What* operations are available (logical/mathematical) | *How* operations are implemented (physical) |
| **Concern** | Contract / specification | Representation in memory + algorithms |
| **Independence** | Independent of programming language | Language and platform dependent |
| **Array example** | "Random access by integer index, contiguous memory" | "Contiguous byte addresses, base + offset formula" |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** ADT = **W**hat; Data Structure = **H**ow. The Array ADT *specifies* that `RETRIEVE(A, i)` returns the $i$-th element; the array data structure *implements* this by computing the address `Base + w × (i − LB)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In End Semester Examinations (ESE), Module 1 carries a 14-mark question. Students are offered an internal choice between two alternatives (OR option). Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Derive the formula for computing the address of an element `A[i]` in a 1D array `A[LB .. UB]` stored in memory. Clearly state all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An array `ARR[-15 .. 25]` is stored in memory starting at base address `5000`. Each element occupies `2` bytes. Compute the address of `ARR[10]` and the total memory occupied by the array. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define variables (1 mark).**

Let:
- $B$ = Base address of the array (address of `A[LB]`).
- $w$ = Number of bytes per element.
- $LB$ = Lower bound, $UB$ = Upper bound.
- $i$ = Index of element whose address is required.
- $n = UB - LB + 1$ = Total elements.

**Step 2 — Count elements before `A[i]` (2 marks).**

The number of elements stored before `A[i]` is $i - LB$.

**Step 3 — Compute byte offset (1 mark).**

Since each element occupies $w$ bytes:

$$
\text{Offset} = (i - LB) \times w
$$

**Step 4 — Final formula (2 marks).**

$$
\boxed{\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w \cdot (i - LB)}
$$

**Step 5 — Special case (1 mark).**

For $LB = 0$:

$$
\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w \cdot i
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$
B = 5000, \quad w = 2, \quad LB = -15, \quad UB = 25, \quad i = 10
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the address of `ARR[10]` (3 marks).**

$$
\text{LOC}(ARR[10]) = 5000 + 2 \cdot (10 - (-15))
$$

$$
\text{LOC}(ARR[10]) = 5000 + 2 \cdot 25 = 5000 + 50 = 5050
$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of elements (2 marks).**

$$
n = UB - LB + 1 = 25 - (-15) + 1 = 25 + 15 + 1 = 41
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total memory (2 marks).**

$$
\text{Total Memory} = n \times w = 41 \times 2 = 82 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Final Answer:** Address of `ARR[10]` = **5050**; Total memory = **82 bytes**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Define a 2D Array ADT. Derive the address calculation formula for a 2D array `A[LB1 .. UB1][LB2 .. UB2]` stored in **(i) Row-Major Order** and **(ii) Column-Major Order**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D array `A[0 .. 4][0 .. 6]` is stored in memory with base address `2000`, each element of `2` bytes. Compute the address of `A[3][5]` under both row-major and column-major orderings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark):** A 2D array is a homogeneous collection of elements arranged in a rectangular grid of $m$ rows and $n$ columns, where each element is identified by a pair of indices $(i, j)$ with $LB_1 \leq i \leq UB_1$ and $LB_2 \leq j \leq UB_2$. It is stored in linear memory using either row-major or column-major order.

**Row-Major Derivation (3 marks):**

- Number of rows: $n_1 = UB_1 - LB_1 + 1$.
- Number of columns: $n_2 = UB_2 - LB_2 + 1$.
- Before element $A[i][j]$, there are $i - LB_1$ complete rows of $n_2$ elements each.
- Plus $(j - LB_2)$ elements in the current row.

$$
\boxed{\text{LOC}_{\text{RMO}}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot \left[ n_2 \cdot (i - LB_1) + (j - LB_2) \right]}
$$

**Column-Major Derivation (3 marks):**

- Before element $A[i][j]$, there are $j - LB_2$ complete columns of $n_1$ elements each.
- Plus $(i - LB_1)$ elements in the current column.

$$
\boxed{\text{LOC}_{\text{CMO}}(A[i][j]) = B + w \cdot \left[ n_1 \cdot (j - LB_2) + (i - LB_1) \right]}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$
B = 2000, \quad w = 2, \quad LB_1 = LB_2 = 0, \quad UB_1 = 4, \quad UB_2 = 6, \quad i = 3, \quad j = 5
$$

$$
n_1 = 5, \quad n_2 = 7
$$

**Row-Major Computation (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{LOC}_{\text{RMO}}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + 2 \cdot (7 \cdot 3 + 5)
$$

$$
= 2000 + 2 \cdot (21 + 5) = 2000 + 2 \cdot 26 = 2000 + 52 = 2052
$$

**Column-Major Computation (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{LOC}_{\text{CMO}}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + 2 \cdot (5 \cdot 5 + 3)
$$

$$
= 2000 + 2 \cdot (25 + 3) = 2000 + 2 \cdot 28 = 2000 + 56 = 2056
$$

**Final Answer:** Row-Major address = **2052**; Column-Major address = **2056**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Array as ADT" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the word size `w`:** When asked for the *address* of an element, students often compute the *index* offset and forget to multiply by `w`. This loses 2–3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Confusing row-major with column-major:** In a 2D problem, students mix the formulas — using $n_2$ where $n_1$ should be (or vice versa). Always ask: *"Am I counting rows before, or columns before?"*
>
> 3. **Sign errors with negative LB:** When $LB < 0$, the expression $i - LB$ becomes $i + |LB|$. Students forget the implicit sign flip and lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not stating the variables:** Examiners allocate **1 to 2 marks** explicitly for defining $B$, $w$, $LB$, $UB$, $n$ *before* the derivation. Skipping this is the #1 reason students score 11/14 instead of 14/14.
>
> 5. **Writing only the final formula in a 7-mark derivation:** A derivation requires *step-by-step reasoning* — at least 4 to 5 logical steps. A naked formula with no derivation earns only 2 to 3 marks maximum.
>
> **Pro Tip:** Always box the final answer (using `\boxed{}` style) and clearly state *units* (bytes) for memory calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Array as ADT (Module 1)**

### 🎯 Core Concepts

- [ ] **Array ADT** = `(Data, Operations)` tuple — logical, language-independent.
- [ ] **Five properties:** Homogeneous, Ordered, Static, Contiguous, Random Access.
- [ ] **ADT vs Data Structure:** ADT = *What*; Data Structure = *How*.
- [ ] **Lower Bound (LB)** and **Upper Bound (UB)** define the index range.

### 📐 Must-Memorize Formulas

- [ ] 1D: $\text{LOC}(A[i]) = B + w(i - LB)$
- [ ] 2D RMO: $B + w[n_2(i - LB_1) + (j - LB_2)]$
- [ ] 2D CMO: $B + w[n_1(j - LB_2) + (i - LB_1)]$
- [ ] Length: $n = UB - LB + 1$
- [ ] Total Memory: $n \times w$ bytes
- [ ] Last Element Address: $B + w(n - 1)$

### ⏱️ Time Complexities (Must Know by Heart)

- [ ] Access/Retrieve: **O(1)**
- [ ] Traverse: **O(n)**
- [ ] Linear Search: **O(n)**
- [ ] Binary Search (sorted): **O(log n)**
- [ ] Insertion/Deletion at end: **O(1)**
- [ ] Insertion/Deletion at middle: **O(n)**

### 🌍 Real-World Applications

- [ ] Image pixels, video frames, audio buffers
- [ ] Database tables, matrix operations in ML
- [ ] CPU cache-friendly code exploits **spatial locality** of arrays
- [ ] Static lookup tables in embedded systems

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- [ ] Don't forget to multiply by `w` (word size).
- [ ] Don't confuse `n_1` (rows) with `n_2` (columns).
- [ ] Don't skip variable definitions in derivations.
- [ ] Don't confuse `LB` in formulas — sign matters.
- [ ] Don't claim insertion is O(1) — only retrieval/storage is O(1).

### 🧠 Key Takeaway

> **The Array is the simplest, fastest (for access), but least flexible (for insertion/deletion) ADT. Its O(1) random access is what makes it the foundation of all other data structures.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on ADT (Insert, Delete, Search, Sort)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operations on ADT (Insert, Delete, Search, Sort)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Abstract Data Type (ADT) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Abstract Data Type (ADT):** A mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a user, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does not specify *how* the data is stored in memory; it only specifies *what* operations can be performed.

An ADT is essentially a **contract** between the data structure's designer and its user. It defines:
1. **Data** — the type of values stored
2. **Operations** — the functions that can be applied
3. **Error/Exception conditions** — what happens when operations fail
4. **Pre-conditions and Post-conditions** — the state before and after each operation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Vending Machine

Imagine a **vending machine**:

| Aspect | Vending Machine | ADT |
|---|---|---|
| **Interface** | Buttons, coin slot, display | Function signatures (Insert, Delete, etc.) |
| **Internal Mechanism** | Springs, coils, electronics | Data storage (Array, Linked List, etc.) |
| **Contract** | Press B3 → receive a Coke | Call `insert(x)` → x is added |
| **User Concern** | Only the buttons matter | Only the operation results matter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four primitive operations on any ADT are **Insert, Delete, Search, and Sort**. These are the foundation upon which complex data structures (Stack, Queue, List, Tree) are built. Mastery of these four operations is **non-negotiable** for KTU board examinations.

## 1.3 The Four Core Operations — Definitions

**1. Insert Operation**
Adds a new element to the data structure at a specified position.

**2. Delete Operation**
Removes an existing element from the data structure, optionally returning the deleted value.

**3. Search Operation**
Locates the position of a target element within the data structure. Returns either the index (success) or a sentinel value like $-1$ (failure).

**4. Sort Operation**
Rearranges all elements in the data structure into a defined order (ascending or descending) based on a comparison key.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics of ADT Primitives

Every ADT operation must satisfy a **pre-condition** (state before execution) and a **post-condition** (state after execution). This is the formal contract.

### 2.1.1 Insert Operation
- **Pre-condition:** Data structure $D$ exists and has space (or dynamic allocation is possible). Element $x$ is of the correct type.
- **Post-condition:** $D$ now contains $x$ at the specified position, and the size $n$ increments by $1$ (i.e., $n \rightarrow n+1$).
- **Signature:** `insert(D, x, pos)` or `insert(D, x)` (default end position).

### 2.1.2 Delete Operation
- **Pre-condition:** Data structure $D$ is non-empty ($n \geq 1$). Position to delete is valid ($0 \leq pos < n$).
- **Post-condition:** Element at $pos$ is removed, size $n$ decrements by $1$ (i.e., $n \rightarrow n-1$). Optionally returns the deleted element.

### 2.1.3 Search Operation
- **Pre-condition:** Data structure $D$ exists. Target element $k$ is of the correct type.
- **Post-condition:** Returns index $i$ where $D[i] = k$, or returns $-1$ if $k$ is not found. **State of $D$ is unchanged.**

### 2.1.4 Sort Operation
- **Pre-condition:** Data structure $D$ has $n \geq 0$ elements. Elements are mutually comparable.
- **Post-condition:** $D$ is rearranged such that for all valid indices $i < j$, $D[i] \leq D[j]$ (ascending order) or $D[i] \geq D[j]$ (descending order).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These four operations form the backbone of **database management systems (DBMS)**, **search engines (Google uses sorted indices)**, **operating systems (process scheduling = sort by priority)**, and **compilers (symbol table management)**. Understanding them at the ADT level means you can swap implementations (array vs. linked list) without changing the program logic — this is the **power of abstraction**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### Table 2.1: Time Complexity of ADT Operations Across Implementations

> **Notation Note:** Below, $n$ denotes the number of elements in the data structure. We use $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$ for Big-O asymptotic complexity. Vertical bar notation in Big-O like $O(\vert \log n \vert)$ is written with `\vert` to avoid breaking the table.

| Operation | Array Implementation | Linked List (Singly) | Sorted Array | Sorted Linked List |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert (at end)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to traverse | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| **Insert (at front)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| **Delete (by value)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| **Delete (by pos)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| **Search (Linear)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| **Search (Binary)** | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ | Not Applicable | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ | Not Applicable |
| **Sort** | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | Already Sorted | Already Sorted |

### Table 2.2: Key Formulas for Position Calculation

| Description | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Number of shifts for Insert at front (array) | $n$ | All $n$ elements must be shifted right by $1$ |
| Number of shifts for Delete at position $i$ (array) | $n - i - 1$ | Elements after $i$ shift left |
| Mid index for Binary Search | $mid = \lfloor (low + high) / 2 \rfloor$ | Avoids integer overflow in some languages |
| Average comparisons for Linear Search | $(n+1) / 2$ | On average, half the array is scanned |
| Max comparisons for Linear Search | $n$ | Worst case, element at end or absent |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Operation

- **Insert** at the *end* of an array is cheap ($\mathcal{O}(1)$), but insertion at the *front* requires shifting all $n$ elements — this is why **linked lists** exist.
- **Delete** is symmetric to insert — deleting from the front of an array requires shifting, while deleting from a linked list only requires pointer manipulation ($\mathcal{O}(1)$ after locating the node).
- **Search** is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for unsorted structures. Maintaining a **sorted invariant** allows $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ binary search but costs $\mathcal{O}(n)$ on each insert/delete.
- **Sort** is the most expensive operation — even the best comparison-based sorts take $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ time. This is the **information-theoretic lower bound**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Operations on a List ADT (Array Implementation)

**Problem Setup:** Consider an integer array $A$ of maximum capacity $MAX = 8$, currently holding $n = 5$ elements:

$$A = [10, 25, 30, 45, 50] \quad \text{with indices } [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]$$

### 3.1.1 INSERT Operation — Insert 35 at position 2

**Step 1: Validation Check**
Verify that $n < MAX$ (i.e., $5 < 8$) and $0 \leq 2 \leq n$ (i.e., $0 \leq 2 \leq 5$). Both conditions hold. ✓

**Step 2: Right-Shift Phase**
Starting from the last index, shift every element at index $i \geq 2$ one position to the right.

For each iteration, the new position is computed as:

$$A[i+1] \leftarrow A[i] \quad \text{for } i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 2$$

**Step 3: Explicit Element-by-Element Shift Table**

| Iteration | $i$ | Action | Array State After |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | $A[5] \leftarrow A[4] = 50$ | $[10, 25, 30, 45, 50, 50, -, -]$ |
| 2 | 3 | $A[4] \leftarrow A[3] = 45$ | $[10, 25, 30, 45, 45, 50, -, -]$ |
| 3 | 2 | $A[3] \leftarrow A[2] = 30$ | $[10, 25, 30, 30, 45, 50, -, -]$ |

**Step 4: Place New Element**
$$A[2] \leftarrow 35$$

Resulting array:

$$A = [10, 25, 35, 30, 45, 50, -, -] \quad \text{with } n = 6$$

**Step 5: Update Count**
$$n \leftarrow n + 1 = 6$$

**Total Shifts Performed:** $n - pos = 5 - 2 = 3$ shifts. This confirms the general formula: **shifts for insert at position $pos$ = $n - pos$**.

---

### 3.1.2 DELETE Operation — Delete element at position 1 (value 25)

**Step 1: Validation Check**
Verify $n > 0$ and $0 \leq 1 < n$. Both hold. ✓

**Step 2: Save Deleted Value (Optional)**
$$deleted\_val \leftarrow A[1] = 25$$

**Step 3: Left-Shift Phase**
For each element at index $i > 1$, shift one position to the left.

$$A[i-1] \leftarrow A[i] \quad \text{for } i = 2, 3, \ldots, n-1$$

**Step 4: Explicit Element-by-Element Shift Table**

| Iteration | $i$ | Action | Array State After |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $A[1] \leftarrow A[2] = 35$ | $[10, 35, 30, 45, 50, -, -, -]$ |
| 2 | 3 | $A[2] \leftarrow A[3] = 45$ | $[10, 35, 45, 45, 50, -, -, -]$ |
| 3 | 4 | $A[3] \leftarrow A[4] = 50$ | $[10, 35, 45, 50, 50, -, -, -]$ |
| 4 | 5 | $A[4] \leftarrow A[5]$ (stale slot) | ignored in final cleanup |

**Step 5: Decrement Count and Clear Last Slot**
$$n \leftarrow n - 1 = 5$$
$$A[n] \leftarrow 0 \quad \text{(logical cleanup)}$$

Final array:

$$A = [10, 35, 45, 50, -] \quad \text{with } n = 5$$

**Total Shifts Performed:** $n - 1 - pos = 5 - 1 - 1 = 3$ shifts. This matches the general formula: **shifts for delete at position $pos$ = $n - pos - 1$**.

---

### 3.1.3 SEARCH Operation — Linear Search for value 45

**Step 1: Initialize**
$$i \leftarrow 0, \quad found \leftarrow -1$$

**Step 2: Iteration Table**

| Step | $i$ | $A[i]$ | Comparison $A[i] == 45$? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 10 | No | $i \leftarrow 1$ |
| 1 | 1 | 35 | No | $i \leftarrow 2$ |
| 2 | 2 | 45 | **Yes** | $found \leftarrow 2$, break |

**Step 3: Return Result**
$$found = 2 \quad \text{(45 is located at index 2)}$$

**Average Case Analysis:** For a successful search, the average number of comparisons is:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n}{n} = \frac{n(n+1)/2}{n} = \frac{n+1}{2}$$

**Worst Case Analysis:** The element is at the last position or not present:

$$T_{worst} = n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{O}(n)$$

---

### 3.1.4 SORT Operation — Bubble Sort on $[50, 10, 45, 35, 25]$

**Step 1: Bubble Sort Pass-by-Pass**

**Pass 1** ($i = 0$): Compare adjacent pairs and swap if out of order.

$$\begin{aligned}
[50, 10, 45, 35, 25] &\rightarrow [10, 50, 45, 35, 25] \quad (50>10, \text{swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 45, 50, 35, 25] \quad (50>45, \text{swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 45, 35, 50, 25] \quad (50>35, \text{swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 45, 35, 25, 50] \quad (50>25, \text{swap})
\end{aligned}$$

Largest element **50** bubbles to the end.

**Pass 2** ($i = 1$): Repeat on the first $n-1$ elements.

$$\begin{aligned}
[10, 45, 35, 25, 50] &\rightarrow [10, 45, 35, 25, 50] \quad (10<45, \text{no swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 35, 45, 25, 50] \quad (45>35, \text{swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 35, 25, 45, 50] \quad (45>25, \text{swap})
\end{aligned}$$

**45** is now in the second-largest position.

**Pass 3** ($i = 2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
[10, 35, 25, 45, 50] &\rightarrow [10, 35, 25, 45, 50] \quad (10<35, \text{no swap}) \\
&\rightarrow [10, 25, 35, 45, 50] \quad (35>25, \text{swap})
\end{aligned}$$

**Pass 4** ($i = 3$):

$$[10, 25, 35, 45, 50] \rightarrow \text{no swaps, array is sorted}$$

**Step 2: Final Sorted Array**

$$A_{sorted} = [10, 25, 35, 45, 50]$$

**Step 3: Complexity Verification**

The number of comparisons in Bubble Sort is:

$$C(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - i - 1) = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

$$C(n) = \frac{n^2 - n}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operation Classification

| Operation | Mutates Structure? | Returns Value? | Pre-condition Strictness | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert** | Yes (Yes) | No (void) | High — bounds + capacity | Adding record to database |
| **Delete** | Yes (Yes) | Optional | High — non-empty + valid pos | Removing from queue |
| **Search** | No | Yes (index) | Low — only D must exist | Lookup in phonebook |
| **Sort** | Yes (Yes) | No (in-place) | Medium — comparable elements | Ranking exam scores |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ADT Operations Engine

This diagram models the internal architecture of how an ADT (e.g., List) responds to user-level operation calls and delegates them to the underlying storage.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USERLAYER["USER LAYER (Client Code)"]
        clientA["Client Program A"]
        clientB["Client Program B"]
    end

    subgraph ADTINTERFACE["ADT INTERFACE (Specification)"]
        ifInsert["insert D x pos"]
        ifDelete["delete D pos"]
        ifSearch["search D key"]
        ifSort["sort D order"]
    end

    subgraph COREENGINE["ADT CORE ENGINE (Implementation Logic)"]
        validator["Pre-condition Validator"]
        shifter["Memory Shifter Module"]
        comparator["Comparator Engine"]
        sizeTracker["Size Counter N"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["PHYSICAL STORAGE LAYER"]
        arrayImpl["Array Implementation Block"]
        listImpl["Linked List Implementation Block"]
        treeImpl["Tree Implementation Block"]
    end

    clientA --> ifInsert
    clientA --> ifDelete
    clientB --> ifSearch
    clientB --> ifSort

    ifInsert --> validator
    ifDelete --> validator
    ifSearch --> comparator
    ifSort --> comparator

    validator --> shifter
    validator --> sizeTracker
    comparator --> shifter
    shifter --> sizeTracker

    shifter --> arrayImpl
    shifter --> listImpl
    shifter --> treeImpl

    arrayImpl --> sizeTracker
    listImpl --> sizeTracker
    treeImpl --> sizeTracker
```

## 4.2 Insert Operation Flow (Detailed Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Call insert D x pos"]) --> checkCap{"Is N less than MAX capacity?"}
    checkCap -- "No" --> overflow["Return OVERFLOW ERROR"]
    checkCap -- "Yes" --> checkPos{"Is pos valid range 0 to N?"}
    checkPos -- "No" --> invalidPos["Return INVALID POSITION ERROR"]
    checkPos -- "Yes" --> initLoop["Set I equals N minus 1"]
    initLoop --> loopCheck{"Is I greater than or equal to pos?"}
    loopCheck -- "Yes" --> shift["A of I plus 1 equals A of I"]
    shift --> decI["Decrement I by 1"]
    decI --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- "No" --> place["A of pos equals x"]
    place --> incN["Increment N by 1"]
    incN --> success(["Return SUCCESS"])
```

## 4.3 Delete Operation Flow (Detailed Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Call delete D pos"]) --> checkEmpty{"Is N greater than 0?"}
    checkEmpty -- "No" --> underflow["Return UNDERFLOW ERROR"]
    checkEmpty -- "Yes" --> checkPos2{"Is pos valid range 0 to N-1?"}
    checkPos2 -- "No" --> invalidPos2["Return INVALID POSITION ERROR"]
    checkPos2 -- "Yes" --> saveVal["deletedVal equals A of pos"]
    saveVal --> initLoop2["Set I equals pos"]
    initLoop2 --> loopCheck2{"Is I less than N minus 1?"}
    loopCheck2 -- "Yes" --> shift2["A of I equals A of I plus 1"]
    shift2 --> incI["Increment I by 1"]
    incI --> loopCheck2
    loopCheck2 -- "No" --> decN["Decrement N by 1"]
    decN --> clean["A of N equals 0 or null"]
    clean --> ret(["Return deletedVal and SUCCESS"])
```

## 4.4 Search Operation Flow (Linear Scan)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Call search D key"]) --> init["I equals 0, found equals minus 1"]
    init --> loopCheck{"Is I less than N?"}
    loopCheck -- "No" --> ret(["Return found"])
    loopCheck -- "Yes" --> compare{"Does A of I equal key?"}
    compare -- "Yes" --> setFound["found equals I, break loop"]
    compare -- "No" --> incI["Increment I by 1"]
    incI --> loopCheck
    setFound --> ret
```

## 4.5 Sort Operation Flow (Generic Comparison Sort)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Call sort D order"]) --> checkN{"Is N greater than 1?"}
    checkN -- "No" --> done(["Return Already Sorted"])
    checkN -- "Yes" --> pickAlgo["Algorithm Selector Bubble or Selection or Insertion or Merge"]
    pickAlgo --> outerLoop["Outer Loop I from 0 to N minus 2"]
    outerLoop --> innerLoop["Inner Loop J from 0 to N minus I minus 2"]
    innerLoop --> compare{"A of J greater than A of J plus 1?"}
    compare -- "Yes" --> swap["Swap A of J and A of J plus 1"]
    compare -- "No" --> nextJ["Continue Inner Loop"]
    swap --> nextJ
    nextJ --> innerLoop
    innerLoop --> nextI["Continue Outer Loop"]
    nextI --> outerLoop
    outerLoop --> finish(["Return Sorted Array"])
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an Abstract Data Type (ADT). List any four standard operations performed on a List ADT.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a mathematical model that defines a data type by specifying the **type of data stored**, the **operations** that can be performed on it, and the **behavior** of those operations, without specifying the underlying implementation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Standard Operations (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. **Insert** — Add an element to the list at a given position.
> 2. **Delete** — Remove an element from the list at a given position.
> 3. **Search (Find)** — Locate the position of a given element in the list.
> 4. **Sort** — Rearrange the elements of the list in a specified order.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of (a) an array-based list, and (b) a linked-list-based list? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Implementation | Time Complexity | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Array-based List** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | All $n$ existing elements must be shifted one position to the right to make room at index $0$. This requires exactly $n$ assignments. |
| **(b) Linked List** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | A new node is created, its `next` pointer is set to the current head, and the head pointer is updated — only constant-time pointer manipulation. |

> **Memory Phrase for Exam:** *"Array shifts, Linked List points."*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as Part (a) for **7 marks** and Part (b) for **7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Operations Analysis on Array-Implemented List

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Write the algorithm (pseudocode) for the **INSERT** and **DELETE** operations on a List ADT implemented using an array. Clearly state the pre-conditions and post-conditions for each.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm: INSERT(D, pos, x)**

```
Pre-condition: D is an array of size MAX, n < MAX, 0 <= pos <= n
Post-condition: x is stored at D[pos], n becomes n+1

1. IF n == MAX THEN
2.     PRINT "Overflow: List is full"
3.     RETURN FAILURE
4. END IF
5. IF pos < 0 OR pos > n THEN
6.     PRINT "Invalid Position"
7.     RETURN FAILURE
8. END IF
9. FOR i = n-1 DOWNTO pos DO
10.     D[i+1] <- D[i]
11. END FOR
12. D[pos] <- x
13. n <- n + 1
14. RETURN SUCCESS
```

**Algorithm: DELETE(D, pos)**

```
Pre-condition: D is an array, n > 0, 0 <= pos < n
Post-condition: D[pos] is removed, n becomes n-1, returns deleted value

1. IF n == 0 THEN
2.     PRINT "Underflow: List is empty"
3.     RETURN FAILURE
4. END IF
5. IF pos < 0 OR pos >= n THEN
6.     PRINT "Invalid Position"
7.     RETURN FAILURE
8. END IF
9. val <- D[pos]
10. FOR i = pos TO n-2 DO
11.     D[i] <- D[i+1]
12. END FOR
13. n <- n - 1
14. RETURN val
```

**Complexity Analysis:** Both algorithms have time complexity $\mathcal{O}(n)$ in the worst case due to the shifting loop.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Consider the following array: $A = [15, 8, 22, 7, 31, 14]$, where $n = 6$. Perform the following operations **in sequence** and show the state of the array after each:

(i) Insert 10 at position 2
(ii) Delete element at position 4
(iii) Search for element 31 using linear search
(iv) Sort the array in ascending order using Bubble Sort (show all passes)

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Insert 10 at position 2 (1.5 Marks):**
Shift elements from index 5 down to index 2 one position to the right.

$$A = [15, 8, 22, 7, 31, 14] \rightarrow A = [15, 8, \mathbf{10}, 22, 7, 31, 14]$$

Shifts performed: $6 - 2 = 4$ shifts. New $n = 7$.

**(ii) Delete element at position 4 (1.5 Marks):**
After step (i), $A = [15, 8, 10, 22, 7, 31, 14]$. The element at position 4 is $7$. Delete it.

$$A = [15, 8, 10, 22, 7, 31, 14] \rightarrow A = [15, 8, 10, 22, 31, 14]$$

Shifts performed: $7 - 4 - 1 = 2$ shifts. New $n = 6$.

**(iii) Linear search for 31 (1 Mark):**

| Index | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 15 | 8 | 10 | 22 | **31** | 14 |

Element 31 is found at **index 4** after 5 comparisons. Average comparisons = $(6+1)/2 = 3.5$.

**(iv) Bubble Sort in ascending order (3 Marks):**

**Pass 1:** $[15, 8, 10, 22, 31, 14] \rightarrow [8, 15, 10, 22, 31, 14] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 22, 31, 14] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 22, 31, 14] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 22, 14, 31]$

**Pass 2:** $[8, 10, 15, 22, 14, 31] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 22, 14, 31] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 22, 14, 31] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 14, 22, 31]$

**Pass 3:** $[8, 10, 15, 14, 22, 31] \rightarrow [8, 10, 15, 14, 22, 31] \rightarrow [8, 10, 14, 15, 22, 31]$

**Pass 4:** $[8, 10, 14, 15, 22, 31] \rightarrow$ No swaps. **Sorted.**

$$A_{sorted} = [8, 10, 14, 15, 22, 31]$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Operations on Linked List ADT)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **SEARCH** operation on a **singly linked list** with a clear algorithm. What is its time complexity, and how does it compare with searching in a sorted array?

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm: SEARCH(head, key)**

```
Pre-condition: head is a pointer to the first node (or NULL)
Post-condition: Returns position of key, or -1 if not found. List unchanged.

1. curr <- head
2. pos <- 0
3. WHILE curr != NULL DO
4.     IF curr->data == key THEN
5.         RETURN pos
6.     END IF
7.     curr <- curr->next
8.     pos <- pos + 1
9. END WHILE
10. RETURN -1
```

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — in the worst case, we traverse all $n$ nodes. Average case is $(n+1)/2$.

**Comparison with Sorted Array:**

| Aspect | Singly Linked List | Sorted Array |
|---|---|---|
| **Best Search** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (must traverse) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (direct access by index) |
| **Binary Search** | Not Applicable (no random access) | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ |
| **Memory Layout** | Non-contiguous (pointer-based) | Contiguous (cache-friendly) |
| **Insertion after search** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ if node is found | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ due to shifting |

**Conclusion:** A sorted array supports **binary search** ($\mathcal{O}(\log n)$) but pays a high cost on insert/delete. A linked list allows cheap insert/delete but cannot do binary search. **Trade-off is fundamental.**

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Given a linked list: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$. Perform the following:

(i) Insert a new node with value 25 after the node containing 20.
(ii) Delete the node containing 30 from the modified list.
(iii) Sort the linked list in ascending order using any sort algorithm and show step-by-step pointer rearrangements.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Insert 25 after 20 (2 Marks):**

Traverse to find node with value 20. Create a new node with data 25 and adjust pointers:

$$10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow \mathbf{25} \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**(ii) Delete node 30 (2 Marks):**

To delete 30, traverse to the node *before* 30 (which is 25) and bypass 30:

$$10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

Deleted node 30.

**(iii) Sort in ascending order (3 Marks):**

**Method: Swap data (not pointers).** This is the simplest approach for singly linked lists.

Starting list: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$

This list is **already sorted** because the original elements were in sorted order and both insert/delete preserved that order. No swaps needed.

$$A_{sorted} = [10, 20, 25, 40, 50]$$

If the list were unsorted (e.g., $40 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 50$), we would use **Bubble Sort on Linked List** by repeatedly comparing adjacent nodes and swapping their data fields.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update `n` (size counter):** Many students perform the shift correctly but forget the line `n <- n + 1` (for insert) or `n <- n - 1` (for delete). This costs **1 full mark** every time.
>
> 2. **Wrong loop boundaries:** In INSERT, the loop should be `FOR i = n-1 DOWNTO pos`, not `FOR i = pos TO n-1`. Reversing the direction destroys the original data! In DELETE, the loop is `FOR i = pos TO n-2`. Examiners check this **strictly**.
>
> 3. **Missing pre-conditions:** Writing the algorithm without stating pre-conditions is considered **incomplete** in KTU 2024 Scheme — you lose **0.5 to 1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Confusing average and worst case:** In linear search, students often write $\mathcal{O}(n/2)$ for average case. Big-O notation gives the **asymptotic upper bound**, so it is still $\mathcal{O}(n)$, not $\mathcal{O}(n/2)$. The $(n+1)/2$ is a *count*, not a Big-O class.
>
> 5. **Bubble Sort off-by-one errors:** The outer loop runs from $0$ to $n-2$ (total $n-1$ passes), and the inner loop runs from $0$ to $n-i-2$. Memorize this formula. Writing $n-1$ for both is a **fatal error**.
>
> 6. **Confusing "position" with "index":** Position in KTU textbooks is often **1-based** (position 1 = index 0). Always clarify in your answer which convention you are using. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **ADT = Behavior Contract** — defines *what* operations do, not *how* they are implemented.
- **Four Pillars:** Insert, Delete, Search, Sort — every other data structure operation is built on these.
- **Array Insert at front = $\mathcal{O}(n)$** (shifts all elements right). **Linked List Insert at front = $\mathcal{O}(1)$** (just pointer update).
- **Array Delete at arbitrary position = $\mathcal{O}(n)$** (shifts left). **Linked List Delete = $\mathcal{O}(1)$** after locating the predecessor.
- **Linear Search is always $\mathcal{O}(n)$** — no faster way exists for unsorted data.
- **Binary Search needs sorted data** and gives $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, but only works on random-access structures (arrays).
- **Comparison-based sorting has a lower bound of $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$** — this is a **theoretical limit**, not just an implementation detail.
- **Bubble Sort swaps = $(n-1)(n-2)/2$** in worst case. **Comparisons = $n(n-1)/2$**.
- **Always state pre-conditions and post-conditions** for ADT operations in your algorithm answers.
- **Use `n` for size, `pos` for insertion/deletion position, `key` for search target** — this is the standard KTU notation.
- **Logical cleanup after delete:** Decrement $n$ and optionally set $A[n] = 0$ to mark the slot as free.
- **Overflow (insert into full) and Underflow (delete from empty) are the two classic error conditions** — always check for them first.
- **Mid formula for binary search:** $mid = \lfloor (low + high) / 2 \rfloor$ — KTU accepts both floor and integer division since we are working in integer arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Queue as ADT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Queue as ADT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Queue (ADT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstract Data Type (ADT)**: An Abstract Data Type is a theoretical concept in computer science that defines a data type purely by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a user, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does not specify *how* the data is stored in memory; it only specifies *what* operations can be performed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Queue (ADT Definition)**: A **Queue** is a linear Abstract Data Type (ADT) that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** principle. In a queue, the element that is inserted first is the one that gets removed first. Insertions occur at one end called the **rear** (or **tail** or **back**), and deletions occur at the other end called the **front** (or **head**). This discipline guarantees that elements are processed in the exact order in which they arrived, making it a fundamental **ordered** data structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

To make this concept instantly accessible, imagine the following real-world scenarios:

- **Cinema Ticket Counter Line**: People stand in a line to buy tickets. The person who arrives **first** is served **first** and leaves the line. New people join at the **rear** of the line. Once a person is served, they exit from the **front**. This is a perfect physical embodiment of a queue.

- **One-Way Traffic on a Narrow Bridge**: Cars enter the bridge from one end (rear) and exit from the other end (front). No overtaking is permitted, and the order of entry equals the order of exit.

- **Printer Spooler**: When multiple documents are sent to a single shared printer, they are queued. The first document sent is printed first, while later documents wait their turn at the back of the queue.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember**: Think **FIFO = First In, First Out**. Compare this with a **Stack** which is **LIFO = Last In, First Out** (like a stack of plates in a cafeteria). Queues are the opposite of stacks in terms of access discipline.

## 1.3 Queue ADT — Formal Specification (Mathematical Model)

A Queue ADT can be formally defined as an ordered pair:

$$Q = (D, O)$$

where:

- $D$ is the **domain** (a finite sequence of homogeneous elements): $D = \{d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n\}$
- $O$ is the **set of operations** defined on the queue.

The **state** of the queue at any instant is fully described by two indices or pointers: **front** and **rear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Students must be able to **state the ADT specification** of a queue, including the preconditions, postconditions, input parameters, and output parameters of every primitive operation. This is a frequently asked short-answer question in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primitive Operations of Queue ADT

A complete Queue ADT is defined by the following **primitive operations**. Each operation has a specific signature, preconditions, and postconditions.

### 2.1.1 Operation 1: `enqueue(x)` — Insertion

- **Signature**: `Procedure enqueue(Q, x)`
- **Input**: Queue $Q$, element $x$ to be inserted.
- **Precondition**: Queue must not be **full** (i.e., `isFull(Q) = false`).
- **Postcondition**: $x$ is added to the **rear** of $Q$; queue size increases by 1.
- **Effect**: $Q_{new} = (Q_{old} \cdot x)$ — concatenation of the old queue with $x$.

### 2.1.2 Operation 2: `dequeue()` — Deletion

- **Signature**: `Function dequeue(Q) → element`
- **Input**: Queue $Q$.
- **Precondition**: Queue must not be **empty** (i.e., `isEmpty(Q) = false`).
- **Output**: The element at the **front** of $Q$ is returned and removed.
- **Postcondition**: Front element is removed; queue size decreases by 1.

### 2.1.3 Operation 3: `front()` / `peek()` — Inspection

- **Signature**: `Function front(Q) → element`
- **Input**: Queue $Q$.
- **Precondition**: Queue must not be empty.
- **Output**: Returns the front element **without removing it**.

### 2.1.4 Operation 4: `rear()` — Rear Inspection

- **Signature**: `Function rear(Q) → element`
- **Output**: Returns the rear (last) element without removing it.

### 2.1.5 Operation 5: `isEmpty(Q)` — Empty Test

- **Output**: Returns **true** if queue has zero elements, else **false**.

### 2.1.6 Operation 6: `isFull(Q)` — Full Test

- **Output**: Returns **true** if queue has reached maximum capacity $N$, else **false**.

### 2.1.7 Operation 7: `size(Q)` — Size Query

- **Output**: Returns the number of elements currently in the queue.

## 2.2 Variants of Queue ADT

| Variant | Insertion End | Deletion End | Special Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Queue** (Linear Queue) | Rear | Front | Basic FIFO; suffers from "false overflow" |
| **Circular Queue** | Rear (mod N) | Front (mod N) | Reuses freed slots; eliminates false overflow |
| **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)** | Both ends | Both ends | Most flexible; supports `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear` |
| **Priority Queue** | Based on priority | Front (highest priority) | Order of processing is based on priority, not arrival time |
| **Input-Restricted Deque** | Only Rear | Both ends | Insertion allowed at one end only |
| **Output-Restricted Deque** | Both ends | Only Front | Deletion allowed at one end only |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Queue Size (Linear) | $\text{size} = \text{rear} - \text{front} + 1$ | When using 0-based indexing | Standard for non-circular queues |
| Empty Condition | $\text{front} = -1$ AND $\text{rear} = -1$ | Initial state | Used in array-based queue |
| Full Condition (Linear) | $\text{rear} = N - 1$ | $N$ = array capacity | Causes "false overflow" |
| Rear Update (Linear) | $\text{rear} = \text{rear} + 1$ | Then `Q[rear] = x` | Increments before insertion |
| Front Update (Linear) | $\text{front} = \text{front} + 1$ | After dequeue | Increments after deletion |
| Rear Update (Circular) | $\text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \mod N$ | Modular arithmetic | Wraps around to 0 |
| Front Update (Circular) | $\text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \mod N$ | Modular arithmetic | Wraps around to 0 |
| Full Condition (Circular) | $(\text{rear} + 1) \mod N = \text{front}$ | One slot wasted | Standard convention to distinguish from empty |
| Empty Condition (Circular) | $\text{front} = -1$ | Alternative: $\text{rear} = \text{front}$ with counter | Both conventions are used |
| Size (Circular) | $\text{size} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \mod N$ | Adds $N$ to handle negative | Robust modulo formula |
| Time Complexity (Enqueue) | $O(1)$ | Constant time | Independent of queue size |
| Time Complexity (Dequeue) | $O(1)$ | Constant time | Independent of queue size |
| Time Complexity (Front/peek) | $O(1)$ | Constant time | Direct index access |
| Space Complexity | $O(N)$ | Linear in capacity | Where $N$ is the maximum size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on vertical bars**: All absolute value notations in the formulas above use the `mod` operator instead of the `$\vert$` symbol to preserve markdown table integrity, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications of Queues

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: Questions like *"List four real-world applications of queues"* or *"Why is queue preferred over stack in OS scheduling?"* are common 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

- **Operating Systems**: CPU scheduling algorithms (FCFS — First Come First Serve), process synchronization, ready queue, wait queue, I/O buffers, and spooling.
- **Computer Networks**: Packet routing, buffers in routers, mail queues, message queues in distributed systems (Kafka, RabbitMQ).
- **Disk Scheduling**: FCFS disk scheduling where requests are processed in the order they arrive.
- **Web Servers**: Request handling queues to manage concurrent client requests in a fair order.
- **Data Structures**: BFS (Breadth-First Search) traversal of graphs and trees, level-order traversal of binary trees.
- **Simulation**: Modeling real-world waiting systems such as bank queues, airport runway traffic, hospital triage.

## 2.5 The "False Overflow" Problem (Why Linear Queue Fails)

A critical conceptual issue with a naive array-based linear queue:

- After several `enqueue` and `dequeue` operations, the `front` pointer moves forward.
- Even though there may be free slots at the **beginning** of the array (indices $0, 1, 2, \ldots$), the `rear` pointer may already be at the end ($N-1$).
- A new `enqueue` will report **"Queue Full"** even though the queue is logically not full. This is called **false overflow**.

**Solution**: Use a **Circular Queue**, which wraps the rear and front pointers back to index 0 using modular arithmetic, thereby reclaiming the wasted space.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Circular Queue Pointer Arithmetic

We will exhaustively derive the **rear update** and **front update** formulas for a circular queue, since this is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question topic.

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Circular Queue Model

Consider an array `Q[0..N-1]` of capacity $N$ to implement the queue. We maintain two integer pointers: `front` and `rear`.

**Initial State** (empty queue):

$$\text{front} = -1, \quad \text{rear} = -1$$

**Invariant 1 (Empty Queue Condition)**: The queue is empty if and only if:

$$\text{front} = -1 \quad \text{(equivalently, rear = -1)}$$

**Invariant 2 (Full Queue Condition)**: To distinguish the empty state from the full state, we intentionally **waste one slot**. The queue is full if and only if:

$$(\text{rear} + 1) \mod N = \text{front}$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Enqueue (Insertion) Formula

**Goal**: Insert element $x$ at the rear of the circular queue.

**Step 1 — Check Overflow**: Verify that the queue is not full.

$$\text{If } (\text{rear} + 1) \mod N = \text{front} \Rightarrow \text{Queue is Full. Abort.}$$

**Step 2 — Special Case (First Insertion)**: If the queue is initially empty ($\text{front} = -1$), set both pointers to 0.

$$\text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 0$$

**Step 3 — General Case Update of Rear**: For subsequent insertions, increment `rear` circularly.

$$\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \mod N$$

**Step 4 — Place the Element**: Store $x$ at the updated rear position.

$$Q[\text{rear}] \leftarrow x$$

**Why does `(rear + 1) mod N` work?**

The `mod N` operation ensures that when `rear` reaches $N - 1$, the next increment yields:

$$(N - 1 + 1) \mod N = N \mod N = 0$$

Thus, the pointer **wraps around** to the beginning of the array, reusing freed slots. Without `mod`, the pointer would attempt to access `Q[N]`, which is out of bounds.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Dequeue (Deletion) Formula

**Goal**: Remove and return the element at the front.

**Step 1 — Check Underflow**: Verify the queue is not empty.

$$\text{If } \text{front} = -1 \Rightarrow \text{Queue is Empty. Abort.}$$

**Step 2 — Save the Front Value**:

$$x \leftarrow Q[\text{front}]$$

**Step 3 — Special Case (Last Element)**: If after removal the queue becomes empty (i.e., `front == rear`), reset both pointers to -1.

$$\text{If } \text{front} = \text{rear} \Rightarrow \text{front} = -1, \; \text{rear} = -1$$

**Step 4 — General Case Update of Front**: Otherwise, advance the front pointer circularly.

$$\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \mod N$$

**Step 5 — Return the Saved Value**: Return $x$.

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Queue Size Formula

The size (number of elements) of a circular queue must always be a non-negative integer, even when `rear < front` due to wrap-around.

**Step 1 — Naive Difference**: Consider the linear difference:

$$\Delta = \text{rear} - \text{front}$$

**Step 2 — Problem**: If $\text{rear} = 2$ and $\text{front} = 5$ (wrap-around case), then $\Delta = -3$, which is meaningless for size.

**Step 3 — Add $N$ to Force Positivity**:

$$\Delta' = \text{rear} - \text{front} + N$$

**Step 4 — Apply Mod $N$ to Normalize**: This bounds the result in $[0, N-1]$.

$$\text{size} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \mod N$$

**Numerical Example**: Let $N = 7$, $\text{rear} = 2$, $\text{front} = 5$.

$$\text{size} = (2 - 5 + 7) \mod 7 = 4 \mod 7 = 4$$

This correctly indicates 4 elements in the queue (occupying indices 5, 6, 0, 1).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Array-based Linear Queue, $N=5$)

**Operations performed in order**:

1. `enqueue(10)`
2. `enqueue(20)`
3. `enqueue(30)`
4. `dequeue()` → returns 10
5. `enqueue(40)`
6. `enqueue(50)`
7. `enqueue(60)` → should this succeed?

**Trace Table**:

| Step | Operation | front | rear | Q[0] | Q[1] | Q[2] | Q[3] | Q[4] | Output / Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | -1 | -1 | — | — | — | — | — | Empty |
| 1 | enqueue(10) | 0 | 0 | 10 | — | — | — | — | Inserted |
| 2 | enqueue(20) | 0 | 1 | 10 | 20 | — | — | — | Inserted |
| 3 | enqueue(30) | 0 | 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | — | — | Inserted |
| 4 | dequeue() | 1 | 2 | — | 20 | 30 | — | — | Returns 10 |
| 5 | enqueue(40) | 1 | 3 | — | 20 | 30 | 40 | — | Inserted |
| 6 | enqueue(50) | 1 | 4 | — | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | Inserted |
| 7 | enqueue(60) | 1 | 4 | — | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | **Queue Full!** False overflow: indices 0 is free. |

This example **demonstrates the false overflow** problem — at step 7, although `Q[0]` is free, the linear queue declares itself full. This is the central motivation for using a **circular queue**.

## 3.3 The Same Example with a Circular Queue ($N=5$)

Reusing the same operations with modular arithmetic:

- At step 7, $\text{rear} = 4$, so the new rear is computed as:

$$(4 + 1) \mod 5 = 0$$

- Therefore, $\text{rear} = 0$, and $Q[0] = 60$. The insertion **succeeds**, demonstrating the circular reuse of freed slots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip**: When drawing a queue diagram in the exam, **always label the front and rear arrows clearly**, show the array indices ($0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$), and use a **modular arrow** for circular queues. Examiners reward clean, well-labeled diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Queue ADT and Its Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input / Caller Module"]
        A["User / Application Code"]
    end

    subgraph ADT["Queue ADT Layer"]
        B["enqueue x"]
        C["dequeue"]
        D["front / peek"]
        E["rear"]
        F["isEmpty"]
        G["isFull"]
        H["size"]
    end

    subgraph IMPL["Implementation Layer (Hidden from User)"]
        I["Array Storage Q 0 to N minus 1"]
        J["Pointer front"]
        K["Pointer rear"]
    end

    A -- invokes --> B
    A -- invokes --> C
    A -- invokes --> D
    A -- invokes --> E
    A -- invokes --> F
    A -- invokes --> G
    A -- invokes --> H

    B -- modifies --> K
    C -- modifies --> J
    B -- writes to --> I
    C -- reads from --> I
    D -- reads --> I
    E -- reads --> I
    F -- uses --> J
    G -- uses --> J
    G -- uses --> K
    H -- uses --> J
    H -- uses --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The diagram visually separates the **public interface (ADT layer)** from the **internal implementation**. The user only interacts with the operations in the middle layer; the array, front pointer, and rear pointer are encapsulated. This is the core philosophical idea behind ADTs — **separation of interface from implementation**.

## 4.2 Flowchart — Enqueue Operation in a Circular Queue

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start enqueue x"] --> B{"Is front equal to minus 1? Empty?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Set front = 0, rear = 0"]
    C --> D["Q rear = x"]
    B -- No --> E{"Is rear plus 1 mod N equal to front? Full?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Display Queue Full. Return error"]
    E -- No --> G["rear = rear plus 1 mod N"]
    G --> D
    D --> H["End enqueue"]
    F --> H
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Circular Queue After Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARR["Circular Array Q 0 to 6 capacity 7"]
        N0["Index 0: 70"]
        N1["Index 1: empty"]
        N2["Index 2: empty"]
        N3["Index 3: empty"]
        N4["Index 4: 10"]
        N5["Index 5: 20"]
        N6["Index 6: 30"]
    end
    F["FRONT points to Index 4 value 10"] -.-> N4
    R["REAR points to Index 0 value 70"] -.-> N0
    N6 -. wrap to .-> N0
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the schematic**: `front` is at index 4, `rear` is at index 0. The logical order of elements is $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 70$, traversing indices 4 → 5 → 6 → 0. The **dashed wrap-around arrow** illustrates the modular behavior.

## 4.4 Comparison: Stack vs Queue Access Discipline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["Stack LIFO"]
        S1["Top element is the only accessible end"]
        S2["Push adds to top"]
        S3["Pop removes from top"]
    end
    subgraph QU["Queue FIFO"]
        Q1["Rear end for insertion enqueue"]
        Q2["Front end for deletion dequeue"]
        Q3["Elements processed in arrival order"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
