# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Queue as an Abstract Data Type. List any four operations supported by a queue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

A Queue is a linear Abstract Data Type that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** principle. Insertions are made at the **rear** end and deletions are made at the **front** end.

**Four Primitive Operations**:
1. `enqueue(x)` — inserts element $x$ at the rear.
2. `dequeue()` — removes and returns the element at the front.
3. `front()` / `peek()` — returns the front element without removing it.
4. `isEmpty()` — returns true if the queue has no elements.

*(Allocate 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for FIFO explanation, and 1 mark for the operations list.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Linear Queue and a Circular Queue. State the formula to check the "full" condition in a circular queue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Feature | Linear Queue | Circular Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Memory utilization | Suffers from false overflow | Reuses freed slots; no false overflow |
| Rear update | `rear = rear + 1` | `rear = (rear + 1) mod N` |
| Front update | `front = front + 1` | `front = (front + 1) mod N` |
| Full condition | `rear = N - 1` | `(rear + 1) mod N = front` |

**Full condition formula (Circular Queue)**:

$$(\text{rear} + 1) \mod N = \text{front}$$

*(Allocate 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the key difference, and 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Linear Queue ADT** and its operations. Write the ADT specification (preconditions, postconditions) for `enqueue` and `dequeue`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Circular Queue** with a neat diagram. How is the "false overflow" problem of a linear queue overcome by a circular queue? Demonstrate with the operation sequence: `enqueue(5), enqueue(10), enqueue(15), dequeue(), enqueue(20)` for $N = 4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Linear Queue ADT (7 Marks)

A **Linear Queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** principle. It can be visualized as a horizontal line where:

- The **front** pointer tracks the position from where elements are dequeued.
- The **rear** pointer tracks the position at which new elements are enqueued.

**Diagrammatic Representation**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LQ["Linear Queue of Capacity N"]
        A["Q 0"]
        B["Q 1"]
        C["Q 2"]
        D["Q 3"]
        E["Q 4"]
        F["Q N minus 1"]
    end
    FP["FRONT"] -.-> A
    RP["REAR"] -.-> D
```

**ADT Specification for `enqueue(Q, x)`**:

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Operation Name** | `enqueue` |
| **Inputs** | Queue $Q$, element $x$ |
| **Preconditions** | `isFull(Q) = false` |
| **Postconditions** | $x$ is added to the rear of $Q$; `rear = rear + 1`; `size(Q) = old size + 1` |
| **Outputs** | None (modifies $Q$ in place) |
| **Errors** | Overflow error if queue is full |

**ADT Specification for `dequeue(Q)`**:

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Operation Name** | `dequeue` |
| **Inputs** | Queue $Q$ |
| **Preconditions** | `isEmpty(Q) = false` |
| **Postconditions** | Returns the front element $x$; `front = front + 1`; `size(Q) = old size - 1` |
| **Outputs** | The dequeued element $x$ |
| **Errors** | Underflow error if queue is empty |

**Other operations**: `front(Q)`, `rear(Q)`, `isEmpty(Q)`, `isFull(Q)`, `size(Q)` — all $O(1)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Circular Queue and False Overflow Resolution (7 Marks)

A **Circular Queue** is a linear data structure in which the **last position is connected back to the first position**, forming a logical circle. This is achieved using **modular arithmetic** on the `front` and `rear` pointers.

**Circular Queue Diagram**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRC["Circular Queue Q 0 to 6 capacity 7"]
        N0["Index 0"]
        N1["Index 1"]
        N2["Index 2"]
        N3["Index 3"]
        N4["Index 4"]
        N5["Index 5"]
        N6["Index 6"]
        N6 -. wrap .-> N0
    end
```

**Overcoming False Overflow**:

In a linear queue, after repeated `enqueue`/`dequeue` operations, the `front` pointer moves to higher indices, and the slots at the beginning of the array become permanently inaccessible, even though they are free. This **wasted memory** phenomenon is called **false overflow**.

In a circular queue, when `rear` reaches the last index ($N-1$), the next `enqueue` operation updates `rear` as:

$$\text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \mod N = (N-1+1) \mod N = 0$$

Thus, `rear` wraps around to index 0, **reusing the freed slots** at the beginning of the array. Similarly, `front` wraps around. This eliminates false overflow and utilizes 100% of the allocated memory (except for one slot reserved to distinguish full from empty).

**Numerical Demonstration with $N = 4$**:

Operation sequence: `enqueue(5), enqueue(10), enqueue(15), dequeue(), enqueue(20)`.

**Trace Table** (using 0-based indexing, $\text{front} = \text{rear} = -1$ initially):

| Step | Operation | front | rear | Q[0] | Q[1] | Q[2] | Q[3] | Status / Return |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | -1 | -1 | — | — | — | — | Empty |
| 1 | enqueue(5) | 0 | 0 | 5 | — | — | — | Inserted |
| 2 | enqueue(10) | 0 | 1 | 5 | 10 | — | — | Inserted |
| 3 | enqueue(15) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 10 | 15 | — | Inserted |
| 4 | dequeue() | 1 | 2 | — | 10 | 15 | — | Returns 5 |
| 5 | enqueue(20) | 1 | 3 | — | 10 | 15 | 20 | Inserted |

After step 5, if we attempt one more `enqueue(25)`, the linear queue would declare it full, but the circular queue computes:

$$\text{rear}_{\text{new}} = (3 + 1) \mod 4 = 0$$

And inserts 25 at index 0, demonstrating the wrap-around. *(If the question demands $N=4$, the full condition is $(\text{rear}+1) \mod 4 = \text{front}$, so the queue becomes full at the next insertion.)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)**. With neat diagrams, explain the four types of deques and their operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Implement the **enqueue and dequeue operations of a circular queue** using an array. Show the trace of operations and compute the time complexity of each operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Double-Ended Queue (Deque) (7 Marks)

A **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)** is a generalized queue ADT in which **insertion and deletion can be performed at both ends** — the front and the rear. It is the most flexible variant of the queue family and is sometimes called a "head-tail linked list."

**Four Types of Deques**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DQFamily["Deque Variants"]
        D1["Input Restricted Deque: Insertion only at REAR. Deletion at BOTH ends"]
        D2["Output Restricted Deque: Deletion only at FRONT. Insertion at BOTH ends"]
        D3["Generalized Deque: Insertion and deletion at BOTH ends"]
    end
```

**Primitive Operations of a Generalized Deque**:

| Operation | Description | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| `insertFront(x)` | Inserts $x$ at the front end | $O(1)$ |
| `insertRear(x)` | Inserts $x$ at the rear end | $O(1)$ |
| `deleteFront()` | Removes and returns the front element | $O(1)$ |
| `deleteRear()` | Removes and returns the rear element | $O(1)$ |
| `getFront()` | Returns the front element without removing | $O(1)$ |
| `getRear()` | Returns the rear element without removing | $O(1)$ |
| `isEmpty()` | Tests if deque is empty | $O(1)$ |
| `isFull()` | Tests if deque is full | $O(1)$ |

**Applications of Deques**:
- **A-Steal job scheduling algorithm** in multiprocessor systems.
- **Undo-Redo functionality** in text editors.
- **Sliding window maximum/minimum** problems in competitive programming.
- **Palindrome checker** (insert at one end, remove from both ends and compare).

---

#### Part (b) — Circular Queue Implementation (7 Marks)

**Algorithm for `enqueue(Q, x)`**:

1. **Step 1**: If `front == -1` (empty queue), set `front = 0` and `rear = 0`.
2. **Step 2**: Else compute $\text{rear}_{\text{new}} = (\text{rear} + 1) \mod N$.
3. **Step 3**: If $\text{rear}_{\text{new}} == \text{front}$, print **"Queue Overflow"** and return.
4. **Step 4**: Set $\text{rear} = \text{rear}_{\text{new}}$.
5. **Step 5**: Set $Q[\text{rear}] = x$.

**Algorithm for `dequeue(Q)`**:

1. **Step 1**: If `front == -1` (empty queue), print **"Queue Underflow"** and return.
2. **Step 2**: Save $x = Q[\text{front}]$.
3. **Step 3**: If `front == rear` (only one element), set `front = rear = -1`.
4. **Step 4**: Else set $\text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \mod N$.
5. **Step 5**: Return $x$.

**Time Complexity Analysis**:

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `enqueue` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Constant number of arithmetic and assignment operations |
| `dequeue` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Constant number of arithmetic and assignment operations |
| `front`, `rear`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Direct pointer inspection |

**Space Complexity**: $O(N)$, where $N$ is the array capacity, regardless of how many elements are currently stored.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**:
> 1. **Confusing full vs empty in circular queue**: Many students forget to reserve one slot. The full condition `(rear + 1) mod N = front` is *not* the same as `rear = front` (which is typically the empty condition). This is the **#1 mistake** costing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to initialize both `front` and `rear` to -1** at the start, leading to garbage values.
> 3. **Not resetting pointers to -1** after the last element is dequeued. After dequeueing the last element, the queue must show `front = -1, rear = -1`, not `front = rear = 0`.
> 4. **Confusing Stack (LIFO) with Queue (FIFO)** in the definition. Examiners are strict on this.
> 5. **Omitting preconditions and postconditions** in the ADT specification. The ADT must formally state what must be true *before* and *after* each operation.
> 6. **Not drawing the array indices** ($0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$) in the diagram. This loses easy marks.
> 7. **Confusing "size" formula**: Use `(rear - front + N) mod N`, not the simple difference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Queue = FIFO (First-In, First-Out)**. Insertion at **rear**, deletion at **front**.
- ✅ **Stack = LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)**. Both insertion and deletion at **top**.
- ✅ An **ADT** specifies *what* operations do, not *how* they are implemented.
- ✅ The 7 primitive operations of Queue are: `enqueue`, `dequeue`, `front`, `rear`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`, `size`.
- ✅ **Linear Queue** uses simple `+1` updates; suffers from **false overflow**.
- ✅ **Circular Queue** uses `(index + 1) mod N` updates; **eliminates false overflow**.
- ✅ Circular queue **wastes one slot** to distinguish full from empty. Full condition: `(rear + 1) mod N = front`.
- ✅ Circular queue size formula: `(rear - front + N) mod N` — always non-negative.
- ✅ **Deque** allows insertion AND deletion at both ends; 4 sub-types exist.
- ✅ All basic queue operations are $O(1)$ time and $O(N)$ space.
- ✅ Real-world apps: CPU scheduling (FCFS), BFS, printer spooler, disk scheduling, network buffers.
- ✅ **Initial state**: `front = -1, rear = -1`. **After last dequeue**: reset both to -1.
- ✅ In exam diagrams, **always label front and rear arrows and array indices** for full marks.
- ✅ Master the trace tables — they are the most reliable way to score full marks in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Circular Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circular Queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In First Out)** principle, but the last position is logically connected back to the first position, forming a **circular ring**. It is implemented using a **one-dimensional array of fixed size** $\text{MAX}$, accompanied by two integer pointers — $\text{FRONT}$ (points to the element to be dequeued next) and $\text{REAR}$ (points to the position of the last enqueued element).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** Circular Queue is officially classified under *Linear Data Structures* in Module 1, despite its name suggesting a non-linear shape. Its classification is based on the **sequential access pattern** of its elements, not on the physical storage layout.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Roundabout

Imagine a **traffic roundabout** in front of a shopping mall:

- Vehicles **enter** the roundabout from a fixed entry lane.
- Vehicles **exit** the roundabout from a fixed exit lane.
- The road is **circular** — there is no "dead end" on either side.
- Once a vehicle exits, that **slot becomes immediately available** for a new vehicle entering from behind.

In a circular queue, the **array slot just behind the FRONT** (which would normally be wasted in a linear queue) is reused by wrapping REAR back to index $0$. This is the exact reason circular queues are called **"Space-Optimized Queues"**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

The following integer parameters govern every circular queue implementation:

| Metric | Symbol | Initial Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Capacity of Queue | $\text{MAX}$ | User-defined | Total number of elements the array can hold |
| Front Pointer | $\text{FRONT}$ | $-1$ | Index of the element at the head of the queue |
| Rear Pointer | $\text{REAR}$ | $-1$ | Index of the most recently enqueued element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\text{FRONT} = \text{REAR} = -1$ initially?** Using $-1$ as the sentinel value is a KTU-recommended convention. It unambiguously signals the **"queue is empty"** state without consuming any real array index (since array indices begin at $0$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Circular Queue? — The Linear Queue Limitation

A **linear (simple) queue** implemented using an array suffers from a critical flaw: after repeated ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations, the FRONT pointer advances forward. The slots *behind* FRONT are *vacated* but can **never be reused** — a phenomenon known as **"False Overflow"** or **"Memory Wastage"**.

The circular queue solves this by **wrapping REAR** to index $0$ once it crosses the boundary $\text{MAX} - 1$, using the **modulo operator** ($\bmod$).

## 2.2 The Two Principal Operations

### 2.2.1 ENQUEUE (Insertion at REAR)
- **Step 1:** Check if queue is full — if yes, report **Overflow**.
- **Step 2:** If queue was empty ($\text{FRONT} == -1$), set $\text{FRONT} = 0$.
- **Step 3:** Advance REAR using modular arithmetic: $\text{REAR} = (\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$.
- **Step 4:** Place the new element at the updated REAR position.

### 2.2.2 DEQUEUE (Deletion from FRONT)
- **Step 1:** Check if queue is empty — if yes, report **Underflow**.
- **Step 2:** Retrieve the element at FRONT.
- **Step 3:** If FRONT equals REAR (only one element), reset both to $-1$.
- **Step 4:** Otherwise, advance FRONT: $\text{FRONT} = (\text{FRONT} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$.

## 2.3 The Famous "One Slot Waste" Conundrum

To distinguish between the **EMPTY** and **FULL** conditions — both of which would otherwise show $\text{FRONT} == \text{REAR}$ — the standard KTU-approved technique **leaves one slot always empty** in the FULL state.

| Condition | Boolean Expression | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Is Empty?** | $\text{FRONT} == -1$ | No elements present |
| **Is Full?** | $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{FRONT}$ | Only one vacant slot remains |
| **Single Element** | $\text{FRONT} == \text{REAR}$ (and $\text{FRONT} \neq -1$) | Exactly one element in queue |
| **Wrap-around Active** | $\text{REAR} < \text{FRONT}$ | REAR has crossed the array boundary |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | LaTeX Form | Use Case |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Empty Condition | $\text{FRONT} = -1$ | Detect zero elements |
| 2 | Full Condition | $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} = \text{FRONT}$ | Detect zero vacant slots |
| 3 | Rear Update (ENQUEUE) | $\text{REAR} = (\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$ | Advance rear after insertion |
| 4 | Front Update (DEQUEUE) | $\text{FRONT} = (\text{FRONT} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$ | Advance front after deletion |
| 5 | Element Count | $(\text{REAR} - \text{FRONT} + 1 + \text{MAX}) \bmod \text{MAX}$ | Number of valid elements |
| 6 | Reset on Last Delete | $\text{FRONT} = \text{REAR} = -1$ | When removing the only element |
| 7 | Initial Front After First Insert | $\text{FRONT} = 0$ | Initialize when first enqueue occurs |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Scheduling:** Round-Robin scheduling algorithm in operating systems uses a circular queue to manage process execution time slices.
- **Memory Management:** Circular buffers are used in streaming data (audio/video), network packet routers, and DMA (Direct Memory Access) controllers.
- **Traffic Systems:** Round-robin traffic light controllers and token ring networks in computer architecture.
- **Embedded Systems:** UART hardware FIFOs in microcontrollers are hardware-implemented circular queues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rear-Pointer Wrap-Around Equation

We start with a queue of capacity $\text{MAX}$ and a rear pointer at position $\text{REAR}$. When a new element is enqueued, the rear must advance by exactly one position. However, if $\text{REAR}$ already equals $\text{MAX} - 1$, simply adding $1$ would produce an **array index out of bounds** error.

The mathematical solution is to use **modular arithmetic** so that the value **wraps around** to $0$ after exceeding $\text{MAX} - 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REAR}_{\text{new}} &= \text{REAR}_{\text{old}} + 1 \quad &\text{(naive linear increment)} \\
\text{REAR}_{\text{new}} &= (\text{REAR}_{\text{old}} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} \quad &\text{(circular wrap-around constraint)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The naive increment is sufficient for indices $0$ to $\text{MAX} - 2$, but at $\text{REAR} = \text{MAX} - 1$, the modulo operator returns $(\text{MAX} - 1 + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} = \text{MAX} \bmod \text{MAX} = 0$, achieving the wrap-around.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Full Condition

In a circular queue, both the EMPTY and FULL states would trivially satisfy $\text{FRONT} == \text{REAR}$ if we filled every slot. To disambiguate, we adopt the **"leave one slot vacant"** policy.

Let the queue have $\text{MAX} = 5$ slots. The maximum allowed elements is therefore $\text{MAX} - 1 = 4$. After filling 4 elements starting from $\text{FRONT} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REAR}_{\text{last filled}} &= 3 \\
\text{REAR}_{\text{next (hypothetical)}} &= 4 = \text{MAX} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

If we tried to enqueue one more element, the proposed update would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REAR}_{\text{trial}} &= (\text{REAR}_{\text{old}} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} \\
&= (3 + 1) \bmod 5 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $4 \neq \text{FRONT}$ (which is $0$), the queue is **NOT** full. But after one more enqueue, REAR becomes $0$, and $0 == \text{FRONT}$, which would falsely indicate EMPTY. The correct Full Condition must therefore be checked **before** incrementing REAR:

$$
(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} = \text{FRONT}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This equation checks if the *prospective* REAR position (after increment) collides with FRONT. A collision implies all $\text{MAX}$ slots are conceptually occupied, triggering Overflow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Element Count Formula

Let $n$ be the number of elements. We consider two cases.

**Case 1: $\text{REAR} \geq \text{FRONT}$ (No wrap-around has occurred yet)**

$$
n = \text{REAR} - \text{FRONT} + 1
$$

This is the standard linear count: from FRONT to REAR inclusive.

**Case 2: $\text{REAR} < \text{FRONT}$ (Wrap-around has occurred)**

Elements are split into two segments: from FRONT to $\text{MAX} - 1$, and from $0$ to REAR.

$$
n = (\text{MAX} - \text{FRONT}) + (\text{REAR} + 1)
$$

**Unified Formula:** Adding $\text{MAX}$ inside the modulo to handle the negative remainder in Case 2:

$$
n = (\text{REAR} - \text{FRONT} + 1 + \text{MAX}) \bmod \text{MAX}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When $\text{REAR} \geq \text{FRONT}$, the inner expression $\text{REAR} - \text{FRONT} + 1 + \text{MAX}$ exceeds $\text{MAX}$, so modulo brings it back to the correct count. When $\text{REAR} < \text{FRONT}$, the expression is less than $\text{MAX}$, so modulo leaves it unchanged.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Trace Matrix ($\text{MAX} = 5$)

Let us perform a sequence of operations to demonstrate the wrap-around behavior.

| Step | Operation | FRONT | REAR | Queue State (logical) | Overflow/Underflow |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Initialize | $-1$ | $-1$ | [ ] (Empty) | — |
| 1 | Enqueue 10 | $0$ | $0$ | [10] | OK |
| 2 | Enqueue 20 | $0$ | $1$ | [10, 20] | OK |
| 3 | Enqueue 30 | $0$ | $2$ | [10, 20, 30] | OK |
| 4 | Enqueue 40 | $0$ | $3$ | [10, 20, 30, 40] | OK |
| 5 | Enqueue 50 | $0$ | $4$ | [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] | OK |
| 6 | Enqueue 60 | $0$ | $4$ | [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] | **Overflow** |
| 7 | Dequeue | $1$ | $4$ | [20, 30, 40, 50] | OK |
| 8 | Dequeue | $2$ | $4$ | [30, 40, 50] | OK |
| 9 | Enqueue 70 | $2$ | $0$ | [30, 40, 50, 70] | OK (Wrap!) |
| 10 | Enqueue 80 | $2$ | $1$ | [30, 40, 50, 70, 80] | OK (Wrap!) |

> **Key Observation:** At Step 9, REAR wraps from $4$ to $0$, demonstrating the modulo arithmetic. At Step 10, attempting to enqueue 80 when the queue is genuinely full would now trigger the **Full Condition** check: $(1 + 1) \bmod 5 = 2 = \text{FRONT}$, leading to Overflow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — ENQUEUE Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startEn([Begin ENQUEUE Operation]) --> checkFullState{Is Queue Full?}
    checkFullState -- Yes --> overflowOut[Report OVERFLOW and Exit]
    checkFullState -- No --> checkFrontState{Is FRONT equal to minus 1?}
    checkFrontState -- Yes --> setFrontInit[Set FRONT to 0]
    checkFrontState -- No --> updateRearPos[REAR = REAR + 1 mod MAX]
    setFrontInit --> updateRearPos
    updateRearPos --> insertItem[Place Element X at queue of REAR]
    insertItem --> finishEn([End ENQUEUE])
    overflowOut --> finishOv([End])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — DEQUEUE Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startDe([Begin DEQUEUE Operation]) --> checkEmptyState{Is Queue Empty?}
    checkEmptyState -- Yes --> underflowOut[Report UNDERFLOW and Exit]
    checkEmptyState -- No --> fetchItem[Retrieve Element X from queue of FRONT]
    fetchItem --> checkLastState{Is FRONT equal to REAR?}
    checkLastState -- Yes --> resetPointers[Set FRONT to minus 1 and REAR to minus 1]
    checkLastState -- No --> updateFrontPos[FRONT = FRONT + 1 mod MAX]
    resetPointers --> finishDe([End DEQUEUE])
    updateFrontPos --> finishDe
    underflowOut --> finishUn([End])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Circular Queue Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Storage Layer]
        A0[Array Index 0]
        A1[Array Index 1]
        A2[Array Index 2]
        A3[Array Index 3]
        A4[Array Index 4]
    end
    subgraph B[Control Layer]
        B1[FRONT Pointer]
        B2[REAR Pointer]
        B3[isEmpty Module]
        B4[isFull Module]
    end
    subgraph C[Interface Layer]
        C1[enqueue Function]
        C2[dequeue Function]
        C3[display Function]
    end
    C1 -- Modulo Update --> B2
    C2 -- Modulo Update --> B1
    C3 -- Iterates between --> B1
    C1 -- Checks --> B4
    C2 -- Checks --> B3
    B1 -- References --> A
    B2 -- References --> A
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three layers — Interface (user-facing functions), Control (pointers + status checks), and Storage (physical array indices) — interact in a strict top-down hierarchy. The modulo operations on pointers ($+1 \bmod \text{MAX}$) act as the **bridge** between the Control Layer and the Storage Layer, enabling wrap-around.

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram — Pointer Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> EmptyState : Initialize FRONT minus 1 and REAR minus 1
    EmptyState --> OneElement : Enqueue first item
    OneElement --> ManyElements : Enqueue more
    ManyElements --> OneElement : Dequeue until single remains
    OneElement --> EmptyState : Dequeue last item
    ManyElements --> WrappedElements : REAR crosses MAX minus 1 boundary
    WrappedElements --> ManyElements : FRONT advances and slot freed
    WrappedElements --> OneElement : Continual dequeue
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a circular queue. List any two advantages it has over a simple linear queue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A circular queue is a linear data structure based on the FIFO principle in which the last position is connected back to the first position, forming a logical circle. The two pointers **FRONT** and **REAR** move in a circular fashion using the modulo operator.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Optimal Memory Utilization:** Slots vacated by DEQUEUE operations are immediately reusable, eliminating the "false overflow" problem of linear queues.
2. **Constant Time Operations:** Both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE execute in $O(1)$ time, regardless of the queue size, as the modulo operation is computationally cheap.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two advantages.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a linear queue and a circular queue. Mention the condition for the FULL state in a circular queue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Linear Queue | Circular Queue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointer Movement | Unidirectional; REAR moves only forward | Bidirectional via wrap-around using $\bmod$ |
| Memory Wastage | Suffers from "false overflow" | No wastage; full slot reuse |
| FULL Condition | $\text{REAR} == \text{MAX} - 1$ | $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{FRONT}$ |

**FULL Condition Formula:** $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} = \text{FRONT}$

*(2 marks for differentiation table, 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

## 5.2 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **The "Single Element Reset" Trap:** Many students forget to reset both FRONT and REAR to $-1$ after dequeuing the last element. This causes subsequent ENQUEUE operations to malfunction. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 2. **The "Off-by-One" Wraparound Error:** Writing $\text{REAR} = \text{REAR} \bmod \text{MAX}$ instead of $\text{REAR} = (\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX}$. This skips a slot. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing EMPTY and FULL:** Forgetting the "leave one slot empty" rule, then declaring both states by $\text{FRONT} == \text{REAR}$. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Missing Initialization:** Not setting $\text{FRONT} = 0$ before the first ENQUEUE leads to garbage values. **Cost: 1 mark.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Write the algorithm (pseudocode/C) for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations in a circular queue implemented using an array of size $\text{MAX}$. Clearly state all boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Boundary Conditions to State (1 mark):**
- $\text{EMPTY} \iff \text{FRONT} == -1$
- $\text{FULL} \iff (\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{FRONT}$

**C Code for ENQUEUE (3 marks):**

```c
void enqueue(int queue[], int *front, int *rear, int max, int item) {
    if ((*rear + 1) % max == *front) {           // Check Full
        printf("Queue Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    if (*front == -1) {                          // First insertion
        *front = 0;
    }
    *rear = (*rear + 1) % max;                   // Circular advance
    queue[*rear] = item;
    printf("Inserted %d\n", item);
}
```

**C Code for DEQUEUE (3 marks):**

```c
int dequeue(int queue[], int *front, int *rear, int max) {
    if (*front == -1) {                          // Check Empty
        printf("Queue Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int item = queue[*front];
    if (*front == *rear) {                       // Last element removal
        *front = *rear = -1;
    } else {
        *front = (*front + 1) % max;             // Circular advance
    }
    return item;
}
```

---

**(b)** Consider a circular queue of size $\text{MAX} = 6$ with initial FRONT $= 2$ and REAR $= 2$. Perform the following operations and show the queue state (with FRONT and REAR values) after each step:  
**ENQUEUE 15, ENQUEUE 25, DEQUEUE, DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE 35, ENQUEUE 45, ENQUEUE 55, ENQUEUE 65, DEQUEUE.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Operation | FRONT | REAR | Queue Contents (logical) | Status |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Initial | $2$ | $2$ | [15] (single) | — |
| 1 | Enqueue 15 | $2$ | $3$ | [15, 25] | OK |
| 2 | Enqueue 25 | $2$ | $4$ | [15, 25, 35] | OK |
| 3 | Dequeue | $3$ | $4$ | [25, 35] | OK |
| 4 | Dequeue | $4$ | $4$ | [35] | OK |
| 5 | Enqueue 35 | $4$ | $5$ | [35, 45] | OK |
| 6 | Enqueue 45 | $4$ | $0$ | [35, 45, 55, 65] (wrapped) | OK |
| 7 | Enqueue 55 | $4$ | $1$ | [35, 45, 55, 65, X] | OK |
| 8 | Enqueue 65 | $4$ | $2$ | [35, 45, 55, 65, X, Y] | OK |
| 9 | Dequeue | $5$ | $2$ | [45, 55, 65, X, Y] | OK |

**Detailed Step Explanations:**

- **Step 1:** $\text{REAR} = (2 + 1) \bmod 6 = 3$; insert 15 at index 3.
- **Step 2:** $\text{REAR} = (3 + 1) \bmod 6 = 4$; insert 25 at index 4.
- **Step 3:** $\text{FRONT} = (2 + 1) \bmod 6 = 3$; remove element at index 2 (value 15).
- **Step 4:** $\text{FRONT} = (3 + 1) \bmod 6 = 4$; remove element at index 3 (value 25).
- **Step 5:** $\text{REAR} = (4 + 1) \bmod 6 = 5$; insert 35 at index 5.
- **Step 6:** $\text{REAR} = (5 + 1) \bmod 6 = 0$; **wrap-around occurs**; insert 45 at index 0.
- **Step 7:** $\text{REAR} = (0 + 1) \bmod 6 = 1$; insert 55 at index 1.
- **Step 8:** $\text{REAR} = (1 + 1) \bmod 6 = 2$; insert 65 at index 2.
- **Step 9:** $\text{FRONT} = (4 + 1) \bmod 6 = 5$; remove element at index 4 (value 35).

*(7 marks distributed as: 1 mark for initial state, 1 mark per row of trace × 6 critical transitions = 6 marks, 1 mark for final verification of FRONT and REAR values.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **"One Slot Wasted"** policy used to distinguish the EMPTY and FULL states in a circular queue. Why is this necessary? Provide an alternative method that does not waste a slot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why the Policy is Necessary (2 marks):**
In a circular queue, the relationship $\text{FRONT} == \text{REAR}$ can occur in two distinct scenarios:
- The queue is **EMPTY** (no elements).
- The queue is **FULL** (all slots used, and REAR has wrapped to FRONT's position).

Without an additional mechanism, the system cannot distinguish between these two states, leading to ambiguous behavior (data loss or false underflow).

**The "One Slot Wasted" Policy (3 marks):**
The standard KTU-recommended approach **leaves one array slot permanently vacant** in the FULL state. As a consequence:
- The maximum number of elements that can be stored is $\text{MAX} - 1$, not $\text{MAX}$.
- The **EMPTY** state is identified by $\text{FRONT} == -1$.
- The **FULL** state is identified by $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{FRONT}$.

For example, with $\text{MAX} = 4$, a full queue occupies indices $\{1, 2, 3\}$, leaving index $0$ (or the slot just behind FRONT) vacant.

**Alternative Method — Count Variable (2 marks):**
Maintain a separate integer variable $\text{count}$ that tracks the current size of the queue.
- Increment $\text{count}$ on every successful ENQUEUE.
- Decrement $\text{count}$ on every successful DEQUEUE.
- **EMPTY:** $\text{count} == 0$
- **FULL:** $\text{count} == \text{MAX}$

This method uses **all $\text{MAX}$ slots** but adds $1$ integer of overhead. Alternatively, a **boolean flag** (e.g., $\text{fullFlag}$) can be set when REAR catches up to FRONT and reset on the next DEQUEUE.

---

**(b)** Write a complete C program to implement a circular queue with operations: **init, enqueue, dequeue, display**, using a menu-driven approach. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 5

int queue[MAX];
int front = -1, rear = -1;

void init() {
    front = -1;
    rear = -1;
    printf("Queue initialized.\n");
}

void enqueue(int item) {
    if ((rear + 1) % MAX == front) {
        printf("Queue Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    if (front == -1)
        front = 0;
    rear = (rear + 1) % MAX;
    queue[rear] = item;
    printf("Inserted %d\n", item);
}

int dequeue() {
    if (front == -1) {
        printf("Queue Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int item = queue[front];
    if (front == rear)
        front = rear = -1;
    else
        front = (front + 1) % MAX;
    return item;
}

void display() {
    if (front == -1) {
        printf("Queue is Empty\n");
        return;
    }
    printf("Queue elements: ");
    int i = front;
    while (1) {
        printf("%d ", queue[i]);
        if (i == rear) break;
        i = (i + 1) % MAX;
    }
    printf("\n");
}

int main() {
    int choice, item;
    init();
    while (1) {
        printf("\n1.Enqueue 2.Dequeue 3.Display 4.Exit\n");
        printf("Enter choice: ");
        scanf("%d", &choice);
        if (choice == 4) break;
        switch (choice) {
            case 1: printf("Enter item: "); scanf("%d", &item); enqueue(item); break;
            case 2: item = dequeue();
                    if (item != -1) printf("Deleted: %d\n", item);
                    break;
            case 3: display(); break;
            default: printf("Invalid choice\n");
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Distribution:** init (1 mark) + enqueue (2 marks) + dequeue (2 marks) + display (1 mark) + main/menu logic (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Circular Queue** is an array-based FIFO structure where REAR wraps to index $0$ after crossing $\text{MAX} - 1$ using modular arithmetic.
- ✅ The **modulo operator** is the mathematical heart of circular queue behavior — appearing in every pointer update.
- ✅ **EMPTY** is identified by $\text{FRONT} == -1$ (sentinel initialization).
- ✅ **FULL** is identified by $(\text{REAR} + 1) \bmod \text{MAX} == \text{FRONT}$ (one slot vacant policy).
- ✅ Always **reset $\text{FRONT}$ and $\text{REAR}$ to $-1$** when dequeuing the last element to avoid logical errors.
- ✅ The element count formula is $n = (\text{REAR} - \text{FRONT} + 1 + \text{MAX}) \bmod \text{MAX}$.
- ✅ Time complexity: ENQUEUE is $O(1)$, DEQUEUE is $O(1)$, DISPLAY is $O(n)$.
- ✅ Space complexity: $O(\text{MAX})$ for the array, $O(1)$ auxiliary.
- ✅ **Applications** include CPU scheduling (Round-Robin), traffic management, buffering in I/O systems, and memory management in embedded devices.
- ✅ **Alternative FULL detection** uses a `count` variable or a boolean flag to utilize all $\text{MAX}$ slots.
- ✅ KTU 2024 examiners expect students to **troubleshoot wrap-around trace questions** — practice this exhaustively.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Priority Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📚 Module 1 : Priority Queue — Section 1: Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Queue (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Priority Queue** is an abstract data type (ADT) that operates as a specialized queue in which each element is associated with a **priority value**, and the element with the **highest priority** (or lowest, depending on the convention) is always removed before elements with lower priority. In the event of a tie, elements are typically dequeued according to their **insertion order (FIFO tie-breaking)**.

Mathematically, a priority queue $Q$ can be defined as a set of ordered pairs:

$$Q = \{(k_1, v_1), (k_2, v_2), \ldots, (k_n, v_n)\}$$

where $k_i$ is the **priority key** and $v_i$ is the **associated value**. The structural invariant requires that for every $i < j$ in the dequeue order, $k_i \geq k_j$ (Max-Heap convention).

### 1.2 Two Standard Variants

| Variant | Extraction Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Max-Priority Queue** | Removes the element with the **largest key** | CPU task scheduling, max-heap sort |
| **Min-Priority Queue** | Removes the element with the **smallest key** | Dijkstra's shortest path, event simulation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine walking into a **hospital emergency room**. Patients do not get treated in the order they arrive (FIFO). Instead:
- A patient having a **heart attack** (Priority = 1, highest) is treated **immediately**, even if 50 patients with mild fevers are already waiting.
- Two patients with the same critical condition are treated in **arrival order**.

This is exactly how a **Priority Queue** works — **urgency (priority) dictates service**, not arrival sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a normal Queue or Stack?**
> - A normal Queue follows **FIFO** strictly (ignores importance).
> - A Stack follows **LIFO** (last-in-first-out), which is irrelevant for scheduling.
> - A Priority Queue gives the programmer **explicit control** over which element leaves next.

## 1.4 Core Properties Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Each inserted element carries a **distinct priority value** (or a tie-breaking rule exists).
> - The **highest-priority element is always available in O(1)** time at the logical "front".
> - The structure is **dynamic**: insertions and deletions occur continuously.
> - It is an **ADT, not a concrete data structure** — it must be implemented using arrays, linked lists, heaps, or BSTs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Module 1 : Priority Queue — Section 2: Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Primitive Operations (ADT Specification)

A Priority Queue ADT must support the following well-defined operations:

| Operation | Description | Return Type |
|---|---|---|
| `Insert(Q, x, k)` | Inserts element `x` with key `k` into queue `Q` | `void` |
| `Maximum(Q)` / `Minimum(Q)` | Returns (does not remove) the highest/lowest priority element | Element |
| `ExtractMax(Q)` / `ExtractMin(Q)` | Removes and returns the highest/lowest priority element | Element |
| `IncreaseKey(Q, x, k')` | Increases the key of element `x` to `k'` | `void` |
| `DecreaseKey(Q, x, k')` | Decreases the key of element `x` to `k'` | `void` |
| `IsEmpty(Q)` | Tests whether the queue is empty | `Boolean` |
| `Size(Q)` | Returns the number of elements | `Integer` |

> [!NOTE]
> The last two are **observers**, not mutators — they do not alter the state of the queue.

## 2.2 Implementation Strategies & Comparative Analysis

The choice of underlying structure **drastically changes time complexity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Maximum Priority Queue):**

| Implementation | Insert | Extract-Max | Peek | Build (n elements) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unordered Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Slow extraction |
| **Ordered Array** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Slow insertion |
| **Unordered Linked List** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Same as unordered array |
| **Ordered Linked List** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Same as ordered array |
| **Binary Max-Heap** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐ **Industry Standard** |
| **Fibonacci Heap** | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(\log n)$ amortized | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | Used in advanced graph algos |

## 2.3 The Binary Heap — Why It Wins

The **Binary Heap** is the canonical implementation taught across the KTU syllabus. It is a **complete binary tree** stored as a contiguous array, satisfying the **heap-order property**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Key Properties of a Binary Heap:**
> 1. **Structural Property (Shape):** The tree is **complete** — every level is fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled **left-to-right**.
> 2. **Heap-Order Property:** For every node $i$ (other than the root), the key of the parent is $\geq$ (Max-Heap) or $\leq$ (Min-Heap) the key of node $i$.
> $$\text{Max-Heap:} \quad A[\text{parent}(i)] \geq A[i]$$
> $$\text{Min-Heap:} \quad A[\text{parent}(i)] \leq A[i]$$

### 2.3.1 Array Indexing Formulae (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

For a node stored at array index $i$ (0-indexed):

$$\text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{Left-Child}(i) = 2i + 1$$

$$\text{Right-Child}(i) = 2i + 2$$

For 1-indexed arrays (often used in textbook derivations):

$$\text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{2} \right\rfloor, \quad \text{Left}(i) = 2i, \quad \text{Right}(i) = 2i + 1$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Time Complexity Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears almost every year in KTU ESE Module 1 papers.**

| Operation | Best Case | Worst Case | Average Case | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insert into Heap | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Extract-Max/Min | $\Omega(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Maximum / Minimum | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Increase / Decrease Key | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Heapify (Build-Heap) | $\Omega(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Heap-Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ in-place |
| Heap-Delete arbitrary | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Priority Queues are deployed in industry:**
> - **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling, CPU task queues (Linux CFS uses a Red-Black Tree variant).
> - **Network Routers:** QoS packet scheduling (high-priority VoIP packets first).
> - **Graph Algorithms:** Dijkstra's Single-Source Shortest Path, Prim's Minimum Spanning Tree.
> - **Event-Driven Simulation:** Discrete-event systems in VLSI design.
> - **Data Compression:** Huffman Encoding tree construction.
> - **AI Search:** A* and Best-First Search use priority queues as the Open List.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Module 1 : Priority Queue — Section 3: Derivations & Algorithmic Walkthrough

## 3.1 Derivation of the Array-Index Mapping

Consider a complete binary tree of height $h$. The root sits at level $0$, and at each level $l$ there are exactly $2^l$ nodes. The total number of nodes up to level $h-1$ is:

$$N_{h-1} = \sum_{l=0}^{h-1} 2^l = 2^h - 1$$

This justifies the choice of **0-indexed array layout**, where the root is at index $0$, the next two children are at indices $1, 2$, and so on, filling the tree **level by level from left to right**.

### Proof of Left-Child Formula

Let node $v$ be at array index $i$. The level of $v$ in the tree is:

$$\text{level}(i) = \left\lfloor \log_2(i + 1) \right\rfloor$$

All nodes in levels $0$ through $\text{level}(i) - 1$ are completely filled. The total count of those nodes is:

$$C(i) = 2^{\text{level}(i)} - 1$$

The first index at level $\text{level}(i)$ is $C(i)$, so node $v$ is the $(i - C(i))$-th node (0-indexed) at its level. Its left child is the $(2(i - C(i)))$-th node at level $\text{level}(i) + 1$, which is at index:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Left}(i) &= C(i) + 1 + 2(i - C(i)) \\
&= 2i - C(i) + 1 \\
&= 2i - \left(2^{\lfloor \log_2(i+1) \rfloor} - 1\right) + 1 \\
&= 2i + 2 - 2^{\lfloor \log_2(i+1) \rfloor}
\end{aligned}$$

For 0-indexed arrays (the common KTU convention), the simplification yields the standard **closed-form**:

$$\boxed{\text{Left}(i) = 2i + 1, \quad \text{Right}(i) = 2i + 2, \quad \text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor}$$

## 3.2 Max-Heapify (Sift-Down) — Formal Recurrence

Let $n$ be the number of nodes in a subtree rooted at node $i$. The worst-case recurrence for `Max-Heapify` is:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{2n}{3}\right) + \Theta(1)$$

The factor $\frac{2n}{3}$ arises because in the worst case, the last level of a complete tree is **half-full**, so the larger of the two subtrees contains at most $\frac{2n}{3}$ nodes.

### Solving by Master Theorem (Case 2)

Compare with $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a = 1$, $b = 3/2$, $f(n) = \Theta(1)$.

Compute the critical exponent:

$$\log_b a = \log_{3/2} 1 = 0$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^0)$ matches exactly:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(\log n)}$$

## 3.3 Build-Max-Heap — The Surprising $O(n)$ Bound

`Build-Max-Heap` calls `Max-Heapify` from index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ down to $1$. Summing the cost over all levels:

$$T(n) = \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log n \rfloor} \left( \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right) \cdot O(h)$$

The number of nodes at height $h$ is at most $\lceil n / 2^{h+1} \rceil$, and each `Max-Heapify` call on such a node costs $O(h)$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log n \rfloor} \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \cdot O(h) \\
&= O\!\left( n \sum_{h=0}^{\log n} \frac{h}{2^{h+1}} \right) \\
&= O\!\left( n \cdot 2 \right) \quad \text{(since } \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}} = 2\text{)} \\
&= O(n)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common student error is claiming `Build-Heap` is $O(n \log n)$. The **tighter bound is $O(n)$** because most nodes sit near the bottom and have small subtrees.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Insertion (Max-Heap)

Consider inserting key **15** into the following max-heap stored as an array:

$$A = [10, 8, 9, 5, 7, 6]$$

**Step 1 — Append at next free position (to preserve completeness):**

$$A = [10, 8, 9, 5, 7, 6, 15]$$

**Step 2 — Call `Sift-Up` (Heapify-Up):**
Compare `15` with its parent at index $\lfloor (6-1)/2 \rfloor = 2$. $A[2] = 9 < 15$. **Swap.**

$$A = [10, 8, 15, 5, 7, 6, 9]$$

**Step 3 — Repeat at index $0$:**
Parent at $\lfloor (2-1)/2 \rfloor = 0$. $A[0] = 10 < 15$. **Swap.**

$$A = [15, 8, 10, 5, 7, 6, 9]$$

**Step 4 — Index $0$ is the root, stop.** Heap property restored.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Extract-Max (Max-Heap)

Using $A = [15, 8, 10, 5, 7, 6, 9]$.

**Step 1 — Save root:** Max = $15$.

**Step 2 — Move last element to root:**

$$A = [9, 8, 10, 5, 7, 6]$$

**Step 3 — Call `Sift-Down`:** Compare `9` with children `8` (idx 1) and `10` (idx 2). Larger child is $10 > 9$, so **swap with index 2.**

$$A = [10, 8, 9, 5, 7, 6]$$

**Step 4 — Sift down at index 2:** Children are `9` at idx 5 and `6` at idx 6. Larger is $9 > 6$. Wait — actual children of idx 2 are at $2(2)+1 = 5$ and $2(2)+2 = 6$. $A[5] = 6, A[6]$ does not exist (out of bounds). No swap.

**Final Heap:** $A = [10, 8, 9, 5, 7, 6]$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Module 1 : Priority Queue — Section 4: Visual Architecture

## 4.1 Logical Architecture — Abstract View

This Mermaid diagram shows the **ADT layer** separating user-facing operations from the underlying implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[Client Application]
    subgraph ADT["Priority Queue ADT Interface"]
        Op1[Insert item with priority]
        Op2[Extract Max or Min]
        Op3[Peek at top element]
        Op4[Increase or Decrease Key]
        Op5[IsEmpty Check]
    end
    subgraph Impl["Implementation Layer"]
        Impl1[Binary Heap Array]
        Impl2[Fibonacci Heap]
        Impl3[Ordered Linked List]
    end
    User --> Op1
    User --> Op2
    User --> Op3
    User --> Op4
    User --> Op5
    Op1 --> Impl1
    Op2 --> Impl1
    Op3 --> Impl1
    Impl1 -.Alternative.-> Impl2
    Impl1 -.Alternative.-> Impl3
```

## 4.2 Heap Insertion — State Transition

This diagram captures the **Sift-Up** behavior after inserting a new element.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Insert key K at index n]) --> Append[Append K at end of array]
    Append --> SetIdx[Set current index i = n - 1]
    SetIdx --> Check{Parent exists AND A i greater than A parent i}
    Check -->|Yes| Swap[Swap A i with A parent i]
    Swap --> Update[Update i = Parent of i]
    Update --> Check
    Check -->|No| Done([Heap Property Restored])
```

## 4.3 Extract-Max — State Transition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Call ExtractMax]) --> Save[Save root value as Max]
    Save --> Move[Move last element to root]
    Move --> DecSize[Decrement heap size]
    DecSize --> Sift[Call MaxHeapify on root]
    Sift --> Check{i greater than children}
    Check -->|Yes| Swap[Swap with largest child]
    Swap --> Recurse[Recurse at child index]
    Recurse --> Check
    Check -->|No| Return([Return saved Max])
```

## 4.4 Heap as a Complete Binary Tree — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    R["15 (root idx 0)"]
    N1["10 idx 1"]
    N2["9 idx 2"]
    N3["8 idx 3"]
    N4["7 idx 4"]
    N5["6 idx 5"]
    R --- N1
    R --- N2
    N1 --- N3
    N1 --- N4
    N2 --- N5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The tree is filled **level by level, left to right**, matching array indices 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. This is exactly why index arithmetic works seamlessly.

## 4.5 Operation Cost Flow — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Priority Queue Operation Required] --> Q1{Need to find maximum element?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Peek Operation - O of 1]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need to remove maximum element?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Extract Max - O log n]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to insert element?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Insert - O log n]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need to change priority?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Increase or Decrease Key - O log n]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 Module 1 : Priority Queue — Section 5: KTU Exam Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Priority Queue. List any two applications where priority queues are used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Priority Queue is an Abstract Data Type (ADT) in which each element is assigned a priority, and the element with the highest priority is removed before others. Unlike a normal FIFO queue, the order of removal is governed by priority rather than insertion time.

**Applications:**
1. **CPU Process Scheduling:** The OS keeps ready-to-run processes in a priority queue. The process with the highest priority (lowest numerical nice value) gets the CPU next.
2. **Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm:** Vertices to be explored next are stored in a min-priority queue keyed on their current shortest distance from the source.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Binary Heap? State the two properties that a binary max-heap must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Binary Heap is a complete binary tree that is commonly used to implement a priority queue, stored efficiently in a contiguous array.

**Two Properties:**
1. **Structural (Shape) Property:** Every level of the tree is completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled from **left to right**.
2. **Heap-Order Property:** For every node $i$ that is not the root, the key of the parent node is **greater than or equal to** the key of node $i$:
$$A[\text{parent}(i)] \geq A[i]$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often confuse **Sift-Up** (used in Insertion) with **Sift-Down** (used in Extraction). Misnaming these costs **at least 2 marks**.
> - When asked to "build a heap", students forget the **bottom-up approach** (starting from $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ going up) and lose 1–2 marks.
> - Always **show array state after every swap** in heap construction. Skipping visual steps costs partial marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the array representation of a binary heap. For a node stored at index $i$, derive the formulas for parent, left-child, and right-child indices. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Insert the keys **20, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, 35** one by one into an initially empty **Max-Heap**. Show the heap state as both a tree and an array after every insertion. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Array Representation (3 marks):**
A binary heap is a complete binary tree, which allows it to be stored compactly in a contiguous array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$. Because there are no gaps in the tree (the shape property), the array has **no unused slots**. The root is stored at $A[0]$, the next level occupies indices $1, 2$, and so on.

**Index Formulas (4 marks):**
For a node at index $i$ in a 0-indexed array:

$$\text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(for } i > 0\text{)}$$

$$\text{Left-Child}(i) = 2i + 1$$

$$\text{Right-Child}(i) = 2i + 2$$

**Derivation Sketch:**
Consider node at index $i$. Its level is $\lfloor \log_2(i+1) \rfloor$. All earlier levels are full, containing $2^{\lfloor \log_2(i+1) \rfloor} - 1$ nodes. Since the array fills level-by-level left-to-right, the left child is exactly **two array slots ahead of where the current node's sibling group begins**. This geometric doubling yields the constant-time formulas above.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Insertion Trace (Sift-Up at every step):**

**Step 1:** Insert **20** → Tree: `[20]`

**Step 2:** Insert **15** → Append, parent = 20 ≥ 15 → No swap.
- Array: `[20, 15]`

**Step 3:** Insert **30** → Append at index 2. Parent = 20 < 30 → **Swap.**
- Array: `[30, 15, 20]`
- 30 is now root ✓

**Step 4:** Insert **5** → Append at index 3. Parent = 15 ≥ 5 → No swap.
- Array: `[30, 15, 20, 5]`

**Step 5:** Insert **10** → Append at index 4. Parent = 15 ≥ 10 → No swap.
- Array: `[30, 15, 20, 5, 10]`

**Step 6:** Insert **25** → Append at index 5. Parent = 20 < 25 → **Swap.**
- New position index 2. Parent at index 0 = 30 ≥ 25 → Stop.
- Array: `[30, 15, 25, 5, 10, 20]`

**Step 7:** Insert **35** → Append at index 6. Parent = 25 < 35 → **Swap.**
- New position index 2. Parent at index 0 = 30 < 35 → **Swap.**
- New position index 0 → Stop.
- Array: `[35, 15, 30, 5, 10, 20, 25]`

**Final Max-Heap Array:** `[35, 15, 30, 5, 10, 20, 25]`

**Final Tree (rendered for clarity):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    R["35"]
    L["15"]
    R2["30"]
    L2["5"]
    L3["10"]
    R3["20"]
    R4["25"]
    R --- L
    R --- R2
    L --- L2
    L --- L3
    R2 --- R3
    R2 --- R4
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sessional Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** What is Heapify? Explain the `Max-Heapify` (Sift-Down) operation with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Given the array `[10, 20, 15, 30, 40, 5, 25]`, apply the `Build-Max-Heap` procedure. Show the heap state as a tree and an array after every iteration. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
Heapify is the process of rearranging a binary tree to satisfy the heap-order property. `Max-Heapify` (also called Sift-Down) is the specific routine that, given a node $i$ whose two subtrees are already valid max-heaps, restores the max-heap property by allowing the larger value among $A[i]$, $A[\text{Left}(i)]$, and $A[\text{Right}(i)]$ to "bubble up" via successive swaps down the tree.

**Procedure Logic (3 marks):**
1. Let `largest = i`.
2. If `Left(i)` exists and $A[\text{Left}(i)] > A[\text{largest}]$, set `largest = Left(i)`.
3. If `Right(i)` exists and $A[\text{Right}(i)] > A[\text{largest}]$, set `largest = Right(i)`.
4. If `largest ≠ i`, swap $A[i]$ with $A[\text{largest}]$, then recursively call `Max-Heapify` on the subtree rooted at `largest`.

**Complexity:** $O(\log n)$ — proven via Master Theorem with recurrence $T(n) = T(2n/3) + \Theta(1)$.

**Diagram (2 marks) — Conceptual Flow:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Node i violates heap property"] --> B["Find largest among A i, A left i, A right i"]
    B --> C{Largest is one of the children?}
    C -->|Yes| D["Swap A i with the largest child"]
    D --> E["Recurse MaxHeapify on child index"]
    E --> C
    C -->|No| F["Heap property restored - Stop"]
```

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Initial Array:** `[10, 20, 15, 30, 40, 5, 25]`

`Build-Max-Heap` calls `Max-Heapify` from index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1 = 2$ down to $0$ (0-indexed).

**Iteration 1: Heapify at index 2 (value 15)**
Children: index 5 (value 5), index 6 (value 25). Largest = 25. Swap $A[2]$ and $A[6]$.
- Array: `[10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 5, 15]`
- 15 has no children → stop.

**Iteration 2: Heapify at index 1 (value 20)**
Children: index 3 (30), index 4 (40). Largest = 40. Swap $A[1]$ and $A[4]$.
- Array: `[10, 40, 25, 30, 20, 5, 15]`
- Recurse at index 4: children = index 9 (out of bounds) → stop.

**Iteration 3: Heapify at index 0 (value 10)**
Children: index 1 (40), index 2 (25). Largest = 40. Swap $A[0]$ and $A[1]$.
- Array: `[40, 10, 25, 30, 20, 5, 15]`
- Recurse at index 1: children index 3 (30), index 4 (20). Largest = 30 > 10. Swap.
- Array: `[40, 30, 25, 10, 20, 5, 15]`
- Recurse at index 3: no valid children → stop.

**Final Max-Heap Array:** `[40, 30, 25, 10, 20, 5, 15]`

**Final Heap Tree:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    R["40 idx 0"]
    L["30 idx 1"]
    R2["25 idx 2"]
    L2["10 idx 3"]
    L3["20 idx 4"]
    R3["5 idx 5"]
    R4["15 idx 6"]
    R --- L
    R --- R2
    L --- L2
    L --- L3
    R2 --- R3
    R2 --- R4
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Things you MUST remember before the exam:**

- **Definition:** Priority Queue = ADT where removal order is by priority, not arrival.
- **Two Variants:** Max-PQ (largest first) and Min-PQ (smallest first).
- **Best Implementation:** Binary Heap — because all key operations are $O(\log n)$ and Peek is $O(1)$.
- **Two Heap Properties:** (1) **Complete tree** (shape), (2) **Parent ≥ children** (order for max-heap).
- **Array Indexing (0-based):** Parent = $\lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, Left = $2i+1$, Right = $2i+2$.
- **Sift-Up** is used for **Insertion**; **Sift-Down** is used for **Extraction**.
- **Build-Heap complexity is $O(n)$**, NOT $O(n \log n)$ — this is a common trap question.
- **Heap-Sort is $O(n \log n)$ in all cases** and uses $O(1)$ extra space.
- **Master Theorem gives $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$** for `Max-Heapify` (Case 2).
- **Common Applications:** OS scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, Huffman coding, Prim's MST.
- **Fibonacci Heap:** Insert is $O(1)$ amortized — best for dense graph algorithms.
- **Heap is NOT sorted** — only the root is guaranteed to be the max/min. The in-order traversal is unsorted.
- **D-ary Heap variant:** For 0-indexed node $i$, child $k$ is at $di + k + 1$ and parent is at $\lfloor (i-1)/d \rfloor$. Used in Prim's algorithm optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic — "SHAPE & ORDER":**
> **S**tructural (complete) **H**eap property **A**rrays **P**arent-formulas **E**xist; **&** (and) **O**rder-property **R**estricts **D**escendants **E**quality. Always state **both** properties to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack as ADT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack as ADT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Stack** is a **linear Abstract Data Type (ADT)** that maintains a collection of homogeneous elements arranged sequentially, governed strictly by the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** discipline. Formally, as an ADT, a stack is defined by its **behavioral specification** — a tuple of (Domain, Operations, Axioms) — independent of any concrete implementation strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** An ADT describes *what* operations are performed, not *how* they are implemented. The Stack ADT specifies the **mathematical model** and **semantic contracts (pre/post-conditions)** without binding to arrays or linked lists.

The Stack ADT can be formally expressed as:

$$ \text{STACK} = \langle D, \Sigma, A \rangle $$

where $D$ = domain (finite collection of elements), $\Sigma$ = set of operations, and $A$ = set of axioms (pre/post conditions).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spring-Loaded Plate Dispenser"

Imagine the plate holder in a **canteen**:
- Plates are stacked vertically inside a cylindrical tube.
- A **spring** pushes the stack upward, so only the **topmost** plate is accessible.
- When you need a plate, you take the one on top.
- When you return a plate, you place it on top of the existing stack.
- You can **never** access the bottom plate without first removing all plates above it.

This real-world dispenser perfectly mirrors the Stack ADT — the **topmost plate = TOS (Top of Stack)**, and access is restricted to one end only.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Mandatory KTU Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Top of Stack** | `TOS` | The position of the most recently inserted (accessible) element |
| **Stack Pointer** | `SP` | Variable/integer tracking the index of TOS |
| **Capacity** | `MAX` | The maximum number of elements the stack can hold (array form) |
| **Underflow** | — | Error condition: trying to `pop()` from an **empty** stack |
| **Overflow** | — | Error condition: trying to `push(x)` into a **full** stack |
| **Peek / Top** | — | Non-destructive read of the TOS element |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In a linked-list implementation, the term **Overflow** is *logically absent* (capacity = memory heap), but **Underflow** still exists and must be checked.

### 1.4 Why "Abstract" Matters in ADT

A data structure becomes *abstract* when its **logical description is decoupled from its physical implementation**. The same Stack ADT can be implemented using:
- A **contiguous memory block (Array)** — Static Stack
- **Dynamically allocated nodes (Linked List)** — Dynamic Stack
- **Two Queues, Recursion, or even another Stack** — Advanced/auxiliary implementations

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love asking: *"Justify why Stack is called an Abstract Data Type."*  
> **Model Answer:** Because it specifies **what** operations (push, pop, peek) are allowed and their behavioral contracts, while hiding the **how** (storage mechanism) from the user. The same ADT accepts multiple implementations without changing the calling logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Model of Stack ADT

A stack over a generic data type $T$ is defined by the quintuple:

$$ \text{STACK}(T) = \langle \text{State}, \text{Init}, \{ \text{push}, \text{pop}, \text{top}, \text{isEmpty}, \text{isFull}, \text{size} \}, \text{Axioms} \rangle $$

### 2.2 Operation-by-Operation Analysis (The "Why" and "How")

**1. `push(x)` — Insert Operation**
- **Why:** To add a new element at the top while preserving LIFO access.
- **How:** Increment `SP` (or move head pointer) and place `x` at that location.
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is **not full** (for bounded stack).
- **Post-condition:** `TOS` points to newly inserted element; `size` increases by 1.

**2. `pop()` — Delete Operation**
- **Why:** To remove the most recently added element (LIFO discipline).
- **How:** Read the element at `TOS`, then decrement `SP` (or free node).
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is **not empty**.
- **Post-condition:** `TOS` moves to the previous element; `size` decreases by 1.

**3. `top()` / `peek()` — Inspection Operation**
- **Why:** To query the TOS without disturbing stack integrity.
- **How:** Return the value at `TOS` position.
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is **not empty**.
- **Post-condition:** Stack remains **unchanged** (this is the *non-destructive* property).

**4. `isEmpty()` — Status Check**
- **Why:** Required before every `pop()` to prevent **underflow**.
- **How:** Returns `true` if `SP = -1` (array) or `head = NULL` (linked list).

**5. `isFull()` — Capacity Check (Array-based only)**
- **Why:** Required before every `push()` to prevent **overflow**.
- **How:** Returns `true` if `SP = MAX - 1`.

**6. `size()` — Cardinality Query**
- **Why:** Allows users to track the dynamic cardinality of the stack.
- **How:** Maintains a counter or computes `SP + 1` (array) or traverses (linked list, typically $O(1)$ with stored counter).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Pre-condition | Post-condition | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Return Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `push(x)` | $\text{isFull}() = \text{false}$ | $\text{TOS} \leftarrow x$, $\vert S \vert = \vert S \vert + 1$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ per call | void / boolean |
| `pop()` | $\text{isEmpty}() = \text{false}$ | $\vert S \vert = \vert S \vert - 1$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | element of type $T$ |
| `top()` | $\text{isEmpty}() = \text{false}$ | Stack unchanged | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | element of type $T$ |
| `isEmpty()` | None | None (read-only) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | boolean |
| `isFull()` | None | None (read-only) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | boolean |
| `size()` | None | None (read-only) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | integer $\geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Property of Stacks:** Regardless of the implementation strategy (array or linked list), the **amortized time complexity of every Stack ADT operation is $\Theta(1)$**. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Stack ADT |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Parsing, syntax analysis, recursive descent |
| **Operating Systems** | Function call stack, interrupt handling, context switching |
| **Browser Engines** | Back-button navigation history |
| **Text Editors** | Undo / Redo mechanism |
| **Memory Management** | Stack-based memory allocation in embedded systems |
| **Graph Algorithms** | Depth-First Search (DFS) traversal |
| **Numerical Methods** | Evaluation of postfix / prefix expressions |

### 2.5 Axiomatic Specification (KTU 14-Mark Depth)

The behavior of the Stack ADT is governed by the following **axioms** (let $S$ denote a stack and $x$ an element):

$$ \text{A1: } \text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x $$

$$ \text{A2: } \text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S $$

$$ \text{A3: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{createStack}()) = \text{true} $$

$$ \text{A4: } \text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false} $$

$$ \text{A5: } \text{size}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{size}(S) + 1 $$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form for the Address Calculation (Array-Based Stack)

For a contiguous (array) implementation, the memory address of the element at the **top of the stack** can be derived from the base address of the array.

**Given:**
- $B$ = Base address of the stack array (address of `S[0]`)
- $\sigma$ = Size (in bytes) of one element of type $T$
- $\text{SP}$ = Current stack pointer index

**To find:** The address $\text{Loc}(\text{TOS})$ of the topmost element.

**Step 1 — Identify the index of the top element.**

By definition, the top element resides at index $\text{SP}$ in the array (using 0-based indexing).

**Step 2 — Apply the standard array address formula.**

For an array $A$ starting at base $B$ with element size $\sigma$, the address of element at index $i$ is:

$$ \text{Loc}(A[i]) = B + i \cdot \sigma $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $i = \text{SP}$.**

$$ \text{Loc}(\text{TOS}) = B + \text{SP} \cdot \sigma $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \sigma = 2 \text{ bytes (e.g., short int)} &\Rightarrow \text{Loc}(\text{TOS}) = B + 2 \cdot \text{SP} \\
\text{If } \sigma = 4 \text{ bytes (e.g., float)} &\Rightarrow \text{Loc}(\text{TOS}) = B + 4 \cdot \text{SP} \\
\text{If } \sigma = 8 \text{ bytes (e.g., double)} &\Rightarrow \text{Loc}(\text{TOS}) = B + 8 \cdot \text{SP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary analysis.**

$$ \text{SP} = -1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Stack is Empty} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; B + (-1)\sigma = B - \sigma $$

$$ \text{SP} = \text{MAX} - 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Stack is Full} $$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Space Complexity of Stack ADT

Let $n$ denote the number of elements currently present in the stack. For an **array-based** stack:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{array}}(n) &= n \cdot \sigma \;+\; \sigma \cdot (\text{MAX} - n) \quad \text{[Unused pre-allocated slots]} \\
&= \text{MAX} \cdot \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **worst-case** space — the entire array is reserved, even if underutilized. Hence:

$$ S_{\text{array}}(n) = \Theta(\text{MAX}) $$

For a **linked-list** implementation (each node has data $d$ and a pointer $p$, with $d = \sigma$ bytes and $p = 8$ bytes on a 64-bit system):

$$ S_{\text{linked}}(n) = n \cdot (\sigma + 8) = \Theta(n) $$

The linked-list variant has **no wasted space** but pays a per-element pointer overhead.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Stack Implementations

| Parameter | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List Based Stack |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Static (pre-allocated) | Dynamic (heap-allocated) |
| **Overflow Possible?** | ✅ Yes (bounded) | ❌ No (until memory exhausts) |
| **Underflow Possible?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Push Complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Pop Complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Memory Per Element** | $\sigma$ bytes | $\sigma + 8$ bytes (or $\sigma + 4$) |
| **Wasted Space** | Up to $\text{MAX} - n$ slots | Negligible (pointer overhead only) |
| **Cache Performance** | Excellent (contiguous) | Poor (scattered nodes) |
| **Resizing** | Not possible (fixed) | Possible (automatic) |
| **KTU Use-Case** | Embedded / fixed-buffer | General-purpose / variable size |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Validating LIFO Order via Trace

Consider the following sequence of operations on an initially empty stack $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \; \text{push}(S, 10) && \Rightarrow S = \langle 10 \rangle,\; \text{TOS} = 10 \\
\text{Step 2: } & \; \text{push}(S, 20) && \Rightarrow S = \langle 10,\; 20 \rangle,\; \text{TOS} = 20 \\
\text{Step 3: } & \; \text{push}(S, 30) && \Rightarrow S = \langle 10,\; 20,\; 30 \rangle,\; \text{TOS} = 30 \\
\text{Step 4: } & \; \text{pop}(S) && \Rightarrow \text{returns } 30,\; S = \langle 10,\; 20 \rangle \\
\text{Step 5: } & \; \text{push}(S, 40) && \Rightarrow S = \langle 10,\; 20,\; 40 \rangle \\
\text{Step 6: } & \; \text{pop}(S) && \Rightarrow \text{returns } 40,\; S = \langle 10,\; 20 \rangle \\
\text{Step 7: } & \; \text{pop}(S) && \Rightarrow \text{returns } 20,\; S = \langle 10 \rangle \\
\text{Step 8: } & \; \text{pop}(S) && \Rightarrow \text{returns } 10,\; S = \langle \;\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

This trace explicitly demonstrates that the **last pushed element (30) is the first popped**, confirming LIFO discipline.

---

### 3.5 The C-Style Reference Implementation (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The following C code is the **KTU-recommended** static implementation of the Stack ADT. It is included because PEAET414 is a CS-adjacent data structures course; for non-CS branches, the pseudocode interpretation in Section 2 is sufficient.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#define MAX 100

typedef struct {
    int data[MAX];
    int top;
} Stack;

void initStack(Stack *s)        { s->top = -1; }
bool isEmpty(Stack *s)          { return s->top == -1; }
bool isFull(Stack *s)           { return s->top == MAX - 1; }

void push(Stack *s, int x) {
    if (isFull(s)) { printf("Overflow\n"); return; }
    s->data[++s->top] = x;
}

int pop(Stack *s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) { printf("Underflow\n"); return -1; }
    return s->data[s->top--];
}

int peek(Stack *s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) return -1;
    return s->data[s->top];
}
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Stack ADT — Top-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the complete **ADT specification layer** and how it interfaces with two valid physical implementations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef adt fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef op fill:#0d9488,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef impl fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef err fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    adtRoot["STACK ADT\nLIFO Discipline\nDomain: Collection of T"]:::adt

    opPush["push x\nInsert at TOS"]:::op
    opPop["pop\nRemove and return TOS"]:::op
    opPeek["peek / top\nRead TOS non-destructively"]:::op
    opEmpty["isEmpty\nBoolean check"]:::op
    opFull["isFull\nBoolean check (bounded)"]:::op
    opSize["size\nReturn cardinality"]:::op

    adtRoot --> opPush
    adtRoot --> opPop
    adtRoot --> opPeek
    adtRoot --> opEmpty
    adtRoot --> opFull
    adtRoot --> opSize

    arrImpl["ARRAY IMPLEMENTATION\nStatic\nBounded by MAX\nContiguous Memory"]:::impl
    llImpl["LINKED LIST IMPLEMENTATION\nDynamic\nHeap Allocated\nNode-by-node"]:::impl

    opPush -.implements.-> arrImpl
    opPush -.implements.-> llImpl
    opPop -.implements.-> arrImpl
    opPop -.implements.-> llImpl
    opPeek -.implements.-> arrImpl
    opPeek -.implements.-> llImpl

    err1["OVERFLOW\npush to full stack"]:::err
    err2["UNDERFLOW\npop from empty stack"]:::err
    opPush -.may trigger.-> err1
    opPop -.may trigger.-> err2
    opPeek -.may trigger.-> err2
```

---

### 4.2 Visual Stack Layout After Successive Push Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef top fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bot fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef empty fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    ptrTop["TOS POINTER\nindex = 2"]:::top
    elemA["S[2] = 40\nTOP ELEMENT"]:::top
    elemB["S[1] = 20"]:::mid
    elemC["S[0] = 10\nBOTTOM ELEMENT"]:::bot
    elemD["S[3] ... S[MAX-1]\nUNUSED SLOTS"]:::empty
    ptrSP["SP = 2\nsize = 3"]:::top

    ptrTop === elemA
    elemA --- elemB
    elemB --- elemC
    elemC --- elemD
    ptrSP -.- elemA
```

---

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram for `pop()` Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> PopAttempt : pop called
    PopAttempt --> UnderflowError : isEmpty true
    UnderflowError --> Empty : error returned
    Empty --> WithElements : push x
    WithElements --> PopSuccess : pop called
    PopSuccess --> WithElements : elements remain
    PopSuccess --> Empty : last element removed
    UnderflowError --> [*] : terminate
```

### 4.4 Application Architecture of Stack in a Compiler Front-End

