<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Stack as an Abstract Data Type. List any four operations supported by the Stack ADT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Stack is a **linear Abstract Data Type** that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle, wherein the element inserted most recently is the first to be removed. It is *abstract* because its definition specifies only the **logical behavior and operation semantics**, not the underlying storage mechanism.

**Four Core Operations (½ mark each):**

1. **`push(x)`** — Inserts element `x` at the Top of Stack (TOS).
2. **`pop()`** — Removes and returns the element at TOS.
3. **`top()` / `peek()`** — Returns the TOS element without removing it.
4. **`isEmpty()`** — Returns `true` if the stack contains no elements.

> *Closing statement (½ mark): "These operations collectively enforce the LIFO discipline and have constant time complexity $O(1)$."*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Stack Overflow and Stack Underflow conditions. When does each occur in an array-based implementation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Stack Overflow | Stack Underflow |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition** | Trying to `push()` into a **full** stack | Trying to `pop()` or `peek()` from an **empty** stack |
| **Trigger Operation** | `push(x)` | `pop()` or `top()` |
| **Array State** | $\text{SP} = \text{MAX} - 1$ | $\text{SP} = -1$ |
| **Prevention** | Check `isFull()` before push | Check `isEmpty()` before pop/peek |
| **KTU Notation** | Bound violation | Empty violation |

> *Closing remark (½ mark): "Overflow is specific to bounded implementations; underflow applies universally to all Stack ADT implementations."*

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing `pop()` returning the **new** top instead of the **removed** element — KTU deducts **2 marks**.  
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *Abstract* with *Implementation* — describing arrays/linked lists as the ADT itself instead of the ADT's *realizations*. Deduct **1 mark**.  
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to mention **pre-conditions** for `push()` and `pop()` — deduct **1 mark per missing condition**.  
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing $O(n)$ for `pop()` because of array shifting — Stack ADT operations are always $O(1)$; shifting is **not** required.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Stack ADT with its complete specification, including all primitive operations, their pre-conditions, post-conditions, and axiomatic definitions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Implement the Stack ADT using a **linked list** in C. Provide a clearly commented program demonstrating `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, and display operations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stack ADT Specification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Formal Definition (2 Marks):**

A Stack $S$ over a data type $T$ is the ADT defined as:

$$ S = \langle T, \text{operations}, \text{axioms} \rangle $$

The defining property is **LIFO** — only the most recently added element is accessible.

**Step 2 — Operations Table (3 Marks):**

| Operation | Signature | Pre-condition | Post-condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| `createStack` | $\rightarrow S$ | None | $S$ is empty, $\text{size}(S) = 0$ |
| `push` | $S \times T \rightarrow S$ | $\text{isFull}(S) = \text{false}$ | $S' = S \cup \{x\}$ with $x$ at TOS |
| `pop` | $S \rightarrow T \times S$ | $\text{isEmpty}(S) = \text{false}$ | $S'$ has TOS removed |
| `top` | $S \rightarrow T$ | $\text{isEmpty}(S) = \text{false}$ | $S$ unchanged |
| `isEmpty` | $S \rightarrow \text{Boolean}$ | None | Returns `true` iff $S = \emptyset$ |
| `isFull` | $S \rightarrow \text{Boolean}$ | None | Returns `true` iff $\vert S \vert = \text{MAX}$ |
| `size` | $S \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ | None | Returns $\vert S \vert$ |

**Step 3 — Axioms (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A1: } & \text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x \\
\text{A2: } & \text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S \\
\text{A3: } & \text{isEmpty}(\text{createStack}()) = \text{true} \\
\text{A4: } & \text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false} \\
\text{A5: } & \text{size}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{size}(S) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Linked List Implementation (7 Marks)**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

typedef struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
} Node;

typedef struct {
    Node *top;
    int size;
} Stack;

void init(Stack *s)            { s->top = NULL; s->size = 0; }
bool isEmpty(Stack *s)         { return s->top == NULL; }

void push(Stack *s, int x) {                   // (1 mark)
    Node *n = (Node *)malloc(sizeof(Node));    // (1 mark)
    n->data = x;
    n->next = s->top;                          // (1 mark)
    s->top = n;
    s->size++;
}

int pop(Stack *s) {                            // (2 marks)
    if (isEmpty(s)) { printf("Underflow\n"); return -1; }
    Node *temp = s->top;
    int val = temp->data;
    s->top = s->top->next;
    free(temp);
    s->size--;
    return val;
}

int peek(Stack *s) {                           // (1 mark)
    if (isEmpty(s)) return -1;
    return s->top->data;
}

void display(Stack *s) {                       // (1 mark)
    Node *cur = s->top;
    printf("Stack (top to bottom): ");
    while (cur) { printf("%d ", cur->data); cur = cur->next; }
    printf("\n");
}
```

**Mark Distribution Breakdown:**
- Correct node structure definition — **1 mark**
- `push` with proper head pointer update — **2 marks**
- `pop` with underflow check and memory free — **2 marks**
- `peek` and `display` — **1 mark each**
- Working main demonstrating all operations — **1 mark**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **array-based** and **linked-list based** implementations of the Stack ADT. Use a comparison matrix and discuss overflow/underflow handling, memory usage, and time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program that uses the Stack ADT to **check whether a given expression containing parentheses `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}` is balanced**. Trace your algorithm for the input `{[()]}`. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline (Full working code):**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

Provide the analytical matrix from Section 3.3 verbatim (3 marks) and write a 4-mark prose discussion covering:
- *Overflow semantics* (array: hard bound; linked list: none).
- *Memory efficiency* (array: $\text{MAX} \cdot \sigma$; linked list: $n(\sigma + 8)$).
- *Cache locality* (array: better due to spatial locality).
- *Resizing flexibility* (linked list: inherently dynamic).

**(b) Balanced Parenthesis Checker (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm (1 mark for logic, rest for code):**

1. Initialize an empty character stack.
2. For each character `c` in the input string:
   - If `c` is an opening bracket `(` `[` `{`, **push** `c` onto the stack.
   - If `c` is a closing bracket, check if stack is empty → **unbalanced**. Else, pop the top and verify it matches the corresponding opening bracket.
3. After traversal, the stack must be empty for the expression to be **balanced**.

```c
bool isMatching(char open, char close) {       // 1 mark
    return (open == '(' && close == ')') ||
           (open == '[' && close == ']') ||
           (open == '{' && close == '}');
}

bool isBalanced(char *expr) {                  // 5 marks
    Stack s; init(&s);
    for (int i = 0; expr[i]; i++) {
        char c = expr[i];
        if (c == '(' || c == '[' || c == '{')
            push(&s, c);
        else if (c == ')' || c == ']' || c == '}') {
            if (isEmpty(&s)) return false;
            if (!isMatching(pop(&s), c)) return false;
        }
    }
    return isEmpty(&s);
}
```

**Trace for `{[()]}` (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read `\{`} &\Rightarrow \text{push, Stack} = \langle \{ \rangle \\
\text{Read `[`} &\Rightarrow \text{push, Stack} = \langle \{,\; [ \rangle \\
\text{Read `(`} &\Rightarrow \text{push, Stack} = \langle \{,\; [,\; ( \rangle \\
\text{Read `)`} &\Rightarrow \text{pop `(`, matches → OK, Stack} = \langle \{,\; [ \rangle \\
\text{Read `]`} &\Rightarrow \text{pop `[`, matches → OK, Stack} = \langle \{ \rangle \\
\text{Read `\}`} &\Rightarrow \text{pop `\{`, matches → OK, Stack} = \langle \;\rangle \\
\text{End} &\Rightarrow \text{Stack empty → BALANCED}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 🚀

- ✅ **Stack = LIFO** discipline. Remember: **L**ast **I**n **F**irst **O**ut.
- ✅ **ADT ≠ Implementation.** Stack ADT is a *specification*; arrays/linked lists are *realizations*.
- ✅ **All primitive operations are $O(1)$** — this is a guaranteed KTU question.
- ✅ **Underflow** is universal; **Overflow** applies only to bounded (array) implementations.
- ✅ The `top` / `peek` operation is **non-destructive** — it does not modify the stack.
- ✅ The defining axioms are: $\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$ and $\text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S$.
- ✅ The **C language** is the KTU standard for code-based answers — avoid Java/Python in board exams unless specifically asked.
- ✅ Common applications to memorize: **function call stack, parenthesis matching, postfix evaluation, DFS, undo/redo, browser history**.
- ✅ Linked-list implementation is preferred when the maximum size is **unknown** or highly **variable**.
- ✅ Array implementation is preferred in **embedded/real-time** systems due to predictable memory and cache friendliness.
- ✅ KTU Examiners award partial marks for **correct pre/post-conditions** even if code has minor syntax issues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting and Searching Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sorting and Searching Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sorting — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting** is the algorithmic process of rearranging the elements of a finite sequence (or list) into a specific monotonic order — either **non-decreasing** (ascending) or **non-increasing** (descending) — based on a predefined total ordering relation defined over the element's key domain.

In formal terms, given an input sequence:
$$ S = \langle a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n \rangle $$

A sorting algorithm produces a permutation:
$$ S' = \langle a_{\pi(1)}, a_{\pi(2)}, \ldots, a_{\pi(n)} \rangle $$

such that for a chosen total order $\leq$ on the keys, we have:
$$ key(a_{\pi(1)}) \leq key(a_{\pi(2)}) \leq \cdots \leq key(a_{\pi(n)}) $$

### 1.2 Searching — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Searching** is the algorithmic operation of locating a specific target element (the *search key*) within a given data structure, and returning either its position (index) or a boolean indicator of presence/absence.

For an array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ and search key $k$, the goal is to find an index $i$ such that:
$$ A[i] = k $$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Sorting Analogy — The Playing Card Hand:**
Imagine you are dealt 13 random playing cards. To arrange them in order from smallest (2) to largest (Ace), you instinctively perform one of several strategies:
- **Bubble Sort**: You repeatedly scan the hand and swap any two adjacent cards that are out of order, until everything looks right.
- **Selection Sort**: You scan the entire hand to find the smallest card and place it first; then find the next smallest for the second position, and so on.
- **Insertion Sort**: You pick up cards one by one and insert each into its correct position within the already-sorted portion of your hand.
- **Merge Sort**: You split the deck into smaller piles, sort each pile independently, and then merge the piles together.
- **Quick Sort**: You pick a "pivot" card and divide all other cards into two piles — those smaller and those larger — then recursively sort each pile.

**Searching Analogy — The Dictionary Lookup:**
- **Linear Search**: You start from page 1 of a dictionary and flip pages one-by-one until you find the word "Algorithm." Slow, but works on any unsorted dictionary.
- **Binary Search**: You open the dictionary roughly at the middle. If your word comes alphabetically before the middle word, you discard the second half; if after, you discard the first half. You repeat this halving until you find the word. **Lightning-fast**, but **only works on a pre-sorted dictionary**.

### 1.4 Key Categorical Metrics in Sorting

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics are used universally by KTU examiners to grade any sorting question:

| Metric | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Time Complexity** | Number of elementary operations as a function of input size $n$ |
| **Space Complexity** | Auxiliary memory consumed beyond the input array |
| **Stability** | A sort is **stable** if equal-key elements retain their original relative order |
| **In-place** | Uses only **O(1)** additional memory beyond the input |
| **Adaptive** | Performance improves when the input is already partially sorted |
| **Comparison-based** | Decides order only via pairwise key comparisons |

### 1.5 Classification of Sorting Algorithms

Sorting algorithms can be broadly classified into two grand families:

1. **Comparison-Based Sorts**: Decide the order of elements solely by comparing pairs using a comparison operator. Lower bound proven at $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons.
2. **Non-Comparison Sorts**: Use properties of the keys themselves (e.g., digit value, range). Can break the $n \log n$ barrier (e.g., Counting Sort: $O(n+k)$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Steps — Sorting

Each sorting algorithm can be broken down into a small set of primitive operations. The KTU examiner rewards students who can articulate these primitives clearly.

**2.1.1 Bubble Sort — Logic Steps**
- Compare each pair of adjacent elements: $(A[i], A[i+1])$.
- If $A[i] > A[i+1]$, swap them.
- After **Pass 1**, the largest element "bubbles up" to index $n-1$.
- After **Pass $k$**, the top-$k$ largest elements are in their final sorted positions.
- Terminate early if a complete pass produces **zero swaps** (already sorted).

**2.1.2 Selection Sort — Logic Steps**
- For position $i$ from $0$ to $n-2$:
  - Scan the unsorted sub-array $A[i \ldots n-1]$ to locate the index $minIdx$ of the minimum element.
  - Swap $A[i]$ and $A[minIdx]$.
- The sorted region grows from the left; unsorted region shrinks from the right.

**2.1.3 Insertion Sort — Logic Steps**
- Treat $A[0]$ as a trivially sorted sub-array of size 1.
- For each $i$ from $1$ to $n-1$:
  - Store $key = A[i]$.
  - Shift all elements $A[j] > key$ (where $j < i$) one position to the right.
  - Insert $key$ into the vacated slot.

**2.1.4 Merge Sort — Logic Steps**
- **Divide**: Split the array into two halves of size $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ and $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
- **Conquer**: Recursively sort each half using Merge Sort.
- **Combine**: Merge the two sorted halves into a single sorted array using a linear-time merge routine with two auxiliary pointers.

**2.1.5 Quick Sort — Logic Steps**
- **Partition**: Choose a **pivot** element (typically last, first, or median-of-three). Rearrange the array so that all elements $\leq$ pivot are on the left, all elements $>$ pivot are on the right.
- **Conquer**: Recursively apply Quick Sort to the left and right partitions.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Logic Steps — Searching

**2.2.1 Linear Search**
- Iterate index $i$ from $0$ to $n-1$.
- If $A[i] = k$, return $i$.
- If the loop terminates, return **NOT FOUND** (typically $-1$).

**2.2.2 Binary Search (Precondition: Sorted Array)**
- Initialize $low = 0$, $high = n - 1$.
- While $low \leq high$:
  - Compute $mid = \lfloor (low + high)/2 \rfloor$.
  - If $A[mid] = k$, return $mid$.
  - Else if $A[mid] < k$, set $low = mid + 1$.
  - Else set $high = mid - 1$.
- Return **NOT FOUND**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for Module 2 KTU questions. Memorize every cell verbatim.

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | Stable? | In-Place? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bubble Sort** | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes | Yes |
| **Selection Sort** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | No | Yes |
| **Insertion Sort** | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes | Yes |
| **Merge Sort** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Yes | No |
| **Quick Sort** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | No | Yes |
| **Heap Sort** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | No | Yes |
| **Linear Search** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | N/A | Yes |
| **Binary Search** | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ iterative | N/A | Yes |

### 2.4 Master Theorem for Recurrence Analysis

For divide-and-conquer recurrences of the form:
$$ T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) $$

| Case | Condition | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| 2 | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$ |
| 3 | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Indexing**: B-Tree indices internally use modified Merge/Quick Sort for maintaining sorted order during bulk loads.
- **E-commerce**: Search engines rank product listings via sorting by relevance score (uses **Tim Sort**, a hybrid of Merge + Insertion Sort).
- **Computer Graphics**: Painter's algorithm sorts polygons by depth before rendering.
- **Compilers**: Symbol tables in lexically scoped languages are often kept sorted for **binary search** during identifier resolution.
- **Bioinformatics**: Sorting by genomic coordinate enables **Binary Search** for gene lookup in reference genomes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Bubble Sort — Time Complexity Derivation

**Step 1: Count the number of passes.**
Bubble sort performs exactly $n-1$ passes in the worst case.

**Step 2: Count comparisons per pass.**
In pass $i$ (0-indexed), the algorithm performs $n-1-i$ comparisons.

**Step 3: Sum the total number of comparisons.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - 1 - i) \\
&= (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \cdots + 1 \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} j \\
&= \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Big-O.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \frac{n^2 - n}{2} = O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Best case analysis.**
If the array is already sorted, the optimized version performs $n-1$ comparisons and **zero swaps**, terminating early.

$$
T_{best}(n) = n - 1 = O(n)
$$

### 3.2 Selection Sort — Time Complexity Derivation

**Step 1: Count comparisons.**
For position $i$, the algorithm scans $n-1-i$ elements to find the minimum.

**Step 2: Sum total comparisons.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - 1 - i) \\
&= (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 1 \\
&= \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \\
&= O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Key observation.**
Selection sort performs the **same number of comparisons** regardless of input order — it is **not adaptive**.

### 3.3 Insertion Sort — Time Complexity Derivation

**Step 1: Best case.**
If the array is already sorted, the inner loop performs **zero shifts** for each element.

$$
T_{best}(n) = n - 1 = O(n)
$$

**Step 2: Worst case (reverse sorted).**
For each $i$, the inner loop shifts $i$ elements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{worst}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average case.**
On average, each new element $A[i]$ must travel about half-way through the sorted prefix.

$$
T_{avg}(n) = \frac{1}{4} n^2 + O(n) = O(n^2)
$$

### 3.4 Merge Sort — Recurrence Derivation

**Step 1: Define the recurrence.**
The algorithm divides the problem into two halves of size $n/2$ and spends $cn$ time to merge them.

$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Master Theorem.**
Here, $a = 2$, $b = 2$, and $f(n) = cn$.

$$
\log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^1)$, we fall into **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.

**Step 3: Solve.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Step 4: Verify via recursion tree.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + cn \\
&= 2[2T(n/4) + c(n/2)] + cn = 4T(n/4) + 2cn \\
&= 4[2T(n/8) + c(n/4)] + 2cn = 8T(n/8) + 3cn \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= 2^k T(n/2^k) + kcn
\end{aligned}
$$

The recursion bottoms out at $n/2^k = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$
T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + cn \log_2 n = n \cdot c_1 + cn \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### 3.5 Quick Sort — Recurrence Derivation

**Step 1: Define the recurrence (general form).**
Suppose the partition splits the array into sizes $q$ and $n-q-1$.

$$
T(n) = T(q) + T(n-q-1) + \Theta(n)
$$

The $\Theta(n)$ term accounts for the partition scan.

**Step 2: Best/Average case — balanced partition.**
When the partition splits roughly in half on average:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)
$$

Applying the Master Theorem (Case 2):

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Step 3: Worst case — degenerate partition.**
When the pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element (e.g., already sorted array with last-element pivot):

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + \Theta(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)
$$

**Step 4: Solve the worst-case recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + cn \\
&= T(n-2) + c(n-1) + cn \\
&= T(n-3) + c(n-2) + c(n-1) + cn \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(1) + c \sum_{i=2}^{n} i \\
&= c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - c \\
&= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Binary Search — Recurrence Derivation

**Step 1: Define the recurrence.**
Each iteration eliminates half the search space.

$$
T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
$$

**Step 2: Apply Master Theorem.**
Here $a = 1$, $b = 2$, and $f(n) = O(1) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 1} \cdot \log^0 n) = \Theta(1)$.

This falls under **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 3: Solve.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(\log n)
$$

**Step 4: Closed-form verification.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + 1 \\
&= T(n/4) + 2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(1) + \log_2 n \\
&= 1 + \log_2 n \\
&= \Theta(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Algorithm | Strategy | Comparisons | Swaps/Shifts | Lower Bound Match |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bubble** | Adjacent swap, pass-based | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ worst | Up to $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Far above $\Omega(n \log n)$ |
| **Selection** | Min extraction, pass-based | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ always | $n-1$ maximum | Far above |
| **Insertion** | Shift-based insertion | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ worst | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ worst | Far above; optimal for nearly-sorted |
| **Merge** | Divide, sort, merge | $n \log n - n + 1$ | $O(n \log n)$ moves | Optimal (asymptotic) |
| **Quick** | Pivot partition, recursive | $O(n \log n)$ avg, $O(n^2)$ worst | $O(n \log n)$ avg | Optimal avg, sub-optimal worst |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting inside the double quotes.

### 4.2 Bubble Sort Pass-Wise Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START PASS 1: COMPARE A0 AND A1] --> B{A0 greater than A1}
    B -- YES --> C[SWAP A0 A1]
    B -- NO --> D[KEEP ORDER]
    C --> E[COMPARE A1 AND A2]
    D --> E
    E --> F{A1 greater than A2}
    F -- YES --> G[SWAP A1 A2]
    F -- NO --> H[KEEP ORDER]
    G --> I[CONTINUE TILL END]
    H --> I
    I --> J[LARGEST ELEMENT BUBBLED TO END]
    J --> K[START NEXT PASS]
    K --> L{ANY SWAP OCCURRED IN THIS PASS}
    L -- YES --> A
    L -- NO --> M[ARRAY IS SORTED TERMINATE]
```

### 4.3 Merge Sort Divide-and-Conquer Recursion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ARRAY OF SIZE 8] --> B0[LEFT HALF SIZE 4]
    A0 --> B1[RIGHT HALF SIZE 4]
    B0 --> C0[LEFT LEFT SIZE 2]
    B0 --> C1[LEFT RIGHT SIZE 2]
    C0 --> D0[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C0 --> D1[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C1 --> D2[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C1 --> D3[SIZE 1 BASE]
    B1 --> C2[RIGHT LEFT SIZE 2]
    B1 --> C3[RIGHT RIGHT SIZE 2]
    C2 --> D4[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C2 --> D5[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C3 --> D6[SIZE 1 BASE]
    C3 --> D7[SIZE 1 BASE]
    D0 --> E0[MERGE TWO SIZE 1]
    D1 --> E0
    D2 --> E1[MERGE TWO SIZE 1]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E2[MERGE TWO SIZE 1]
    D5 --> E2
    D6 --> E3[MERGE TWO SIZE 1]
    D7 --> E3
    E0 --> F0[MERGE TO SIZE 4 LEFT]
    E1 --> F0
    E2 --> F1[MERGE TO SIZE 4 RIGHT]
    E3 --> F1
    F0 --> G0[FINAL MERGE SIZE 8]
    F1 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[SORTED OUTPUT]
```

### 4.4 Quick Sort Partitioning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[SELECT PIVOT AS LAST ELEMENT] --> P1[INITIALIZE I LESS THAN INDEX MINUS ONE]
    P1 --> P2[SCAN J FROM LOW TO HIGH MINUS ONE]
    P2 --> P3{AJ LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO PIVOT}
    P3 -- YES --> P4[INCREMENT I]
    P4 --> P5[SWAP AI WITH AJ]
    P5 --> P6{SCAN COMPLETE}
    P3 -- NO --> P6
    P6 -- NO --> P2
    P6 -- YES --> P7[SWAP AI PLUS ONE WITH APIVOT]
    P7 --> P8[PIVOT NOW AT CORRECT POSITION]
    P8 --> P9[RECURSIVE SORT LEFT SUBARRAY]
    P8 --> P10[RECURSIVE SORT RIGHT SUBARRAY]
    P9 --> P11[FINAL SORTED ARRAY]
    P10 --> P11
```

### 4.5 Binary Search Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[INITIALIZE LOW ZERO HIGH N MINUS ONE] --> S1{LOW LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO HIGH}
    S1 -- NO --> S2[RETURN NOT FOUND MINUS ONE]
    S1 -- YES --> S3[COMPUTE MID FLOOR LOW PLUS HIGH DIVIDED BY TWO]
    S3 --> S4{AMID EQUAL TO KEY}
    S4 -- YES --> S5[RETURN MID SUCCESS]
    S4 -- NO --> S6{AMID LESS THAN KEY}
    S6 -- YES --> S7[SET LOW EQUAL TO MID PLUS ONE]
    S6 -- NO --> S8[SET HIGH EQUAL TO MID MINUS ONE]
    S7 --> S1
    S8 --> S1
```

### 4.6 Comparison-Based Sort Performance Tiers — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TIER1[TIER ONE QUADRATIC O OF N SQUARED] --> A1[BUBBLE SORT]
    TIER1 --> A2[SELECTION SORT]
    TIER1 --> A3[INSERTION SORT]
    TIER2[TIER TWO N LOG N COMPARISON] --> A4[MERGE SORT]
    TIER2 --> A5[QUICK SORT AVERAGE]
    TIER2 --> A6[HEAP SORT]
    TIER3[TIER THREE LINEAR NON COMPARISON] --> A7[COUNTING SORT]
    TIER3 --> A8[RADIX SORT]
    TIER3 --> A9[BUCKET SORT]
    TIER1 -->|INFERIOR| NOTE1[PRACTICAL ONLY FOR SMALL N LESS THAN 50]
    TIER2 -->|PREFERRED| NOTE2[DEFAULT CHOICE FOR GENERAL PURPOSE SORTS]
    TIER3 -->|SPECIALIZED| NOTE3[REQUIRES INTEGER KEYS OR KNOWN RANGE]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Question 1: [3 Marks]**
Differentiate between a stable and an unstable sorting algorithm. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A sorting algorithm is **stable** if two records with equal keys retain their **original relative order** after sorting. If their relative order may change, the algorithm is **unstable**.

- **Stable Example**: Bubble Sort. When $A[i] = A[i+1]$, no swap occurs, so equal elements never cross each other.
- **Unstable Example**: Selection Sort. The minimum-swap strategy can jump equal elements past one another.

---

**Question 2: [3 Marks]**
State the precondition for Binary Search and derive its time complexity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Precondition**: The input array $A$ must be **sorted in ascending (or descending) order** according to the same total ordering used for the search key.

**Derivation**: Let $T(n)$ be the worst-case running time on an array of size $n$. Each iteration reduces the search space by half.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + c \\
&= T(n/4) + 2c \\
&= \cdots \\
&= T(1) + c \log_2 n \\
&= O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, binary search runs in **$O(\log n)$** time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Merge Sort algorithm in detail. Write its recurrence relation and solve it using the Master Theorem. Mention its space complexity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Sort the array $A = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$ using **Merge Sort**. Show all intermediate merge steps clearly.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Algorithm Outline (2 Marks):**
Merge Sort is a classic **divide-and-conquer** algorithm:
1. **Divide**: Split array into two halves.
2. **Conquer**: Recursively sort each half.
3. **Combine**: Merge the two sorted halves using an auxiliary buffer.

**Recurrence Relation (2 Marks):**
For input size $n$:
$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn
$$
where $cn$ represents the linear-time merge step.

**Master Theorem Application (2 Marks):**
Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, so $\log_b a = 1$. The non-recursive cost $f(n) = cn = \Theta(n^1)$, matching **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.

**Result (1 Mark):**
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Space Complexity**: $O(n)$ auxiliary for the merge buffer.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Divide (1 Mark):**
Original: $[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$
Split into: $[38, 27, 43, 3]$ and $[9, 82, 10]$

**Step 2 — Recursive Splitting (1 Mark):**
Left half: $[38, 27]$ and $[43, 3]$
Right half: $[9, 82]$ and $[10]$

**Step 3 — Base Case Splits (1 Mark):**
Each sub-array of size 2 is split into size-1 arrays (trivially sorted).

**Step 4 — Merge Step on Left Half (1 Mark):**
Merge $[38]$ and $[27]$ $\Rightarrow$ $[27, 38]$
Merge $[43]$ and $[3]$ $\Rightarrow$ $[3, 43]$
Merge $[27, 38]$ and $[3, 43]$
  - Compare $27$ vs $3$ $\Rightarrow$ take $3$
  - Compare $27$ vs $43$ $\Rightarrow$ take $27$
  - Compare $38$ vs $43$ $\Rightarrow$ take $38$
  - Remaining: $43$
  - Result: $[3, 27, 38, 43]$

**Step 5 — Merge Step on Right Half (1 Mark):**
Merge $[9]$ and $[82]$ $\Rightarrow$ $[9, 82]$
Merge $[9, 82]$ and $[10]$
  - Compare $9$ vs $10$ $\Rightarrow$ take $9$
  - Compare $82$ vs $10$ $\Rightarrow$ take $10$
  - Remaining: $82$
  - Result: $[9, 10, 82]$

**Step 6 — Final Merge (2 Marks):**
Merge $[3, 27, 38, 43]$ and $[9, 10, 82]$
  - Compare $3$ vs $9$ $\Rightarrow$ take $3$ → $[3]$
  - Compare $27$ vs $9$ $\Rightarrow$ take $9$ → $[3, 9]$
  - Compare $27$ vs $10$ $\Rightarrow$ take $10$ → $[3, 9, 10]$
  - Compare $27$ vs $82$ $\Rightarrow$ take $27$ → $[3, 9, 10, 27]$
  - Compare $38$ vs $82$ $\Rightarrow$ take $38$ → $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38]$
  - Compare $43$ vs $82$ $\Rightarrow$ take $43$ → $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43]$
  - Remaining: $82$ → $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$

**Final Sorted Array**: $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Quick Sort algorithm with partition procedure. Discuss its worst-case time complexity and explain how pivot selection can avoid it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply Quick Sort on array $A = [50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 90, 40, 60]$ using the **last element as pivot**. Show each partition step.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Quick Sort Overview (2 Marks):**
Quick Sort selects a pivot element and partitions the array such that:
- All elements $\leq$ pivot lie to the left.
- All elements $>$ pivot lie to the right.
The pivot is now in its final sorted position. The algorithm recursively sorts both partitions.

**Lomuto Partition Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. Choose pivot $= A[high]$.
2. Set $i = low - 1$.
3. For $j$ from $low$ to $high - 1$:
   - If $A[j] \leq$ pivot, increment $i$ and swap $A[i]$ with $A[j]$.
4. Swap $A[i+1]$ with $A[high]$.
5. Return $i+1$ as the pivot's final index.

**Worst-Case Time Complexity (2 Marks):**
When the pivot consistently produces highly unbalanced partitions (e.g., already sorted array with last-element pivot), the recurrence becomes:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + cn = T(n-1) + cn = \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Avoidance via Better Pivot Selection (1 Mark):**
- **Median-of-Three**: Pick median of $A[low]$, $A[mid]$, $A[high]$.
- **Randomized Pivot**: Choose pivot uniformly at random, breaking adversarial input patterns.
- **3-Way Partition**: Groups equal-to-pivot elements, reducing degenerate cases.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Initial Array**: $[50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 90, 40, 60]$, pivot $= 60$

**Pass 1 — Partition around pivot 60 (3 Marks):**
- $i = -1$
- $j = 0$: $50 \leq 60$ → $i = 0$, swap $A[0],A[0]$ → $[50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 90, 40, 60]$
- $j = 1$: $20 \leq 60$ → $i = 1$, swap $A[1],A[1]$ → same
- $j = 2$: $70 > 60$ → no swap
- $j = 3$: $10 \leq 60$ → $i = 2$, swap $A[2],A[3]$ → $[50, 20, 10, 70, 30, 90, 40, 60]$
- $j = 4$: $30 \leq 60$ → $i = 3$, swap $A[3],A[4]$ → $[50, 20, 10, 30, 70, 90, 40, 60]$
- $j = 5$: $90 > 60$ → no swap
- $j = 6$: $40 \leq 60$ → $i = 4$, swap $A[4],A[6]$ → $[50, 20, 10, 30, 40, 90, 70, 60]$
- Final swap: $A[5], A[7]$ → $[50, 20, 10, 30, 40, 60, 70, 90]$
- Pivot 60 at index 5.

**Pass 2 — Sort left sub-array $[50, 20, 10, 30, 40]$, pivot $= 40$ (2 Marks):**
- After partitioning: $[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]$ + pivot at index 3.

**Pass 3 — Sort right sub-array $[70, 90]$, pivot $= 90$ (1 Mark):**
- Already sorted after partition.

**Final Sorted Array (1 Mark):**
$$[10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90]$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Merge Sort Space Confusion**: Students frequently mark Merge Sort space complexity as $O(1)$ because it "looks like" in-place splitting. **Correction**: Merge Sort requires an auxiliary array of size $n$ for the merging step — strictly $O(n)$ auxiliary memory.
>
> 2. **Quick Sort Stability Claim**: Do **not** claim Quick Sort is stable. Many students lose 1–2 marks here. Quick Sort's partitioning routinely swaps equal elements past each other.
>
> 3. **Binary Search Off-By-One**: The classic bug is writing $mid = (low + high)/2$ without flooring, or using `<` instead of `<=` in the while-condition. KTU examiners specifically watch for these.
>
> 4. **Bubble Sort Best Case**: Forgetting the optimized version's $O(n)$ best case (with the early-exit flag) costs full marks.
>
> 5. **Master Theorem Misapplication**: When $f(n)$ equals exactly $n^{\log_b a}$ with no log factor, students must classify it under **Case 2** with $k = 0$, not Case 1.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sorting** rearranges elements into monotonic order; **Searching** locates a target key within a data structure.
- **Time Complexity Categories**: Quadratic family (Bubble, Selection, Insertion) versus $n \log n$ family (Merge, Quick, Heap).
- **Binary Search is unconditionally faster** than Linear Search **only when the array is pre-sorted**.
- **Stability matters** when sorting records with composite keys (e.g., sort students by name, then by grade).
- **Merge Sort** is the gold standard for **guaranteed $O(n \log n)$** performance and stability, at the cost of $O(n)$ extra space.
- **Quick Sort** is the de-facto choice for **in-memory, general-purpose sorting** due to excellent cache performance and low constant factors, despite worst-case $O(n^2)$.
- **Bubble Sort** is essentially a pedagogical tool — rarely used in production code due to $O(n^2)$ performance.
- **Insertion Sort** is the **optimal choice for small arrays** ($n < 16$) and **nearly-sorted data** (e.g., Tim Sort's "run" detection).
- **Selection Sort** uses the minimum number of swaps ($n-1$), making it suitable when **swaps are expensive** (e.g., large record sizes on disk).
- **Lower Bound Theorem**: Any comparison-based sort requires $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons in the worst case — proven via decision tree argument.
- **Master Theorem** applies cleanly to Merge Sort and Quick Sort's average case but **not directly** to Quick Sort's worst case (which yields a non-divide-and-conquer recurrence).
- **In-place** property: Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Quick, and Heap Sorts use $O(1)$ auxiliary space; **only Merge Sort requires $O(n)$**.
- **Linear Search** cannot be improved asymptotically without preprocessing, but **Binary Search** achieves logarithmic time at the cost of requiring sorted input.
- **Hashing** (covered in later modules) can achieve $O(1)$ expected search time, but it sacrifices the total-ordering information that Binary Search preserves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sorting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Sorting is the process of rearranging a sequence of elements (records) into a specified order, typically **ascending** or **descending**, based on one or more **keys**. It is a fundamental operation in computer science, used as a building block for searching, merging, and data analysis.

The primary efficiency metrics evaluated by the KTU 2024 Scheme are:
- **Time Complexity** — How the running time scales with input size $n$.
- **Space Complexity (Auxiliary)** — Extra memory required besides the input array.
- **Stability** — A sort is *stable* if two equal keys retain their original relative order.
- **In-place vs. Out-of-place** — Whether the algorithm uses $O(1)$ auxiliary space.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogies for Each Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Classroom Line-up"**
> Imagine a teacher (the algorithm) arranging a row of **n** students by height. Different teachers use different strategies. Some find the shortest and place him at the front. Some slide each new student into the correct gap. Some split the row into halves, sort each half, and merge. Some pick a "pivot" student and rearrange everyone shorter on one side and taller on the other. Each strategy yields a sorted line, but the **effort (time)** and **chalkboard space (memory)** differ drastically.

### A. Selection Sort — *The "Find the Shortest" Strategy*
The teacher scans the entire row, finds the shortest remaining student, and swaps him with the student at the current position. Repeats for every position.

- **Mental Model:** *Brute-force greedy minimum selection.*
- **Analogy Extension:** Always does the same amount of work whether the row is nearly sorted or completely random.

### B. Insertion Sort — *The "Card Player's Hand" Strategy*
Like sorting playing cards in hand: pick up the next card and slide it backward into its correct position among the already-sorted cards.

- **Mental Model:** *Adaptive — works lightning-fast if the array is nearly sorted; painfully slow if reverse-sorted.*
- **Real-life use:** Excel's small internal sorts, the final pass in Timsort and Introsort.

### C. Merge Sort — *The "Divide, Sort, Conquer" Strategy*
The teacher splits the row into two halves, sorts each half recursively, then merges the two sorted halves back together.

- **Mental Model:** *Divide-and-conquer; needs a temporary chalkboard (auxiliary array) for merging.*
- **Key trait:** Predictable performance — always $O(n \log n)$ regardless of input order.

### D. Quick Sort — *The "Pivot Partition" Strategy*
The teacher picks one student as the **pivot**, then asks all shorter students to stand on his left and all taller students on his right. Recursively sorts each side.

- **Mental Model:** *In-place divide-and-conquer — the practical workhorse of sorting libraries.*
- **Key trait:** Fastest average-case sort, but a poor pivot choice (already-sorted input) can degrade it to $O(n^2)$.

---

## 1.3 Why KTU Tests These Four Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> These four algorithms form the cornerstone for analyzing the trade-offs between **comparison-based** sorting techniques. They are tested heavily in ESE questions because they cover all major design paradigms:
> - *Brute Force (Selection)*
> - *Incremental/Adaptive (Insertion)*
> - *Divide & Conquer with extra space (Merge)*
> - *Divide & Conquer in-place (Quick)*

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

### A. Selection Sort

**Core Idea:** Repeatedly select the minimum from the unsorted portion and place it at the front.

**Logic Steps:**
1. For each index $i$ from $0$ to $n-2$:
   - Assume $min\_idx = i$.
   - Scan indices $j = i+1$ to $n-1$; update $min\_idx$ if $A[j] < A[min\_idx]$.
   - **Swap** $A[i]$ and $A[min\_idx]$.
2. After pass $i$, the prefix $A[0..i]$ is permanently sorted.

**Pseudocode:**

```
SELECTION-SORT(A, n)
1  for i ← 0 to n - 2
2      min_idx ← i
3      for j ← i + 1 to n - 1
4          if A[j] < A[min_idx]
5              min_idx ← j
6      SWAP(A[i], A[min_idx])
```

---

### B. Insertion Sort

**Core Idea:** Grow a sorted sub-array $A[0..i-1]$ one element at a time.

**Logic Steps:**
1. For each $i$ from $1$ to $n-1$:
   - Set $key = A[i]$.
   - Shift elements $A[j] > key$ one position to the right (for $j = i-1$ down to $0$).
   - Insert $key$ at the vacated position.
2. After pass $i$, the prefix $A[0..i]$ is sorted.

**Pseudocode:**

```
INSERTION-SORT(A, n)
1  for i ← 1 to n - 1
2      key ← A[i]
3      j ← i - 1
4      while j ≥ 0 and A[j] > key
5          A[j + 1] ← A[j]
6          j ← j - 1
7      A[j + 1] ← key
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why > key and not ≥ key?** Using strict `>` guarantees **stability** — equal elements keep their relative order. If `≥` were used, equal elements would be swapped, breaking stability.

---

### C. Merge Sort

**Core Idea:** Divide the array into halves, recursively sort each, then merge the two sorted halves.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Divide:** Find middle index $mid = (low + high) / 2$.
2. **Conquer:** Recursively call MergeSort on $A[low..mid]$ and $A[mid+1..high]$.
3. **Combine (Merge):** Use two pointers to merge the two sorted halves into a single sorted array, then copy back.

**Merge Procedure Pseudocode:**

```
MERGE(A, low, mid, high)
1  n1 ← mid - low + 1
2  n2 ← high - mid
3  Create arrays L[0..n1-1] and R[0..n2-1]
4  Copy A[low..mid] into L; Copy A[mid+1..high] into R
5  i ← 0; j ← 0; k ← low
6  while i < n1 and j < n2
7      if L[i] ≤ R[j]              // ≤ ensures stability
8          A[k] ← L[i]; i ← i + 1
9      else
10         A[k] ← R[j]; j ← j + 1
11     k ← k + 1
12 Copy remaining elements of L (if any) into A
13 Copy remaining elements of R (if any) into A
```

---

### D. Quick Sort

**Core Idea:** Select a pivot, partition the array so that elements ≤ pivot are on the left and elements > pivot are on the right, then recursively sort each partition.

**Partition Scheme 1 — Lomuto Partition** (easier, slightly less efficient):

```
QUICKSORT(A, low, high)
1  if low < high
2      p ← PARTITION-LOMUTO(A, low, high)
3      QUICKSORT(A, low, p - 1)
4      QUICKSORT(A, p + 1, high)

PARTITION-LOMUTO(A, low, high)
1  pivot ← A[high]                 // last element as pivot
2  i ← low - 1                     // boundary of "≤ pivot" region
3  for j ← low to high - 1
4      if A[j] ≤ pivot
5          i ← i + 1
6          SWAP(A[i], A[j])
7  SWAP(A[i + 1], A[high])
8  return i + 1                    // final pivot position
```

**Partition Scheme 2 — Hoare Partition** (faster, fewer swaps, used in KTU questions):

```
PARTITION-HOARE(A, low, high)
1  pivot ← A[low]                  // first element as pivot
2  i ← low - 1
3  j ← high + 1
4  loop
5      do j ← j - 1 while A[j] > pivot
6      do i ← i + 1 while A[i] < pivot
7      if i < j
8          SWAP(A[i], A[j])
9      else
10         return j
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference:** Lomuto returns the **pivot's final index** (used in two subsequent recursive calls). Hoare returns the **partition boundary** (used differently — both sides include that index).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU ESE Module 2 question.**

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space (Aux) | Stable? | In-place? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Selection Sort** | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Insertion Sort** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Merge Sort** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | **Yes** | **No** |
| **Quick Sort** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ stack | **No** | **Yes** |

### Recurrence Relations (High-Yield)

| Algorithm | Recurrence | Solution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Quick Sort (Best/Avg)** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Quick Sort (Worst)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| **Selection/Insertion** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection Sort:** Used in tiny embedded systems where code simplicity outweighs speed, and as a teaching baseline.
- **Insertion Sort:** Hybrid sorts (Timsort in Python/Java, Introsort in C++) switch to Insertion Sort for sub-arrays of size $\leq 16$ or so. Excellent for *nearly sorted* data streams.
- **Merge Sort:** The standard choice for **external sorting** (sorting data on disk or tape where random access is impossible). Also used in `std::stable_sort` and mergesort-based linked-list sorting.
- **Quick Sort:** Default sort in many language standard libraries due to its excellent **cache locality** and **in-place** nature — minimizing memory bandwidth.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Selection Sort — Complexity Derivation

The outer loop runs $n - 1$ times. For each iteration $i$, the inner loop performs $(n - i - 1)$ comparisons.

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2}(n - i - 1) = (n-1) + (n-2) + \dots + 1
$$

$$
T(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}
$$

**Number of Swaps:** Exactly $n - 1$ (one per outer iteration — Selection Sort minimizes swaps).

---

## 3.2 Insertion Sort — Best vs. Worst Case Derivation

**Best Case** (already sorted): The inner `while` loop exits immediately because the condition $A[j] > key$ is false on the first check.

$$
T_{best}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 = n - 1 = \Theta(n)
$$

**Worst Case** (reverse sorted): For each $i$, the key must bubble all the way down, requiring $i$ comparisons and $i$ shifts.

$$
T_{worst}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)
$$

---

## 3.3 Merge Sort — Master Theorem Derivation

**Recurrence:**
$$
T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn, \qquad T(1) = \Theta(1)
$$

**Solution via Recursion Tree:**

At each level $k$ of the recursion tree, there are $2^k$ sub-problems, each of size $\frac{n}{2^k}$. The merge cost per sub-problem is $c \cdot \frac{n}{2^k}$. So total work per level:

$$
W_k = 2^k \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2^k} = cn
$$

The depth of the tree is $\log_2 n$ (until sub-problems reach size 1).

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} W_k = cn \cdot \log_2 n + \Theta(n)
$$

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem Verification for Merge Sort:**
> Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$. Since $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$, and $f(n) = \Theta(n)$, this falls under **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, confirming $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 3.4 Quick Sort — Best, Average, and Worst Case Derivations

### A. Best Case (Balanced Partition)

Pivot splits the array into equal halves of size $n/2 - 1$ and $n/2$:

$$
T_{best}(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{best}(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### B. Worst Case (Degenerate Partition)

Pivot is always the smallest (or largest) element — every partition is of size $0$ and $n - 1$:

$$
T_{worst}(n) = T_{worst}(n - 1) + cn
$$

Expanding the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{worst}(n) &= T_{worst}(n-1) + cn \\
&= T_{worst}(n-2) + c(n-1) + cn \\
&= T_{worst}(1) + c\bigl(2 + 3 + \dots + n\bigr) \\
&= \Theta(1) + c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - c
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{worst}(n) = \Theta(n^2)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** A reverse-sorted input with the **Lomuto scheme using the last element as pivot** produces the worst case. Randomized pivot selection (random index swap before partitioning) makes this probabilistically negligible.

### C. Average Case (Probabilistic Argument)

Assume each element is equally likely to be the pivot. If the pivot is the $k$-th smallest, partition sizes are $(k-1)$ and $(n-k)$.

$$
T_{avg}(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl[T(k-1) + T(n-k)\bigr] + cn
$$

By symmetry, $\sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k)$. Multiplying both sides by $n$ and using the substitution $S(n) = n \cdot T_{avg}(n)$:

$$
S(n) = 2 \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} S(k) + cn^2
$$

Solving (subtracting $S(n-1)$ from $S(n)$ and simplifying):

$$
\frac{S(n)}{n} - \frac{S(n-1)}{n-1} = 2c \quad \Rightarrow \quad S(n) = 2cn^2 \log n + \Theta(n)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{avg}(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Stability Proof — Why Merge Sort is Stable

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Argument:** In `MERGE`, when $L[i] == R[j]$, the algorithm picks $L[i]$ first (line 7 uses $\leq$). Since all elements of $L$ originated **before** all elements of $R$ in the original array, equal elements from $L$ retain priority — hence the original order is preserved.

Selection Sort is **not stable** because swapping the minimum with $A[i]$ can leapfrog an equal element. Quick Sort is **not stable** because Hoare/Lomuto partitioning performs long-distance swaps.

## 4.1 Selection Sort — Pass-by-Pass Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Unsorted Array]) --> pass1[Pass 1: i=0]
    pass1 --> scan1[Scan A1 to An-1 for minimum]
    scan1 --> swap1[Swap min with A0]
    swap1 --> pass2[Pass 2: i=1]
    pass2 --> scan2[Scan A2 to An-1 for minimum]
    scan2 --> swap2[Swap min with A1]
```

## 4.2 Insertion Sort — Iterative Insertion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: Unsorted Array]) --> outer[For i = 1 to n-1]
    outer --> pick[Pick key = Ai]
    pick --> shift[Shift Aj greater than key to Aj+1]
    shift --> cond{Aj greater than key?}
    cond -- Yes --> shift
    cond -- No --> insert[Insert key at position j+1]
    insert --> check{i less than n?}
    check -- Yes --> outer
    check -- No --> doneB([End: Sorted Array])

    style startB fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a
    style doneB fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e
    style pick fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2
    style insert fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2
```

## 4.3 Merge Sort — Divide-and-Conquer Recursion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["A 0 7"]:::root --> node2["A 0 3"]
    node1 --> node3["A 4 7"]
    node2 --> node4["A 0 1"]
    node2 --> node5["A 2 3"]
    node3 --> node6["A 4 5"]
    node3 --> node7["A 6 7"]
    node4 --> leaf1["A 0 0"]:::leaf
    node4 --> leaf2["A 1 1"]:::leaf
    node5 --> leaf3["A 2 2"]:::leaf
    node5 --> leaf4["A 3 3"]:::leaf
    node6 --> leaf5["A 4 4"]:::leaf
    node6 --> leaf6["A 5 5"]:::leaf
    node7 --> leaf7["A 6 6"]:::leaf
    node7 --> leaf8["A 7 7"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e

    node1:::root
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree:** The recursion depth is $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$. At each level, the **total merge work** is $cn$ — that is the source of the $n \log n$ term.

---

## 4.4 Quick Sort — Partition Process Architecture (Lomuto)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ([Start: Sub-array A low to high]) --> pivotSel{Pick pivot}
    pivotSel --> lastP[Pivot = A high]:::lomuto
    pivotSel --> firstP[Pivot = A low]:::hoare
    pivotSel --> randP[Pivot = A random index]:::rand

    lastP --> lom[Initialize i = low - 1]
    lom --> lomLoop[For j = low to high - 1]
    lomLoop --> condL{A j less than or equal to pivot?}
    condL -- Yes --> incI[Increment i, swap A i and A j]
    condL -- No --> skip[Skip]
    incI --> checkL{j less than high - 1?}
    skip --> checkL
    checkL -- Yes --> lomLoop
    checkL -- No --> finalSwap[Swap A i+1 with A high]
    finalSwap --> recLeft[Recurse on A low to p-1]
    recLeft --> recRight[Recurse on A p+1 to high]
    recRight --> endQ([End: Sorted])

    style startQ fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a
    style endQ fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e
    style lastP fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00
    style firstP fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2
    style randP fill:#f3d9fa,stroke:#862e9c
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the conditions under which Insertion Sort outperforms Merge Sort. Justify with reference to time complexities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Insertion Sort outperforms Merge Sort when the input array is **nearly sorted** (very few inversions). In such cases, Insertion Sort's best-case time complexity is $\Theta(n)$, whereas Merge Sort always takes $\Theta(n \log n)$ due to the mandatory merging step. Additionally, Insertion Sort is preferred for very small arrays (size $\leq 15$ or so), as it has lower constant overhead and excellent cache behaviour compared to the auxiliary arrays of Merge Sort. This is why hybrid algorithms like Timsort switch to Insertion Sort for small runs.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between stable and unstable sorting algorithms. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sorting algorithm is **stable** if the relative order of equal-key elements remains the same before and after sorting. It is **unstable** if equal-key elements may be reordered. Examples: **Merge Sort** and **Insertion Sort** are stable (Merge Sort uses `≤` in merge; Insertion Sort only shifts strictly greater elements). **Selection Sort** and **Quick Sort** are unstable — long-distance swaps during partitioning or minimum-selection can shuffle equal elements.

---

## PART B — COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

### ✅ SOLUTION TO CHOICE A:

#### Part (a) — Merge Sort Trace on A = {29, 13, 22, 7, 35, 18, 41, 5}

**Step 1 — Recursive Division (Top-Down):**

```
                [29, 13, 22, 7, 35, 18, 41, 5]
                       /                    \
           [29, 13, 22, 7]              [35, 18, 41, 5]
            /         \                   /          \
       [29, 13]    [22, 7]            [35, 18]    [41, 5]
        /   \       /   \              /   \       /   \
     [29]  [13]  [22]  [7]          [35]  [18]  [41]  [5]
```

**Step 2 — Merging (Bottom-Up):**

| Merge Call | Left Sub-array | Right Sub-array | Result After Merge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Merge([29],[13]) | [29] | [13] | [13, 29] |
| Merge([22],[7]) | [22] | [7] | [7, 22] |
| Merge([13,29],[7,22]) | [13, 29] | [7, 22] | [7, 13, 22, 29] |
| Merge([35],[18]) | [35] | [18] | [18, 35] |
| Merge([41],[5]) | [41] | [5] | [5, 41] |
| Merge([18,35],[5,41]) | [18, 35] | [5, 41] | [5, 18, 35, 41] |
| Final Merge | [7, 13, 22, 29] | [5, 18, 35, 41] | **[5, 7, 13, 18, 22, 29, 35, 41]** |

**Final Sorted Array:** $\{5, 7, 13, 18, 22, 29, 35, 41\}$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Time Complexity Analysis via Master Theorem

The recurrence relation for Merge Sort:

$$
T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn, \qquad T(1) = \Theta(1)
$$

The $cn$ term accounts for the **merge** step, which must touch every element.

**Apply Master Theorem:**

Comparing $T(n) = a\,T(n/b) + f(n)$ with our recurrence, we have $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$.

Compute $n^{\log_b a}$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n
$$

Since $f(n) = cn = \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, this falls under **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}
$$

**Space Complexity:** $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space for the temporary arrays $L$ and $R$ during each merge.

---

### ✅ SOLUTION TO CHOICE B:

#### Part (a) — Quick Sort (Lomuto) Trace on A = {50, 23, 9, 18, 61, 32, 78, 11}

**Call 1: QUICKSORT(A, 0, 7), pivot = A[7] = 11**

| j | Element Compared | Condition (≤ 11?) | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 50 | No | Skip |
| 1 | 23 | No | Skip |
| 2 | 9 | **Yes** | i = 0, Swap(50, 9) |
| 3 | 18 | No | Skip |
| 4 | 61 | No | Skip |
| 5 | 32 | No | Skip |
| 6 | 78 | No | Skip |

Final swap: Swap(A[1], A[7]) → array becomes:
$$\{9, 11, 50, 18, 61, 32, 78, 23\}$$
**Pivot 11 is now at index 1.** Recurse on [0..0] and [2..7].

**Call 2: QUICKSORT(A, 2, 7), pivot = A[7] = 23**

| j | Element | ≤ 23? | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 50 | No | Skip |
| 3 | 18 | Yes | i = 2, Swap(50, 18) |
| 4 | 61 | No | Skip |
| 5 | 32 | No | Skip |
| 6 | 78 | No | Skip |

Final swap: Swap(A[3], A[7]) → array becomes:
$$\{9, 11, 18, 23, 61, 32, 78, 50\}$$
**Pivot 23 at index 3.** Recurse on [2..2] and [4..7].

**Call 3: QUICKSORT(A, 4, 7), pivot = A[7] = 50**

| j | Element | ≤ 50? | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 4 | 61 | No | Skip |
| 5 | 32 | Yes | i = 4, Swap(61, 32) |
| 6 | 78 | No | Skip |

Final swap: Swap(A[5], A[7]) → array becomes:
$$\{9, 11, 18, 23, 32, 50, 78, 61\}$$
**Pivot 50 at index 5.** Recurse on [4..4] and [6..7].

**Call 4: QUICKSORT(A, 6, 7), pivot = A[7] = 61**

| j | Element | ≤ 61? | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 6 | 78 | No | Skip |

Final swap: Swap(A[6], A[7]) → array becomes:
$$\{9, 11, 18, 23, 32, 50, 61, 78\}$$

**Final Sorted Array:** $\{9, 11, 18, 23, 32, 50, 61, 78\}$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Complexity Discussion

**Best Case** — pivot splits array into equal halves:

$$
T_{best}(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Average Case** — random pivot (Master Theorem Case 2):

$$
T_{avg}(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Worst Case** — pivot is always the smallest or largest:

$$
T_{worst}(n) = T(n-1) + cn \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Theta(n^2)
$$

**When does the worst case occur?** When the input is **already sorted** or **reverse-sorted** AND the pivot selection strategy always picks an extreme (e.g., first or last element).

**Mitigation Strategies:**
1. **Randomized Pivot:** Swap `A[low]` with `A[random index]` before partitioning. Expected worst-case probability becomes negligibly small ($O(1/n!)$).
2. **Median-of-Three Pivot:** Use the median of `A[low]`, `A[mid]`, `A[high]` as the pivot — gives excellent balance on partially sorted input.
3. **Introsort Variant:** Switch to Heap Sort if recursion depth exceeds $2 \log_2 n$, guaranteeing $O(n \log n)$ worst-case while preserving Quick Sort's average speed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `≤` in `MERGE`:** Using `<` instead of `≤` in the merge comparison makes Merge Sort **unstable** — you'll lose marks on the stability sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing Hoare vs. Lomuto Returns:** Hoare returns the **partition boundary** (one less than Lomuto's pivot index). Mixing them up gives wrong recursion bounds.
> 3. **Forgetting Auxiliary Space:** Merge Sort's space complexity is $\Theta(n)$, NOT $\Theta(1)$. Students often miswrite it as in-place — guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Missing Stability Justification:** Simply stating "stable/unstable" without the `≤` argument or the long-distance-swap argument scores zero in theory questions.
> 5. **Skipping the Recurrence Solution:** Writing $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ without applying Master Theorem or recursion tree is incomplete — the *evaluation* of the recurrence is what carries the marks.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Your Exam**

- ✅ **Selection Sort** — Always $O(n^2)$, **unstable**, in-place, minimum swaps ($n-1$). Use for tiny arrays only.
- ✅ **Insertion Sort** — Best $O(n)$ on sorted data, worst $O(n^2)$, **stable**, in-place. Used as the base case in hybrid sorts.
- ✅ **Merge Sort** — Always $O(n \log n)$, **stable**, but $O(n)$ auxiliary space. Preferred for linked lists and external sorting.
- ✅ **Quick Sort** — Average $O(n \log n)$, worst $O(n^2)$, **unstable**, in-place. Use randomized pivot or median-of-three to avoid worst case.
- ✅ **Stability Rule of Thumb:** Comparison-based sorts that **never swap across equal elements** are stable.
- ✅ **Master Theorem Application:** For Merge Sort, $a = b = 2$, $f(n) = \Theta(n)$ → **Case 2** → $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Recurrence for Quick Sort Worst Case:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n) \rightarrow \Theta(n^2)$.
- ✅ **Recurrence for Quick Sort Best/Avg Case:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n) \rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Lomuto Pivot Choice:** Last element; simple but degrades on sorted input.
- ✅ **Hoare Pivot Choice:** First element; faster (fewer swaps) but trickier to code correctly.
- ✅ **Trace Verification Rule:** Always verify that the array size shrinks across recursive calls — a forgotten base case `low < high` causes **infinite recursion** (and zero marks).
- ✅ **Merge Step Stability Trick:** Use `≤` when comparing `L[i]` and `R[j]` to preserve the original order of equal elements.
- ✅ **Time vs. Space Trade-off:** Merge Sort trades space for guaranteed time; Quick Sort trades stability for speed and in-place memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching Algorithms - Linear and Binary Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Searching Algorithms - Linear and Binary Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Searching Algorithm (KTU Definition):** A *searching algorithm* is a computational procedure designed to retrieve a specific **target element (key)** from a collection of data items stored in a linear (array, list) or non-linear (tree, graph) data structure. The fundamental objective is to determine whether the target exists in the collection and, if so, return its **location (index/address)**; otherwise, report failure.

According to the **PEAET414 – Data Structures and Algorithms** syllabus under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Module 2 explicitly focuses on two foundational searching techniques that every B.Tech Computer Science student must master:

1. **Linear Search (Sequential Search)** – operates on both *sorted* and *unsorted* arrays.
2. **Binary Search** – operates **only** on *sorted* arrays using the **Divide and Conquer** paradigm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — Searching Paradigm Foundation**
> This module lays the algorithmic groundwork for advanced topics such as Hashing (Module 3), AVL Trees (Module 4), and Graph Traversal (Module 5). The efficiency of searching is quantified using **Asymptotic Notation (Big-O, Big-Ω, Big-Θ)** — a core Course Outcome (CO2) of PEAET414.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

| Real-World Scenario | Algorithm Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Looking for your friend *Rahul* in an **unorganized school assembly** by checking each row one-by-one | **Linear Search** |
| Finding the word *"Pneumonia"* in a **dictionary** by opening the middle page and deciding left/right | **Binary Search** |
| Searching for a house on a **numbered street** starting from house #1 sequentially | **Linear Search** |
| Guessing a number between 1–100 with feedback *"higher"* or *"lower"* | **Binary Search** |

**Plain English Intuition:**
- **Linear Search** = *"I don't know where it is, so I'll check every single item, one after the other."* — Simple, but slow.
- **Binary Search** = *"I know the data is sorted, so I can be smart: I'll jump to the middle and throw away half the candidates with every comparison."* — Fast, but requires the data to be **pre-sorted**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

| Metric | Notation | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| Worst-case Time | $T_{worst}$ | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ | $\mathbf{O(\log_2 n)}$ |
| Best-case Time | $T_{best}$ | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ |
| Average-case Time | $T_{avg}$ | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ | $\mathbf{O(\log_2 n)}$ |
| Space Complexity | $S(n)$ | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ iterative / $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ recursive |
| Pre-condition | — | None | **Array MUST be sorted** |
| Data structure | — | Array / Linked List | **Sorted Array only** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Search — Operational Logic

Linear Search inspects each element of the array **sequentially** from index $0$ to $n-1$ until a match is found or the array is exhausted.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. Start at index $\mathbf{i = 0}$.
2. Compare $A[i]$ with the target key $K$.
3. If $A[i] == K$ → **Success**: return index $i$.
4. If $A[i] \neq K$ → increment $i = i + 1$.
5. If $i \geq n$ (array traversed completely) → **Failure**: return $-1$.

### Pseudocode (Algorithm Box — KTU Standard)

```
ALGORITHM: LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], K)
BEGIN
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF A[i] = K THEN
            RETURN i          // Success — Element found
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1                 // Failure — Element not found
END
```

### Key Characteristics
- **No pre-condition** on input order — works on *unsorted* data.
- **Sequential traversal** — single pass through the array.
- **Early termination** possible when element is found before the end.

## 2.2 Binary Search — Operational Logic

Binary Search is a **Divide and Conquer** algorithm that works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.

### Pre-condition (Mandatory for KTU Board)
$$\boxed{\text{Array } A \text{ must be sorted in ascending (or descending) order.}}$$

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. Initialize $\mathbf{low = 0}$ and $\mathbf{high = n - 1}$.
2. While $\mathbf{low \leq high}$:
   - Compute mid = $\lfloor (low + high) / 2 \rfloor$.
   - If $A[mid] == K$ → **Success**: return $mid$.
   - Else if $K < A[mid]$ → discard right half: $high = mid - 1$.
   - Else ($K > A[mid]$) → discard left half: $low = mid + 1$.
3. Loop terminates → **Failure**: return $-1$.

### Pseudocode (Iterative Version — KTU Standard)

```
ALGORITHM: BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], K)
BEGIN
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← (low + high) / 2
        IF A[mid] = K THEN
            RETURN mid
        ELSE IF K < A[mid] THEN
            high ← mid - 1
        ELSE
            low ← mid + 1
        END IF
    END WHILE
    RETURN -1
END
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it appears in almost every KTU ESE Module 2 paper.**

| # | Quantity | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Best Case $T_{best}$ | $O(1)$ — element at index 0 | $O(1)$ — element at middle |
| 2 | Worst Case $T_{worst}$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| 3 | Average Case $T_{avg}$ | $\dfrac{n+1}{2} \approx O(n)$ | $\dfrac{\log_2(n+1)}{2} \approx O(\log n)$ |
| 4 | Successful Search Comparisons (avg) | $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$ | $\log_2 n - 1$ |
| 5 | Unsuccessful Search Comparisons | $n$ | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ |
| 6 | Recurrence Relation | — | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ |
| 7 | Space Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ iterative, $O(\log n)$ recursive |
| 8 | Applicable Data | Sorted + Unsorted | **Sorted ONLY** |
| 9 | Algorithm Paradigm | Brute Force | **Divide and Conquer** |
| 10 | Stability | Deterministic | Deterministic |

### 2.3.1 Derived Formula — Number of Comparisons in Binary Search

For an array of size $n$, the **maximum number of comparisons** in the worst case equals the **height of the binary decision tree**:

$$\boxed{T_{worst}(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1}$$

This is because every comparison **splits the remaining candidates into two halves**, so after $k$ comparisons at most $\frac{n}{2^k}$ candidates remain. Setting $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$ gives $k = \log_2 n$.

### 2.3.2 Recurrence Relation — Master Theorem Application

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Comparing with Master Theorem form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$:
- $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1 = n^{0}$, so $\log_b a = \log_2 1 = 0$.
- Case 2 of Master Theorem applies → $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear Search** is used in small datasets, unsorted logs, packet inspection in firewalls, and brute-force cryptographic attacks where simplicity > speed.
- **Binary Search** powers **database indexing (B-trees)**, **version control (git bisect)**, **dictionary lookups**, **autocomplete engines**, and **debugging tools**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Working Example — Linear Search

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Board Pattern):** Apply Linear Search to find **K = 45** in the array $A = \{12, 45, 7, 23, 45, 9\}$ of size $n = 6$. Report the index of the **first occurrence**.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step $i$ | Index $i$ | $A[i]$ | Comparison $A[i] \stackrel{?}{=} K$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 12 | $12 \neq 45$ | Continue → $i \leftarrow 1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 45 | $45 = 45$ ✓ | **RETURN 1** (Success) |

**Result:** Element found at index $1$ in **2 comparisons**.

### Mathematical Comparison Count (Linear Search)

$$C(n) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } A[0] = K \\
2, & \text{if } A[1] = K \\
\vdots & \\
n, & \text{if } A[n-1] = K \\
n+1, & \text{if element not present (failure)}
\end{cases}$$

**Average number of comparisons for successful search (uniformly distributed):**

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{n+1}{2}$$

Derivation of arithmetic sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} i &= 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n \\
&= \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \quad \text{(standard arithmetic series formula)} \\
T_{avg} &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence average-case complexity is $\Theta(n)$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Working Example — Binary Search

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Board Pattern):** Apply Binary Search to find **K = 47** in the sorted array $A = \{11, 22, 33, 44, 47, 55, 66, 77, 88\}$ of size $n = 9$.

### Step-by-Step Trace (Iterative Version)

| Step | low | high | $mid = \lfloor (low+high)/2 \rfloor$ | $A[mid]$ | Compare with K=47 | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 8 | $\lfloor 8/2 \rfloor = 4$ | 47 | $47 = 47$ ✓ | **RETURN 4** |

**Result:** Found at index **4** in **1 comparison** (best case).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Second Example (Worst Case Trace):** Find **K = 88** in the same array.

| Step | low | high | mid | $A[mid]$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 8 | 4 | 47 | $88 > 47$ → $low = 5$ |
| 2 | 5 | 8 | $\lfloor 13/2 \rfloor = 6$ | 66 | $88 > 66$ → $low = 7$ |
| 3 | 7 | 8 | $\lfloor 15/2 \rfloor = 7$ | 77 | $88 > 77$ → $low = 8$ |
| 4 | 8 | 8 | $\lfloor 16/2 \rfloor = 8$ | 88 | $88 = 88$ ✓ | **RETURN 8** |

**Total comparisons:** $4 = \lfloor \log_2 9 \rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Worst-Case Complexity Derivation — Binary Search

We derive the maximum number of comparisons $T(n)$ for a Binary Search on an array of size $n$.

### Recurrence Setup

At each step, the algorithm makes **one comparison** and reduces the search space to **half** (since the problem is split into two equal sub-problems, only one of which is recursively explored):

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

### Solving the Recurrence (Substitution Method)

We hypothesize that $T(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.

**Base case:** $n = 1$:
$$T(1) = \lfloor \log_2 1 \rfloor + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \;\checkmark$$

**Inductive step:** Assume $T(n/2) = \lfloor \log_2(n/2) \rfloor + 1$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + 1 \\
&= \lfloor \log_2(n/2) \rfloor + 1 + 1 \\
&= \lfloor \log_2 n - 1 \rfloor + 2 \\
&= \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor - 1 + 2 \quad (\text{since } \log_2 n - 1 \text{ is not necessarily integer}) \\
&= \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the **worst-case time complexity** of Binary Search is:

$$\boxed{T_{worst}(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1 = O(\log_2 n)}$$

---

## 3.4 Comparison Decision Tree (Binary Search Visual Logic)

For $n = 8$, the binary search decision tree is a **complete binary tree** of height $3$:

```
                    [ mid=3 ]
                   /        \
              < K          ≥ K
              /                \
         [ mid=1 ]          [ mid=5 ]
         /      \            /      \
       <K       ≥K         <K       ≥K
       /          \         /          \
   [mid=0]   [mid=2]  [mid=4]    [mid=6]
                                          \
                                         [mid=7] (leaf — failure)
```

- Each **internal node** = one comparison.
- Each **leaf** = one possible position (success) or one failure.
- **Tree height** = $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ = worst-case comparisons.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Unsorted small array ($n < 50$) | Linear Search | Sorting overhead > search gain |
| Sorted large array ($n \geq 1000$) | Binary Search | $O(\log n) \ll O(n)$ |
| Single search in unsorted data | Linear Search | Sorting cost not amortized |
| Frequent searches on static data | Sort once + Binary Search | $O(n \log n)$ sort, then $O(\log n)$ per query |
| Linked List (no random access) | Linear Search | Binary Search needs $O(1)$ indexing |
| Real-time / streaming data | Linear Search | Cannot pre-sort |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Search — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initI["i = 0"]
    initI --> condLoop{"i < n AND not found?"}
    condLoop -- "No (i ≥ n)" --> failure["RETURN -1 (NOT FOUND)"]
    failure --> stopFail([END])
    condLoop -- "Yes" --> compareStep["Compare A i with K"]
    compareStep --> equalCheck{"A i == K?"}
    equalCheck -- "Yes" --> successOut["RETURN i (FOUND at index i)"]
    successOut --> stopSucc([END])
    equalCheck -- "No" --> increment["i = i + 1"]
    increment --> condLoop
```

## 4.2 Binary Search — Mermaid Flowchart (Iterative)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START]) --> initVars["low = 0, high = n-1"]
    initVars --> mainLoop{"low <= high?"}
    mainLoop -- "No" --> notFound["RETURN -1 (NOT FOUND)"]
    notFound --> endFail([END])
    mainLoop -- "Yes" --> calcMid["mid = floor low + high divided by 2"]
    calcMid --> midCheck{"A mid equals K?"}
    midCheck -- "Yes" --> foundOut["RETURN mid (FOUND)"]
    foundOut --> endSucc([END])
    midCheck -- "No, K less than A mid" --> goLeft["high = mid - 1"]
    midCheck -- "No, K greater than A mid" --> goRight["low = mid + 1"]
    goLeft --> mainLoop
    goRight --> mainLoop
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Searching Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        inputData["Dataset A n elements"]
        targetKey["Search Key K"]
    end

    subgraph PreCheckLayer["Pre-condition Checker"]
        sortCheck{"Is A sorted?"}
    end

    subgraph AlgoSelector["Algorithm Selector"]
        linearBlock["Linear Search Engine"]
        binaryBlock["Binary Search Engine"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        indexOut["Return Index i"]
        failOut["Return -1 Failure"]
    end

    inputData --> sortCheck
    targetKey --> sortCheck
    sortCheck -- "No" --> linearBlock
    sortCheck -- "Yes + n small" --> linearBlock
    sortCheck -- "Yes + n large" --> binaryBlock
    linearBlock --> indexOut
    linearBlock --> failOut
    binaryBlock --> indexOut
    binaryBlock --> failOut
```

## 4.4 Binary Search Decision Tree — Visualizing Logarithmic Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["n = 16 candidates, mid = 7"]
    root --> left1["n = 7, mid = 3"]
    root --> right1["n = 8, mid = 11"]
    left1 --> left2a["n = 3, mid = 1"]
    left1 --> right2a["n = 3, mid = 5"]
    right1 --> left2b["n = 3, mid = 9"]
    right1 --> right2b["n = 4, mid = 13"]
    left2a --> leafA1["n = 1, mid = 0"]
    left2a --> leafA2["n = 1, mid = 2"]
    right2a --> leafA3["n = 1, mid = 4"]
    right2a --> leafA4["n = 1, mid = 6"]
    left2b --> leafB1["n = 1, mid = 8"]
    left2b --> leafB2["n = 1, mid = 10"]
    right2b --> leafB3["n = 1, mid = 12"]
    right2b --> leafB4["n = 2, mid = 14"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For $n = 16$, the maximum depth of the tree is $4 = \log_2 16$. Each level halves the candidate pool — this is the geometric essence of $O(\log n)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define searching. Differentiate between linear search and binary search with respect to time complexity and pre-conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Searching** is the process of finding the location of a specific element (key) within a given collection of data.

| Aspect | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-condition | Works on sorted and unsorted data | Requires sorted data |
| Time Complexity | $O(n)$ worst case | $O(\log n)$ worst case |
| Approach | Sequential, brute-force | Divide and Conquer |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Correct differentiation table with both criteria (2M).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the recurrence relation for binary search and solve it using the Master Theorem to derive the worst-case complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Recurrence Relation:**
$$T(n) = T(n/2) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

**Master Theorem Application:**
Here $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1 = n^0$, hence $\log_b a = \log_2 1 = 0$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, **Case 2** of Master Theorem applies:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n) = \Theta(\log n)$$

**Mark Distribution:** Recurrence (1M) + Master Theorem substitution (1M) + Final answer $\Theta(\log n)$ (1M).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Search algorithm** with a suitable pseudocode. What are its best, average, and worst-case complexities? Apply linear search to find element **K = 38** in the array $A = \{15, 38, 7, 22, 38, 41, 9\}$. Show each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Binary Search algorithm** with iterative pseudocode. Apply it to find **K = 41** in the sorted array $A = \{11, 22, 33, 41, 55, 66, 77, 88\}$. Show complete trace table. Derive the worst-case time complexity using recurrence relation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], K)
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF A[i] = K THEN
            RETURN i
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1
```

**Complexity Analysis (1 mark):**
- Best case: $O(1)$ — element at index 0
- Average case: $O(n)$ — element in middle on average
- Worst case: $O(n)$ — element at end or not present

**Trace Table for K = 38 (4 marks):**

| Step $i$ | Index $i$ | $A[i]$ | $A[i] = 38?$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 15 | No | Continue |
| 2 | 1 | 38 | **Yes** ✓ | **RETURN 1** |
