**Answer:** Found at index **1** in **2 comparisons** (first occurrence).

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Algorithm (1.5 marks):**
```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], K)
    low ← 0; high ← n-1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← (low + high) / 2
        IF A[mid] = K THEN RETURN mid
        ELSE IF K < A[mid] THEN high ← mid - 1
        ELSE low ← mid + 1
    RETURN -1
```

**Trace Table for K = 41 (3 marks):**

| Step | low | high | mid | $A[mid]$ | Compare with 41 | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 7 | 3 | 41 | Equal ✓ | **RETURN 3** |

**Answer:** Found at index **3** in **1 comparison** (best case).

**Worst-case complexity derivation (2.5 marks):**

Recurrence: $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$.

By repeated substitution:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + 1 \\
&= T(n/4) + 1 + 1 \\
&= T(n/8) + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n/2^k) + k
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $n/2^k = 1$ gives $k = \log_2 n$. Hence:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(\log_2 n)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Linear Search and Binary Search** in detail covering: pre-condition, algorithm logic, time complexity (best, average, worst), space complexity, and a scenario-based recommendation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **recursive version** of Binary Search with pseudocode. Apply it on the array $A = \{2, 5, 8, 12, 16, 23, 38, 56, 72, 91\}$ to find **K = 23**. Show the recursion tree. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-condition | None | Sorted array mandatory |
| Algorithm paradigm | Brute force | Divide and conquer |
| Best case | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Average case | $(n+1)/2$ comparisons | $\log_2 n$ comparisons |
| Worst case | $O(n)$ — $n$ comparisons | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| Space | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ iter / $O(\log n)$ rec |
| Data structure | Array / Linked List | Sorted Array only |
| Easy to implement | ✓✓ Very easy | ✓ Moderate |
| Performance on $n=10^6$ | $\sim 10^6$ ops | $\sim 20$ ops |

**Scenario Recommendation (2 marks):**
- Use **Linear Search** for unsorted data, small datasets, or linked lists.
- Use **Binary Search** for large sorted datasets with frequent lookups (e.g., phone directory, dictionary).

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**Recursive Pseudocode (2 marks):**
```
ALGORITHM RecBinarySearch(A, low, high, K)
BEGIN
    IF low > high THEN
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    mid ← (low + high) / 2
    IF A[mid] = K THEN
        RETURN mid
    ELSE IF K < A[mid] THEN
        RETURN RecBinarySearch(A, low, mid-1, K)
    ELSE
        RETURN RecBinarySearch(A, mid+1, high, K)
    END IF
END
```

**Trace for K = 23 (Array: $\{2, 5, 8, 12, 16, 23, 38, 56, 72, 91\}$, n = 10) (3 marks):**

- Call 1: $low=0, high=9, mid=4, A[4]=16$. Since $23 > 16$, go right.
- Call 2: $low=5, high=9, mid=7, A[7]=56$. Since $23 < 56$, go left.
- Call 3: $low=5, high=6, mid=5, A[5]=23$. **Match → RETURN 5**.

**Recursion Tree (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    call1["Call 1: low=0, high=9, mid=4, A mid =16. K greater"]
    call2["Call 2: low=5, high=9, mid=7, A mid =56. K smaller"]
    call3["Call 3: low=5, high=6, mid=5, A mid =23. MATCH"]
    call1 --> call2
    call2 --> call3
    call3 --> ret["RETURN 5"]
```

**Answer:** Element found at index **5** in **3 recursive calls**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sorted pre-condition** for Binary Search — examiners deduct 1–2 marks instantly. Always write: *"Binary Search requires the input array to be sorted."*
> 2. **Incorrect mid calculation** — use $mid = low + (high - low)/2$ to avoid integer overflow in languages like C/Java (especially for large arrays). Examiners often check this.
> 3. **Confusing best-case with worst-case** — Linear Search best case is $O(1)$, NOT $O(n)$. State both clearly.
> 4. **Loop boundary error** — using `$<$` instead of `$\leq$` in `while (low <= high)` causes infinite loops or missed elements.
> 5. **Not returning $-1$** for failure — partial marking requires a complete algorithm.
> 6. **Writing average case as $\log n$ instead of $(n+1)/2$** for linear search — read the question carefully!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam:**

- **Linear Search** = sequential, no pre-condition, $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space.
- **Binary Search** = divide & conquer, **sorted array mandatory**, $O(\log n)$ time.
- **Recurrence for Binary Search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ → solves to $O(\log n)$ via Master Theorem Case 2.
- **Worst-case comparisons in Binary Search:** $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.
- **Average comparisons in Linear Search:** $(n+1)/2$.
- **Mid calculation (overflow-safe):** $mid = low + (high - low) / 2$.
- **Loop invariant for Binary Search:** At every iteration, if $K$ exists, it lies in $A[low..high]$.
- **Binary Search fails (returns $-1$)** when $low > high$ (empty sub-array).
- **Decision tree height = number of worst-case comparisons** = $\log_2 n$.
- **Linear Search is preferred for:** unsorted data, linked lists, small $n$, single-shot queries.
- **Binary Search is preferred for:** sorted arrays, large $n$, multiple lookups, indexed databases.
- **Time complexity hierarchy to remember:** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2)$.
- **KTU Module 2 also covers sentinel Linear Search** as an optimization — it reduces one comparison per iteration by placing $K$ at $A[n]$ as a sentinel.
- **Recursive Binary Search space** = $O(\log n)$ due to call stack depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hashing - Basic Concepts and Hash Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hashing - Basic Concepts and Hash Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hashing (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Hashing is a searching technique that uses a **hash function** to map a large set of keys into a smaller, finite set of indices in a fixed-size data structure called a **Hash Table**, enabling approximately **constant-time O(1)** average-case search, insertion, and deletion operations — independent of the number of elements stored.

Formally, a hash function $h$ transforms a key $k$ (drawn from a universe $U$) into an integer index within the range $[0, m-1]$ of the hash table $T$ of size $m$:

$$h : U \rightarrow \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, m-1\}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Library Book Analogy (Intuitive Explanation):**
> Imagine a massive library with **10 million books** and no catalogue system. Finding one book would take hours. Now, imagine if every book were assigned a unique number based on its **title and category**, and placed in a numbered locker directly. When you want a book, you calculate the locker number from the title and go **straight to it** — no searching required. This is exactly what hashing does: it converts a "search" problem into a "direct addressing" problem using a mathematical function.

**Key Terminology for First-Time Learners:**

| Term | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Key** | The identifier of the data item (e.g., roll number, employee ID) |
| **Hash Function** | The mathematical rule that converts a key to an index |
| **Hash Table** | The array where data is stored at the computed index |
| **Hash Value / Hash Code** | The output index produced by the hash function |
| **Bucket / Slot** | A single storage location in the hash table |
| **Synonyms** | Two different keys that hash to the same index |
| **Collision** | The event when two keys produce the same hash value |
| **Load Factor ($\alpha$)** | Ratio of filled slots to total slots in the table |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The load factor $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$ (where $n$ is the number of stored keys and $m$ is the table size) is a **critical metric** that determines the performance of any hashing scheme. Always state its value when solving hash table problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Hashing (Step-by-Step)

The hashing process follows a strict sequence of operations:

- **Step 1 — Key Identification:** Extract the searchable field (key) from the data record. For example, in a student database, the roll number `KTE2024CS101` serves as the key.

- **Step 2 — Hash Computation:** Apply the hash function $h(k)$ to convert the key into an integer in the range $[0, m-1]$. This is the **"Why"** — it transforms arbitrary-sized keys into bounded, indexable integers.

- **Step 3 — Table Access:** Use the computed index to directly access the corresponding slot in the hash table $T$. This is the **"How"** of constant-time access — no traversal, no comparison loop.

- **Step 4 — Collision Check:** If two different keys $k_1$ and $k_2$ yield the same index ($h(k_1) = h(k_2)$), a **collision** has occurred. Collision resolution strategies (open addressing, chaining) are studied in the next module section.

- **Step 5 — Verification (for search):** Confirm the stored key matches the search key (handle the synonym situation carefully).

## 2.2 Desirable Properties of a Good Hash Function

A KTU-board-quality hash function **must** satisfy the following four properties:

- **Determinism:** For a given input key $k$, the function must always produce the same hash value $h(k)$. A non-deterministic function defeats the entire purpose.

- **Uniform Distribution:** The function should distribute keys uniformly across all $m$ slots. This minimizes collisions and keeps the load balanced. The **ideal** uniform distribution satisfies:
$$P(h(k) = i) = \frac{1}{m} \quad \text{for all } i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$$

- **Computational Efficiency:** $h(k)$ must be computable in **constant time $O(1)$** or near-constant time. A slow hash function negates the $O(1)$ advantage of hashing.

- **Avalanche Effect:** A small change in the input key (e.g., changing one bit) should produce a drastically different hash value. This prevents clustering of similar keys.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part A and Part B question on hashing.**

| Method | Formula | Critical Parameter | Range of Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Division Method** | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | $m$ (table size) | $[0,\ m-1]$ |
| **Multiplication Method** | $h(k) = \lfloor m \cdot (kA \bmod 1) \rfloor$ | $A \in (0,1)$, typically $A \approx 0.6180339$ | $[0,\ m-1]$ |
| **Mid-Square Method** | $h(k) = \text{middle } r \text{ digits of } k^2$ | $r$ (number of digits extracted) | $[0,\ 10^r - 1]$ |
| **Folding (Shift)** | $h(k) = \sum_{i} \text{part}_i$ | Partition size (e.g., 2 digits) | Depends on parts |
| **Folding (Boundary)** | $h(k) = \sum_{i} \text{reverse}(\text{part}_i)$ | Partition size | Depends on parts |
| **Digit Analysis** | $h(k) = \text{selecting certain digits of } k$ | Selected digit positions | $[0,\ m-1]$ |
| **Load Factor** | $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$ | $n$ = keys stored, $m$ = table size | $[0,\ 1]$ typically |
| **Average Chain Length (Chaining)** | $L = \frac{n}{m} = \alpha$ | Independent of hash function quality | Same as $\alpha$ |
| **Successful Search Cost** | $\frac{1}{\alpha} \ln(1+\alpha)$ (chaining) | Requires $\alpha$ value | Real number |
| **Unsuccessful Search Cost** | $1 + \frac{\alpha}{2}$ (linear probing) | Requires $\alpha$ value | Real number |

**Note on Notation:** For any expression $x \bmod m$, the result is in the range $[0, m-1]$, never negative when $x \geq 0$.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Hashing is Used in Industry:**
> - **Database Indexing:** Oracle, PostgreSQL, and MySQL use hash indexes for equality lookups.
> - **Cryptography:** SHA-256, MD5 (legacy), and bcrypt are specialized hash functions for security.
> - **Compilers:** Symbol tables in compilers are implemented as hash tables for variable lookup.
> - **Caching:** Memcached and Redis use hash tables as their core data structure.
> - **Network Routers:** Hash-based routing tables accelerate packet forwarding.
> - **Git Version Control:** Git uses SHA-1 hashing to uniquely identify every commit and file.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Division Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Insert the keys $K = \{45, 23, 89, 12, 67, 34, 56, 78\}$ into a hash table of size $m = 11$ using the division method. Compute the load factor after all insertions.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Division Method)

**Step 1 — Verify Table Size:**
Table size $m = 11$ is a **prime number** (only divisible by 1 and 11). Prime $m$ is preferred because it reduces clustering when keys share common factors with $m$.

**Step 2 — Apply Hash Function $h(k) = k \bmod 11$ to each key:**

For key 45:
$$h(45) = 45 \bmod 11$$
We perform integer division: $45 = 4 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(45) = 1$$

For key 23:
$$h(23) = 23 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $23 = 2 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(23) = 1$$

For key 89:
$$h(89) = 89 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $89 = 8 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(89) = 1$$

For key 12:
$$h(12) = 12 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $12 = 1 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(12) = 1$$

For key 67:
$$h(67) = 67 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $67 = 6 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(67) = 1$$

For key 34:
$$h(34) = 34 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $34 = 3 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(34) = 1$$

For key 56:
$$h(56) = 56 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $56 = 5 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(56) = 1$$

For key 78:
$$h(78) = 78 \bmod 11$$
We compute: $78 = 7 \times 11 + 1$, so the remainder is $1$.
$$h(78) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Identify Collisions:**
All 8 keys hash to index **1**, which is a catastrophic collision. This happens because every key is congruent to $1 \bmod 11$. This demonstrates why hash function **selection is critical** — division by a poorly-chosen $m$ leads to degenerate behavior.

**Step 4 — Compute Load Factor:**
Number of keys inserted $n = 8$. Table size $m = 11$.
$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Mid-Square Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Apply the mid-square method with $r = 2$ digits to the key $k = 4529$. Table size $m = 100$ (since $r=2$ gives 2-digit output).

### Step-by-Step Solution (Mid-Square Method)

**Step 1 — Square the key:**
$$k^2 = 4529^2$$
Computing explicitly:
$$4529 \times 4529 = 4529 \times 4000 + 4529 \times 500 + 4529 \times 29$$
$$= 18{,}116{,}000 + 2{,}264{,}500 + 131{,}341$$
$$= 20{,}511{,}841$$

So $k^2 = 20{,}511{,}841$.

**Step 2 — Identify the middle $r = 2$ digits:**
The number $20{,}511{,}841$ has 8 digits. To extract the middle 2 digits, we take digits from positions 4 and 5 (counting from the left):
- Position 1: `2`
- Position 2: `0`
- Position 3: `5`
- **Position 4: `1` (first middle digit)**
- **Position 5: `1` (second middle digit)**
- Position 6: `8`
- Position 7: `4`
- Position 8: `1`

**Step 3 — Form the hash value:**
$$h(4529) = 11$$

**Step 4 — Verify range:**
The output $11$ is in the range $[0, 99]$, which matches $m = 100$. ✓

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Folding Method (Shift Folding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Insert the key $k = 12345678$ into a hash table of size $m = 1000$ using shift folding with 3-digit partitions.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Shift Folding)

**Step 1 — Partition the key into 3-digit parts:**
$$12345678 \rightarrow 12 \mid 345 \mid 678$$

**Step 2 — Sum the parts:**
$$h(12345678) = 12 + 345 + 678$$
$$= 12 + 345 = 357$$
$$357 + 678 = 1035$$

**Step 3 — Apply modulus to fit the table:**
Since the table size is $m = 1000$, we take the result modulo 1000:
$$h(12345678) = 1035 \bmod 1000 = 35$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Folding Variant:**
For boundary folding, we reverse alternate parts before summing:
- Part 1: $12$ (kept as-is)
- Part 2: $345 \rightarrow 543$ (reversed)
- Part 3: $678$ (kept as-is)

$$h_{\text{boundary}}(12345678) = 12 + 543 + 678 = 1233$$
$$h_{\text{boundary}}(12345678) = 1233 \bmod 1000 = 233$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Multiplication Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compute $h(123456)$ using the multiplication method with $A = 0.6180339$ and $m = 10000$.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Multiplication Method)

**Step 1 — Compute $kA$:**
$$kA = 123456 \times 0.6180339$$
$$= 123456 \times 0.6 + 123456 \times 0.0180339$$
$$= 74073.6 + 2226.8736 \approx 76300.4736$$

**Step 2 — Extract the fractional part $kA \bmod 1$:**
$$76300.4736 \bmod 1 = 0.4736$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $m$ and take the floor:**
$$h(k) = \lfloor 10000 \times 0.4736 \rfloor$$
$$= \lfloor 4736.0 \rfloor = 4736$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**
The result $4736$ lies in the range $[0, 9999]$ for $m = 10000$. ✓

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Hash Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Comparison Table — memorize this for direct 7-mark comparison questions:**

| Criterion | Division | Multiplication | Mid-Square | Folding | Digit Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed of Computation** | Fastest | Fast | Moderate | Fast | Fast |
| **Distribution Quality** | Good (with prime $m$) | Excellent (independent of $m$) | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Sensitivity to $m$** | High | Low | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Trivial | Moderate | Moderate | Easy | Easy |
| **Best Use Case** | Numeric keys, small tables | Universal (any $m$) | Keys with known size | Long numeric keys | Keys with known patterns |
| **Worst Case** | All keys map to same slot | Poor $A$ choice causes clustering | Loss of leading digits | Sum overflow | Pattern changes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Hashing Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The flow below shows the complete journey of a key from input to storage, including the critical decision point at the collision check.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[INPUT KEY k] --> stepB[EXTRACT KEY FIELD FROM RECORD]
    stepB --> stepC[APPLY HASH FUNCTION h of k]
    stepC --> stepD[COMPUTE INDEX i = h k mod m]
    stepD --> stepE[ACCESS SLOT T AT INDEX i]
    stepE --> stepF{SLOT i EMPTY}
    stepF -- YES --> stepG[STORE KEY AT SLOT i]
    stepF -- NO --> stepH[COLLISION DETECTED]
    stepH --> stepI[APPLY COLLISION RESOLUTION]
    stepI --> stepJ[FIND ALTERNATE SLOT]
    stepG --> stepK[INSERTION COMPLETE]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> endL[END]
```

## 4.2 Hash Function Classification Block Diagram

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The four major hash function families are shown as top-level blocks. Each block maps to its corresponding formula and characteristic use case.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startX[HASH FUNCTION FAMILIES] --> nodeA[DIVISION METHOD]
    startX --> nodeB[MULTIPLICATION METHOD]
    startX --> nodeC[MID SQUARE METHOD]
    startX --> nodeD[FOLDING METHOD]
    startX --> nodeE[DIGIT ANALYSIS METHOD]
    nodeA --> formulaA[h k = k mod m]
    nodeB --> formulaB[h k = floor m times kA mod 1]
    nodeC --> formulaC[h k = middle r digits of k squared]
    nodeD --> formulaD[h k = sum of digit partitions]
    nodeE --> formulaE[h k = selected digit positions of k]
    formulaA --> noteA[REQUIRES PRIME m]
    formulaB --> noteB[A APPROX 0.6180339]
    formulaC --> noteC[CHOOSE r BASED ON m]
    formulaD --> noteD[PARTITION SIZE FLEXIBLE]
    formulaE --> noteE[REQUIRES KNOWN KEY DISTRIBUTION]
```

## 4.3 Hash Table Slot Occupancy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tableX["HASH TABLE T OF SIZE m EQUAL TO 10"]
        slot0[INDEX 0 EMPTY]
        slot1[INDEX 1 KEY 23]
        slot2[INDEX 2 EMPTY]
        slot3[INDEX 3 KEY 78]
        slot4[INDEX 4 EMPTY]
        slot5[INDEX 5 KEY 45]
        slot6[INDEX 6 EMPTY]
        slot7[INDEX 7 EMPTY]
        slot8[INDEX 8 KEY 89]
        slot9[INDEX 9 KEY 12]
    end
    nodeZ[KEYS TO INSERT 23 45 89 78 12] --> computeY[H ASH K = K MOD 10]
    computeY --> tableX
```

## 4.4 Collision Occurrence Schematic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:** The KTU board frequently asks: *"What is a collision? Illustrate with an example."* This diagram is your reference answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    keyA[KEY 25] --> hashOp[HASH FUNCTION h k = k mod 7]
    keyB[KEY 46] --> hashOp
    keyC[KEY 18] --> hashOp
    keyD[KEY 11] --> hashOp
    hashOp --> idx4[INDEX 4]
    hashOp --> idx4B[INDEX 4 COLLISION]
    hashOp --> idx3[INDEX 3]
    hashOp --> idx4C[INDEX 4 COLLISION]
    idx4 --> store4[SLOT 4 OCCUPIED BY 25]
    idx4B --> collisionBox[COLLISION SYNONYMS 46 AND 11 HASH TO SAME SLOT]
    idx3 --> store3[SLOT 3 OCCUPIED BY 18]
    store4 --> collisionBox
    idx4C --> collisionBox
```

## 4.5 Hash Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[CHOOSING A HASH FUNCTION] --> q1{KEY TYPE NUMERIC OR STRING}
    q1 -- NUMERIC --> q2{IS TABLE SIZE PRIME}
    q1 -- STRING --> q5[CONVERT STRING TO NUMERIC FIRST]
    q5 --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> q3[USE DIVISION METHOD]
    q2 -- NO --> q4[USE MULTIPLICATION METHOD]
    q3 --> q6{IS DISTRIBUTION UNIFORM}
    q6 -- YES --> endR[FINAL CHOICE DIVISION]
    q6 -- NO --> q4
    q4 --> endS[FINAL CHOICE MULTIPLICATION]
    q5 --> q7[CONSIDER MID SQUARE OR FOLDING]
    q7 --> endT[FINAL CHOICE MID SQUARE OR FOLDING]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in the 2024 scheme are direct, definition-based, and require precise terminology. Each question carries **3 marks**, with the answer expected in **3-4 lines**.

### Part A — Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q1.** Define hashing. List any **four** desirable properties of a good hash function.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hashing Definition (1 Mark):**
Hashing is a searching technique that uses a hash function $h(k)$ to map keys $k$ from a universe $U$ to indices in a hash table $T$ of size $m$, enabling approximately $O(1)$ average-time search, insertion, and deletion.

**Four Desirable Properties (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
- **Determinism:** $h(k)$ must always produce the same output for the same key $k$.
- **Uniform Distribution:** Keys must be spread evenly across all $m$ slots to minimize collisions.
- **Computational Efficiency:** $h(k)$ must be computable in $O(1)$ time.
- **Avalanche Effect:** A small change in input must cause a large change in output.

---

### Part A — Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** What is a **collision** in hashing? Why is the division method preferred with a **prime** table size $m$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Collision Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A collision occurs when two distinct keys $k_1 \neq k_2$ map to the same hash table index, i.e., $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$. The colliding keys are called **synonyms**.

**Why Prime $m$ (1.5 Marks):**
When $m$ is prime, the modulo operation $k \bmod m$ depends on **all bits** of $k$, not just a subset. If $m$ shares a common factor with the keys (e.g., $m$ is even and all keys are even), then all keys hash to even indices, leaving odd slots empty and causing severe clustering. A prime $m$ prevents this pathology.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** with internal choice between two questions. Each part has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Solutions require working, formulas, and final numerical/state answers.

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.A(a).** Consider the keys $K = \{34, 56, 23, 91, 47, 12, 68, 83, 29, 75\}$. Insert them into a hash table of size $m = 13$ using the **division method** $h(k) = k \bmod 13$. Show the final table and compute the load factor $\alpha$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute hash values (4 Marks):**

$$h(34) = 34 \bmod 13 = 34 - 2(13) = 34 - 26 = 8$$
$$h(56) = 56 \bmod 13 = 56 - 4(13) = 56 - 52 = 4$$
$$h(23) = 23 \bmod 13 = 23 - 1(13) = 23 - 13 = 10$$
$$h(91) = 91 \bmod 13 = 91 - 7(13) = 91 - 91 = 0$$
$$h(47) = 47 \bmod 13 = 47 - 3(13) = 47 - 39 = 8$$
$$h(12) = 12 \bmod 13 = 12$$
$$h(68) = 68 \bmod 13 = 68 - 5(13) = 68 - 65 = 3$$
$$h(83) = 83 \bmod 13 = 83 - 6(13) = 83 - 78 = 5$$
$$h(29) = 29 \bmod 13 = 29 - 2(13) = 29 - 26 = 3$$
$$h(75) = 75 \bmod 13 = 75 - 5(13) = 75 - 65 = 10$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Hash Table (2 Marks):**

| Index | Key Stored | Note |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 91 | First key at this index |
| 1 | (empty) | — |
| 2 | (empty) | — |
| 3 | 68, 29 | **Synonyms** — collision noted |
| 4 | 56 | First key at this index |
| 5 | 83 | First key at this index |
| 6 | (empty) | — |
| 7 | (empty) | — |
| 8 | 34, 47 | **Synonyms** — collision noted |
| 9 | (empty) | — |
| 10 | 23, 75 | **Synonyms** — collision noted |
| 11 | (empty) | — |
| 12 | 12 | First key at this index |

**Step 3 — Compute Load Factor (1 Mark):**
Number of stored keys $n = 10$. Table size $m = 13$.
$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{10}{13} \approx 0.769$$

---

**Q.A(b).** Explain the **multiplication method** of hashing with the constant $A = 0.6180339$. Compute the hash value of $k = 56789$ for a table of size $m = 100$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Explanation of the Method (3 Marks):**

The multiplication method computes the hash value using the formula:
$$h(k) = \lfloor m \cdot (kA \bmod 1) \rfloor$$
where $A$ is a constant in the range $(0, 1)$. The value $A = 0.6180339$ is derived from the **golden ratio conjugate** $\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}$, which is empirically proven to provide the most uniform distribution of keys across slots. The advantage of this method is that the quality of distribution is **independent of the table size $m$, making it superior to the division method when $m$ cannot be chosen as prime.

**Step 2 — Compute $kA$ (2 Marks):**
$$kA = 56789 \times 0.6180339$$
$$= 56789 \times 0.6 + 56789 \times 0.0180339$$
$$= 34073.4 + 1024.2 \approx 35097.6$$

**Step 3 — Extract Fractional Part (1 Mark):**
$$35097.6 \bmod 1 = 0.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute Final Hash Value (1 Mark):**
$$h(56789) = \lfloor 100 \times 0.6 \rfloor = \lfloor 60.0 \rfloor = 60$$

The hash value $h(56789) = 60$ lies in the range $[0, 99]$ for $m = 100$. ✓

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B(a).** Apply the **mid-square method** with $r = 2$ to the keys $\{1234, 5678, 9012, 3456\}$ for a hash table of size $m = 100$. Show all steps and identify the table after insertion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Square Each Key (3 Marks):**

For $k = 1234$:
$$1234^2 = 1{,}522{,}756$$

For $k = 5678$:
$$5678^2 = 32{,}239{,}684$$

For $k = 9012$:
$$9012^2 = 81{,}216{,}144$$

For $k = 3456$:
$$3456^2 = 11{,}943{,}936$$

**Step 2 — Extract Middle 2 Digits (2 Marks):**

For $k = 1234$: Squared value has 7 digits. Middle 2 digits are positions 4 and 5: `22` (from `1522756`). Thus $h(1234) = 22$.

For $k = 5678$: Squared value has 8 digits. Middle 2 digits are positions 4 and 5: `39` (from `32239684`). Thus $h(5678) = 39$.

For $k = 9012$: Squared value has 8 digits. Middle 2 digits are positions 4 and 5: `16` (from `81216144`). Thus $h(9012) = 16$.

For $k = 3456$: Squared value has 8 digits. Middle 2 digits are positions 4 and 5: `43` (from `11943936`). Thus $h(3456) = 43$.

**Step 3 — Construct the Hash Table (2 Marks):**

| Index | Key Stored |
|---|---|
| 0 to 15 | (empty) |
| 16 | 9012 |
| 17 to 21 | (empty) |
| 22 | 1234 |
| 23 to 38 | (empty) |
| 39 | 5678 |
| 40 to 42 | (empty) |
| 43 | 3456 |
| 44 to 99 | (empty) |

Load factor: $\alpha = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04$.

---

**Q.B(b).** Explain the **folding method** (both shift and boundary variants) of hashing. Apply both variants to the key $k = 58237146$ for a table of size $m = 1000$ with 3-digit partitions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Explanation (2 Marks):**

**Folding Method** splits a long key into smaller fixed-size parts (typically 2-4 digits), then combines them (usually by addition) to form the hash value.

- **Shift Folding:** All partitions are summed as they are (no reversal). Simple and fast.
- **Boundary Folding:** Alternate partitions are reversed before summation. This reduces the effect of digit position bias — for instance, if all keys start with `5`, shift folding would produce consistently higher sums, but boundary folding distributes the digit values more uniformly.

**Step 2 — Partition the Key (1 Mark):**
$$58237146 \rightarrow 58 \mid 237 \mid 146$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shift Folding (2 Marks):**
$$h_{\text{shift}}(58237146) = 58 + 237 + 146$$
$$= 58 + 237 = 295$$
$$295 + 146 = 441$$

Now apply modulo to fit table size $m = 1000$:
$$h_{\text{shift}}(58237146) = 441 \bmod 1000 = 441$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Folding (2 Marks):**
Reverse alternate partitions:
- Part 1: $58$ (kept as-is)
- Part 2: $237 \rightarrow 732$ (reversed)
- Part 3: $146$ (kept as-is)

$$h_{\text{boundary}}(58237146) = 58 + 732 + 146$$
$$= 58 + 732 = 790$$
$$790 + 146 = 936$$

Apply modulo:
$$h_{\text{boundary}}(58237146) = 936 \bmod 1000 = 936$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Modular Reduction Mistake:** Students frequently forget to take the final result modulo $m$ when the sum exceeds the table size. **Always verify:** $\text{result} \in [0, m-1]$.
> - **Division Method Table Size:** Using a non-prime $m$ (e.g., $m = 10$) leads to clustering. Examiners award **partial credit** for noting this limitation.
> - **Mid-Square Digit Counting:** When $k^2$ has an even number of digits, the middle 2 digits are unambiguous. When odd, the convention is to drop the leftmost digit first. **State your convention** explicitly.
> - **Folding Reversal Pattern:** In boundary folding, the **first** partition is kept as-is, the **second** is reversed, the **third** kept, and so on. Students often reverse the **first** partition — this loses 1-2 marks.
> - **Load Factor Computation:** Forget to include the **load factor $\alpha$** in the final answer, even when not explicitly asked. Examiners expect it as a **mandatory conclusion**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- **Hashing** converts search from $O(n)$ to $O(1)$ on average by using a function $h: U \rightarrow [0, m-1]$.

- **Hash Table Size $m$:** Prefer **prime numbers** to ensure uniform distribution with the division method.

- **Load Factor $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$:** Always quote this value at the end of any insertion problem.

- **Division Method:** $h(k) = k \bmod m$. Simple, fast, but sensitive to $m$.

- **Multiplication Method:** $h(k) = \lfloor m \cdot (kA \bmod 1) \rfloor$ with $A \approx 0.6180339$ (golden ratio conjugate). Robust to $m$ choice.

- **Mid-Square Method:** Square the key, extract middle $r$ digits. Good for keys with unknown distribution.

- **Folding Method (Shift):** Sum of digit partitions. Good for long keys.

- **Folding Method (Boundary):** Sum of partitions with alternate reversal. Reduces positional bias.

- **Digit Analysis:** Use pre-known patterns in keys (e.g., employee IDs) to select specific digit positions.

- **Collision:** When $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$ for $k_1 \neq k_2$. Always state this clearly: **"Two distinct keys mapping to the same index"**.

- **Synonyms:** The set of all keys that hash to the same index.

- **Key Properties Checklist (for any hash function answer):** Determinism, Uniform Distribution, Efficiency, Avalanche Effect.

- **Mandatory Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$
  - $h(k) = k \bmod m$ (Division)
  - $h(k) = \lfloor m \cdot (kA \bmod 1) \rfloor$ (Multiplication)
  - Mid-square: middle $r$ digits of $k^2$
  - Shift folding: sum of partitions
  - Boundary folding: sum of partitions with alternate reversal

- **Exam Strategy:** Always show **intermediate computations** (squaring, multiplying, mod operations) — never skip to the final answer. The KTU board awards **step marks** for showing work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linked List and Self-Referential Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Linked List and Self-Referential Structures # 🔗 Linked List and Self-Referential Structures ## 1.1 Self-Referential Structures — The Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > A **self-referential structure** is a `struct` data type in C/C++ that contains one or more **pointers** that point to structures of the **same type**. This recursive type definition is the fundamental building block of dynamic data structures such as linked lists, trees, and graphs. ### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Nesting Dolls" Imagine a set of **Russian nesting dolls** (Matryoshka). Each doll has two compartments: 1. **Storage area** — holds the data (a small picture, a trinket). 2. **A connection** — a string or handle that ties it to the **next doll** of the **same type**. A self-referential structure works identically: it is a structure that "knows" about and can be linked to another structure of its own kind. The pointer member inside the structure acts like the string connecting one doll to the next. > [!NOTE] > **Why are self-referential structures important?** > - They allow **dynamic memory allocation** (size known at runtime). > - They enable creation of **chains** whose length is not fixed at compile time. > - They are the **backbone of dynamic data structures** — Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues (linked implementation), Trees, Graphs, and Hash Tables. ### 1.2 The Generic Node Definition Every linked list is composed of **nodes**. A node is an instance of a self-referential structure containing two fields: | Field | Purpose | Type | |---|---|---| | `data` | Stores the actual useful information | Any data type (`int`, `float`, `char`, `struct`, etc.) | | `next` | Stores the address of the next node in the sequence | Pointer to the same `struct` type | ```c /* Standard KTU-approved node definition */ struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` > [!TIP] > **Critical Compilation Note:** The tag name `Node` (or the structure tag you choose) is **mandatory** in the pointer declaration `struct Node *next;`. You cannot use a `typedef` name here because at that point, the `typedef` alias has not yet been defined by the compiler. This is a **frequently tested KTU viva question**. ## 1.3 Linked List — The Big Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > A **linked list** is a linear, dynamic data structure consisting of a sequence of **nodes**, where each node contains a data field and a pointer (link) to the next node in the sequence. Unlike arrays, elements are **not stored in contiguous memory locations**; they are linked by pointers. ### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Treasure Hunt" Picture a **treasure hunt** in your college campus. Each clue (a small paper note) contains two things: 1. **Information** — the actual clue to find the treasure. 2. **A direction** — telling you where to go next (e.g., *"Go to the library's third floor"*). The clues are scattered across the campus — **not in one place**, but each one tells you how to reach the next. This is exactly how a linked list works: - Each **node** = a clue. - The **data** field = the actual information (clue text). - The **next** pointer = the direction to the next clue. - The **last node's pointer** = `NULL` (no more clues — hunt ends). If you lose your position (lose the `head` pointer), you cannot find any other node — just like forgetting your first clue in the treasure hunt! # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Linked Lists (KTU 2024 Taxonomy) | # | Type | Traversal Direction | Last Node Points To | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Singly Linked List (SLL)** | Forward only | `NULL` | Simplest, one pointer per node | | 2 | **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** | Forward & Backward | `NULL` | Two pointers per node (`prev`, `next`) | | 3 | **Circular Linked List (CLL)** | Forward (loops back) | Head (first node) | No `NULL`; forms a ring | | 4 | **Circular Doubly Linked List** | Forward, Backward, Loops | Head (first node) | Most flexible; used in LRU caches, OS process schedulers | ## 2.2 Why Linked Lists Over Arrays? | Property | Array | Linked List | |---|---|---| | Memory Allocation | **Static / Contiguous** (one block) | **Dynamic / Scattered** (linked by pointers) | | Size | **Fixed** at compile time | **Growable** at runtime | | Random Access by Index | **O(1)** — direct indexing | **O(n)** — must traverse from head | | Insertion / Deletion at beginning | **O(n)** — shift elements | **O(1)** — rewire pointers | | Insertion / Deletion at end | O(1) amortized (dynamic array) | **O(1)** with tail pointer; **O(n)** without | | Insertion / Deletion in middle | **O(n)** — shift elements | **O(1)** if node pointer known; **O(n)** to locate | | Memory Overhead | None (data only) | **Extra pointer per node** (8 bytes on 64-bit) | | Cache Friendliness | **High** (spatial locality) | **Low** (nodes scattered in heap) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Time Complexity Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — at least 1 question (3 marks) in every KTU university exam tests these complexities directly.** | Operation | Singly LL (no tail) | Singly LL (with tail) | Doubly LL | Circular LL | |---|---|---|---|---| | Traverse / Print all | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | Search an element | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | Insert at **head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | | Insert at **tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ *(with tail)* | | Insert at **position k** | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | | Delete at **head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | | Delete at **tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ *(need prev)* | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ *(with tail)* | | Delete at **position k** | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | | Reverse the list | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ### 2.4 Memory Footprint Formula For a linked list with **$n$ nodes**, where each data field occupies **$d$ bytes** and each pointer occupies **$p$ bytes** (typically $p = 8$ bytes on 64-bit systems): $$ \text{Total Memory} = n \times (d + p) \text{ bytes} $$ **Example (KTU favourite):** For $n = 100$ nodes, `int data` ($d = 4$), 64-bit pointer ($p = 8$): $$ \text{Total} = 100 \times (4 + 8) = 1200 \text{ bytes} $$ Compare to a static array: $100 \times 4 = 400$ bytes. The **overhead** is $1200 - 400 = 800$ bytes — this is the cost of dynamic linking. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling queues, memory management free lists. - **Web Browsers:** Back/Forward navigation history (doubly linked list). - **Music Players:** Playlist navigation (circular doubly linked list — loops back to first song). - **Hash Tables:** Separate chaining collision resolution uses linked lists. - **Symbol Tables in Compilers:** Symbol storage uses linked lists / hash chains. - **Undo/Redo Mechanisms:** Doubly linked list of states. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Algorithms & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Canonical Node Structure (Singly Linked List) ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; typedef struct Node NODE; ``` **Step-by-step explanation of the self-reference:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Declare the structure tag} &\rightarrow \text{`struct Node` is now known to the compiler.} \\ \text{Step 2: Add a data field} &\rightarrow \text{`int data;` stores an integer payload.} \\ \text{Step 3: Add a self-pointer} &\rightarrow \text{`struct Node *next;` points to ANOTHER node of the same type.} \\ \text{Step 4: `typedef` for convenience} &\rightarrow \text{`NODE *head;` becomes a shorthand for `struct Node *head;`.} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The `typedef` happens **after** the structure is fully defined. That is why inside the structure we **must** write `struct Node *next;` and not `NODE *next;` — the alias `NODE` does not exist yet at that line of code. ## 3.2 Helper Function — `createNode()` This function allocates memory dynamically using `malloc()` and initializes the new node. It returns a pointer to the newly created node. ```c NODE* createNode(int value) { NODE *newNode = (NODE *)malloc(sizeof(NODE)); if (newNode == NULL) { printf("Memory allocation failed!\n"); exit(1); } newNode->data = value; newNode->next = NULL; return newNode; } ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: \texttt{malloc(sizeof(NODE))}} &\rightarrow \text{Reserves memory on the heap of size `d + p` bytes.} \\ \text{Step 2: Type-cast to \texttt{(NODE *)}} &\rightarrow \text{Best practice in C to avoid warning.} \\ \text{Step 3: NULL check} &\rightarrow \text{Mandatory — if heap is exhausted, dereferencing crashes the program.} \\ \text{Step 4: Initialize \texttt{data}} &\rightarrow \text{Stores the user's value.} \\ \text{Step 5: Initialize \texttt{next = NULL}} &\rightarrow \text{Crucial — a dangling pointer is a security risk.} \\ \text{Step 6: Return pointer} &\rightarrow \text{Caller receives the address of the new node.} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Core Operations — Step-by-Step ### 3.3.1 Insertion at the Beginning — O(1) **Algorithm (Pseudocode):** ``` 1. CREATE newNode with given value 2. newNode->next = head // Step A: point new node to old head 3. head = newNode // Step B: update head to new node 4. return head ``` **C Implementation:** ```c NODE* insertAtBeginning(NODE *head, int value) { NODE *newNode = createNode(value); newNode->next = head; // Step A head = newNode; // Step B return head; } ``` **Pointer-rewiring walkthrough** (initial: `head → [10|N] → [20|N] → NULL`): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Before} &: \quad \texttt{head} \to [10 | \bullet] \to [20 | \text{NULL}] \\ \text{Step A} &: \quad \texttt{newNode} \to [5 | \bullet \to \texttt{head}] \\ \text{Step B} &: \quad \texttt{head} \to [5 | \bullet] \to [10 | \bullet] \to [20 | \text{NULL}] \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1):** If you reverse Steps A and B (i.e., set `head = newNode` *first*), you will **lose the entire list** because the original `head` pointer's address is overwritten. The original chain becomes unreachable, causing a **memory leak**. This is the most frequent logic error in KTU answer sheets. ### 3.3.2 Insertion at the End — O(n) without tail / O(1) with tail **Algorithm (without tail pointer):** ``` 1. CREATE newNode 2. IF head == NULL THEN head = newNode; RETURN 3. SET temp = head 4. WHILE temp->next != NULL: temp = temp->next 5. temp->next = newNode 6. RETURN head ``` **C Implementation:** ```c NODE* insertAtEnd(NODE *head, int value) { NODE *newNode = createNode(value); if (head == NULL) return newNode; NODE *temp = head; while (temp->next != NULL) { // Step 4 temp = temp->next; } temp->next = newNode; // Step 5 return head; } ``` **Traversal trace** for inserting `99` at end of `head → [10] → [20] → [30] → NULL`: | Iteration | `temp` points to | `temp->next` | |---|---|---| | 1 | Node containing 10 | Pointer to 20 (not NULL) | | 2 | Node containing 20 | Pointer to 30 (not NULL) | | 3 | Node containing 30 | NULL → **EXIT LOOP** | | Final | Node containing 30 | Now points to newNode [99] | ### 3.3.3 Deletion of a Node by Value — O(n) **Algorithm:** ``` 1. IF head == NULL: print "List is empty"; RETURN NULL 2. IF head->data == key: free(head); RETURN head->next 3. SET temp = head 4. WHILE temp->next != NULL AND temp->next->data != key: temp = temp->next 5. IF temp->next == NULL: print "Not found"; RETURN head 6. SET del = temp->next 7. temp->next = del->next 8. free(del) 9. RETURN head ``` **C Implementation:** ```c NODE* deleteNode(NODE *head, int key) { if (head == NULL) { printf("Empty list\n"); return NULL; } if (head->data == key) { NODE *toFree = head; head = head->next; free(toFree); return head; } NODE *temp = head; while (temp->next != NULL && temp->next->data != key) { temp = temp->next; } if (temp->next == NULL) { printf("Not found\n"); return head; } NODE *del = temp->next; temp->next = del->next; free(del); return head; } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #2:** Many students forget to call `free()` on the deleted node. This causes a **memory leak**. The examiner strictly checks that you: > 1. Save the pointer to the node to be deleted. > 2. Rewire the previous node's `next`. > 3. Call `free()` on the saved pointer. > 4. Return the (possibly updated) `head`. ### 3.3.4 Traversal / Display — O(n) ```c void display(NODE *head) { if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return; } NODE *temp = head; while (temp != NULL) { printf("%d -> ", temp->data); temp = temp->next; } printf("NULL\n"); } ``` ### 3.3.5 Reverse a Singly Linked List — O(n) **Three-pointer technique** (`prev`, `curr`, `next`): ```c NODE* reverseList(NODE *head) { NODE *prev = NULL, *curr = head, *next = NULL; while (curr != NULL) { next = curr->next; // save next node curr->next = prev; // reverse the link prev = curr; // move prev forward curr = next; // move curr forward } head = prev; // new head is the last node processed return head; } ``` **Step-by-step trace** for `head → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`: | Iteration | `prev` | `curr` | `next` | After `curr->next = prev` | |---|---|---|---|---| | Init | NULL | 10 | NULL | — | | 1 | NULL | 10 | 20 | 10 → NULL | | 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 20 → 10 | | 3 | 20 | 30 | NULL | 30 → 20 | | End | 30 | NULL | — | Final list: `30 → 20 → 10 → NULL` | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world use:** This O(n) in-place reverse is the **classic KTU 14-mark question** for Module 3. It tests pointer manipulation mastery. Practice writing this **5 times by hand** before the exam. ## 3.4 Doubly Linked List — Node Structure ```c struct DNode { int data; struct DNode *prev; struct DNode *next; }; typedef struct DNode DNODE; ``` **Three key operations (KTU expected answers):** | Operation | Pointer Rewiring Steps | |---|---| | Insert after node `p` | `new->next = p->next; new->prev = p; if (p->next != NULL) p->next->prev = new; p->next = new;` | | Delete node `p` | `if (p->prev != NULL) p->prev->next = p->next; if (p->next != NULL) p->next->prev = p->prev; free(p);` | | Traverse backward | Start from tail, repeatedly follow `prev` until NULL | > [!IMPORTANT] > The total pointer rewiring for DLL insert is **4 assignments** (vs. 2 for SLL). The benefit: **O(1) deletion of the tail node** because you have a direct `prev` link. ## 3.5 Circular Linked List — Special Cases In a CLL, the **last node's `next` pointer points back to `head`**, not `NULL`. This eliminates end-of-list checks: **Insertion at end (O(n)):** ```c NODE* insertAtEndCLL(NODE *head, int value) { NODE *newNode = createNode(value); if (head == NULL) { newNode->next = newNode; // points to itself return newNode; } NODE *temp = head; while (temp->next != head) { // stop when we reach the last node temp = temp->next; } temp->next = newNode; newNode->next = head; // close the loop return head; } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #3:** Students often write `while (temp->next != NULL)` for CLL — this creates an **infinite loop** because the last node points to `head`, never to `NULL`. **Always use `temp->next != head`** for circular linked lists. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Singly Linked List — Node Anatomy ```mermaid graph LR A["DATA FIELD"] --- B["NEXT POINTER"] A1["int data"]:::mem --> A B1["struct Node *next"]:::ptr --> B classDef mem fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef ptr fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 ``` **ASCII Schematic of a 3-Node SLL** storing 10 → 20 → 30: ``` HEAD | v +-----+-----+ +-----+-----+ +-----+------+ | 10 | o------> | 20 | o------> | 30 | NULL | +-----+-----+ +-----+-----+ +-----+------+ Node 1 Node 2 Node 3 ``` ## 4.2 Singly Linked List — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD HEAD["HEAD POINTER"] --> N1["NODE 1
data=10
next=o"] N1 --> N2["NODE 2
data=20
next=o"] N2 --> N3["NODE 3
data=30
next=o"] N3 --> N4["NODE 4
data=40
next=NULL"] OP1["Insert at Head
O of 1"] -.-> HEAD OP2["Insert at End
O of n"] -.-> N4 OP3["Delete by Value
O of n"] -.-> N1 OP4["Reverse List
O of n"] -.-> N1 OP5["Search Element
O of n"] -.-> N1 classDef op fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 class OP1,OP2,OP3,OP4,OP5 op ``` ## 4.3 Doubly Linked List — Bidirectional Traversal ```mermaid graph LR H["HEAD"] --> N1["NULL | 10 | o"] N1 <--> N2["o | 20 | o"] N2 <--> N3["o | 30 | NULL"] classDef dll fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class N1,N2,N3 dll ``` **ASCII Schematic (DLL with 3 nodes):** ``` HEAD (start) | v NULL <--+----+-----+ +----+-----+ +----+-----+--> NULL | 10 | o------> | 20 | o------> | 30 | | +----+<------o--+----+<------o----+----+ prev=NULL prev=o prev=o next=o next=NULL ``` ## 4.4 Circular Linked List — The Ring Structure ```mermaid graph LR N1["10
next=o"] --> N2["20
next=o"] N2 --> N3["30
next=o"] N3 --> N4["40
next=o"] N4 -.-> N1 HEAD["HEAD"] --> N1 classDef cll fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 class N1,N2,N3,N4 cll ``` **Key visual:** Notice the **dashed arrow** from Node 4 back to Node 1 — this is what makes it **circular**. There is no `NULL` terminator in a CLL. ## 4.5 Insertion Walkthrough — Visual Sequence ```mermaid graph TD S1["Step 1
Locate insertion point
Traverse to position k"] --> S2["Step 2
Allocate newNode
newNode = malloc sizeof NODE"] S2 --> S3["Step 3
Wire newNode to next
newNode->next = prev->next"] S3 --> S4["Step 4
Wire prev to newNode
prev->next = newNode"] S4 --> S5["Step 5
Return updated head
If k==0, update head"] classDef step fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 step ``` ## 4.6 Complete Block Architecture of a Linked List Module ```mermaid graph TD A["Main Function"] --> B["createNode"] A --> C["insertAtBeginning"] A --> D["insertAtEnd"] A --> E["insertAtPosition"] A --> F["deleteNode"] A --> G["display"] A --> H["searchNode"] A --> I["reverseList"] A --> J["freeList"] B --> K["malloc + Initialize"] C --> L["Rewire head pointer"] D --> M["Traverse to tail + Append"] E --> N["Traverse to k-1 + Insert"] F --> O["Search + Bypass + free"] G --> P["Linear scan + print"] H --> Q["Linear scan + compare"] I --> R["Three-pointer technique"] J --> S["Iterate + free each node"] classDef main fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef util fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 class A main class B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J util ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > - **Part A (3 marks):** Short-answer concepts, definitions, complexity analysis. > - **Part B (14 marks):** Full programs, derivations, comparative analysis (internal choice). > - Module 3 contributes **15–20%** of the End Semester Exam (ESE) marks. --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a self-referential structure. Write the C declaration for a node of a singly linked list that stores an integer data value.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **self-referential structure** is a `struct` type that contains a member which is a **pointer to a structure of the same type**. It allows a structure instance to be linked to another instance of itself, forming chains such as linked lists. **C declaration:** ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` The member `data` holds the integer payload, and `next` is a pointer that stores the address of the next node in the list (or `NULL` if it is the last node). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for declaration, 1 for explanation)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare arrays and linked lists based on: (i) memory allocation, (ii) access time, and (iii) insertion/deletion at the beginning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | Array | Linked List | |---|---|---| | (i) Memory Allocation | Static, **contiguous** | Dynamic, **scattered** (linked by pointers) | | (ii) Access Time | $O(1)$ (random access) | $O(n)$ (sequential traversal from head) | | (iii) Insertion/Deletion at beginning | $O(n)$ (shift all elements) | $O(1)$ (rewire `head` pointer) | Arrays offer **better cache locality** but lack flexibility; linked lists offer **dynamic resizing** but incur pointer overhead. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Write a C function to insert a new node at the end of a singly linked list. Discuss its time complexity.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Write a C function to reverse a singly linked list in-place using pointer manipulation. Trace your algorithm for the list `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`.** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to (a) — Insert at End (7 marks)** **C Program:** ```c NODE* insertAtEnd(NODE *head, int value) { NODE *newNode = (NODE *)malloc(sizeof(NODE)); newNode->data = value; newNode->next = NULL; if (head == NULL) { // Case: empty list return newNode; } NODE *temp = head; while (temp->next != NULL) { // Traverse to last node temp = temp->next; } temp->next = newNode; // Link new node at end return head; } ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. `malloc` allocates heap memory for the new node. 2. If the list is empty (`head == NULL`), the new node becomes the head and is returned. 3. Otherwise, we traverse the list using `temp` until `temp->next == NULL` (i.e., we reach the last node). 4. The last node's `next` pointer is updated to point to `newNode`. 5. The function returns the (unchanged) `head`. **Time Complexity Analysis (KTU favourite):** - **Best Case:** $O(1)$ — when the list is empty. - **Average / Worst Case:** $O(n)$ — we must traverse all $n$ nodes to reach the tail. > To improve to $O(1)$, maintain a **tail pointer** that always references the last node. --- #### **Solution to (b) — Reverse a Singly Linked List (7 marks)** **C Program:** ```c NODE* reverseList(NODE *head) { NODE *prev = NULL; NODE *curr = head; NODE *next = NULL; while (curr != NULL) { next = curr->next; // save next curr->next = prev; // reverse link prev = curr; // move prev curr = next; // move curr } head = prev; return head; } ``` **Trace for `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`:** | Iteration | `prev` | `curr` | `next` | Action | State after iteration | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Init | NULL | 10 | — | Initialize | `10 → 20 → 30 → 40` | | 1 | NULL | 10 | 20 | `10->next = NULL` | `10 → NULL`, `20 → 30 → 40` | | 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | `20->next = 10` | `20 → 10 → NULL`, `30 → 40` | | 3 | 20 | 30 | 40 | `30->next = 20` | `30 → 20 → 10`, `40` | | 4 | 30 | 40 | NULL | `40->next = 30` | `40 → 30 → 20 → 10` | | End | 40 | NULL | — | `head = prev` | Final: **`40 → 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL`** | **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — single pass through the list. **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — only three pointer variables used (in-place). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the structure of a doubly linked list node. Write a C function to delete a given node from a doubly linked list.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Explain the concept of a circular linked list. Write a C function to insert a node at the end of a circular singly linked list.** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to (a) — Doubly Linked List Deletion (7 marks)** **Node Structure:** ```c struct DNode { int data; struct DNode *prev; struct DNode *next; }; typedef struct DNode DNODE; ``` Each node has **three fields**: `data`, `prev` (points to previous node or `NULL`), and `next` (points to next node or `NULL`). This enables **bidirectional traversal** and **O(1) deletion** when the node pointer is known. **C Function to Delete a Node (given pointer to that node):** ```c void deleteNode(DNODE **head, DNODE *del) { if (*head == NULL || del == NULL) return; // If node to be deleted is the head if (*head == del) { *head = del->next; } // Change next only if del->next is not NULL if (del->next != NULL) { del->next->prev = del->prev; } // Change prev only if del->prev is not NULL if (del->prev != NULL) { del->prev->next = del->next; } free(del); } ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. Validate inputs: if list is empty or `del` is `NULL`, return. 2. If `del` is the head, update `*head` to `del->next`. 3. Bypass `del` from the forward chain: `del->next->prev = del->prev`. 4. Bypass `del` from the backward chain: `del->prev->next = del->next`. 5. Free memory occupied by `del` to prevent leaks. **Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — direct pointer manipulation, no traversal required. --- #### **Solution to (b) — Circular Linked List Insertion at End (7 marks)** **Concept:** In a **circular singly linked list (CSLL)**, the last node's `next` pointer points back to the **head** node instead of `NULL`. This forms a closed ring. CSLLs are useful in applications requiring **cyclic rotation** (e.g., round-robin scheduling, music playlists). **Visualization:** ``` HEAD | v +---+---+ +---+---+ +---+---+ | 10| o------> | 20| o------> | 30| o--+ +---+---+ +---+---+ +---+---+ | ^ | |__________________________________| ``` **C Function to Insert at End of CSLL:** ```c NODE* insertAtEndCSLL(NODE *head, int value) { NODE *newNode = (NODE *)malloc(sizeof(NODE)); newNode->data = value; if (head == NULL) { newNode->next = newNode; // points to itself return newNode; } NODE *temp = head; while (temp->next != head) { // stop when next points back to head temp = temp->next; } temp->next = newNode; // last node now points to newNode newNode->next = head; // newNode closes the loop return head; } ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. Allocate and initialize the new node. 2. **Empty list case:** If `head == NULL`, the new node's `next` is set to itself (a single-node circular list) and returned as head. 3. **General case:** Traverse the list using `temp` until `temp->next == head` (i.e., we have reached the **last node**). 4. Rewire the last node's `next` to point to `newNode`. 5. Rewire `newNode->next` to point to `head`, **closing the loop**. 6. Return the original `head`. **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — must traverse the entire ring to reach the last node. **Optimization:** With a **tail pointer**, this becomes $O(1)$. --- > [!WARNING] > **🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Across All Linked List Questions** > 1. **Forgetting `free()`** after deletion — direct **2-mark deduction** in most valuation keys. > 2. **NULL checks missing** — examiners test whether you handle edge cases (empty list, single-node list). > 3. **Wrong loop termination** in CLL — using `temp->next != NULL` causes an **infinite loop** (deducted 100% if traced). > 4. **Misordered pointer rewiring** in insertion — if you overwrite `head` before saving the old `head`, you lose the entire list (memory leak + wrong output). > 5. **Confusing `*head` vs `head`** in functions that modify the head pointer — pass `NODE **head` (double pointer) or return the new head. > 6. **In the reverse program**, students forget to set `head = prev;` at the end, leaving `head` pointing to the **old first node** (which now has `next = NULL`). > 7. **Syntax error:** Writing `typedef struct { ... } Node;` and then `Node *next;` inside it — this is **illegal** because the `typedef` alias is not yet in scope. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before exam!)** - ✅ A **self-referential structure** contains a pointer to a structure of the **same type** — it is the foundation of all dynamic linked structures. - ✅ Always use the **structure tag** (e.g., `struct Node *next;`) inside the structure definition, **not** a `typedef` alias. - ✅ **Singly Linked List (SLL):** one pointer per node, forward-only traversal, last node → `NULL`. - ✅ **Doubly Linked List (DLL):** two pointers per node, bidirectional traversal, **O(1) tail deletion**. - ✅ **Circular Linked List (CLL):** last node points to `head`; loop termination condition is `temp->next != head`. - ✅ **Time Complexities to Memorize:** - SLL insert at head: $O(1)$ - SLL insert at end: $O(n)$ without tail, $O(1)$ with tail - SLL delete at head: $O(1)$ - SLL search: $O(n)$ - Reverse: $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space - ✅ **Three-pointer technique for reverse:** `prev`, `curr`, `next` — this is the most-tested KTU 14-mark question. - ✅ **Always `free()` deleted nodes** to prevent memory leaks. - ✅ **Always check for `NULL`** after `malloc()` and before dereferencing any pointer. - ✅ **Memory overhead:** Each linked list node costs $d + p$ bytes; the pointer overhead is the price of dynamic linking. - ✅ **Real-world uses:** OS process queues (SLL), browser history (DLL), music playlists (CLL), hash table chaining (SLL). - ✅ For DLL, **4 pointer assignments** are needed for insertion (vs. 2 for SLL), but you get **bidirectional traversal** and **O(1) tail deletion** in return.

topics.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Dynamic Memory Allocation (DMA)** is a memory management mechanism in which the size of the data structure is determined **at runtime** rather than at compile-time, allowing programs to request memory from the **heap** (free store) using standard library functions such as `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()`, and to release it back using `free()`.

In the C programming model, the operating system grants every process a virtual address space, partitioned into well-defined regions: the **code segment**, **static/global data segment**, **stack**, and the **heap**. While the stack handles automatic, short-lived allocations (local variables, function parameters), the heap is dedicated to *dynamic* memory — the territory where data structures such as linked lists, trees, graphs, and hash tables actually come alive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:**
> Dynamic memory allocation is the **foundational prerequisite** for implementing *non-linear* and *pointer-based* data structures (Linked Lists, Stacks/Queues using linked representation, Trees, Graphs, Hash Tables). It carries **direct weightage in Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (7–14 marks)** of the University End-Semester Examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Room Model"

Imagine you are a traveler planning a trip. Two strategies exist:

| Strategy | Real-World Analogy | Programming Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Allocation** | Booking a fixed number of hotel rooms in advance (even if you don't use them) | Declaring `int arr[100];` at compile-time |
| **Dynamic Allocation** | Walking into the hotel lobby and asking, "I need *N* rooms *now*, give me what I need" | Calling `malloc(N * sizeof(int));` at runtime |

The key insight: in the **static** case, you pay for rooms you may never occupy, and the booking happens before you even know your plans. In the **dynamic** case, you request memory *exactly when* you need it, *exactly the size* you require, and you can *return* it (via `free()`) when you're done — just like checking out of a hotel.

### 1.3 Why Dynamic Memory Allocation Matters in DSA

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Students:**
> - **Linked Lists** cannot exist without DMA — every node must be created at runtime.
> - **Trees and Graphs** are fundamentally dynamic structures.
> - **Hash Tables with chaining** rely on DMA for each bucket's linked list.
> - **Sparse matrices, adjacency lists, and priority queues** all use dynamic allocation for memory efficiency.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> **[!IMPORTANT] Standard Sizes to Memorize (Architecture-Dependent):**
> - `sizeof(char)` = **1 byte**
> - `sizeof(int)` = **4 bytes** (on 32/64-bit GCC)
> - `sizeof(float)` = **4 bytes**
> - `sizeof(double)` = **8 bytes**
> - `sizeof(pointer)` = **8 bytes** (on 64-bit systems) / **4 bytes** (on 32-bit systems)

These constants are repeatedly used in KTU numerical problems (e.g., "calculate total memory allocated by `malloc(5 * sizeof(int))`").

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Paradigms of Memory Allocation

### A. Static (Compile-Time) Memory Allocation
- Memory is allocated **by the compiler** before program execution begins.
- Size and type must be known at compile-time.
- Stored in the **stack** or **data segment**.
- Examples: `int x;`, `float marks[50];`
- **Drawbacks:** Wastes memory when fully used array slots are unused; cannot resize.

### B. Dynamic (Run-Time) Memory Allocation
- Memory is allocated **at runtime** from the **heap** using library functions.
- Size can be decided based on user input or computation.
- Stored in the **heap segment** of the process address space.
- **Advantages:** Efficient memory utilization, flexibility, enables pointer-based data structures.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Dynamic Memory Management in C

### 2.2.1 `malloc()` — Memory Allocation
- **Header:** `#include <stdlib.h>`
- **Signature:** `void *malloc(size_t size);`
- **Behavior:** Allocates a **single contiguous block** of `size` bytes from the heap. Returns a `void*` pointer to the beginning of the block. **Does NOT initialize** the memory (contains garbage values).
- **On Failure:** Returns `NULL`.
- **Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (int *)malloc(n * sizeof(int));
```

### 2.2.2 `calloc()` — Contiguous Allocation
- **Signature:** `void *calloc(size_t num, size_t size);`
- **Behavior:** Allocates memory for an array of `num` elements, each of `size` bytes. **Initializes all bytes to zero.**
- **On Failure:** Returns `NULL`.
- **Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (int *)calloc(n, sizeof(int));
```

### 2.2.3 `realloc()` — Reallocation
- **Signature:** `void *realloc(void *ptr, size_t new_size);`
- **Behavior:** Resizes an already-allocated memory block. If expansion is not possible at the current location, it allocates a new block, **copies the existing contents**, and frees the old block. May return a different address.
- **On Failure:** Returns `NULL`; the **original block remains valid**.
- **Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (int *)realloc(ptr, 2 * n * sizeof(int));
```

### 2.2.4 `free()` — Deallocation
- **Signature:** `void free(void *ptr);`
- **Behavior:** Releases the memory pointed to by `ptr` back to the heap. The pointer becomes **dangling** (invalid for use).
- **Best Practice:** Always set `ptr = NULL;` after `free(ptr);`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Watch:** Memory leaks, dangling pointers, and double-free errors are the most common reason students lose marks in viva-voce and Part A questions. **Always free what you malloc!**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Memory Calculation Formulas

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Total bytes allocated by `malloc` | $\text{Total} = n \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ | $n$ = number of elements, $T$ = data type |
| Total bytes allocated by `calloc` | $\text{Total} = \text{num} \times \text{size}$ | Both arguments passed to `calloc` |
| Number of elements in block | $n = \dfrac{\text{Total Bytes}}{\text{sizeof}(T)}$ | Used to find array size from pointer |
| Heap growth direction | Typically **upward** (high to low addresses) | Architecture-dependent but conventionally upward |
| Stack growth direction | Typically **downward** (high to low addresses) | |
| Memory leak formula (conceptual) | $\text{Leaked} = \text{Allocated} - \text{Freed}$ | Bytes never returned to OS |
| Address arithmetic for pointer `p` | $p + k = \text{address of } p + k \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ | Pointer arithmetic |
| Effective address after `realloc` | $p_{\text{new}} = \text{could be same or new location}$ | Implementation-defined |

### Comparison Table: `malloc` vs `calloc` vs `realloc`

| Feature | `malloc()` | `calloc()` | `realloc()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of arguments | **1** | **2** | **2** |
| Initialization | **No (garbage)** | **Yes (zero)** | Preserves old data |
| Contiguous block | Yes | Yes (array form) | Yes |
| Return on failure | `NULL` | `NULL` | `NULL` (old block intact) |
| Typical use | Single block / custom struct | Arrays requiring zero-init | Resizing arrays |
| Header file | `<stdlib.h>` | `<stdlib.h>` | `<stdlib.h>` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of DMA:**
> - **Operating Systems:** Kernel memory allocators (`kmalloc`, `vmalloc` in Linux) use DMA principles.
> - **Databases:** Buffer pool management (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB) dynamically allocates pages.
> - **Compilers:** Symbol tables and AST nodes are allocated dynamically during parsing.
> - **Game Engines:** Real-time asset loading (textures, meshes) uses DMA.
> - **Web Servers (Nginx/Apache):** Per-connection memory pools.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Internal Mechanics of `malloc(n * sizeof(int))`

The function `malloc` performs the following logical steps at runtime:

**Step 1: Argument Validation**
The size argument is checked. If `size == 0`, behavior is implementation-defined (may return `NULL` or a unique pointer that can be passed to `free`).

**Step 2: Heap Walk**
The C runtime's memory manager maintains a free-list (or a more sophisticated structure like a *bin* in glibc's `ptmalloc2`). It searches for a contiguous free block of at least `size` bytes.

**Step 3: Block Splitting**
If a larger free block is found, it is split — the requested size is allocated, and the remainder stays in the free-list.

**Step 4: Metadata Update**
The allocator writes a hidden header (typically 8–16 bytes *before* the returned pointer) containing the block size and status flags for `free()` to use later.

**Step 5: Return Pointer**
The user receives a pointer to the *first usable byte* of the block. The total heap consumption is:

$$
\text{Heap Used} = \text{User Request} + \text{Metadata Overhead}
$$

> In glibc, metadata overhead is typically **16 bytes** (for 64-bit systems), so `malloc(1)` may actually consume 17–32 bytes internally.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation — KTU Standard Problem

> **Problem:** What is the total memory allocated on the heap by the following statement on a 64-bit system?
> ```c
> int *p = (int *)malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
> ```

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the size of `int` on the target system.

$$
\text{sizeof(int)} = 4 \text{ bytes (standard for GCC on 64-bit Linux)}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the requested allocation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Requested Size} &= 5 \times \text{sizeof(int)} \\
&= 5 \times 4 \\
&= 20 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for allocator metadata (glibc overhead ≈ **16 bytes**).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Heap Consumption} &= \text{User Request} + \text{Metadata} \\
&= 20 + 16 \\
&= 36 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Number of array elements that can be stored in the *user-visible* region.

$$
n = \frac{\text{User Bytes}}{\text{sizeof(int)}} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ elements}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "how much memory is allocated?", distinguish between **user-requested memory** (what the program can use) and **total heap memory** (including hidden metadata). Examiners often test this distinction.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Memory State Transitions

| State | `ptr` Value | Memory Block | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | Uninitialized (wild) | — | Declare `int *ptr;` |
| After `malloc` | Valid heap address | Allocated, uninitialized | `ptr = malloc(N * sizeof(int));` |
| After assignment | Valid | Used for data | `ptr[0] = 10;` |
| After `free` | **Dangling** (invalid) | Returned to heap | `free(ptr);` |
| After `ptr = NULL` | `NULL` (safe) | Heap may be reused | `ptr = NULL;` |
| After lost reference | Unreachable | **Memory leak** | `ptr` goes out of scope without `free` |

## 3.4 Reallocation Logic — `realloc` Internal Algorithm

When `realloc(ptr, new_size)` is called, the runtime executes the following decision tree:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{shrink in-place} & \text{if } \text{new\_size} < \text{old\_size} \text{ and block can be split} \\
\text{extend in-place} & \text{if adjacent memory is free and } \text{new\_size} > \text{old\_size} \\
\text{allocate new, copy, free old} & \text{if in-place extension fails}
\end{cases}
$$

**Mathematical model:** Let $S_{\text{old}}$ be the previous size and $S_{\text{new}}$ be the requested size. The number of bytes effectively copied is:

$$
\text{Bytes Copied} = \min(S_{\text{old}}, S_{\text{new}})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:**
> ```c
> ptr = realloc(ptr, new_size);  // ❌ DANGEROUS — if realloc fails, original is lost!
> ```
> **Correct pattern:**
> ```c
> int *temp = realloc(ptr, new_size);
> if (temp != NULL) {
>     ptr = temp;
> } else {
>     // handle failure, ptr still valid
> }
> ```

## 3.5 Memory Leak Derivation

Consider a program that performs $N$ allocations in a loop and frees only $N - k$ of them. The leaked memory is:

$$
L_{\text{leaked}} = \sum_{i \in \text{unfreed}} S_i
$$

where $S_i$ is the size of the $i$-th leaked block. For a loop of 1000 iterations each leaking 4 bytes:

$$
L_{\text{leaked}} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 \text{ bytes} = \approx 3.9 \text{ KB per execution}
$$

Repeated execution causes **memory exhaustion** and eventual `malloc` failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Address Space Layout (C Program Memory Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[High Address 0xFFFFFFFF] --> B[Kernel Space]
    B --> C[Stack Segment - grows downward]
    C --> D[Heap Segment - grows upward]
    D --> E[Uninitialized Data BSS]
    E --> F[Initialized Data]
    F --> G[Code Text Segment]
    G --> H[Low Address 0x00000000]

    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note:** The **Heap** is where all dynamically allocated memory resides. The **Stack** handles automatic variables. The **arrow direction** indicates growth direction during program execution.

## 4.2 Dynamic Memory Allocation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Program] --> B[Declare Pointer ptr]
    B --> C[Call malloc or calloc]
    C --> D{Allocation Successful?}
    D -->|Yes - ptr not NULL| E[Use Memory - Read Write Data]
    D -->|No - ptr equals NULL| F[Handle Error - Print Message and Exit]
    E --> G[Need More Memory?]
    G -->|Yes| H[Call realloc]
    H --> I{realloc Successful?}
    I -->|Yes| E
    I -->|No| F
    G -->|No| J[Call free]
    J --> K[Set ptr to NULL]
    K --> L[Continue Program or End]
    F --> L
    L --> M[End Program]

    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Heap Block Lifecycle — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Free Block in Heap Free-List] -->|malloc called| B[Allocated Block with Metadata Header]
    B -->|In use by program| C[Block Contains User Data]
    C -->|realloc called with larger size| D[New Block Copied to New Location]
    C -->|free called| E[Block Returned to Free-List]
    D --> E
    E --> A

    style A fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Memory Leak vs Proper Deallocation — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LEAKY[Memory Leak Scenario]
        A1[malloc 10 bytes] --> A2[Pointer goes out of scope]
        A2 --> A3[Block remains allocated - LEAK]
    end

    subgraph PROPER[Proper Deallocation]
        B1[malloc 10 bytes] --> B2[Use the memory]
        B2 --> B3[free the pointer]
        B3 --> B4[Set pointer to NULL]
    end

    style A3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between static and dynamic memory allocation. Mention any two situations where dynamic memory allocation is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Memory Allocation | Dynamic Memory Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Compile-time | Run-time |
| **Memory region** | Stack / Data segment | Heap |
| **Size** | Fixed, must be known at compile-time | Variable, decided at run-time |
| **Flexibility** | Cannot be resized | Can be resized using `realloc()` |
| **Deallocation** | Automatic (scope-based) | Manual using `free()` |
| **Speed** | Faster (no runtime overhead) | Slightly slower (heap management) |

**Two situations where DMA is preferred:**
1. When the size of the data structure is **not known in advance** (e.g., number of nodes in a linked list depends on user input).
2. When implementing **linked data structures** (linked lists, trees, graphs) where nodes are created and destroyed dynamically.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the difference between `malloc()` and `calloc()` with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `malloc()` | `calloc()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Arguments** | 1 (total bytes) | 2 (count, size-per-element) |
| **Initialization** | No (garbage values) | Yes (all bytes set to 0) |
| **Syntax** | `ptr = malloc(n * sizeof(int));` | `ptr = calloc(n, sizeof(int));` |

**Example:**
```c
int *a = (int *)malloc(5 * sizeof(int));  // 5 ints with garbage values
int *b = (int *)calloc(5, sizeof(int));   // 5 ints all initialized to 0
```
`malloc` is faster (no zeroing overhead) but unsafe; `calloc` is safer and gives a clean zero-initialized array.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the following dynamic memory allocation functions in C with their syntax and usage:**
**(i) `malloc()` (ii) `calloc()` (iii) `realloc()` (iv) `free()`**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) `malloc()` — Memory Allocation**
```c
#include <stdlib.h>
void *malloc(size_t size);
```
- Allocates a contiguous block of `size` bytes on the heap.
- Returns a `void*` to the start of the block, or `NULL` on failure.
