- Contents are **uninitialized** (garbage).
- **Usage:**
  ```c
  int *p = (int *)malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
  if (p == NULL) { printf("Allocation failed"); exit(1); }
  ```

**(ii) `calloc()` — Contiguous Allocation**
```c
void *calloc(size_t num, size_t size);
```
- Allocates memory for an array of `num` elements, each of `size` bytes.
- **Initializes** all bytes to zero.
- Returns `void*` or `NULL` on failure.
- **Usage:**
  ```c
  int *p = (int *)calloc(5, sizeof(int));  // 5 zero-initialized ints
  ```

**(iii) `realloc()` — Reallocation**
```c
void *realloc(void *ptr, size_t new_size);
```
- Changes the size of an already-allocated memory block.
- If the new size is larger and cannot be expanded in place, a new block is allocated, old contents are copied, and the old block is freed.
- Returns the new pointer (may differ from `ptr`) or `NULL` on failure.
- **Usage:**
  ```c
  p = (int *)realloc(p, 10 * sizeof(int));  // grow to 10 ints
  ```

**(iv) `free()` — Deallocation**
```c
void free(void *ptr);
```
- Releases the memory pointed to by `ptr` back to the heap.
- After `free`, the pointer becomes **dangling** — must not be dereferenced.
- **Best practice:** always set `ptr = NULL;` after `free`.
- **Usage:**
  ```c
  free(p);
  p = NULL;
  ```

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write a C program to dynamically allocate memory for an array of `n` integers, accept values from the user, find the sum and average, and then properly deallocate the memory using `free()`.**

**Model Solution:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    int n, i;
    int *arr;
    int sum = 0;
    float avg;

    printf("Enter number of elements: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    // Dynamic allocation using malloc
    arr = (int *)malloc(n * sizeof(int));
    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Memory allocation failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Accept values
    printf("Enter %d integers:\n", n);
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        scanf("%d", &arr[i]);
        sum += arr[i];
    }

    avg = (float)sum / n;
    printf("Sum = %d\n", sum);
    printf("Average = %.2f\n", avg);

    // Proper deallocation
    free(arr);
    arr = NULL;  // Avoid dangling pointer

    return 0;
}
```

**Output Trace (for n=4, input 10 20 30 40):**
```
Enter number of elements: 4
Enter 4 integers: 10 20 30 40
Sum = 100
Average = 25.00
```

**Memory Calculation:** `malloc(4 * sizeof(int))` = `4 * 4` = **16 bytes** requested on the heap.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is a memory leak? Explain with a suitable C code example. Also explain the concept of a dangling pointer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Memory Leak:**
A **memory leak** occurs when a program allocates memory on the heap but **fails to release it** back to the OS after use. The memory remains occupied and unrecoverable until the program terminates.

**Code Example Demonstrating Memory Leak:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

void leakyFunction() {
    int *p = (int *)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
    // p is local to this function
    // No free(p) before function returns!
    // The 40 bytes are lost forever (until program exits)
}

int main() {
    while (1) {
        leakyFunction();  // Repeatedly loses 40 bytes per call
    }
    return 0;
}
```
Eventually, `malloc` will fail and return `NULL` due to heap exhaustion.

**Dangling Pointer:**
A **dangling pointer** is a pointer that continues to reference a memory location **after that memory has been freed**. Dereferencing it causes *undefined behavior*.

**Code Example:**
```c
int *p = (int *)malloc(sizeof(int));
*p = 50;
free(p);          // Memory is released
printf("%d", *p); // ❌ UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR — p is dangling!
p = NULL;         // ✅ Safe practice
```

**Causes of Dangling Pointers:**
1. Deallocation of memory (`free`) without reassigning pointer.
2. Returning the address of a local variable from a function.
3. Variable going out of scope while a pointer to it still exists.

**Prevention:** Always set pointers to `NULL` immediately after `free()`.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Compare `malloc`, `calloc`, and `realloc` in detail. Write a C program to demonstrate the use of `realloc()` to dynamically grow an array.**

**Model Solution:**

**Detailed Comparison Table (see Section 2.3).**

**C Program Demonstrating `realloc`:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    int n1 = 3, n2 = 6, i;
    int *arr;

    // Initial allocation
    arr = (int *)malloc(n1 * sizeof(int));
    if (arr == NULL) return 1;

    printf("Initial %d elements:\n", n1);
    for (i = 0; i < n1; i++) {
        arr[i] = (i + 1) * 10;
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");

    // Reallocate to a larger size
    arr = (int *)realloc(arr, n2 * sizeof(int));
    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Reallocation failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize the new elements
    for (i = n1; i < n2; i++) {
        arr[i] = (i + 1) * 10;
    }

    printf("After realloc, %d elements:\n", n2);
    for (i = 0; i < n2; i++) {
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");

    free(arr);
    arr = NULL;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Initial 3 elements:
10 20 30
After realloc, 6 elements:
10 20 30 40 50 60
```

**Memory Trace:**
- Initial: `malloc(3 * 4) = 12` bytes
- After: `realloc(12 → 6 * 4 = 24)` bytes
- The first 12 bytes are preserved; the new 12 bytes contain garbage before assignment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to typecast the return of `malloc`/`calloc`:** In modern C, typecasting is optional but recommended in KTU answers. Missing it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Not checking for `NULL` return:** A major deduction (1–2 marks) in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Confusing `malloc(n * sizeof(T))` with `malloc(n) * sizeof(T)`:** Operator precedence — `sizeof` binds tighter than `*`. Always parenthesize!
> 4. **Forgetting `#include <stdlib.h>`:** Loss of 0.5 marks; sometimes code doesn't even compile.
> 5. **Writing `free(arr)` but not setting `arr = NULL`:** Loses marks on the "good programming practice" criterion.
> 6. **Using `realloc` unsafely** (overwriting original pointer on failure): The examiner will deduct for the unsafe pattern.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **DMA** stands for *Dynamic Memory Allocation* — memory assigned at **runtime** from the **heap**.
- [ ] **Four core functions:** `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free` — all declared in `<stdlib.h>`.
- [ ] `malloc(n)` → uninitialized, single block of `n` bytes.
- [ ] `calloc(count, size)` → zero-initialized, array of `count × size` bytes.
- [ ] `realloc(ptr, new_size)` → resizes existing block; may move data.
- [ ] `free(ptr)` → releases memory; **always** follow with `ptr = NULL`.
- [ ] **Memory leak** = allocated but never freed.
- [ ] **Dangling pointer** = points to freed memory.
- [ ] `malloc(5 * sizeof(int))` on 64-bit GCC allocates **20 user bytes** (+ ~16 bytes metadata).
- [ ] Pointer arithmetic: `ptr + k` advances by `k × sizeof(T)` bytes.
- [ ] Always **check for `NULL`** after every allocation call.
- [ ] DMA is the **backbone of all linked data structures** in upcoming modules.
- [ ] Standard sizes: `char=1`, `int=4`, `float=4`, `double=8`, `pointer=8` (64-bit).
- [ ] Heap grows **upward**, stack grows **downward** in typical implementations.
- [ ] KTU weightage: ~3 marks (Part A) + 7–14 marks (Part B) per exam cycle.

> **🎯 One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"**M**alloc is **M**essy, **C**alloc is **C**lean, **R**ealloc is **R**esizing, **F**ree is **F**arewell."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singly Linked List - Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Singly Linked List - Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a dynamic, linear data structure composed of a sequence of **nodes**, where each node contains two fields: a **DATA** field that stores the element value, and a **LINK / NEXT** field (a pointer) that stores the address of the immediate successor node. The list is accessed through an external **HEAD** pointer that points to the first node; the last node's link field contains **NULL** to mark the logical end of the list.

Mathematically, an SLL of *n* nodes can be represented as an ordered tuple:

$$
L = \langle n_1, n_2, n_3, \dots, n_k \rangle
$$

where each node $n_i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \dots, k-1$) satisfies the constraint $\text{NEXT}(n_i) = n_{i+1}$, and $\text{NEXT}(n_k) = \text{NULL}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** "A linked list is a linear collection of self-referential structures (nodes), connected by pointers, in which the order of elements is maintained by the explicit address stored in each node, **not** by contiguous physical memory placement."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train of Bogies" Intuition

Imagine a **treasure hunt** or a **mountain railway train**:

- The **engine (locomotive)** does not carry any cargo. It only knows where the **first bogie** is. This engine is the **HEAD pointer**.
- Each **bogie** (node) holds some **coal** (the DATA) and a **coupling hook** at its rear end (the NEXT pointer).
- The coupling hook of one bogie is connected to the bogie right behind it. The hook of the **last bogie** is left dangling in the air (the NULL link).
- You can **insert a new bogie** anywhere — at the front (before the first), in the middle (uncouple two bogies, slot in a new one), or at the end (attach at the tail).
- To **remove a bogie**, you simply unhook it from its neighbors and reconnect the surrounding bogies.
- Critically, the bogies are **not parked next to each other in a single line of land** (unlike an array). They are scattered across the yard; the **couplings alone define the order**.

This scattered-but-connected nature is the single most important conceptual leap: **logical order is determined by pointers, not by physical adjacency in memory.**

## 1.3 Node Structure (Standard C-style KTU Representation)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;           // The payload / value stored
    struct Node *next;  // Self-referential pointer to the next node
};
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Footprint of a Node (Typical 32-bit/64-bit System):**
> - `int data` → **4 bytes**
> - `struct Node *next` → **4 bytes (32-bit)** or **8 bytes (64-bit)**
> - **Total per node ≈ 8 or 16 bytes**, *excluding* heap allocator overhead. This is the trade-off you pay for dynamic resizing.

## 1.4 Why Singly Linked Lists? — KTU Motivation

| Limitation of Static Arrays | How SLL Solves It |
|---|---|
| Fixed size decided at compile time | SLL grows/shrinks at runtime via `malloc` / `free` |
| Insertion/Deletion at front = $O(n)$ shift cost | Insertion at front = $O(1)$ |
| Wastes memory if under-filled | Allocates exactly what is needed |
| Cannot be split or merged easily | Pointer manipulation enables efficient splits/merges |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says *"dynamic linear structure with efficient insertions/deletions"*, your first answer should be **Singly Linked List** (or **Doubly Linked List** if bidirectional traversal is required).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Operations — The Five Primitive Operations

Every higher-level operation on a Singly Linked List is built from combinations of these **five primitive pointer manipulations**. Mastering them individually is the key to KTU board examination success.

### Primitive 1: **Traversal**
- Walk from `HEAD` to `NULL`, visiting each node exactly once.
- Requires a temporary **trailing pointer** (often called `current`, `ptr`, or `temp`).
- *Why needed:* The only way to "see" a node's value is to follow its `next` link.

### Primitive 2: **Insertion** (Three Variants)
- **At the Beginning** — Modify the `HEAD` pointer.
- **At the End** — Traverse to the last node, then link the new node.
- **At a Given Position** — Traverse to position $p-1$, rewire pointers.

### Primitive 3: **Deletion** (Three Variants)
- **From the Beginning** — Move `HEAD` to `HEAD->next`, free old head.
- **From the End** — Traverse to the second-last node, set its `next = NULL`, free last.
- **At a Given Position / Key** — Find the predecessor of the target, bypass the target, free it.

### Primitive 4: **Searching**
- Linear scan comparing `current->data` with the target key.
- Returns pointer to the node (or its predecessor) if found; `NULL` otherwise.

### Primitive 5: **Counting / Length**
- Increment a counter on every successful traversal step.

## 2.2 The Universal "Rewire Pattern"

Every insertion/deletion follows a strict **two-pointer rewire protocol** to avoid losing the chain. In a Singly Linked List (only forward link available), you must always keep track of the **predecessor (prev)** when modifying any node other than the head.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Time Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Traversal** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Must visit all nodes |
| **Insert at Beginning** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Direct HEAD manipulation |
| **Insert at End** | $O(1)$* | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | *If **tail pointer** maintained |
| **Insert at Position $p$** | $O(1)$ | $O(p)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Worst: insert at tail |
| **Delete from Beginning** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Direct HEAD manipulation |
| **Delete from End** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | No backward link in SLL |
| **Delete at Position $p$** | $O(1)$ | $O(p)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Need predecessor |
| **Search (by value)** | $O(1)$ | $O(n/2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Target may be at end |
| **Get Length** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | No size field by default |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** $O(1)$ means **constant time** — independent of list size $n$. $O(n)$ means **linear time** — proportional to the number of elements.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Singly linked lists underpin the **free-list** in memory managers and the **process scheduling run-queue** in early Unix kernels.
- **Embedded Systems:** SLLs (often **circular** SLLs) implement round-robin task schedulers in microcontrollers because of their $O(1)$ insertion and zero memory waste.
- **Symbol Tables (Compilers):** Linked-list chaining is the classical resolution strategy for **hash collisions**.
- **Undo/Redo Stacks (Lightweight):** Editor action history can be modeled as a singly linked list of delta operations.
- **LRU Caches (Basic Variant):** A doubly linked list is preferred, but the foundational SLL logic — pointer rewiring — is the same.

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions Every KTU Paper Tests

| Scenario | Required Handling |
|---|---|
| List is **empty** (`HEAD == NULL`) | Insert at beginning = create first node; Delete = print "Underflow" |
| Only **one node** exists | Delete must set `HEAD = NULL` |
| Target node is the **head** | Bypass the special case using a common "find predecessor" routine |
| Target node is the **last** | Traverse till `current->next == NULL`; require predecessor |
| **Position $p$** is invalid ($p < 1$ or $p > n+1$) | Print "Invalid Position" |
| Key not found in deletion/search | Print "Element not found" and return original `HEAD` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Trend:** A large fraction of part-(a) marks (typically 2 out of 7) are reserved *purely* for handling these edge cases. Skipping the empty-list check is the #1 reason students lose marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Operational Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Universal Singly Linked List Skeleton (Reference for All Operations)

Below is the canonical structure assumed in **every KTU board question** on this topic. Memorize it.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};

struct Node *head = NULL;  // Global head pointer

// Utility: Create a new node on the heap
struct Node* createNode(int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    return newNode;
}
```

## 3.2 OPERATION 1 — Insertion at the Beginning (Derivation)

### Goal
Insert a new node containing value $x$ as the **first element** of the list.

### Logical Sequence
1. Allocate a new node `newNode` with `data = x`.
2. Set `newNode->next = head`.  *(This attaches the old chain behind the new node.)*
3. Update `head = newNode`.  *(This makes the new node the official first node.)*

### Critical "Why" Explanation
- The order of steps is **non-negotiable**. If you overwrite `head` *before* storing its value in `newNode->next`, you **permanently lose the entire list** — a classic KTU question trick.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void insertAtBeginning(int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = createNode(value);
    newNode->next = head;  // Step 1: chain old list after newNode
    head = newNode;        // Step 2: update HEAD
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{insertBeg}}(n) = T_{\text{malloc}}(1) + T_{\text{assignment}}(1) + T_{\text{update}}(1) = O(1)
$$

## 3.3 OPERATION 2 — Insertion at the End (Derivation)

### Goal
Append a new node containing $x$ as the **last element** of the list.

### Logical Sequence
1. Allocate `newNode` with `data = x`.
2. **Special Case:** If list is empty, set `head = newNode` and return.
3. Otherwise, traverse using a `temp` pointer until `temp->next == NULL`.
4. Set `temp->next = newNode`.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void insertAtEnd(int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = createNode(value);
    if (head == NULL) {            // Edge case: empty list
        head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = head;
    while (temp->next != NULL) {   // Walk to last node
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    temp->next = newNode;          // Attach at tail
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{insertEnd}}(n) = T_{\text{malloc}}(1) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{step}}(1) = O(n)
$$

In the worst case, the loop iterates over all $n$ nodes to reach the tail.

## 3.4 OPERATION 3 — Insertion at a Given Position $p$ (Derivation)

### Goal
Insert $x$ such that it becomes the **$p$-th node** in the list (1-indexed). Valid range: $1 \le p \le n+1$.

### Logical Sequence
1. If $p = 1$, perform `insertAtBeginning` and return.
2. Allocate `newNode` with `data = x`.
3. Traverse using `temp` to reach the node at position $p-1$ (the **predecessor**).
4. If $p-1$-th node does not exist, print "Invalid Position" and abort.
5. Set `newNode->next = temp->next`.
6. Set `temp->next = newNode`.

### Why Two Pointer Manipulations?
Because SLL is **unidirectional**, you must splice the new node **between** `temp` and `temp->next`. The order `newNode->next = temp->next` *first* is critical — flipping the order again destroys the chain.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void insertAtPosition(int value, int p) {
    if (p < 1) { printf("Invalid Position\n"); return; }
    if (p == 1) { insertAtBeginning(value); return; }

    struct Node *newNode = createNode(value);
    struct Node *temp = head;

    for (int i = 1; i < p - 1 && temp != NULL; i++) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }

    if (temp == NULL) {
        printf("Invalid Position\n");
        free(newNode);
        return;
    }

    newNode->next = temp->next;  // Step A: link forward
    temp->next = newNode;        // Step B: link backward
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{insertPos}}(n, p) = T_{\text{malloc}}(1) + (p-2) \cdot T_{\text{loop}} + T_{\text{rewire}}(1)
$$

Best case ($p = 2$): $O(1)$. Worst case ($p = n+1$): $O(n)$.

## 3.5 OPERATION 4 — Deletion from the Beginning (Derivation)

### Logical Sequence
1. If `head == NULL`, print "List is Empty / Underflow" and return.
2. Store `head` in a temporary `toDelete`.
3. Move `head = head->next`.
4. `free(toDelete)`.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void deleteFromBeginning() {
    if (head == NULL) {
        printf("Underflow: List is empty\n");
        return;
    }
    struct Node *toDelete = head;
    head = head->next;     // Advance HEAD
    free(toDelete);        // Release old node
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{delBeg}}(n) = O(1)
$$

## 3.6 OPERATION 5 — Deletion from the End (Derivation)

### Logical Sequence
1. If `head == NULL` → underflow.
2. If `head->next == NULL` (single node) → free `head`, set `head = NULL`, return.
3. Otherwise, traverse with `temp` until `temp->next->next == NULL` (stop at the **second-last** node).
4. Save `last = temp->next`.
5. Set `temp->next = NULL`.
6. `free(last)`.

### Why Stop at the Second-Last?
The SLL has no backward link. To safely sever the tail, you must physically reach the node **before** the tail and rewrite its `next` pointer to `NULL`. Hence the two-step lookahead: `temp->next->next`.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void deleteFromEnd() {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("Underflow\n"); return; }

    if (head->next == NULL) {  // Only one node
        free(head);
        head = NULL;
        return;
    }

    struct Node *temp = head;
    while (temp->next->next != NULL) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    free(temp->next);
    temp->next = NULL;
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{delEnd}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-2} T_{\text{step}}(1) + T_{\text{free}}(1) = O(n)
$$

## 3.7 OPERATION 6 — Deletion by Value (Key-Based) (Derivation)

### Logical Sequence
1. If `head == NULL` → not found.
2. If `head->data == key` → perform delete-from-beginning.
3. Otherwise, traverse with `prev` and `current` until `current->data == key` or `current == NULL`.
4. If not found, print "Element not found".
5. Else: `prev->next = current->next` (bypass the target).
6. `free(current)`.

### Two-Pointer (prev + current) Pattern Justification
Because SLL has no backward link, deleting `current` requires that **its predecessor** rewires its `next` to skip over `current`. Hence the necessity of the `prev` pointer.

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void deleteByValue(int key) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List empty\n"); return; }

    if (head->data == key) {
        struct Node *toDelete = head;
        head = head->next;
        free(toDelete);
        return;
    }

    struct Node *prev = head, *current = head->next;
    while (current != NULL && current->data != key) {
        prev = current;
        current = current->next;
    }

    if (current == NULL) {
        printf("Element not found\n");
        return;
    }

    prev->next = current->next;  // Bypass the target node
    free(current);
}
```

### Complexity Derivation
$$
T_{\text{delKey}}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(scanning the entire list in the worst case)}
$$

## 3.8 OPERATION 7 — Traversal and Display (Derivation)

### C-Style Implementation
```c
void display() {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return; }
    struct Node *temp = head;
    while (temp != NULL) {
        printf("%d -> ", temp->data);
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    printf("NULL\n");
}
```

## 3.9 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why SLL Beats Array (and Where It Loses)

| Property | Static Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Layout | Contiguous | Scattered (heap-allocated) |
| Access $i$-th element | $O(1)$ (random) | $O(i)$ (sequential) |
| Insert at front | $O(n)$ (shift) | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at back | $O(1)$ (amortized) | $O(n)$ without tail pointer |
| Delete at front | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Memory overhead | None | Extra pointer per node (~4–8 B) |
| Resize | Impossible (fixed) | Trivial (just allocate) |
| Cache locality | Excellent | Poor (pointer chasing) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Basic Node Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["data: 10 | next: --->"] --> node2["data: 20 | next: --->"]
    node2 --> node3["data: 30 | next: NULL"]
    head["HEAD (External Pointer)"] -.-> node1
```

**Interpretation:** The `HEAD` pointer is an *external* variable. The dashed line `-.->` indicates that HEAD does not live inside a node; it lives in the calling code's stack frame or as a global. The solid arrows are the `next` pointers stored *inside* each node.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Insertion at the Beginning (Before/After)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        headB["HEAD"] --> nodeB1["10 ->"] --> nodeB2["20 ->"] --> nodeB3["NULL"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        headA["HEAD"] --> nodeA0["5 (NEW) ->"] --> nodeA1["10 ->"] --> nodeA2["20 ->"] --> nodeA3["NULL"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice that the old list `[10, 20, NULL]` is preserved *in its entirety*. The new node `[5]` is prepended. The order of operations in the algorithm ensures `5->next` captures the old `HEAD` *before* `HEAD` is overwritten.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Insertion at Position $p = 3$ (Rewire Detail)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        headB["HEAD"] --> nb1["10 ->"] --> nb2["20 ->"] --> nb3["30 ->"] --> nb4["40 -> NULL"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        headA["HEAD"] --> na1["10 ->"] --> na2["20 ->"] --> naNEW["25 (NEW) ->"] --> na3["30 ->"] --> na4["40 -> NULL"]
    end
```

**Analytical Insight:** To insert `25` at position 3, the algorithm traverses to position $p-1 = 2$ (the node containing `20`). The rewire is:
- `newNode->next = temp->next;`  (newNode points to the old 30)
- `temp->next = newNode;`        (20 now points to newNode)

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Deletion of Node with Value `20` (Bypass)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        headB["HEAD"] --> db1["10 ->"] --> dbT["20 (TO DELETE) ->"] --> db3["30 ->"] --> db4["NULL"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        headA["HEAD"] --> da1["10 --->"] --> da3["30 ->"] --> da4["NULL"]
        freeNote["(Memory at 20 is freed)"]
    end
```

**Key Visual:** The predecessor node (containing `10`) is rewired to point directly to `30`, **bypassing** the node that held `20`. The orphaned node is then released back to the heap via `free()`. This is the universal "bypass" pattern.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Deletion from the End (Two-Step Lookahead)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        headB["HEAD"] --> eb1["10 ->"] --> eb2["20 ->"] --> eb3["30 (TAIL) -> NULL"]
        tempPtr["temp (stops here)"] -.-> eb2
    end
    subgraph AFTER
        headA["HEAD"] --> ea1["10 ->"] --> ea2["20 -> NULL"]
        freeNode["(Memory at 30 is freed)"]
    end
```

**Critical Reading:** The traversal condition `temp->next->next != NULL` causes the loop to halt at the **second-last** node (`20`). This is the only way to manipulate the tail in an SLL, because we cannot walk backward.

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of SLL Operations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["User Call: insert/delete/display"] --> B{"List Empty?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Print Underflow / Set HEAD to newNode"]
    B -- No --> D{"Operation Type"}
    D -- Insert Beginning --> E["Create Node, HEAD = newNode, newNode->next = old HEAD"]
    D -- Insert End --> F["Traverse to tail, attach newNode"]
    D -- Insert Position p --> G["Traverse to p-1, rewire two pointers"]
    D -- Delete Beginning --> H["HEAD = HEAD->next, free old HEAD"]
    D -- Delete End --> I["Traverse to second-last, sever tail, free"]
    D -- Delete by Key --> J["Two-pointer scan, bypass target, free"]
    D -- Display --> K["Single-pointer linear walk to NULL"]
```

**Why a Functional Flow:** This block diagram abstracts the **decision logic** that all SLL operations share. Every operation funnels through the **"Is list empty?"** check first, then branches based on operation type. This is the exact pattern KTU expects you to describe verbally in theory questions.
