# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Singly Linked List and a one-dimensional array with respect to memory allocation, access time, and insertion/deletion complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Contiguous, fixed size | Non-contiguous, dynamic |
| Access | $O(1)$ (random) | $O(n)$ (sequential) |
| Insert at front | $O(n)$ (shift) | $O(1)$ (rewire HEAD) |
| Delete at front | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Memory overhead | None | Extra pointer (~4–8 B/node) |

*(1 mark for memory, 1 mark for access time, 1 mark for insertion/deletion complexity.)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a self-referential structure? Write its declaration in C.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **self-referential structure** is a structure that contains a pointer member which points to a structure of the *same type*. It is the foundation of every linked data structure because it enables one node to "know" the address of another node of the same kind. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct C declaration.)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;   // Pointer to another struct Node
};
```

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme)
Per the latest KTU ESE regulation, every Part B question offers a choice between two questions. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Write a C program to create a singly linked list of *n* integer elements. Provide functions to:
   - (i) Insert a new node at the **beginning** of the list. **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) Insert a new node at the **end** of the list. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Given a singly linked list `L = 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`, perform the following sequence of operations and write the state of the list after each:
   - (i) Insert 5 at the beginning. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) Insert 50 at the end. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Delete the node with value 30. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) (i) Insert at Beginning (4 marks):**

```c
void insertAtBeginning(int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = head;  // Old chain follows new node
    head = newNode;        // New node becomes HEAD
}
```

**Explanation for valuation:**
- `malloc` allocates dynamic memory → (1 mark)
- `newNode->next = head` preserves the chain → (2 marks)
- `head = newNode` updates the entry point → (1 mark)

**(a) (ii) Insert at End (3 marks):**

```c
void insertAtEnd(int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    if (head == NULL) {            // Edge case
        head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = head;
    while (temp->next != NULL) {   // Traverse to last node
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    temp->next = newNode;
}
```

**Explanation for valuation:**
- Empty-list edge case handling → (1 mark)
- Correct traversal loop to the tail → (1 mark)
- Final attachment `temp->next = newNode` → (1 mark)

**(b) Trace the operations:**

Starting list: `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

**(i)** Insert 5 at the beginning:
- `newNode->next` captures the old HEAD (`10`).
- `HEAD` is updated to `newNode`.
- **Result:** `5 → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL` ✅

**(ii)** Insert 50 at the end:
- Traverse from `HEAD` to the last node (`40`).
- Set `40->next = newNode`.
- **Result:** `5 → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50 → NULL` ✅

**(iii)** Delete the node with value `30`:
- `prev` traverses until `current->data == 30`.
- `prev` is the node holding `20`.
- Rewire: `prev->next = current->next` (i.e., `20->next = 40`).
- Free the node holding `30`.
- **Result:** `5 → 10 → 20 → 40 → 50 → NULL` ✅

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the process of **inserting a node at a given position** in a singly linked list. Write the corresponding C function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **deletion of the last node** from a singly linked list. Why can this not be done in $O(1)$ time? Provide the C code and trace it for the list `7 → 14 → 21 → 28 → NULL`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Insertion at a given position — Diagram + Code (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        B1["HEAD"] --> B2["10 ->"] --> B3["20 ->"] --> B4["30 -> NULL"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER (Insert 25 at position 3)
        A1["HEAD"] --> A2["10 ->"] --> A3["20 ->"] --> AN["25 (NEW) ->"] --> A4["30 -> NULL"]
    end
```

**C Function (4 marks):**

```c
void insertAtPosition(int value, int p) {
    if (p < 1) { printf("Invalid Position\n"); return; }
    if (p == 1) { insertAtBeginning(value); return; }

    struct Node *newNode = createNode(value);
    struct Node *temp = head;
    for (int i = 1; i < p - 1 && temp != NULL; i++) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }

    if (temp == NULL) {
        printf("Invalid Position\n");
        free(newNode);
        return;
    }

    newNode->next = temp->next;  // (1) Forward link
    temp->next = newNode;        // (2) Backward link
}
```

**Key conceptual points (for full marks):**
- The rewire order: `newNode->next = temp->next` must come **first**; otherwise, the chain after `temp` is lost.
- Edge case: $p = 1$ reduces to beginning insertion.
- Edge case: $p > n+1$ triggers an "Invalid Position" error and prevents memory leak via `free(newNode)`.

**(b) Deletion of the Last Node (7 marks):**

**Why $O(1)$ is impossible (2 marks):** A singly linked list node has **no backward pointer** (no `prev` field). To sever the last node, the predecessor (second-last node) must be located. The only way to reach the predecessor is to **walk forward from the HEAD**, which takes $O(n)$ time in the worst case. A doubly linked list, which has a `prev` pointer, can perform this in $O(1)$.

**C Code (3 marks):**

```c
void deleteFromEnd() {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("Underflow\n"); return; }
    if (head->next == NULL) {  // Single node case
        free(head);
        head = NULL;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = head;
    while (temp->next->next != NULL) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    free(temp->next);
    temp->next = NULL;
}
```

**Trace for `7 → 14 → 21 → 28 → NULL` (2 marks):**
- Initial state: `temp` points to `7`. Loop condition `temp->next->next != NULL` is `7->next->next = 21 != NULL`, so advance.
- `temp` now points to `14`. Check: `14->next->next = NULL`. Loop exits.
- `free(temp->next)` releases the node containing `28`.
- `temp->next = NULL` severs the link.
- **Final list:** `7 → 14 → 21 → NULL` ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Before Writing Your Exam)**
> 1. **Missing the empty-list check:** Nearly 40% of students forget to verify `head == NULL` before insertion/deletion. This single omission costs **2 full marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Wrong pointer rewire order:** In insertion, writing `temp->next = newNode` *before* `newNode->next = temp->next` severs the chain. The "forward link first, backward link second" rule is inviolable.
> 3. **Memory leak on invalid position:** In `insertAtPosition`, if position $p$ is out of range, you **must** call `free(newNode)` before returning. KTU examiners are explicitly trained to check for this.
> 4. **Forgetting the single-node edge case in delete-from-end:** A naive `while(temp->next != NULL)` will dereference `NULL` when the list has only one node. This is a classic **runtime error** that gets zero marks.
> 5. **Confusing Singly with Doubly Linked List:** When the question says *SLL*, you must **not** introduce a `prev` pointer. Marks are deducted for using out-of-scope data members.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ A **Singly Linked List** is a chain of nodes where each node stores **DATA + a single NEXT pointer**.
- ☐ The list is accessed via an **external HEAD pointer**; the last node's `next` is **NULL**.
- ☐ **Insertion at beginning** is $O(1)$. Always set `newNode->next = head` **before** `head = newNode`.
- ☐ **Insertion at end** is $O(n)$ (or $O(1)$ with a tail pointer). Always handle the empty-list case separately.
- ☐ **Insertion at position $p$** requires traversing to position $p-1$ and then performing a **two-pointer rewire**.
- ☐ **Deletion from beginning** is $O(1)$. Save the old HEAD, advance HEAD, then `free` the saved pointer.
- ☐ **Deletion from end** is $O(n)$ because SLL has no backward link. Stop the traversal at the **second-last** node using the `temp->next->next != NULL` condition.
- ☐ **Deletion by value** uses a **two-pointer (`prev` + `current`)** pattern; the predecessor bypasses the target.
- ☐ **Empty list, single node, and invalid position** are the **three edge cases** you must always check.
- ☐ Array vs SLL: Arrays win on **random access** and **cache locality**; SLLs win on **dynamic size** and **front-end mutations**.
- ☐ A SLL node occupies **8 bytes (32-bit)** or **16 bytes (64-bit)** — the pointer overhead is the price of dynamism.
- ☐ The `free()` call is **non-optional**: every dynamically allocated node must be released exactly once to prevent memory leaks.
- ☐ In KTU examinations, **state diagrams before/after each operation** earn full conceptual marks even if code has a minor bug.
- ☐ Remember: **logical order is determined by pointers, not by physical memory address.** This is the philosophical core of linked data structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Doubly Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a sequential, linear data structure composed of a collection of **nodes** where each node is a composite data element containing three distinct fields: a **data field** to store the payload value, a **forward pointer (`next`)** that references the succeeding node in the sequence, and a **backward pointer (`prev`)** that references the preceding node in the sequence. This bidirectional linkage enables traversal of the list in **both forward and reverse directions**, which is the defining structural property of a Doubly Linked List as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Data Structures and Algorithms*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Doubly Linked List node is the **atomic unit** of the data structure. The presence of *two* pointer fields (next and prev) per node is the strict departure from the Singly Linked List (SLL), which contains only *one* pointer field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions (must be memorized for KTU board exams):**
> - The `prev` pointer of the **head (first) node** is always `NULL`.
> - The `next` pointer of the **tail (last) node** is always `NULL` (for Linear DLL).
> - A list containing exactly one node has both `head->prev = NULL` and `head->next = NULL`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train with a corridor running through every coach** where each coach is connected to its neighbor on *both* sides.

- A **Singly Linked List** is like a one-way train corridor — passengers can only move **forward** from the engine to the last coach; to go back, they must walk the entire length again or get off and run around the train.
- A **Doubly Linked List** is a train where you can walk from **any coach to the coach immediately before it or after it directly**, because each coach is physically connected to both its predecessor and its successor. This is the power of having **two pointers per node**.

Another simple analogy: consider the **"Previous" and "Next" buttons on a music playlist app** (like Spotify). When you press "Next," you go to the next song (forward traversal via `next` pointer), and when you press "Previous," you go back to the previous song (backward traversal via `prev` pointer). Each song in the playlist behaves exactly like a node in a Doubly Linked List.

## 1.3 Variants of Doubly Linked List (KTU Examinable)

| Variant | Property |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Linear Doubly Linked List** | `head->prev = NULL` and `tail->next = NULL`. Traversal is strictly bounded. |
| **Circular Doubly Linked List** | `head->prev` points to the **tail**, and `tail->next` points to the **head**. Forms a closed bidirectional ring. |
| **Header-Based DLL** | An extra **dummy/sentinel header node** is placed at the start to simplify edge cases. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Node

Every node in a Doubly Linked List is typically declared using a `struct` (in C/C++), and contains **three mandatory components**:

1. **`data`** — Stores the actual user value (integer, float, character, or a pointer to a complex object).
2. **`next`** — Pointer to the *next* node of type same as the node itself.
3. **`prev`** — Pointer to the *previous* node of type same as the node itself.

The size in memory for a single DLL node (assuming 4-byte integers and 8-byte pointers on a 64-bit system) is:

$$S_{\text{node}} = S_{\text{data}} + S_{\text{next}} + S_{\text{prev}}$$

For a DLL with `n` nodes, the **total memory overhead** becomes:

$$M_{\text{total}} = n \times S_{\text{node}} + S_{\text{head}}$$

where $S_{\text{head}}$ is the storage for the external head pointer (usually 8 bytes on a 64-bit system).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

### A. Why use a Doubly Linked List over a Singly Linked List?
- **Bidirectional traversal** — Enables backward navigation without a stack or auxiliary structure.
- **O(1) deletion of a given node** — In a SLL, deletion of an arbitrary node requires its predecessor, which takes O(n) traversal. In a DLL, the predecessor is *directly available* via the `prev` pointer, making deletion **O(1)** if the node pointer itself is given.

### B. How is insertion/deletion performed safely?
Every operation must follow a **4-link reattachment protocol** to avoid orphaning sublists. The general rule is: **always modify the `next` and `prev` pointers of the affected neighboring nodes first, then connect the new node** (for insertion) or **disconnect first, then re-link neighbors** (for deletion).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — questions on complexity analysis of DLL operations are frequently asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Insertion at Head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | No traversal required. |
| **Insertion at Tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Requires traversal to last node (Linear DLL). |
| **Insertion at Position $k$** | $O(k)$ worst-case $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Requires traversal to $(k-1)^{\text{th}}$ node. |
| **Deletion at Head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Direct update of head pointer. |
| **Deletion at Tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Requires traversal to last node. |
| **Deletion at Position $k$** | $O(k)$ worst-case $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Predecessor is directly accessible. |
| **Forward Traversal** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Follow `next` pointers. |
| **Backward Traversal** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Follow `prev` pointers. |
| **Searching an element** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Linear search in either direction. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Pointer Arithmetic for Indexing:** If a DLL of $n$ nodes is indexed from 0 to $n-1$ and we want to access the $k^{\text{th}}$ node optimally, the **optimal traversal direction** can be chosen using the rule: traverse from **head** if $k < n/2$, and from **tail** if $k \geq n/2$. This is a classic KTU problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Doubly Linked Lists are used extensively in:
- **LRU Cache Implementation** (Least Recently Used caching in Operating Systems and Databases).
- **Browser History Navigation** — The "Back" and "Forward" buttons are modeled as DLLs of web pages.
- **Undo/Redo Functionalities** in text editors (Microsoft Word, VS Code, Photoshop).
- **Music Player Playlists** with "Previous Track" functionality.
- **Polynomial Representation** in scientific computing where both forward and reverse coefficient traversal is needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Insertion Algorithms & Deletion Algorithms

## 3.1 Generalized Pointer Reattachment Derivation

Consider three consecutive nodes in a Doubly Linked List: $P \leftrightarrow C \leftrightarrow N$, where $P$ is the **predecessor**, $C$ is the **current node**, and $N$ is the **successor**. The pointer state is:

$$P_{\text{next}} = C, \quad C_{\text{prev}} = P, \quad C_{\text{next}} = N, \quad N_{\text{prev}} = C$$

### Derivation of the "Insert a New Node `X` between `P` and `C`" Operation

**Step 1:** Set the forward pointer of the new node `X` to point to `C`.

$$X_{\text{next}} = C$$

**Step 2:** Set the backward pointer of the new node `X` to point to `P`.

$$X_{\text{prev}} = P$$

**Step 3:** Update the backward pointer of `C` to point to `X` (instead of `P`).

$$C_{\text{prev}} = X$$

**Step 4:** Update the forward pointer of `P` to point to `X` (instead of `C`).

$$P_{\text{next}} = X$$

**Resulting state after insertion:** $P \leftrightarrow X \leftrightarrow C \leftrightarrow N$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:** If we update $P_{\text{next}}$ or $C_{\text{prev}}$ *first*, we lose the reference to the other node and cannot complete the reattachment. The **golden rule** is: **always set the pointers of the new node first**, then update the neighbors.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Algorithm: Insertion at the Beginning of a DLL

**Input:** Head pointer `head` of an existing DLL (may be `NULL`), data value `val` to insert.
**Output:** Modified DLL with new node as head.

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** Dynamically allocate memory for a new node `newNode`. Set its data field.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} = val$$

**Step 2:** Initialize the pointers of the new node to safe states.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL}$$

**Step 3:** Link the new node's forward pointer to the current head.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$$

**Step 4:** Check if the list is non-empty. If `head` is not `NULL`, update the current head's backward pointer.

$$\text{if } (\text{head} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{newNode}$$

**Step 5:** Update the external head pointer to point to the new node.

$$\text{head} = \text{newNode}$$

**Final pointer state:**

$$\text{head} = \text{newNode}, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{oldHead}$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:** The algorithm performs a **constant number of pointer manipulations (5 steps)** independent of the list size $n$.

$$T(n) = O(1)$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Algorithm: Insertion at the End of a DLL

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** Allocate `newNode` and assign data.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} = val, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{NULL}$$

**Step 2:** Handle the **empty list edge case** — if `head == NULL`, the new node becomes the head with both pointers as NULL.

$$\text{if } (\text{head} == \text{NULL}) \implies \text{head} = \text{newNode}, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{return}$$

**Step 3:** Traverse from `head` to the last node using a temporary pointer `temp`. Continue while `temp->next != NULL`.

$$\text{while } (\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 4:** Link the last node's `next` pointer to the new node.

$$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{newNode}$$

**Step 5:** Set the new node's `prev` pointer to the old last node.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{temp}$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:** The traversal step visits up to $n$ nodes in the worst case (when inserting into a list of $n$ nodes). The remaining operations are $O(1)$.

$$T(n) = O(n) \text{ (dominant term is the traversal)}$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Algorithm: Insertion at a Given Position `pos`

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** If `pos == 1`, call the *Insertion at Beginning* algorithm and return.

**Step 2:** Allocate `newNode`. Initialize pointers to NULL.

**Step 3:** Traverse to the node at position `pos - 1` using `temp`. Start counter `i = 1`.

$$\text{while } (i < \text{pos} - 1 \ \text{AND} \ \text{temp} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}, \quad i = i + 1$$

**Step 4:** Validate that `temp` is not NULL (i.e., position is within bounds).

$$\text{if } (\text{temp} == \text{NULL}) \implies \text{return ERROR (position out of bounds)}$$

**Step 5:** Link new node's `next` to temp's current `next`.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 6:** Link new node's `prev` to `temp`.

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{temp}$$

**Step 7:** If temp's next is not NULL, update that node's `prev` pointer.

$$\text{if } (\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{newNode}$$

**Step 8:** Update temp's next pointer to the new node.

$$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{newNode}$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$T(n) = O(\text{pos}) \text{ in the best case, } O(n) \text{ in the worst case}$$

## 3.5 Exhaustive Algorithm: Deletion at the Beginning

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** If `head == NULL`, the list is empty — no deletion possible. Return.

**Step 2:** Store the current head in a temporary pointer `temp`.

$$\text{temp} = \text{head}$$

**Step 3:** Move the head pointer to the next node.

$$\text{head} = \text{head} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 4:** If the new head is not NULL, update its `prev` pointer to NULL.

$$\text{if } (\text{head} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL}$$

**Step 5:** Free the memory of the old head node `temp`.

$$\text{free}(\text{temp})$$

**Time Complexity:**

$$T(n) = O(1)$$

## 3.6 Exhaustive Algorithm: Deletion at the End

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** If `head == NULL`, return (empty list).

**Step 2:** If `head->next == NULL` (single-node list), free head and set head to NULL.

$$\text{if } (\text{head} \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{NULL}) \implies \text{free}(\text{head}), \quad \text{head} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{return}$$

**Step 3:** Traverse to the last node `last` using `temp`. Optionally, also maintain `prevNode`.

$$\text{while } (\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 4:** Update the second-to-last node's `next` pointer to NULL.

$$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{prev} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{NULL}$$

**Step 5:** Free the last node.

$$\text{free}(\text{temp})$$

**Time Complexity:**

$$T(n) = O(n)$$

## 3.7 Exhaustive Algorithm: Deletion at a Given Position `pos`

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 1:** If `head == NULL` or `pos == 1`, handle as *Deletion at Beginning* and return.

**Step 2:** Traverse to the node at position `pos` using `temp` and counter `i = 1`.

$$\text{while } (i < \text{pos} \ \text{AND} \ \text{temp} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}, \quad i = i + 1$$

**Step 3:** Validate bounds.

$$\text{if } (\text{temp} == \text{NULL}) \implies \text{return ERROR}$$

**Step 4:** Link temp's predecessor to temp's successor.

$$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{prev} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 5:** If temp's successor is not NULL, update its `prev` pointer.

$$\text{if } (\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{prev}$$

**Step 6:** Free the deleted node.

$$\text{free}(\text{temp})$$

**Time Complexity:**

$$T(n) = O(n) \text{ in worst case}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Anatomy of a Doubly Linked List Node and a Linear DLL

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["NULL PREV POINTER"] --> B["HEAD NODE 10 PREV NULL NEXT NODE20"]
    B --> C["NODE 20 PREV NODE10 NEXT NODE30"]
    C --> D["NODE 30 PREV NODE20 NEXT NODE40"]
    D --> E["NODE 40 PREV NODE30 NEXT NULL TAIL"]
    E --> F["NULL NEXT POINTER"]
```

**Interpretation:** This schematic shows a Linear Doubly Linked List with four nodes holding data 10, 20, 30, and 40. The `head` pointer references the first node (data = 10), and the `prev` of the head is `NULL`, while the `next` of the tail (data = 40) is `NULL`.

## 4.2 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of DLL Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[USER REQUEST] --> A1[OPERATION TYPE DISPATCHER]
    A1 --> B1[INSERTION MODULE]
    A1 --> B2[DELETION MODULE]
    A1 --> B3[TRAVERSAL MODULE]
    A1 --> B4[SEARCH MODULE]
    B1 --> C1[INSERT AT HEAD ON1]
    B1 --> C2[INSERT AT TAIL ON]
    B1 --> C3[INSERT AT POSITION OK]
    B2 --> D1[DELETE AT HEAD ON1]
    B2 --> D2[DELETE AT TAIL ON]
    B2 --> D3[DELETE AT POSITION OK]
    B3 --> E1[FORWARD TRAVERSAL ON]
    B3 --> E2[BACKWARD TRAVERSAL ON]
    B4 --> F1[LINEAR SEARCH ON]
    C1 --> G1[POINTER REATTACHMENT PROTOCOL]
    C2 --> G1
    C3 --> G1
    D1 --> G1
    D2 --> G1
    D3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[MODIFIED DLL STATE]
    E1 --> H1
    E2 --> H1
    F1 --> H1
```

**Interpretation:** This functional block diagram maps the **architectural flow** of a DLL system. Every user request enters the dispatcher, which routes it to one of four operation modules (Insert, Delete, Traverse, Search). All mutating operations funnel through a common **Pointer Reattachment Protocol** to ensure memory safety, after which the modified DLL state is returned to the user.

## 4.3 Diagram: Insertion at Position 2 — Step-by-Step Pointer Rewiring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STEP1["STEP 1 INITIAL LIST HEAD10 NEXT20 NEXT30 NULL"] --> STEP2["STEP 2 CREATE NEWNODE 25"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3 NEWNODE NEXT 20 NEWNODE PREV 10"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4["STEP 4 20 PREV 25"]
    STEP4 --> STEP5["STEP 5 10 NEXT 25 FINAL HEAD10 25 20 30 NULL"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **before-and-after reattachment protocol** for inserting a new node with data 25 at position 2 in the list `[10 -> 20 -> 30]`. Note how the `prev` pointer of node 20 is rewired from 10 to 25, and the `next` pointer of node 10 is rewired from 20 to 25.

## 4.4 Diagram: Deletion of a Middle Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INIT["BEFORE HEAD 10 20 30 40 NULL"] --> IDENT["IDENTIFY TARGET NODE 20"]
    IDENT --> REW1["REATTACH 10 NEXT 30"]
    REW1 --> REW2["REATTACH 30 PREV 10"]
    REW2 --> FREE["FREE MEMORY OF NODE 20"]
    FREE --> FINAL["AFTER HEAD 10 30 40 NULL"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **deletion protocol** for removing node 20 from the list `[10 <-> 20 <-> 30 <-> 40]`. The key reattachments are: `10->next = 30` and `30->prev = 10`. Notice that the `prev` pointer of the target node is *not* needed for deletion (unlike a Singly Linked List), which is the **central efficiency advantage of DLLs**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Doubly Linked List. How does it differ from a Singly Linked List in terms of node structure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Doubly Linked List is a linear data structure in which each node contains **three fields**: a `data` field, a pointer to the `next` node, and a pointer to the `prev` (previous) node. This enables traversal in both forward and backward directions.

| Feature | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointers per node | 1 (next only) | 2 (next and prev) |
| Backward traversal | Not directly possible | Possible |
| Memory per node | Smaller | Larger (extra pointer) |
| Deletion of a known node | $O(n)$ (predecessor required) | $O(1)$ (predecessor directly available) |

**(Awarded: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for tabular comparison with 3 valid points)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the time complexity of inserting a node at the head, tail, and at a given position $k$ in a Doubly Linked List. Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Insertion at Head:** $O(1)$ — Only the head pointer and the new node's pointers are updated; no traversal is required. **(1 mark)**
- **Insertion at Tail:** $O(n)$ — For a Linear DLL, the entire list must be traversed from head to the last node to attach the new node. **(1 mark)**
- **Insertion at position $k$:** $O(k)$ in the best case, $O(n)$ in the worst case — We must traverse $k-1$ nodes to reach the predecessor of the target position. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the structure of a node in a Doubly Linked List with a suitable diagram. Discuss the **three variants** of Doubly Linked Lists with their boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the step-by-step algorithm to **insert a new node at the end of a Doubly Linked List** and **delete a node from the beginning of a Doubly Linked List**. Show the pointer manipulations clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Node Structure:** A node in a DLL contains:

$$\text{Node} = \langle \text{prev} \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{data} \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{next} \rangle$$

(1 mark for declaring the structure: `data`, `next`, `prev`)

**Three Variants:** **(4 marks for variant discussion + 2 marks for boundary conditions)**

1. **Linear Doubly Linked List** — The `prev` of the head is `NULL`, and the `next` of the tail is `NULL`. Traversal is bounded by both ends.

2. **Circular Doubly Linked List** — The `next` of the tail points back to the head, and the `prev` of the head points to the tail. The list forms a closed bidirectional ring; there is no `NULL` termination between head and tail.

3. **Header-Based DLL (with Sentinel Node)** — A dummy header node is placed at the beginning. The actual first element is at `header->next`, and the last element's `next` points back to the header. This eliminates special cases for the first node.

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$\text{For all variants: } \text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL} \text{ (Linear)}$$

$$\text{For Circular: } \text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{tail}, \quad \text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$$

(2 marks for correct boundary equations)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Algorithm 1 — Insertion at the End:**

**Step 1:** Create a new node `newNode` and store the input value.
**Step 2:** Set `newNode->next = NULL`.
**Step 3:** If `head == NULL`, set `head = newNode`, `newNode->prev = NULL`, and return. **(1 mark for edge case)**
**Step 4:** Initialize `temp = head` and traverse to the last node:

$$\text{while } (\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{temp} = \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 5:** Link the last node to the new node:

$$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{newNode}, \quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{temp}$$

(2 marks for traversal logic and 1 mark for reattachment)

**Algorithm 2 — Deletion from the Beginning:**

**Step 1:** If `head == NULL`, the list is empty — return. **(1 mark for edge case)**
**Step 2:** Store the head in a temporary pointer: `temp = head`.
**Step 3:** Move the head pointer forward: `head = head->next`.
**Step 4:** If the new head is not NULL, set its `prev` to NULL:

$$\text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL}$$

**Step 5:** Free the memory of the old head: `free(temp)`. **(1 mark for memory deallocation + 1 mark for correct prev rewiring)**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Given a Doubly Linked List with nodes containing integers, write algorithms for: **(i)** Inserting a node after a node with a given value, and **(ii)** Deleting a node with a specific value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **advantages and disadvantages** of Doubly Linked Lists over Singly Linked Lists with suitable use cases. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Insertion After a Node with a Given Value `X`:** **(3.5 marks)**

**Step 1:** Traverse the list from `head` to find the node with data `X`. Use a pointer `curr`.

$$\text{while } (\text{curr} \neq \text{NULL} \ \text{AND} \ \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{data} \neq X) \implies \text{curr} = \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

**Step 2:** If `curr == NULL`, the value is not present — return an error.

**Step 3:** Create a new node `newNode` and assign the new value.

**Step 4:** Perform the four-link reattachment:

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

$$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{curr}$$

$$\text{if } (\text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{newNode}$$

$$\text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{newNode}$$

**(ii) Deletion of a Node with a Specific Value `X`:** **(3.5 marks)**

**Step 1:** Traverse the list to find the node with data `X`.

**Step 2:** If `curr == NULL`, return (value not found).

**Step 3:** Reattach the predecessor to the successor:

$$\text{curr} \rightarrow \text{prev} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next}$$

$$\text{if } (\text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \implies \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{curr} \rightarrow \text{prev}$$

**Step 4:** Free the memory: `free(curr)`.

(1 mark for correct search logic, 1.5 marks for proper rewiring, 1 mark for memory deallocation)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Advantages of DLL over SLL:** **(3.5 marks)**

1. **Bidirectional Traversal** — DLLs support traversal in both forward and reverse directions, which is impossible in a standard SLL.
2. **Efficient Deletion of a Known Node** — In a DLL, the predecessor of any node is directly accessible via the `prev` pointer, so deletion is $O(1)$. In a SLL, finding the predecessor requires $O(n)$ traversal.
3. **Easier Implementation of Complex Structures** — Data structures like Deque, LRU Cache, and complex navigations are naturally implemented using DLLs.
4. **Quick Insertion Before a Node** — Inserting before a given node in a DLL is $O(1)$ once the node is located, whereas in a SLL it requires finding the predecessor first.

**Disadvantages of DLL over SLL:** **(2 marks)**

1. **Extra Memory** — Each node requires an additional `prev` pointer, increasing the per-node memory overhead.
2. **More Pointer Operations** — Insertions and deletions require more pointer manipulations (4 instead of 2), increasing the chance of bugs.
3. **Slightly Slower Access** — Due to more pointer updates, the constant factor in time complexity is larger.

**Use Cases:** **(1.5 marks)**
- Browser back/forward navigation, LRU caches, music player playlists, undo/redo stacks, polynomial arithmetic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the `prev` pointer of the next node** during insertion is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks**. Always rewire **both sides** of the new node.
> 2. **Memory leak in deletion** — forgetting `free(temp)` after relinking will cost you a full mark in Part B questions.
> 3. **Ignoring edge cases** — empty list (`head == NULL`) and single-node list (`head->next == NULL`) are tested separately by KTU examiners. Always include these as `if` conditions.
> 4. **Confusing `next` and `prev`** in handwritten exams — label arrows clearly in your diagrams.
> 5. **Stating time complexity without justification** — KTU 2024 scheme marks both the **answer and the reasoning** (approx. 1 mark for complexity + 1 mark for justification in Part A).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these to your revision wall before any KTU exam on Module 3.**

- A **Doubly Linked List node** has three fields: `prev`, `data`, `next`. Memorize the structure diagram.
- **Boundary conditions:** `head->prev = NULL` and `tail->next = NULL` for Linear DLL; these become circular pointers in a **Circular DLL**.
- **Insertion at head = $O(1)$**; **Insertion at tail = $O(n)$**; **Insertion at position $k$ = $O(k)$** worst-case $O(n)$.
- **Deletion of a node given its pointer = $O(1)$** (the **killer advantage** of DLLs over SLLs).
- **Traversal** is possible in **both directions** using `next` (forward) and `prev` (backward).
- The **golden reattachment rule**: when inserting, set the new node's pointers *first*, then update the neighbors.
- **Memory overhead per node** = 2 pointers + data. For a list of $n$ nodes, total memory ≈ $n \times (2 \times P_{\text{size}} + D_{\text{size}})$.
- **Optimal indexing trick**: traverse from head if $k < n/2$, from tail if $k \geq n/2$ — saves time in long lists.
- **Common applications:** LRU Cache, Browser History, Undo/Redo, Music Playlists, Deque implementation, Polynomial manipulation.
- **Variant types** to be prepared for: Linear DLL, Circular DLL, and Header-Based DLL (with sentinel/dummy node).
- **For KTU 2024 scheme**, always write the **algorithm steps in plain text or pseudocode** with **clear pointer diagrams** — examiners allocate marks for both logic and visual representation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Circular Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linear data structure derived from the standard linked list in which the pointer/reference of the **last node** does not point to a `NULL` terminator but instead loops back to the **first node (head)** of the list, thereby forming a closed ring or circle of nodes.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAET414, Module 3), circular linked lists are categorized into two principal variants:

- **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL):** Each node contains a single `next` pointer. The `next` pointer of the last node points to the head node.
- **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL):** Each node contains two pointers — `prev` (previous) and `next`. The `next` of the last node points to the head, and the `prev` of the head node points to the last node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike a standard linear linked list which requires an `end` traversal condition (`p != NULL`), a circular linked list requires an `end` traversal condition that compares the current node's `next` pointer with the `head` pointer (i.e., `p->next != head` or `p != head`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Musical Chairs Game**
> Imagine **10 children sitting in a circle playing a game of passing a parcel**. There is no "end" of the circle. The child sitting to the right of any given child always has someone to their right as well — the chain never breaks. The first child to pass the parcel wraps the music back to where it started. A Circular Linked List operates on this exact principle: the "last" node points back to the "first" node, creating an endless, seamless loop. There is **no terminator** (no `NULL`).

### 1.3 Memory & Performance Metrics

- **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ — Where $n$ is the number of nodes, identical to a standard linked list.
- **Time Complexity (Traversal):** $O(n)$ — We must still visit all nodes in the worst case to return to the head.
- **NULL Terminations:** **0** — A defining structural property. No node in a circular linked list contains a `NULL` link.
- **Pointer Count per Node:** **1** for SCLL, **2** for DCLL.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Node Structure Definition

The foundational building block of a Circular Linked List is its **node**. A node is a composite data type bundling the data field and the link field(s).

**For Singly Circular Linked List:**

$$\text{Node}_{\text{SCLL}} = \{\texttt{data} : \text{Datatype},\ \texttt{next} : \text{Node*}\}$$

**For Doubly Circular Linked List:**

$$\text{Node}_{\text{DCLL}} = \{\texttt{prev} : \text{Node*},\ \texttt{data} : \text{Datatype},\ \texttt{next} : \text{Node*}\}$$

### 2.2 Structural Logic Breakdown

- **Why** a circular pointer? In many real-time systems (e.g., CPU round-robin scheduling), the process should never encounter a `NULL` while rotating through all processes. A circular structure guarantees **seamless rotation**.
- **How** is `head` defined? In a **non-empty** CLL, the `head` pointer always points to the first node. The `tail` (last node) is typically tracked separately for $O(1)$ insertions at the end, since traversing to the end costs $O(n)$.
- **How** is the empty list represented? An **empty** CLL is represented by `head = NULL`. Note: This is the only scenario where a circular linked list can legally have a `NULL` head.
- **How** do we stop a traversal? The loop termination condition becomes `p->next == head` (if starting from head) rather than `p->next == NULL`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all key metrics, conditions, and operations expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| **Parameter / Operation** | **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL)** | **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Node Structure** | $\text{data} + \text{next}$ | $\text{prev} + \text{data} + \text{next}$ |
| **Pointers per Node** | **1** | **2** |
| **Memory (Bytes/Node)** | $\text{sizeof(data)} + \text{sizeof(ptr)}$ | $\text{sizeof(data)} + 2 \cdot \text{sizeof(ptr)}$ |
| **Last Node Link** | $\text{last} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$ | $\text{last} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$ AND $\text{head} \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{last}$ |
| **Empty List Condition** | $\text{head} = \text{NULL}$ | $\text{head} = \text{NULL}$ |
| **Traversal Stop Cond.** | $p \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{head}$ | $p \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{head}$ |
| **Insert at Head** | $O(1)$ with tail tracked | $O(1)$ |
| **Insert at Tail** | $O(1)$ with tail tracked | $O(1)$ |
| **Insert at Position $k$** | $O(k)$ | $O(\min(k, n-k))$ |
| **Delete at Head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Delete at Tail** | $O(n)$ or $O(1)$* with `prev` ptr | $O(1)$ with `tail` tracked |
| **Applications** | Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers | LRU cache, navigation history |

\* *For SCLL, deletion at tail without a tail pointer is $O(n)$.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems (OS):** The **Round-Robin CPU Scheduler** maintains a circular list of processes. After the last process is given a time quantum, the scheduler seamlessly loops back to the first process — a perfect, real-world implementation of CLL.
- **Multiplayer Gaming:** Turn rotation in a card game or board game cycles back to the first player.
- **Circular Buffers / Ring Buffers:** Used in audio streaming, network packet buffering, and embedded systems for fixed-size data queues.
- **Undo/Redo in Circular Fashion:** Some advanced text editors wrap the undo stack to use a fixed memory budget.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivational Proof: Node Count and Pointer Arithmetic

Let $n$ denote the total number of nodes in a **non-empty** Singly Circular Linked List. We will derive the position of the $k^{th}$ node from the head using modular arithmetic.

**Step 1: Identify the position modulo.**
Starting from `head` (position 0), the next node is at position 1, then 2, and so on. When we reach `head` again, the position resets. Therefore, the position is a **cyclic counter**.

$$\text{Position}(k) = k \pmod{n}$$

**Step 2: Express the traversal steps mathematically.**
To reach the $k^{th}$ node from the `head` in a CLL, we execute `next` pointer dereferences:

$$T(k, n) = k \bmod n$$

where $T(k, n)$ is the number of pointer traversals needed.

**Step 3: Derive the traversal loop condition.**
A traversal loop runs while we have **not** completed a full cycle:

$$\text{Stop when } i = n \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{head}$$

**Step 4: Prove the equivalence for boundary cases.**
- If $k = 0$: $T(0, n) = 0$. The result is the head node. ✔
- If $k = n$: $T(n, n) = 0$. The result is the head node (full rotation). ✔
- If $k = 2n + 3$: $T(2n+3, n) = 3$. The result is the 3rd node. ✔

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: All Standard Operations on SCLL

Below is the exhaustive analytical breakdown of core operations on a Singly Circular Linked List.

#### Operation 1: Insertion at the Beginning (Head)

Let $T$ be the tail pointer (pointing to the last node) and $H$ be the head pointer.

- **Step 1:** Allocate a new node $N$. Set $N \rightarrow \text{data} = \text{value}$.
- **Step 2:** If list is empty, set $N \rightarrow \text{next} = N$, $H = N$, $T = N$.
- **Step 3:** Otherwise, set $N \rightarrow \text{next} = H$ (point new node to current head).
- **Step 4:** Set $T \rightarrow \text{next} = N$ (the old tail now points to the new head, maintaining circularity).
- **Step 5:** Update $H = N$ (new head is $N$).

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$

#### Operation 2: Insertion at the End (Tail)

- **Step 1:** Allocate a new node $N$. Set $N \rightarrow \text{data} = \text{value}$.
- **Step 2:** If list is empty, set $N \rightarrow \text{next} = N$, $H = N$, $T = N$.
- **Step 3:** Otherwise, set $N \rightarrow \text{next} = H$ (new node's next points to head).
- **Step 4:** Set $T \rightarrow \text{next} = N$ (old tail points to new node).
- **Step 5:** Update $T = N$ (new tail is $N$).

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$

#### Operation 3: Deletion from the Beginning (Head)

- **Step 1:** If list is empty ($H == \text{NULL}$), return.
- **Step 2:** If list has only one node, free it, set $H = T = \text{NULL}$.
- **Step 3:** Otherwise, set $T \rightarrow \text{next} = H \rightarrow \text{next}$ (tail bypasses head to the next node).
- **Step 4:** Store $H$ in a temporary pointer `temp`, update $H = H \rightarrow \text{next}$, free `temp`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$

#### Operation 4: Deletion of a Given Key (Value-Based)

- **Step 1:** Traverse with two pointers: `prev` and `curr`, where `prev` is the node before `curr`.
- **Step 2:** Loop while `curr->data != key` and `curr != H` (did not loop back to head).
- **Step 3:** If key not found, exit with a "Not Found" message.
- **Step 4:** Otherwise, set `prev->next = curr->next` to bypass `curr`.
- **Step 5:** If `curr == T`, update `T = prev` (tail moved).
- **Step 6:** If `curr == H`, update `H = H->next` and `T->next = H`.
- **Step 7:** Free the `curr` node.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ in the worst case.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Traversing a SCLL

Consider a SCLL with **5 nodes**: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow (\text{back to } 10)$. Find the 7th node from the head.

**Step 1:** Apply the modular formula.

$$T(7, 5) = 7 \bmod 5 = 2$$

**Step 2:** Interpret the result.

$$T(7, 5) = 2 \implies \text{The 7th node is the same as the 2nd node from the head.}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the 2nd node.

$$\text{Head} = 10, \quad \text{1st node} = 10, \quad \text{2nd node} = 20$$

**Final Answer:** The 7th node from the head is **20**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: Singly Circular Linked List

The following diagram illustrates a Singly Circular Linked List with 4 nodes and the closed-loop architecture of the `next` pointers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeHead[HEAD Pointer]
    n1[Node 1: Data = 10]
    n2[Node 2: Data = 20]
    n3[Node 3: Data = 30]
    n4[Node 4: Data = 40]
    nodeTail[TAIL Pointer]
    nodeNull[NULL Terminator]
    
    nodeHead --> n1
    n1 -- next --> n2
    n2 -- next --> n3
    n3 -- next --> n4
    n4 -- next --> n1
    nodeTail --> n4
    nodeNull -.X.- n4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dashed line with an **X** from `NULL Terminator` to `Node 4` is explicitly crossed out to emphasize the **defining property** of a CLL: the last node's `next` pointer **does NOT** point to `NULL`. Instead, it loops back to `Node 1` (the head), forming a closed circular chain.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: Doubly Circular Linked List

```mermaid
graph LR
    headP[HEAD Pointer]
    n1D[Node 1: Data = A]
    n2D[Node 2: Data = B]
    n3D[Node 3: Data = C]
    tailP[TAIL Pointer]
    
    headP --> n1D
    n1D -- next --> n2D
    n2D -- next --> n3D
    n3D -- next --> n1D
    n3D -- prev --> n2D
    n2D -- prev --> n1D
    n1D -- prev --> n3D
    tailP --> n3D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bidirectional Traversal:** In a Doubly Circular Linked List, traversal can occur in **both directions**. The `next` pointers move forward (clockwise), and the `prev` pointers move backward (counter-clockwise). The head's `prev` and the tail's `next` close the loop on both ends.

### 4.3 Functional Flow Diagram: Insertion at Head in SCLL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Insert at Head])
    checkEmpty{Is List Empty?}
    allocNode[Allocate New Node N]
    setFields[Set N data = value]
    setSelf[N next = N, H = N, T = N]
    linkNtoH[N next = H]
    linkTtoN[T next = N]
    updateH[H = N]
    endProc([End: Return H])
    
    startA --> checkEmpty
    checkEmpty -- Yes --> allocNode
    checkEmpty -- No --> allocNode
    allocNode --> setFields
    setFields --> setSelf
    setSelf --> endProc
    checkEmpty -- No after alloc --> linkNtoH
    linkNtoH --> linkTtoN
    linkTtoN --> updateH
    updateH --> endProc
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Circular vs Linear Linked List

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LinearLL[LINEAR LINKED LIST - Has NULL End]
        L1[Node A] -- next --> L2[Node B]
        L2 -- next --> L3[Node C]
        L3 -- next --> L4[NULL]
    end
    
    subgraph CircularLL[CIRCULAR LINKED LIST - No NULL End]
        C1[Node A] -- next --> C2[Node B]
        C2 -- next --> C3[Node C]
        C3 -- next --> C1
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a Circular Linked List. How does it differ from a standard singly linked list?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linked list in which the `next` pointer of the last node points back to the head (first) node, forming a closed loop. It differs from a standard singly linked list in two primary ways:
1. **No NULL Terminator:** The last node's `next` does not point to `NULL` but to the head node.
2. **Traversal Logic:** Traversal loop termination is checked using `p->next == head` instead of `p->next == NULL`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
*List any two real-world applications of a Circular Linked List.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Round-Robin CPU Scheduling** in operating systems, where processes are rotated in a circular fashion.
2. **Circular Buffer / Ring Buffer** in networking and audio processing for fixed-size queues.
*(Acceptable alternatives: Multiplayer game turn rotation, circular undo-redo systems, navigation history loops.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to update the **last node's `next` pointer** to maintain circularity after insertion/deletion. This breaks the loop and causes infinite loops during traversal. **(Loses 2-3 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using `while(p->next != NULL)` instead of `while(p->next != head)` for traversal. This will **never terminate** in a CLL or will exit prematurely.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the **empty list case** ($H == NULL$) separately from the single-node case. **(Loses 1-2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not drawing clear diagrams. KTU examiners award marks for well-labeled diagrams showing pointer updates.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to insert a node at the **end** of a Singly Circular Linked List. Explain with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to **delete a node by value** from a Singly Circular Linked List. Trace the algorithm with the list: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow (\text{back to } 10)$ for deleting the value **30**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Insertion at End (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm:**

1. Create a new node `N`. Set `N->data = value` and `N->next = NULL`.
2. **If `head == NULL`** (empty list), set `N->next = N`, `head = N`, `tail = N`, return.
3. **Else** (list is non-empty):
   - Set `N->next = head` (new node's next points to head, closing the loop).
   - Set `tail->next = N` (old tail's next now points to new node).
   - Update `tail = N` (new node becomes the tail).

**Example Trace:** Initial list: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow (10)$. Insert **40** at end.
- Step 1: Create node $N$ with data 40.
- Step 2: List is not empty, proceed.
- Step 3: $N \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$ (i.e., points to 10).
- Step 4: $\text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} = N$ (i.e., 30's next becomes 40).
- Step 5: $\text{tail} = N$ (i.e., 40 is now tail).
- **Final List:** $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow (10)$ ✔

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Deletion by Value (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm:**

1. If `head == NULL`, print "List is empty" and return.
2. Initialize `curr = head`, `prev = tail`.
3. Loop: `while (curr->data != key && curr != head)`: advance `prev = curr`, `curr = curr->next`.
4. If `curr->data != key`, print "Not Found" and return.
5. If `prev == curr` (only one node): free `curr`, set `head = tail = NULL`, return.
6. Set `prev->next = curr->next` (bypass the deleted node).
7. If `curr == head`: update `head = head->next` and `tail->next = head`.
8. If `curr == tail`: update `tail = prev`.
9. Free `curr`.

**Trace:** List: $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow (10)$. Delete **30**.

- Step 1: List is not empty.
- Step 2: `curr = 10`, `prev = 40` (tail).
- Step 3: Loop iteration 1: `curr->data = 10 != 30` and `curr != head`? After moving, `curr = 20`, `prev = 10`.
- Step 3: Loop iteration 2: `curr->data = 20 != 30`. After moving, `curr = 30`, `prev = 20`.
- Step 3: Loop exits (key found).
- Step 6: `prev->next = curr->next` ⇒ `20->next = 40`.
- Step 8: `curr != tail` (tail is 40, curr is 30). No update needed.
- Step 9: Free node 30.
- **Final List:** $10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow (10)$ ✔

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** with a neat diagram. Compare it with a Singly Circular Linked List.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to insert a node at the **beginning** of a Singly Circular Linked List and trace it for the list: $5 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 15 \rightarrow (5)$ inserting the value **2**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — DCLL Structure (7 Marks):**

A **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** is a linked list where each node has three fields: `prev` (pointer to previous node), `data`, and `next` (pointer to next node). The `next` of the last node points to the head, and the `prev` of the head node points to the last node, creating **two circular paths** (forward and backward).

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    H[HEAD] --> A[Data=A prev=tail next=B]
    A -- next --> B[Data=B prev=A next=C]
    B -- next --> C[Data=C prev=B next=tail]
    C -- next --> A
    C -- prev --> B
    B -- prev --> A
    A -- prev --> C
```

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Singly Circular (SCLL)** | **Doubly Circular (DCLL)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointers per node | **1** (`next`) | **2** (`prev`, `next`) |
| Memory per node | Lower | Higher |
| Traversal direction | Forward only | Forward & Backward |
| Delete at tail (with tail ptr) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Complexity of operations | Simpler | More complex |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Insertion at Beginning (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm:**

1. Create a new node `N`. Set `N->data = value` and `N->next = NULL`.
2. **If `head == NULL`** (empty list), set `N->next = N`, `head = N`, `tail = N`, return.
3. **Else**:
   - Set `N->next = head` (new node points to current head).
   - Set `tail->next = N` (last node now points to new head, closing loop).
   - Update `head = N` (new node becomes the head).

**Trace:** List: $5 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 15 \rightarrow (5)$. Insert **2** at beginning.

- Step 1: Create node $N$ with data 2.
- Step 2: List is not empty.
- Step 3a: $N \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head}$ ⇒ 2's next points to 5.
- Step 3b: $\text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} = N$ ⇒ 15's next becomes 2.
- Step 3c: $\text{head} = N$ ⇒ head is now 2.
- **Final List:** $2 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 15 \rightarrow (2)$ ✔

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Circular Linked List**

- **Definition:** A linked list where the last node's `next` pointer points back to the **head** (no `NULL` at the end).
- **Two Variants:** **Singly Circular** (1 pointer per node) and **Doubly Circular** (2 pointers per node).
- **Empty List:** Represented by `head = NULL`. This is the **only** legal use of `NULL` in a CLL.
- **Traversal Stop Condition:** `p->next == head` (or `p != head` if starting from head with `do-while`).
- **Tail Tracking:** Maintaining a `tail` pointer allows $O(1)$ insertion at both head and tail.
- **Last Node Update:** **Always** remember to update the last node's `next` after any insertion/deletion to preserve the circular structure.
- **Time Complexities:**
  - Insert at head/tail: **$O(1)$** (with tail pointer).
  - Delete at head: **$O(1)$**.
  - Delete by value: **$O(n)$**.
  - Traversal: **$O(n)$**.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$.
- **Key Applications:** Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, ring-based multiplayer games.
- **Common Pitfall:** Using `p->next != NULL` instead of `p->next != head` causes an **infinite loop**.
- **DCLL Advantage:** Bidirectional traversal enables $O(1)$ deletion at the tail (with tail pointer) without a full traversal.
- **Visualization Trick:** Draw CLLs as circles, not straight lines — this helps avoid the "end of the list" mental trap.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the pointer updates **before and after** an operation to score full marks on diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stacks and Queues using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stacks and Queues using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Stacks and Queues using Linked List

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Linked List** is a linear data structure in which elements (called **nodes**) are stored in non-contiguous memory locations. Each node contains two fields: a **DATA field** that holds the value, and a **LINK / NEXT field** (a pointer) that holds the address of the next node in the sequence. A **Stack** implemented using a linked list uses a dynamic chain of such nodes, with a pointer named `TOP` that always references the most recently inserted node. Similarly, a **Queue** implemented using a linked list uses two pointers, `FRONT` and `REAR`, to manage insertions at the rear and deletions from the front.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> A *Singly Linked List* is an ordered collection of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node. The list is accessed via a `HEAD` (or `TOP`/`FRONT`) pointer. The last node's link field is set to `NULL`, terminating the chain. In a *Circular Linked List*, the last node's link points back to the first node, forming a closed ring.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Stack as a Stack of Plates in a Cafeteria**
> Imagine a spring-loaded plate dispenser. You can only **PUSH** a new plate on top, and you can only **POP** the topmost plate. You cannot pull a plate from the middle. The linked-list version is exactly this: each plate is a node, the top plate is where `TOP` points, and the spring mechanism below is the `NEXT` pointer leading to the plate beneath.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Queue as a Line at a Movie Ticket Counter**
> People stand in a line. The person who arrives **first** is served **first** (FIFO — First In, First Out). Newcomers join at the **REAR** (tail), and service is given at the **FRONT** (head). The linked-list implementation maintains this with two pointers: `FRONT` for the head (next to be served) and `REAR` for the tail (last to arrive).

### 1.3 Why Linked List Over Array? — The Motivation

| Limitation in Array-Based Stack/Queue | Advantage of Linked List Implementation |
|---|---|
| Fixed maximum size (must declare size in advance) | **Dynamic size** — grows and shrinks at runtime based on heap availability |
| `push`/`pop` on a stack is O(1), but resizing is expensive | No resizing overhead — each operation is O(1) |
| Array-based queue suffers from the **false overflow** problem (wasted slots) | **No false overflow** — every node is allocated on demand |
| Insertions in the middle are O(n) | Easy to insert/delete nodes anywhere in O(1) given a pointer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):**
> The auxiliary **space complexity** of a linked-list-based stack/queue is **O(n)**, where n is the current number of elements — this is **exact** memory usage, unlike arrays that reserve fixed capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Node Structure

Every linked list — whether used for a stack or a queue — is built from a fundamental unit called a **Node**. The structure in C (KTU-expected language) is:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;           // payload / value stored
    struct Node *next;  // pointer to successor node
};
```

> **Glossary of Pointers Used Throughout this Module:**
> - `TOP` — points to the top of a Stack (also serves as `HEAD` of the list).
> - `FRONT` — points to the head of a Queue (next element to be dequeued).
> - `REAR` — points to the tail of a Queue (last inserted element).
> - `NULL` — sentinel value indicating end-of-list.

### 2.2 Stack Using Singly Linked List — Operational Logic

The stack discipline is **LIFO (Last In, First Out)**. The linked list grows and shrinks at the **head** end, because both insertion (`PUSH`) and deletion (`POP`) at the head take **O(1)** time.

#### 2.2.1 PUSH Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The `PUSH(x)` operation inserts `x` as the new top element.

1. **Allocate** a new node `temp` on the heap using `malloc`.
2. **Assign** `temp->data = x`.
3. **Set** `temp->next = TOP` (link the new node to the old top).
4. **Update** `TOP = temp` (move TOP to the new node).

If `TOP == NULL` (empty stack), the new node's `next` remains `NULL` and becomes both top and bottom of the stack.

#### 2.2.2 POP Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The `POP()` operation removes and returns the top element.

1. **Check** underflow: if `TOP == NULL`, stack is empty — print error and return.
2. **Cache** the address: `temp = TOP`.
3. **Retrieve** value: `x = temp->data`.
4. **Advance** TOP: `TOP = TOP->next`.
5. **Free** the old node: `free(temp)`.
6. **Return** `x`.

#### 2.2.3 PEEK Operation

Returns `TOP->data` without modifying the structure. Cost: **O(1)**.

#### 2.2.4 Time & Space Complexity of Stack Operations

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Auxiliary Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PUSH` | **O(1)** | O(1) per call | O(1) |
| `POP` | **O(1)** | O(1) per call | O(1) |
| `PEEK` | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(1) |
| `isEmpty` | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(1) |
| `Display` | **O(n)** | O(1) | O(1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PUSH and POP are O(1):** Both operations modify only the `TOP` pointer. There is no traversal of the list, no shifting of elements, and no capacity check.

### 2.3 Queue Using Singly Linked List — Operational Logic

The queue discipline is **FIFO (First In, First Out)**. Insertions happen at the **REAR** (tail) and deletions happen at the **FRONT** (head). Using two pointers — `FRONT` and `REAR` — makes both operations **O(1)**.

#### 2.3.1 ENQUEUE Operation

The `ENQUEUE(x)` operation appends `x` to the rear of the queue.

1. **Allocate** a new node `temp` and assign `temp->data = x`, `temp->next = NULL`.
2. **If empty** (`FRONT == NULL`): set `FRONT = REAR = temp`.
3. **Else (non-empty)**: set `REAR->next = temp`, then `REAR = temp`.

#### 2.3.2 DEQUEUE Operation

The `DEQUEUE()` operation removes and returns the front element.

1. **Check** underflow: if `FRONT == NULL`, queue is empty — error.
2. **Cache** `temp = FRONT`, retrieve `x = temp->data`.
3. **Advance** `FRONT = FRONT->next`.
4. **Special case**: if `FRONT` becomes `NULL` (last element was removed), set `REAR = NULL` too.
5. **Free** `temp`, return `x`.

#### 2.3.3 Time & Space Complexity of Queue Operations

| Operation | Time Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `ENQUEUE` | **O(1)** | Rear pointer update is constant time |
| `DEQUEUE` | **O(1)** | Front pointer update is constant time |
| `isEmpty` | **O(1)** | Check `FRONT == NULL` |
| `Display` | **O(n)** | Must traverse from FRONT to REAR |

### 2.4 Circular Linked List — The Foundation for Circular Queue

A **Circular Linked List** is a singly linked list where the `next` pointer of the **last node** points back to the **first node** instead of `NULL`. This is the building block for **Circular Queues**, which elegantly solve the "false overflow" problem of array-based queues.

```
[Front] → [10 | •] → [20 | •] → [30 | •] ──┐
              ▲                             │
              └─────────────────────────────┘
                                       [Rear]
```

#### 2.4.1 ENQUEUE in Circular Queue

To avoid traversing from `FRONT` to `REAR` (which would be O(n)), we maintain a single `REAR` pointer. The front is then accessed as `REAR->next`.

- **Empty case**: `REAR = temp`, and `REAR->next = REAR` (single-node circle).
- **Non-empty case**: `temp->next = REAR->next; REAR->next = temp; REAR = temp`.

#### 2.4.2 DEQUEUE in Circular Queue

- **Empty check**: `REAR == NULL`.
- **Single-element case**: free the node, set `REAR = NULL`.
- **Multi-element case**: `temp = REAR->next` (the front), `REAR->next = temp->next`, free `temp`.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{\text{push}}$ | Time for stack PUSH | $T_{\text{push}} = O(1)$ |
| $T_{\text{pop}}$ | Time for stack POP | $T_{\text{pop}} = O(1)$ |
| $T_{\text{enq}}$ | Time for queue ENQUEUE | $T_{\text{enq}} = O(1)$ |
| $T_{\text{deq}}$ | Time for queue DEQUEUE | $T_{\text{deq}} = O(1)$ |
| $S_{\text{total}}$ | Memory used by n nodes | $S_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (D + P)$ bytes |
| $D$ | Size of data field (e.g., int) | $D = 4$ bytes (typical) |
| $P$ | Size of pointer field | $P = 8$ bytes (on 64-bit) |
| $N_{\max}$ | Theoretical max nodes | $N_{max} = \lfloor M_{\text{heap}} / (D+P) \rfloor$ |
| Overflow condition (stack) | Linked-list stack | $M_{\text{heap}}$ exhausted (no explicit limit) |
| Underflow condition | Stack or Queue empty | $\text{TOP} = \text{NULL}$ or $\text{FRONT} = \text{NULL}$ |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Operating Systems** use linked-list-based stacks for function call frames, recursion, and backtracking.
> - **CPU schedulers** use queue data structures (often circular) to manage ready-process lists in Round-Robin scheduling.
> - **Network routers** use queues to buffer packets awaiting transmission.
> - **Compiler symbol tables** are often built on linked-list stacks to handle nested scopes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Trace — Stack using Linked List

Consider the sequence: `PUSH(10)`, `PUSH(20)`, `PUSH(30)`, `POP()`, `PUSH(40)`, `DISPLAY()`.

We will trace **every pointer update** step-by-step.

#### 3.1.1 Initial State

$$
\text{TOP} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{Stack} = \emptyset
$$

#### 3.1.2 PUSH(10)

- Allocate node `n1`. `n1->data = 10`.
- `n1->next = TOP` → `n1->next = NULL`.
- `TOP = n1`.

$$
\text{TOP} \rightarrow [\,10 \mid \text{NULL}\,]
$$

#### 3.1.3 PUSH(20)

- Allocate node `n2`. `n2->data = 20`.
- `n2->next = TOP` → `n2->next = n1`.
- `TOP = n2`.

$$
\text{TOP} \rightarrow [\,20 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,10 \mid \text{NULL}\,]
$$

#### 3.1.4 PUSH(30)

- Allocate node `n3`. `n3->data = 30`.
- `n3->next = TOP` → `n3->next = n2`.
- `TOP = n3`.

$$
\text{TOP} \rightarrow [\,30 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,20 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,10 \mid \text{NULL}\,]
$$

#### 3.1.5 POP() — Returns 30

- `temp = TOP = n3`, `x = n3->data = 30`.
- `TOP = TOP->next = n2`.
- `free(temp)`.

$$
\text{TOP} \rightarrow [\,20 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,10 \mid \text{NULL}\,], \quad \text{returned } x = 30
$$

#### 3.1.6 PUSH(40)

- Allocate node `n4`. `n4->data = 40`.
- `n4->next = TOP = n2`.
- `TOP = n4`.

$$
\text{TOP} \rightarrow [\,40 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,20 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,10 \mid \text{NULL}\,]
$$

#### 3.1.7 DISPLAY() — Output

Traverse from `TOP` printing `data` until `NULL` is hit:

$$
\text{Output: } 40 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 10
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Trace — Queue using Linked List

Consider: `ENQUEUE(5)`, `ENQUEUE(15)`, `ENQUEUE(25)`, `DEQUEUE()`, `ENQUEUE(35)`.

#### 3.2.1 ENQUEUE(5) — Empty case

- `temp` allocated with `data=5, next=NULL`.
- `FRONT == NULL` is true → `FRONT = REAR = temp`.

$$
\text{FRONT} = \text{REAR} \rightarrow [\,5 \mid \text{NULL}\,]
$$

#### 3.2.2 ENQUEUE(15)

- `temp` allocated with `data=15, next=NULL`.
- `REAR->next = temp` → links node(5) to node(15).
- `REAR = temp`.

$$
\text{FRONT} \rightarrow [\,5 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,15 \mid \text{NULL}\,] \leftarrow \text{REAR}
$$

#### 3.2.3 ENQUEUE(25)

$$
\text{FRONT} \rightarrow [\,5 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,15 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,25 \mid \text{NULL}\,] \leftarrow \text{REAR}
$$

#### 3.2.4 DEQUEUE() — Returns 5

- `temp = FRONT`, `x = 5`.
- `FRONT = FRONT->next` → now points to node(15).
- `REAR` is unchanged.
- `free(temp)`.

$$
\text{FRONT} \rightarrow [\,15 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,25 \mid \text{NULL}\,] \leftarrow \text{REAR}, \quad x = 5
$$

#### 3.2.5 ENQUEUE(35) — Critical step

If we forget to do this, the list **breaks** — old `REAR->next` (which is `NULL`) is overwritten only because we are correctly linking `temp` to `REAR->next`. The new rear now points to `NULL`.

$$
\text{FRONT} \rightarrow [\,15 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,25 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,35 \mid \text{NULL}\,] \leftarrow \text{REAR}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Trace — Circular Queue (Rear-only maintenance)

For circular queues, we maintain only `REAR`. Front is always `REAR->next`.

Operations: `ENQUEUE(100)`, `ENQUEUE(200)`, `ENQUEUE(300)`, `DEQUEUE()`.

#### 3.3.1 ENQUEUE(100) — First insertion (empty → circle)

- `temp->data = 100`, `temp->next = temp` (self-loop).
- `REAR = temp`.

$$
\text{REAR} \rightarrow [\,100 \mid \bullet\,] \;\circlearrowleft
$$

#### 3.3.2 ENQUEUE(200)

- `temp->data = 200`, `temp->next = REAR->next` (which is node(100)).
- `REAR->next = temp` (old rear now points to new node).
- `REAR = temp`.

$$
\text{REAR} \rightarrow [\,200 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,100 \mid \bullet\,] \;\circlearrowleft
$$

#### 3.3.3 ENQUEUE(300)

$$
\text{REAR} \rightarrow [\,300 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,200 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,100 \mid \bullet\,] \;\circlearrowleft
$$

#### 3.3.4 DEQUEUE() — Returns 100 (the front)

- `temp = REAR->next` = node(100).
- `x = 100`.
- `REAR->next = temp->next` (rear now points to node(200)).
- `free(temp)`.

$$
\text{REAR} \rightarrow [\,300 \mid \bullet\,] \rightarrow [\,200 \mid \bullet\,] \;\circlearrowleft
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Note how `REAR->next` is *always* the front. We never need a separate `FRONT` pointer — saving 8 bytes per queue!

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Across Implementations

| Feature | Array Stack | Linked Stack | Array Queue (Linear) | Linked Queue | Circular Queue (LL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | Fixed | Dynamic | Fixed | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| `push`/`enqueue` | O(1) | **O(1)** | O(1) | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| `pop`/`dequeue` | O(1) | **O(1)** | O(1) | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Memory waste | Yes (unused slots) | None | **Yes (false overflow)** | None | None |
| Extra pointers | 1 (top index) | 1 (`TOP`) | 2 (front, rear) | 2 (`FRONT`, `REAR`) | 1 (`REAR`) |
| Resize cost | O(n) | N/A | O(n) | N/A | N/A |

### 3.5 Memory Footprint Derivation

For a linked list with `n` nodes on a **64-bit** system:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (D + P)
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (4 + 8) = 12n \text{ bytes}
$$

On a **32-bit** system:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (4 + 4) = 8n \text{ bytes}
$$

If the heap has $M$ bytes free, the maximum number of storable nodes is:

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M}{D + P} \right\rfloor
$$

> **Worked Example:** If heap = 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes, on 64-bit:
> $$
> N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{2^{20}}{12} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 87381.33 \rfloor = 87381 \text{ nodes}
> $$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Stack as a Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pointer fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef nullnode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    TOP["TOP pointer"]:::pointer
    N1["Node 1\ndata: 30\nnext: ->Node2"]:::node
    N2["Node 2\ndata: 20\nnext: ->Node3"]:::node
    N3["Node 3\ndata: 10\nnext: NULL"]:::node
    NIL["NULL terminator"]:::nullnode

    TOP --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> NIL
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The `TOP` pointer always points to the most recently PUSHed node. The `next` field of the last node points to `NULL`, marking the bottom of the stack.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Queue as a Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pointer fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef nullnode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    FRONT["FRONT pointer"]:::pointer
    REAR["REAR pointer"]:::pointer
    N1["Node A\ndata: 5\nnext: ->NodeB"]:::node
    N2["Node B\ndata: 15\nnext: ->NodeC"]:::node
    N3["Node C\ndata: 25\nnext: NULL"]:::node
    NIL["NULL"]:::nullnode

    FRONT --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> NIL
    REAR -.-> N3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** `FRONT` points to the next element to be DEQUEUEd, and `REAR` points to the most recently ENQUEUEd element.

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Circular Queue (Rear-Only)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pointer fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000

    REAR["REAR pointer"]:::pointer
    N1["Node 1\ndata: 300\nnext: ->Node2"]:::node
    N2["Node 2\ndata: 200\nnext: ->Node3"]:::node
    N3["Node 3\ndata: 100\nnext: ->Node1 (loop)"]:::node

    REAR --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 -.-> N1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from Node 3 loops back to Node 1, forming a circular chain. `REAR->next` is always the front of the queue.

### 4.4 Operation Flowchart — Stack PUSH Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    START([Start PUSH x]):::action
    ALLOC["temp = malloc\ntemp->data = x"]:::action
    CHECK{"TOP == NULL?"}:::decision
    LINKNULL["temp->next = NULL"]:::action
    LINKTOP["temp->next = TOP"]:::action
    UPDATETOP["TOP = temp"]:::action
    DONE([Push complete]):::action

    START --> ALLOC --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> LINKNULL --> UPDATETOP
    CHECK -- No --> LINKTOP --> UPDATETOP
    UPDATETOP --> DONE
```

### 4.5 Operation Flowchart — Queue DEQUEUE Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    START([Start DEQUEUE]):::action
    CHECK{"FRONT == NULL?"}:::decision
    UNDERFLOW["Print: Underflow\nReturn"]:::error
    CACHE["temp = FRONT\nx = temp->data"]:::action
    ADVANCE["FRONT = FRONT->next"]:::action
    REARCHK{"FRONT == NULL?"}:::decision
    SETREAR["REAR = NULL"]:::action
    FREE["free(temp)\nReturn x"]:::action

    START --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> UNDERFLOW
    CHECK -- No --> CACHE --> ADVANCE --> REARCHK
    REARCHK -- Yes --> SETREAR --> FREE
    REARCHK -- No --> FREE
```
