## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> Differentiate between stack and queue data structures. Write any two applications of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** discipline, where insertion (`push`) and deletion (`pop`) occur at the same end called the **top**. A **queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** discipline, where insertion (`enqueue`) happens at the **rear** and deletion (`dequeue`) at the **front**.

Applications of Stack: (i) Function call management / recursion in compilers, (ii) Evaluation of postfix/prefix expressions, (iii) Undo operation in text editors, (iv) Backtracking algorithms (DFS, mazes).

Applications of Queue: (i) CPU process scheduling (Round-Robin, FCFS), (ii) Disk scheduling, (iii) Breadth-First Search in graphs, (iv) Buffer management in I/O systems, (v) Printer spooler.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> State and explain the advantages of implementing a stack using a linked list over an array.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The advantages of implementing a stack using a linked list over an array are:
1. **Dynamic size** — The linked list version grows and shrinks at runtime as per demand, whereas an array has a fixed capacity declared at compile time. This eliminates the risk of **stack overflow** due to insufficient pre-allocated memory.
2. **No memory wastage** — In an array, unused slots (if MAXSIZE > n) are wasted. In a linked list, memory is allocated per node, so only the exact required memory is consumed.
3. **No resizing overhead** — Array-based stacks may require costly resizing (allocation + copy), but linked list PUSH/POP remain strictly O(1).
4. **Ease of implementation** — Insertion at the head of a singly linked list is straightforward and natural for stack operations.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]

> **Q.A (a)** Implement a stack using a singly linked list in C. Write the algorithms for `PUSH`, `POP`, `PEEK`, and `DISPLAY` operations. (7 marks)
>
> **Q.A (b)** Starting with an empty stack, perform the following operations and show the linked list state after each step: `PUSH(5)`, `PUSH(15)`, `POP`, `PUSH(25)`, `POP`, `POP`. Also compute the time and space complexity of each operation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Implementation:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};

struct Node *TOP = NULL;   // global top pointer

void PUSH(int x) {
    struct Node *temp = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (temp == NULL) {
        printf("Heap Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = TOP;       // link to old top
    TOP = temp;             // update top
    printf("%d pushed\n", x);
}

int POP() {
    if (TOP == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = TOP;
    int x = temp->data;
    TOP = TOP->next;
    free(temp);
    return x;
}

int PEEK() {
    if (TOP == NULL) {
        printf("Stack is empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return TOP->data;
}

void DISPLAY() {
    struct Node *cur = TOP;
    if (cur == NULL) { printf("Stack is empty\n"); return; }
    printf("Stack (top to bottom): ");
    while (cur != NULL) {
        printf("%d ", cur->data);
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    printf("\n");
}
```

**Part (b) — Trace:**

| Step | Operation | Action | State of Stack (Top → Bottom) | Returned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | TOP = NULL | (empty) | — |
| 1 | PUSH(5) | Allocate n1, n1->next=NULL, TOP=n1 | 5 | — |
| 2 | PUSH(15) | Allocate n2, n2->next=n1, TOP=n2 | 15 → 5 | — |
| 3 | POP | temp=n2, x=15, TOP=n1, free(n2) | 5 | 15 |
| 4 | PUSH(25) | Allocate n3, n3->next=n1, TOP=n3 | 25 → 5 | — |
| 5 | POP | temp=n3, x=25, TOP=n1, free(n3) | 5 | 25 |
| 6 | POP | temp=n1, x=5, TOP=NULL, free(n1) | (empty) | 5 |

**Complexity Analysis:**
- `PUSH`: Time = O(1), Space = O(1) per call.
- `POP`: Time = O(1), Space = O(1) per call.
- `PEEK`: Time = O(1), Space = O(1).
- `DISPLAY`: Time = O(n), Space = O(1) auxiliary.

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]

> **Q.B (a)** Implement a queue using a singly linked list in C. Write the algorithms for `ENQUEUE`, `DEQUEUE`, and `DISPLAY` operations. Handle both empty and single-element cases carefully. (7 marks)
>
> **Q.B (b)** Show the linked list state of the queue after the following operations on an initially empty queue: `ENQUEUE(10)`, `ENQUEUE(20)`, `ENQUEUE(30)`, `DEQUEUE`, `ENQUEUE(40)`, `DEQUEUE`, `DEQUEUE`. Justify why linked-list queues avoid the "false overflow" problem of array queues. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Implementation:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};

struct Node *FRONT = NULL;
struct Node *REAR  = NULL;

void ENQUEUE(int x) {
    struct Node *temp = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (temp == NULL) { printf("Heap Overflow\n"); return; }
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = NULL;
    if (REAR == NULL) {           // empty queue
        FRONT = REAR = temp;
    } else {
        REAR->next = temp;
        REAR = temp;
    }
    printf("%d enqueued\n", x);
}

int DEQUEUE() {
    if (FRONT == NULL) {
        printf("Queue Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = FRONT;
    int x = temp->data;
    FRONT = FRONT->next;
    if (FRONT == NULL) {          // last element removed
        REAR = NULL;
    }
    free(temp);
    return x;
}

void DISPLAY() {
    struct Node *cur = FRONT;
    if (cur == NULL) { printf("Queue is empty\n"); return; }
    printf("Queue (front to rear): ");
    while (cur != NULL) {
        printf("%d ", cur->data);
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    printf("\n");
}
```

**Part (b) — Trace:**

| Step | Operation | State (FRONT → REAR) | Returned |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | FRONT = REAR = NULL | — |
| 1 | ENQUEUE(10) | 10 | — |
| 2 | ENQUEUE(20) | 10 → 20 | — |
| 3 | ENQUEUE(30) | 10 → 20 → 30 | — |
| 4 | DEQUEUE | 20 → 30 (FRONT moved) | 10 |
| 5 | ENQUEUE(40) | 20 → 30 → 40 | — |
| 6 | DEQUEUE | 30 → 40 | 20 |
| 7 | DEQUEUE | 40 | 30 |

**False Overflow Discussion:**

In a **linear array-based queue**, even when slots before `FRONT` are empty, the queue may report "full" because the `REAR` index has reached the end. This is called **false overflow** — memory exists but is unreachable.

In a **linked-list-based queue**, nodes are allocated on demand from the heap and freed when dequeued. There are no pre-fixed slots; each `ENQUEUE` creates a new node, and each `DEQUEUE` frees one. Hence, the only "overflow" condition is actual heap exhaustion — making false overflow **impossible** in linked-list queues.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Valuation Pitfalls)**
> 1. **Forgetting the single-element DEQUEUE case** — When the last node is removed, `FRONT` becomes `NULL`, but if you forget to also set `REAR = NULL`, the next `ENQUEUE` will corrupt the list because `REAR->next` will dereference a freed pointer. **2 marks are routinely docked for this.**
> 2. **Not updating `REAR->next = NULL` in ENQUEUE** — Always initialize the new node's `next` to `NULL` before linking. Otherwise, dangling pointers from `malloc` may appear.
> 3. **Mixing up stack TOP with queue FRONT** — Stacks use only one end (TOP); queues use two ends (FRONT and REAR). Examiners penalize confusion between these.
> 4. **Writing traversal-based operations and claiming O(1)** — In linked list, traversal is O(n). Only head/tail modifications with a stored pointer are O(1).
> 5. **Not drawing arrow diagrams in traces** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clear visual state diagrams after each operation. Always draw the linked list after each step.
> 6. **Forgetting `free()`** — Memory leaks are penalized in code-trace questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ A **Stack** is a **LIFO** structure; a **Queue** is a **FIFO** structure.
> ✅ In a **linked-list stack**, all operations (`PUSH`, `POP`, `PEEK`) execute at the **head** in **O(1)** time.
> ✅ In a **linked-list queue**, `ENQUEUE` is at the **rear** and `DEQUEUE` is at the **front**, both in **O(1)** time — provided `FRONT` and `REAR` pointers are maintained.
> ✅ Always set `REAR = NULL` when `FRONT` becomes `NULL` after the last `DEQUEUE`.
> ✅ The **null terminator** at the end of a singly linked list is `NULL`; in a circular list, the last node points back to the first.
> ✅ **Circular queues** maintain only a `REAR` pointer; the front is implicitly `REAR->next`. This is the structure used in **Round-Robin CPU scheduling**.
> ✅ Linked-list implementations are **not bounded by a fixed MAXSIZE** — they grow until the heap is exhausted.
> ✅ Linked-list implementations eliminate **false overflow**, the chief drawback of linear array-based queues.
> ✅ Memory per node = `sizeof(data) + sizeof(pointer)`. On 64-bit systems, an `int` node uses **12 bytes**; on 32-bit, **8 bytes**.
> ✅ **Time complexity of DISPLAY** for both stack and queue linked list is **O(n)** because it requires a full traversal.
> ✅ Always draw pointer diagrams and state tables in exam answers — they earn easy partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trees and Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trees and Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Trees — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Trees)**
> A **Tree** is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a finite set of one or more **nodes** such that there is a special node called the **root**, and the remaining nodes are partitioned into $n \geq 0$ disjoint subsets $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, each of which is itself a tree. The subsets $T_i$ are called the **subtrees** of the root.

A tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n-1$ edges, and there exists **exactly one unique path** between any two nodes.

### Tree Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Root** | The topmost node with no parent |
| **Leaf / Terminal Node** | A node with no children (degree = 0) |
| **Internal Node** | A node with at least one child |
| **Edge** | The connecting link between a parent and a child |
| **Degree of a Node** | Number of children of that node |
| **Degree of a Tree** | Maximum degree among all its nodes |
| **Depth of a Node** | Length of the path from the **root** to that node |
| **Height of a Node** | Length of the longest path from that node to a **leaf** |
| **Height of a Tree** | Height of the root node |
| **Level** | All nodes at the same depth form one level (root is at level 0) |
| **Sibling** | Nodes that share the same parent |
| **Ancestor / Descendant** | Any node on the path from root to a given node / vice-versa |
| **Subtree** | A tree formed by a node and all its descendants |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Imagine a **family tree** or an **organization chart**. The CEO sits at the top (root). Under the CEO are managers (children). Under each manager are team leads (grandchildren), and so on. Leaves are employees with no one reporting to them. Each person (except the CEO) has exactly **one parent above** them. This one-parent-only rule is what makes a tree a **tree** and not a general graph — there are **no cycles**. You can never go back to your ancestor by following edges.

### 1.1.1 Binary Tree — Special Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Tree**: A tree in which each node has **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.

### 1.1.2 Binary Search Tree (BST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BST Property**: For every node $X$ in the tree: all keys in the **left subtree** of $X$ are **less than** the key of $X$, and all keys in the **right subtree** of $X$ are **greater than** the key of $X$. This property must hold **recursively** for every node.

### 1.1.3 AVL Tree (Self-Balancing BST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AVL Tree**: A height-balanced BST where, for every node, the **balance factor** $BF = h_L - h_R$ satisfies $-1 \leq BF \leq +1$. Here $h_L$ and $h_R$ are the heights of the left and right subtrees respectively.

### 1.1.4 B-Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B-Tree of order $m$**: A self-balancing search tree in which each node can contain **multiple keys** and **multiple children**. It is widely used in databases and filesystems because it minimizes disk I/O by maximizing the number of keys per node.

### 1.1.5 Heap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heap**: A **complete binary tree** satisfying the **heap property**: in a **Max-Heap**, every parent $\geq$ its children; in a **Min-Heap**, every parent $\leq$ its children. Used to implement **priority queues**.

---

## 1.2 Graphs — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Graph)**
> A **Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a non-linear data structure consisting of a finite, non-empty set of **vertices** $V$ (also called nodes) and a set of **edges** $E$ which represent connections between pairs of vertices.

Unlike a tree, a graph may have **no root**, may contain **cycles**, and a node may be connected to **any number** of other nodes.

### Graph Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Vertex (V)** | A node in the graph |
| **Edge (E)** | A connection between two vertices |
| **Adjacent Vertices** | Two vertices joined by an edge |
| **Degree of Vertex** | Number of edges incident on it |
| **In-degree** | For directed graphs, number of incoming edges |
| **Out-degree** | For directed graphs, number of outgoing edges |
| **Path** | A sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge |
| **Simple Path** | A path with no repeated vertices |
| **Cycle** | A path that starts and ends at the same vertex |
| **Connected Graph** | There is a path between every pair of vertices |
| **Complete Graph** | Every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge ($\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ edges) |
| **Weighted Graph** | Each edge carries a numerical weight (cost, distance, time) |
| **Tree (as a Graph)** | A connected, **acyclic** graph |

### Conceptual Analogy — Social Networks

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Think of **Facebook or LinkedIn**. Each user is a **vertex (node)**. A "friend" or "connection" between two users is an **edge**. Some edges are mutual (undirected — friendship) while some are one-way (directed — Twitter "follow"). Some edges have weights (e.g., closeness or frequency of interaction). Unlike a family tree, in a social network, a person can have **many connections** (not just one parent), and you can have **cycles** (A is friend with B, B with C, and C with A). This is exactly why we need graphs and not just trees.

### 1.2.1 Types of Graphs

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Undirected Graph** | Edges have no direction; $(u, v) = (v, u)$ |
| **Directed Graph (Digraph)** | Edges have direction; $(u, v) \neq (v, u)$ |
| **Weighted Graph** | Each edge has an associated weight/cost |
| **Simple Graph** | No self-loops or parallel edges |
| **Multi-graph** | May have parallel edges between the same pair |
| **Cyclic Graph** | Contains at least one cycle |
| **Acyclic Graph** | Contains no cycles (e.g., a tree) |
| **Dense Graph** | $\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2$ |
| **Sparse Graph** | $\vert E \vert \ll \vert V \vert^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Binary Tree — Structural Properties

For a binary tree with $n$ nodes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum number of nodes at level $L$** (root at level 0) $= 2^L$
>
> **Maximum number of nodes in a tree of height $h$** $= 2^0 + 2^1 + 2^2 + \cdots + 2^h = 2^{h+1} - 1$
>
> **Minimum height of a binary tree with $n$ nodes** $= \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$
>
> **Minimum number of nodes in a binary tree of height $h$** $= h + 1$
>
> **Relation** $n_0 = n_2 + 1$, where $n_0$ = number of leaves, $n_2$ = number of nodes with degree 2.

### Detailed Reasoning for $n_0 = n_2 + 1$

Let:
- $n_0$ = number of nodes with 0 children (leaves)
- $n_1$ = number of nodes with exactly 1 child
- $n_2$ = number of nodes with exactly 2 children

**Total nodes:**
$$n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2$$

**Total edges:** Every node except the root has exactly one parent (one incoming edge):
$$E = n - 1$$

**Total edges counted from children side:** Each node with 1 child contributes 1 edge; each node with 2 children contributes 2 edges:
$$E = n_1 + 2 n_2$$

Equating both expressions for $E$:
$$n - 1 = n_1 + 2 n_2$$

Substituting $n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2$:
$$n_0 + n_1 + n_2 - 1 = n_1 + 2 n_2$$
$$\boxed{n_0 = n_2 + 1}$$

---

## 2.2 AVL Tree — Balance Factor Analysis

For any node $X$:
$$BF(X) = h_L(X) - h_R(X)$$
where $h_L, h_R$ are heights of left and right subtrees. **Valid range**: $-1 \leq BF \leq 1$.

If $BF$ violates the range, **rotations** restore balance:

| Case | Rotation Type |
|---|---|
| LL (Inserted in Left-Left) | Single Right Rotation |
| RR (Inserted in Right-Right) | Single Left Rotation |
| LR (Inserted in Left-Right) | Left-Right Rotation (double) |
| RL (Inserted in Right-Left) | Right-Left Rotation (double) |

**Minimum number of nodes** $N(h)$ in an AVL tree of height $h$:
$$N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$$
with base cases $N(0) = 1$ and $N(1) = 2$.

---

## 2.3 Heap — Index Relationships (1-Based Array)

If a node is stored at index $i$ in an array (1-based indexing):

| Relationship | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Parent of $i$** | $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$ |
| **Left child of $i$** | $2i$ |
| **Right child of $i$** | $2i + 1$ |

For 0-based indexing: Parent $= \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, Left $= 2i+1$, Right $= 2i+2$.

**Time Complexity of Heap Operations:**
- Insert: $O(\log n)$
- Extract-Max/Min: $O(\log n)$
- Heapify: $O(\log n)$
- Build Heap (Bottom-Up): $O(n)$
- Heap Sort: $O(n \log n)$

---

## 2.4 Graph Representation — Comparison

| Property | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | $V \times V$ matrix | List per vertex |
| **Space** | $O(V^2)$ | $O(V + E)$ |
| **Edge query $(u, v)$** | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg(u))$ |
| **All neighbors of $u$** | $O(V)$ | $O(\deg(u))$ |
| **Add edge** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Best for** | Dense graphs | Sparse graphs |

For an undirected graph, the adjacency matrix is **symmetric**: $A[u][v] = A[v][u]$.

**Total edges from matrix:** Sum of entries $= 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$ (undirected), or $= \vert E \vert$ (directed).

---

## 2.5 Graph Traversal — Time Complexity

| Algorithm | Data Structure Used | Time | Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** (Breadth-First Search) | Queue | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V)$ |
| **DFS** (Depth-First Search) | Stack (or recursion) | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V)$ |

---

## 2.6 Spanning Tree & MST Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spanning Tree**: A subgraph that is a **tree** and connects **all vertices** with the **minimum possible number of edges** ($V - 1$).
>
> **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)**: A spanning tree of a **weighted** graph with **minimum total edge weight**.

**Key formulas:**
- A connected graph with $V$ vertices has a spanning tree with $V - 1$ edges.
- **Cycle Property**: For any cycle, the edge with the **largest weight** does **NOT** belong to the MST.
- **Cut Property**: For any cut, the edge with the **smallest weight** crossing the cut **belongs** to the MST.

**Number of spanning trees** in a complete graph $K_n$ (by Cayley's theorem): $n^{n-2}$.

---

## 2.7 MST Algorithms Comparison

| Property | Prim's Algorithm | Kruskal's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Grows one tree, adds nearest vertex | Adds edges in ascending weight order |
| **Data Structure** | Min-Heap (Priority Queue) | Union-Find (Disjoint Set) |
| **Best For** | Dense graphs | Sparse graphs |
| **Time Complexity (with PQ + Union-Find)** | $O(E \log V)$ | $O(E \log E)$ |
| **Edge Selection** | Edge from tree to nearest non-tree vertex | Globally smallest edge that doesn't form a cycle |

---

## 2.8 Shortest Path Formulas

For a graph with non-negative edge weights, the **shortest distance** from source $s$ to any vertex $v$:

$$\text{dist}(s, v) = \min_{(s, \dots, v) \in \text{paths}} \sum_{(u,w) \in \text{path}} w(u, w)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangle Inequality for shortest paths**:
> $$\text{dist}(s, v) \leq \text{dist}(s, u) + w(u, v)$$

**Bellman Optimality (Dynamic Programming)** — fundamental recurrence:
$$\text{dist}_k(s, v) = \min \Big[ \text{dist}_{k-1}(s, v),\ \text{dist}_{k-1}(s, u) + w(u, v) \Big]$$

---

## 2.9 Shortest Path Algorithms Comparison

| Algorithm | Single/Multi Source | Negative Weights | Time Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dijkstra's** | Single-source (SSSP) | **No** | $O((V + E) \log V)$ with min-heap | Non-negative weights |
| **Bellman-Ford** | Single-source | **Yes** | $O(V \cdot E)$ | Detects negative cycles |
| **Floyd-Warshall** | All-pairs (APSP) | **Yes** (no neg. cycles) | $O(V^3)$ | Dense graphs, all pairs |
| **BFS** | Single-source | Only unweighted | $O(V + E)$ | Unweighted graphs |

---

## 2.10 Engineering Real-World Applications

| Data Structure | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Binary Search Tree** | In-memory dictionaries, symbol tables in compilers |
| **AVL / Red-Black Tree** | `std::map`, `TreeMap` in Java, database indexes |
| **B-Tree / B+ Tree** | File systems (NTFS, ext4), Database indexes (MySQL InnoDB) |
| **Heap (Priority Queue)** | CPU scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, Huffman coding |
| **Trie** | Autocomplete, IP routing tables, spell-checkers |
| **Graph (BFS)** | Shortest path on unweighted maps, peer-to-peer networks |
| **Graph (DFS)** | Topological sort, cycle detection, strongly connected components |
| **Dijkstra's** | Google Maps, GPS navigation, network routing (OSPF) |
| **MST (Kruskal/Prim)** | Network cable design, circuit layout, clustering algorithms |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Binary Tree Traversals — Exhaustive Example

Consider the following binary tree (used throughout this section):

```
            A
          /   \
         B     C
        / \   / \
       D   E F   G
```

Let us derive each traversal **one node at a time**.

### 3.1.1 Preorder Traversal (Root → Left → Right)

```
Algorithm: PREORDER(node)
    if node = NULL return
    VISIT(node)
    PREORDER(node.left)
    PREORDER(node.right)
```

**Step-by-step application:**

Starting at root A:
- Visit **A**, then recurse into left subtree (rooted at B)
  - Visit **B**, then recurse into left subtree (rooted at D)
    - Visit **D** (no children, both subtrees empty)
  - Back to B, recurse into right subtree (rooted at E)
    - Visit **E**
- Back to A, recurse into right subtree (rooted at C)
  - Visit **C**, then left subtree (rooted at F)
    - Visit **F**
  - Back to C, then right subtree (rooted at G)
    - Visit **G**

$$\boxed{\text{Preorder: } A \to B \to D \to E \to C \to F \to G}$$

### 3.1.2 Inorder Traversal (Left → Root → Right)

Starting at A:
- Recurse into left subtree (B)
  - Recurse into left subtree (D)
    - Left of D is empty, Visit **D**
  - Visit **B**
  - Recurse into right subtree (E) → Visit **E**
- Visit **A**
- Recurse into right subtree (C)
  - Visit **F**, then **C**, then **G**

$$\boxed{\text{Inorder: } D \to B \to E \to A \to F \to C \to G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (BST only)**: For a **Binary Search Tree**, **inorder traversal always produces keys in sorted (ascending) order**. This is the basis for many KTU questions.

### 3.1.3 Postorder Traversal (Left → Right → Root)

Following the recursive logic:
$$\boxed{\text{Postorder: } D \to E \to B \to F \to G \to C \to A}$$

### 3.1.4 Level-Order Traversal (BFS on tree)

Using a queue, we process level by level:
$$\boxed{\text{Level-Order: } A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G}$$

---

## 3.2 AVL Tree — Step-by-Step Insertion & Rotation

### Insert keys in order: 30, 20, 10

**Step 1: Insert 30**
```
30
```
Balanced.

**Step 2: Insert 20**
```
    30
   /
  20
```
$BF(30) = h_L - h_R = 1 - 0 = 1$ → Still balanced.

**Step 3: Insert 10**
```
    30
   /
  20
 /
10
```
$BF(30) = 2 - 0 = 2$ → **Imbalance!**

The insertion occurred in the **Left-Left (LL)** subtree of 30. Apply a **Single Right Rotation** around 30.

**After Right Rotation (around 30):**
```
    20
   /  \
  10   30
```
Now all balance factors are within $[-1, +1]$.

### Why Right Rotation Works

The pivot of rotation is the **left child** of the unbalanced node (here, 20). After rotation:
- The pivot (20) becomes the new root of this subtree.
- The old root (30) becomes the right child of the pivot.
- The right subtree of pivot (originally empty) becomes the left subtree of the old root.

---

## 3.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Exhaustive Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem**: Given a weighted graph $G$ with **non-negative** edge weights and a source vertex $s$, find the shortest distance from $s$ to every other vertex.

**Algorithm (using min-priority queue):**
```
1. dist[s] = 0; dist[v] = ∞ for all other v
2. Insert (dist, vertex) for all vertices into min-heap
3. While heap is not empty:
     (d, u) = EXTRACT-MIN(heap)
     For each neighbor v of u:
         if dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v]:
             dist[v] = dist[u] + w(u,v)
             DECREASE-KEY(heap, v, dist[v])
```

### Worked Example

Consider the graph (vertices 1–5):

```
         (4)
       1 --- 2
       |   / |
    (2)|  /8 |(7)
       | /   |
       3 --- 4
         (3)   (5)
          \   /
           \ / (1)
            5
```

Wait, let me redo this more carefully:

```
            1 ─── 4 ─── 5
            │  ╲     ╱
            2    6   3
            │  ╱     ╲
            3 ──── 4
```
Actually, let me use a cleaner classic textbook example:

```
         (2)        (4)
       A ──────► B ──────► C
       │          │ ╲         │
       │(1)       │(3) (5)    │(1)
       ▼          ▼     ╲     ▼
       D ──────► E ──────► F
            (6)       (2)
```

Let me use the **classic KTU-style example** (5 vertices, source = A):

**Adjacency list with weights:**
- A: B(2), D(1)
- B: C(4), E(3), F(5)
- C: F(1)
- D: E(6)
- E: F(2)
- F: (none)

**Step-by-step Dijkstra's execution:**

| Step | Extract (vertex, dist) | Updates | Final dist after step |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initialize | dist = [A:0, B:∞, C:∞, D:∞, E:∞, F:∞] | — |
| 1 | (A, 0) | dist[B]=2, dist[D]=1 | A:0, B:2, C:∞, D:1, E:∞, F:∞ |
| 2 | (D, 1) | dist[E]=1+6=7 | A:0, B:2, C:∞, D:1, E:7, F:∞ |
| 3 | (B, 2) | dist[C]=2+4=6, dist[E]=min(7,2+3)=5, dist[F]=2+5=7 | A:0, B:2, C:6, D:1, E:5, F:7 |
| 4 | (E, 5) | dist[F]=min(7,5+2)=7 (unchanged) | A:0, B:2, C:6, D:1, E:5, F:7 |
| 5 | (C, 6) | dist[F]=min(7,6+1)=7 (unchanged) | A:0, B:2, C:6, D:1, E:5, F:7 |
| 6 | (F, 7) | No neighbors | A:0, B:2, C:6, D:1, E:5, F:7 |

**Final shortest distances from A:**

$$\boxed{\text{dist}(A) = 0,\ \text{dist}(B) = 2,\ \text{dist}(C) = 6,\ \text{dist}(D) = 1,\ \text{dist}(E) = 5,\ \text{dist}(F) = 7}$$

**Time complexity**: $O((V + E) \log V)$ with binary min-heap.

---

## 3.4 Kruskal's MST Algorithm — Step-by-Step

**Algorithm:**
```
1. Sort all edges in non-decreasing order of weight
2. Initialize MST as empty
3. For each edge (u, v) in sorted order:
     if FIND(u) ≠ FIND(v):   // no cycle
         add (u, v) to MST
         UNION(u, v)
4. Stop when MST has V - 1 edges
```

### Worked Example

Edges sorted by weight:

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| (A, B) | 2 |
| (A, C) | 3 |
| (B, C) | 4 |
| (B, D) | 5 |
| (C, D) | 6 |
| (A, D) | 7 |

Vertices: {A, B, C, D} → MST needs 3 edges.

| Step | Edge | Weight | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (A,B) | 2 | Accept | FIND(A)≠FIND(B) |
| 2 | (A,C) | 3 | Accept | FIND(A)≠FIND(C) |
| 3 | (B,C) | 4 | **Reject** | FIND(B)=FIND(C) (cycle) |
| 4 | (B,D) | 5 | Accept | FIND(B)≠FIND(D); MST now has 3 edges → stop |

**Total weight** = $2 + 3 + 5 = 10$

$$\boxed{\text{MST edges: } \{A-B,\ A-C,\ B-D\},\ \text{Total weight} = 10}$$

---

## 3.5 Prim's MST Algorithm — Step-by-Step

**Algorithm:**
```
1. Choose arbitrary start vertex; add to MST
2. Repeatedly add the cheapest edge that connects
   a vertex in MST to a vertex outside MST
3. Stop when all vertices are included
```

### Worked Example (Same graph as Kruskal's)

Start at vertex **A**. Tree vertices: {A}, non-tree: {B, C, D}.

| Step | Tree Set | Available edges (u,v) | Min-weight chosen edge | Added to MST |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | {A} | (A,B)=2, (A,C)=3, (A,D)=7 | (A,B), w=2 | {A,B} |
| 2 | {A,B} | (A,C)=3, (A,D)=7, (B,C)=4, (B,D)=5 | (A,C), w=3 | {A,B,C} |
| 3 | {A,B,C} | (A,D)=7, (B,D)=5, (C,D)=6 | (B,D), w=5 | {A,B,C,D} — done |

**MST edges:** {(A,B), (A,C), (B,D)}, **Total weight = 10** ✓ (same as Kruskal's)

---

## 3.6 BFS vs DFS — Comparative Trace

Graph (4 vertices): V = {1, 2, 3, 4}, E = {(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (3,4)}

### BFS Trace (Start = 1)
| Step | Queue | Visited | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [1] | {1} | — |
| 2 | [] | {1} | **1** |
| 3 | [2,3] | {1,2,3} | — |
| 4 | [3] | {1,2,3} | **2** |
| 5 | [4] | {1,2,3,4} | **3** |
| 6 | [] | {1,2,3,4} | **4** |

$$\boxed{\text{BFS order: } 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4}$$

### DFS Trace (Start = 1)
| Step | Stack | Visited | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [1] | {1} | — |
| 2 | [] | {1} | **1** |
| 3 | [2,3] (push 2,3) | {1,2,3} | — |
| 4 | [2] | {1,2,3} | **3** (LIFO) |
| 5 | [2,4] (push 4 from 3) | {1,2,3,4} | — |
| 6 | [2] | {1,2,3,4} | **4** |
| 7 | [] | {1,2,3,4} | **2** |

$$\boxed{\text{DFS order: } 1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2}$$

---

## 3.7 Binary Tree vs General Tree — Structural Comparison

| Feature | Binary Tree | General Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Max children per node** | 2 | Any number $k$ |
| **Subtrees are ordered** | Yes (left/right) | No (unordered) |
| **Recursive definition** | Left subtree + Right subtree | Sequence of subtrees |
| **Implementation** | Easy with two pointers | Requires list/array of children |
| **Traversal** | In/Pre/Post/Level | Preorder, Postorder |

---

## 3.8 Time Complexity Summary Table (All Key Algorithms)

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tree traversals (In/Pre/Post) | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack | $h$ = height |
| Level-order (BFS on tree) | $O(n)$ | $O(w)$ queue | $w$ = max width |
| BST Search/Insert/Delete (avg) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Random BST |
| BST Search/Insert/Delete (worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Skewed tree |
| AVL Search/Insert/Delete | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Always balanced |
| Heap Insert/Delete | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | Heapify down/up |
| Build Heap | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Bottom-up |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | In-place |
| BFS / DFS (Graph) | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V)$ | — |
| Dijkstra's | $O((V+E) \log V)$ | $O(V)$ | Min-heap |
| Bellman-Ford | $O(V \cdot E)$ | $O(V)$ | Detects neg. cycles |
| Floyd-Warshall | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V^2)$ | All pairs |
| Prim's MST | $O(E \log V)$ | $O(V)$ | Min-heap |
| Kruskal's MST | $O(E \log E)$ | $O(V + E)$ | Union-Find |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — READ CAREFULLY**
> 1. **Confusing inorder with preorder** in BST questions → Inorder of a BST is **sorted**, not the preorder.
> 2. **Forgetting balance factor recheck** after every rotation in AVL → Always recompute $BF$ for all ancestor nodes.
> 3. **Using Dijkstra with negative weights** → Dijkstra **fails** for negative weights. Use Bellman-Ford instead.
> 4. **Off-by-one in MST edge count** → MST has **exactly $V - 1$ edges**. Stop early or late → lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing time complexities** of Prim's vs Kruskal's → Prim's is $O(E \log V)$ (with heap), Kruskal's is $O(E \log E)$.
> 6. **Wrong adjacency matrix indexing** → Matrix is 1-indexed in algorithm but 0-indexed in C arrays — document this clearly.
> 7. **In tree traversals, drawing an incorrect recursion tree** → Marks deducted for incomplete trace.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a Binary Search Tree (BST). State its property and explain how an inorder traversal of a BST yields a sorted sequence.

**Model Answer:**

> A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree in which for every node $X$:
> - All keys in the **left subtree** of $X$ are **strictly less than** the key of $X$.
> - All keys in the **right subtree** of $X$ are **strictly greater than** the key of $X$.
>
> **Why inorder gives sorted output**: In inorder traversal, we recursively visit Left → Root → Right. By the BST property, every node in the left subtree is smaller, the root sits between, and every node in the right subtree is larger. Therefore, when applied recursively, inorder visits keys in **strictly non-decreasing (sorted) order**.
>
> **Example**: For BST with keys {20, 10, 30, 5, 15}, inorder yields **5 → 10 → 15 → 20 → 30**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between BFS and DFS. Mention the data structures used and state their time complexities.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Explores **level by level** (breadth-wise) | Explores **as deep as possible** before backtracking |
| **Data Structure** | **Queue** (FIFO) | **Stack** (LIFO) or recursion |
| **Completeness** | Complete (finds goal if exists) | Not complete in infinite graphs |
| **Optimality** | Optimal for **unweighted** graphs | Not necessarily optimal |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V + E)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(V)$ (queue storage) | $O(V)$ (stack/recursion depth) |
| **Use Case** | Shortest path in unweighted graphs | Topological sort, cycle detection |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Trees Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define an **AVL tree**. Insert the keys **50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 10** into an initially empty AVL tree. Show the tree after each insertion and clearly state the type of rotation (if any) performed at each step.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the final AVL tree obtained, write the **preorder, inorder, and postorder** traversals. Also compute the **height** of the tree and verify the **AVL property** (balance factor $\in [-1, 1]$) for every node.

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**AVL Definition**: A self-balancing BST where for every node, the difference between heights of left and right subtrees (balance factor $BF$) is at most **1** in absolute value.

**Initial state**: empty tree.

**Insert 50**: 
```
50
```
$BF(50)=0$ ✓

**Insert 30**: 
```
  50
 /
30
```
$BF(50)=1$ ✓

**Insert 70**: 
```
  50
 /  \
30   70
```
$BF(50)=0$ ✓

**Insert 20**: 
```
    50
   /  \
  30   70
 /
20
```
$BF(30)=1$, $BF(50)=1$ ✓ (still balanced)

**Insert 40**: 
```
       50
      /  \
    30    70
   /  \
  20  40
```
$BF(30)=0$, $BF(50)=1$ ✓

**Insert 10**: 
```
       50
      /  \
    30    70
   /  \
  20  40
 /
10
```
$BF(20)=1$, $BF(30)=2$ → **Imbalance at 30!**

Type of imbalance: Insertion went into **left subtree of left child** → **LL case → Single Right Rotation at 30**.

After rotation:
```
       50
      /  \
    20    70
   /  \
  10  30
        \
        40
```
$BF(20)=1$, $BF(50)=1$, $BF(30)=-1$, $BF(70)=0$ ✓

**Insert 70** (already present), then **Insert 40** (already present). Next:

**Insert 40**: already exists. (Skip if unique keys.)

Now inserting final key... actually continuing with **insert 40 again** would be duplicate. Let me re-examine — the sequence was 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 10.

After insertion of 10 and rotation, all balance factors are valid. The AVL tree is:
```
       50
      /  \
    20    70
   /  \
  10  30
        \
        40
```

**Rotations summary**: 1 Single Right Rotation at node 30 during insertion of 10.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

Using the final AVL tree from part (a):

**Preorder (Root → Left → Right)**:
$$\text{Preorder: } 50 \to 20 \to 10 \to 30 \to 40 \to 70$$

**Inorder (Left → Root → Right)**:
$$\text{Inorder: } 10 \to 20 \to 30 \to 40 \to 50 \to 70$$

**Postorder (Left → Right → Root)**:
$$\text{Postorder: } 10 \to 40 \to 30 \to 20 \to 70 \to 50$$

**Height of tree**:
- Height of leaves 10, 40, 70 = 0
- Height of 30 = 1, 20 = 2, 50 = 3

**Height = 3**

**Balance Factor Verification**:
| Node | $h_L$ | $h_R$ | $BF = h_L - h_R$ | Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 30 | 0 | 1 | -1 | ✓ |
| 40 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 20 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ✓ |
| 70 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 50 | 2 | 0 | 2 | ✗ |

Wait — let me recompute. After rotation, the left subtree of 50 has root 20, which has height 2 (10 is at level 2 below 50, 30 is at level 2, 40 at level 3). So $h_L(50) = 2$, $h_R(50) = 0$ → $BF(50) = 2$. This indicates another imbalance!

Let me re-verify the tree structure after rotation carefully:

After single right rotation at 30:
- 20 becomes the new root of the subtree
- 10 remains as 20's left child
- 30 becomes 20's right child
- 40 remains as 30's right child

```
        50
       /  \
     20    70
    /  \
  10   30
        \
        40
```

Recomputing heights:
- $h(10) = 0$, $h(40) = 0$, $h(70) = 0$
- $h(30) = 1$ (40 is its right child)
- $h(20) = 2$ (10 at level 0, 30 at level 1 → height 2 means longest path from 20 to leaf = 2)

Wait, height convention: **height of a leaf = 0** (some texts use 1). Let me use the convention that height = number of edges on longest path from node to leaf.

- $h(10)=0, h(40)=0, h(70)=0$
- $h(30)=\max(h(\text{left of 30}), h(\text{right of 30})) + 1 = \max(-1, 0) + 1 = 1$
- $h(20)=\max(h(10), h(30)) + 1 = \max(0, 1) + 1 = 2$
- $h(50)=\max(h(20), h(70)) + 1 = \max(2, 0) + 1 = 3$

Balance factors:
- $BF(10) = 0$
- $BF(40) = 0$
- $BF(70) = 0$
- $BF(30) = h_L - h_R = 0 - 0 = 0$  *(Wait, 30 has only right child 40, no left child)* → Actually $BF(30) = -1$ if left height = -1 (null) and right = 0. Depending on convention.

Using **null height = -1**, **leaf height = 0**:
- $BF(30) = -1 - 0 = -1$ ✓
- $BF(20) = 0 - 1 = -1$ ✓
- $BF(50) = 2 - 0 = 2$ ✗ — **Still imbalanced!**

This means we need a **second rotation**! The rotation at 30 fixed the local imbalance at 30, but the grandparent 50 is now imbalanced. Since the imbalance at 50 is in the **LL direction** (left-left subtree), we need a **Right Rotation at 50**.

After Right Rotation at 50:
- 20 becomes the new root of this subtree
- 10 stays as left of 20
- 30 stays as right of 20
- 40 stays as right of 30
- 50 becomes right child of 20
- 70 becomes right child of 50 (or stays)

Final tree:
```
        20
       /  \
     10    30
            \
            50
           /  \
         (40)  70
```

Wait, this isn't right either. Let me carefully re-apply the right rotation at 50:

Right rotation at 50:
- Pivot = left child of 50 = 20
- 20 becomes new root
- 50 becomes right child of 20
- The right subtree of 20 (i.e., subtree rooted at 30) becomes the left subtree of 50

After Right Rotation at 50:
```
        20
       /  \
     10    50
          /  \
        30   70
          \
          40
```

Let me recompute heights:
- $h(10)=0, h(40)=0, h(70)=0$
- $h(30)=1$ (right child 40)
- $h(50) = \max(h(30), h(70)) + 1 = \max(1, 0) + 1 = 2$
- $h(20) = \max(h(10), h(50)) + 1 = \max(0, 2) + 1 = 3$

Balance factors:
- $BF(20) = h(10) - h(50) = 0 - 2 = -2$ ✗

Hmm, still imbalanced! This is because **only one rotation doesn't suffice** when the imbalance is in the deeper subtree.

Let me restart this trace more carefully:

**Insert 50**: Tree = {50}, $h=0$. No imbalance.

**Insert 30**: Tree has root 50, left child 30. $BF(50) = 1$. OK.

**Insert 70**: Tree balanced. $BF(50) = 0$. OK.

**Insert 20**: Tree:
```
    50
   /  \
 30    70
 /
20
```
$BF(50)=1$, $BF(30)=1$. OK.

**Insert 40**: Tree:
```
       50
      /  \
    30    70
   /  \
  20  40
```
$BF(50)=1$ (still), $BF(30)=0$. OK.

**Insert 10**: Tree becomes:
```
         50
        /  \
      30    70
     /  \
    20  40
   /
  10
```
Now check from inserted node upward:
- $BF(20) = 1$ (left=10, right=null)
- $BF(30) = 0 - 0 = 0$? Wait, $h_L(30) = 1$ (subtree has 10), $h_R(30) = 0$ (subtree has 40). So $BF(30) = 1 - 0 = 1$. Actually using the formula: $BF = h_L - h_R$ where $h_L$ is height of left subtree (containing 10, height 0 if just leaf 10, but actually subtree rooted at 20 has height 1). 

Let me use the convention $BF = h_L - h_R$ where heights are number of nodes on longest path from the node down.

- $h(10) = 1$ (single node)
- $h(20) = 2$ (20 → 10)
- $h(40) = 1$
- $h(30) = 3$ (30 → 20 → 10 is the longest)
- $h(70) = 1$
- $h(50) = 4$ (50 → 30 → 20 → 10)

Wait this is the convention where height = number of nodes. With this:
- $BF(10) = 0$
- $BF(20) = h(\text{left}) - h(\text{right}) = 1 - 0 = 1$ (left=10, no right)
- $BF(40) = 0$
- $BF(30) = h(20) - h(40) = 2 - 1 = 1$
- $BF(70) = 0$
- $BF(50) = h(30) - h(70) = 3 - 1 = 2$ ✗ **Imbalance!**

So the imbalance is at node 50 with $BF = +2$. The insertion path from 50 is: 50 → 30 → 20 → 10, which is **Left-Left-Left**. This is an **LL case** (the first two steps are both left).

**Single Right Rotation at 50**:
- Pivot = 30 (left child of 50)
- 30 becomes new root
- 50 becomes right child of 30
- Right subtree of 30 (originally rooted at 40) becomes left subtree of 50

After rotation:
```
        30
       /  \
     20    50
    /     /  \
   10   40   70
```
- $h(10)=1, h(40)=1, h(70)=1$
- $h(20) = 2$
- $h(50) = \max(h(40), h(70)) + 1 = \max(1,1) + 1 = 2$
- $h(30) = \max(h(20), h(50)) + 1 = \max(2,2) + 1 = 3$

**Balance factors**:
- $BF(10) = 0$, $BF(40) = 0$, $BF(70) = 0$
- $BF(20) = h(10) - 0 = 1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ (using $h(\text{null}) = 0$ convention)
- $BF(50) = h(40) - h(70) = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓
- $BF(30) = h(20) - h(50) = 2 - 2 = 0$ ✓

**All balance factors are valid!** The AVL tree is balanced.

**Final AVL Tree**:
```
        30
       /  \
     20    50
    /     /  \
   10   40   70
```

**Final Traversal Answers (corrected)**:

Preorder: **30 → 20 → 10 → 50 → 40 → 70**

Inorder: **10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50 → 70** (sorted, as expected for BST)

Postorder: **10 → 20 → 40 → 70 → 50 → 30**

Height of tree = 3 (using edge-counting: longest path from 30 to a leaf has 2 edges; if counting nodes, height = 3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Graphs Focus

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the following **weighted undirected graph**, find the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** using **Kruskal's algorithm**. Show each step clearly and compute the total weight. Also show the adjacency matrix representation of the graph.

Edges: (A,B,4), (A,C,3), (A,D,2), (B,C,1), (B,D,5), (C,D,8)

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using **Dijkstra's algorithm**, find the **shortest path and distance** from vertex **A** to all other vertices in the graph from part (a). Show a step-by-step trace and verify that the path A→B→C has a shorter distance than the direct edge A→C (if applicable).

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Adjacency Matrix** (4×4):

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| **B** | 4 | 0 | 1 | 5 |
| **C** | 3 | 1 | 0 | 8 |
| **D** | 2 | 5 | 8 | 0 |

(Matrix is symmetric since graph is undirected.)

**Kruskal's Algorithm — Step-by-step**:

**Step 0**: Sort all edges by weight (ascending):

| Order | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (B, C) | 1 |
| 2 | (A, D) | 2 |
| 3 | (A, C) | 3 |
| 4 | (A, B) | 4 |
| 5 | (B, D) | 5 |
| 6 | (C, D) | 8 |

Vertices: {A, B, C, D}. MST must have $V - 1 = 3$ edges.

| Step | Edge | Weight | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Cycle? | Action | MST So Far |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (B,C) | 1 | B | C | No | Accept | {(B,C)} |
| 2 | (A,D) | 2 | A | D | No | Accept | {(B,C), (A,D)} |
| 3 | (A,C) | 3 | A | C (via B) | No | Accept | {(B,C), (A,D), (A,C)} |

Now MST has 3 edges → **STOP**.

**Final MST edges**: {(B,C), (A,D), (A,C)}
**Total weight** $= 1 + 2 + 3 = \mathbf{6}$

**MST Visual**:
```
       B
      / \
   (1)   (3)
    /     \
   C       A
            \
            (2)
             \
              D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Cycle/Cut Property**:
> - Edge (A,B) with weight 4: was the **largest edge in the cycle** A→B→C→A. Therefore, by **Cycle Property**, it does NOT belong to MST. ✓
> - Edge (B,D) with weight 5: in the cycle A→B→D→A, it is the largest. Excluded. ✓

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** — Source = A:

**Initialization**:
- dist[A] = 0
- dist[B] = dist[C] = dist[D] = ∞
- Priority Queue: {(A, 0), (B, ∞), (C, ∞), (D, ∞)}
- Visited = {}

**Step 1**: Extract **A** with dist = 0.
- Relax A→B: dist[B] = min(∞, 0 + 4) = 4
- Relax A→C: dist[C] = min(∞, 0 + 3) = 3
- Relax A→D: dist[D] = min(∞, 0 + 2) = 2

State after Step 1:
| Vertex | dist | Prev |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 2 | A |

**Step 2**: Extract **D** with dist = 2 (smallest in PQ).
- Relax D→B: dist[B] = min(4, 2 + 5) = **4** (unchanged, 7 > 4)
- Relax D→C: dist[C] = min(3, 2 + 8) = 3 (unchanged, 10 > 3)

State after Step 2:
| Vertex | dist | Prev |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 2 | A |

**Step 3**: Extract **C** with dist = 3.
- Relax C→B: dist[B] = min(4, 3 + 1) = **4** (unchanged, 4 = 4)
- Relax C→D: dist[D] = 2 (unchanged)

State after Step 3:
| Vertex | dist | Prev |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 2 | A |

**Step 4**: Extract **B** with dist = 4.
- Relax B→C: dist[C] = 3 (unchanged)
- Relax B→D: dist[D] = 2 (unchanged)

State after Step 4:
| Vertex | dist | Prev |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 2 | A |

**Final Shortest Distances from A**:

$$\boxed{\text{dist}(A, A) = 0,\ \text{dist}(A, B) = 4,\ \text{dist}(A, C) = 3,\ \text{dist}(A, D) = 2}$$

**Shortest Paths (reconstructed via Prev array)**:
- A → D: Direct, distance **2**
- A → C: Direct, distance **3**
- A → B: Direct, distance **4** (tied with A→D→B path of length 2+5=7; direct is shorter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of part (b) question**: The question asks whether A→B→C is shorter than direct A→C.
> - Distance A→B→C = 4 + 1 = **5**
> - Distance A→C (direct) = **3**
> - **Conclusion**: The direct path A→C is **shorter** than A→B→C. The intermediate path is not beneficial here.

**Algorithm Complexity**: With binary min-heap, Dijkstra's runs in $O((V + E) \log V) = O((4 + 6) \log 4) = O(10 \cdot 2) = O(20)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trees and Graphs**

### **Trees — Key Facts**
- **Tree with $n$ nodes** has exactly $n - 1$ edges.
- **Binary Tree**: max 2 children per node.
- **BST property**: left < root < right (recursively).
- **Inorder of BST = sorted sequence**.
- **AVL**: balance factor $BF = h_L - h_R \in [-1, 1]$; uses rotations (LL, RR, LR, RL).
- **Heap**: complete binary tree + heap property (max-heap or min-heap); array-indexed.
- **Four traversals**: Preorder (Root-L-R), Inorder (L-Root-R), Postorder (L-R-Root), Level-order (BFS).
- **$n_0 = n_2 + 1$** for any binary tree (leaves = nodes with 2 children + 1).

### **Graphs — Key Facts**
- **Graph $G = (V, E)$**; may have cycles, no designated root.
- **Adjacency matrix**: $O(V^2)$ space, $O(1)$ edge query; good for dense graphs.
- **Adjacency list**: $O(V + E)$ space; good for sparse graphs.
- **BFS uses Queue, DFS uses Stack/recursion**; both $O(V + E)$.
- **Spanning tree** has $V - 1$ edges; MST has minimum total weight.
- **Cycle property**: heaviest edge in a cycle is NOT in MST.
- **Cut property**: lightest edge across a cut IS in MST.
- **Prim's**: grows one tree; uses min-heap; $O(E \log V)$.
- **Kruskal's**: edge-based; uses Union-Find; $O(E \log E)$.
- **Dijkstra's**: SSSP with **non-negative** weights; $O((V+E)\log V)$.
- **Bellman-Ford**: SSSP with **negative weights allowed**; $O(VE)$; detects negative cycles.
- **Floyd-Warshall**: APSP; $O(V^3)$; uses dynamic programming.

### **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid**
- ❌ Using Dijkstra on graphs with negative weights.
- ❌ Forgetting that MST has exactly $V-1$ edges.
- ❌ Confusing preorder with inorder in BST-related questions.
- ❌ Not rechecking balance factor of all ancestors after an AVL rotation.
- ❌ Treating undirected graph's adjacency matrix as asymmetric (it must be symmetric).
- ❌ Reporting time complexity without specifying the data structure used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Binary Trees - Tree Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Tree (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Binary Tree** $T$ is a finite set of nodes such that $T$ is either **empty**, or it consists of a distinguished node called the **root** and two disjoint binary trees $T_L$ (left subtree) and $T_R$ (right subtree). Each node can have **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.

In the hierarchical structure of a binary tree, the position of nodes is formalized as follows:

- **Root** – The topmost node with no parent.
- **Parent / Child** – A node $u$ is the parent of $v$ if there is a directed edge $u \to v$.
- **Siblings** – Nodes that share the same parent.
- **Leaf (External Node)** – A node with **zero** children.
- **Internal Node** – A node with **at least one** child.
- **Ancestor / Descendant** – Recursive extensions of the parent/child relationship.
- **Level of a node** – The length of the path from the root (root is at level **0**).
- **Height ($h$) of a tree** – The maximum level of any node in the tree.
- **Depth of a node** – The length of the path from the root to that node.
- **Degree of a node** – The number of children the node has (0, 1, or 2).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Think of a binary tree like a **royal family lineage chart**, where each monarch (node) can have **at most two direct heirs** — the elder and the younger. The founder of the dynasty is the *root*, generations form *levels*, and the most distant generation represents the *leaves*. Operations on a tree are exactly the kinds of questions you'd ask of such a chart: *"Who comes first in the line of succession?"* (preorder), *"Who is in the middle?"* (inorder), *"Who are the youngest heirs with no children?"* (postorder), *"Who is at every generation?"* (level-order).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Binary Trees (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The KTU syllabus frequently tests the **classification** of binary trees. Master the table below:

| Type | Defining Property | Example Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full (Strict / Proper) Binary Tree** | Every node has **0 or 2** children (no node has exactly 1 child) | $n$ internal $\Rightarrow$ $n+1$ leaves |
| **Complete Binary Tree** | All levels completely filled **except possibly the last**, which is filled **left to right** | Height $h$, nodes between $2^h$ and $2^{h+1}-1$ |
| **Perfect Binary Tree** | **All internal nodes have 2 children** AND all leaves are at the **same level** | Exactly $2^{h+1}-1$ nodes |
| **Balanced Binary Tree** | Height $h = \Theta(\log n)$ (e.g., AVL, Red-Black) | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ for complete |
| **Degenerate (Pathological) Tree** | Each parent has **only one** child; behaves like a linked list | $n$ nodes, height $n-1$ |
| **Skewed Tree (Left / Right)** | A degenerate tree leaning entirely to one side | $h = n-1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** A *complete* binary tree is **NOT** the same as a *full* binary tree. A complete tree can have nodes with one child (as long as the last level fills left to right), but a full tree **never** has a single-child node.

## 1.4 Abstract Data Type (ADT) of a Binary Tree

```text
ADT: BinaryTree
Data : A finite set of nodes; if non-empty, consists of root R,
       left subtree TL, and right subtree TR.
Operations:
  create()                → Initialize empty tree
  isEmpty(T)              → Boolean : is T empty?
  root(T)                 → Element of root node
  leftChild(T) / rightChild(T) → Subtree
  makeTree(root, TL, TR)  → Construct tree from root + 2 subtrees
  traverse(T)             → Visit every node exactly once
  insert(T, x) / delete(T, x)  → Modify the tree (BST-specific)
  search(T, key)          → Locate a node by key
  height(T)               → Integer : tree height
  nodeCount(T)            → Integer : total nodes
  mirror(T)               → Swap left and right subtrees recursively
```

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties & Equations

A binary tree is a **recursive** data structure. Almost every operation on it can be expressed as a recurrence. Below are the high-yield formulas that KTU repeatedly tests in Part A and Part B:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Let $n$ = total nodes, $n_0$ = leaves (0 children), $n_1$ = nodes with 1 child, $n_2$ = nodes with 2 children, $h$ = height, $L$ = number of leaves.

### 2.1.1 Maximum Number of Nodes at Level $i$

The root sits at level $0$. At every subsequent level, the number of nodes can **at most double**:

$$N_{\max}(i) = 2^{i}, \quad i \geq 0$$

### 2.1.2 Maximum Number of Nodes in a Tree of Height $h$

This is a geometric series sum from $i = 0$ to $i = h$:

$$N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

### 2.1.3 Minimum Number of Nodes in a Tree of Height $h$

A degenerate (skewed) tree of height $h$ contains exactly one node per level:

$$N_{\min}(h) = h + 1$$

### 2.1.4 The Universal Identity (Most Tested Formula)

For **any** binary tree:

$$n_0 = n_2 + 1$$

**Why?** Count edges two ways. Let $E$ be the number of edges. Since every node except the root has exactly one incoming edge, $E = n - 1$. But each node with one child contributes 1 edge and each with two children contributes 2 edges, so $E = n_1 + 2n_2$. Also $n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2$. Equating:

$$n_0 + n_1 + n_2 - 1 = n_1 + 2n_2 \;\Rightarrow\; n_0 = n_2 + 1$$

### 2.1.5 Height Bounds for $n$ Nodes

$$\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \;\leq\; h \;\leq\; n - 1$$

The lower bound corresponds to a perfect/complete tree; the upper bound to a degenerate tree.

### 2.1.6 Strictly Binary Tree Property

If every internal node has **exactly 2** children:

$$L = I + 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad n_0 = n_2 + 1 \text{ (with } n_1 = 0\text{)}$$

Total nodes $n = 2I + 1$ (always odd).

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Tree Traversals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A traversal is an **ordering** in which every node of the tree is visited **exactly once**. KTU heavily tests these four traversals and their reconstructions.

| Traversal | Visit Order | Mnemonic | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preorder** (NLR) | **N**ode → **L**eft → **R**ight | "**P**rocess first, then recurse" | Copying a tree, prefix expression evaluation |
| **Inorder** (LNR) | **L**eft → **N**ode → **R**ight | "**I**n the middle" | BST → yields **sorted** sequence |
| **Postorder** (LRN) | **L**eft → **R**ight → **N**ode | "**P**ostpone" (children first) | Deleting a tree, postfix expression evaluation |
| **Level-Order** (BFS) | Level **0**, then **1**, then **2**, … | "**B**readth first" | Level-wise processing, BFS |

## 2.3 Binary Search Tree (BST) — Ordered Binary Tree

A **Binary Search Tree** is a binary tree that satisfies the **BST invariant** for every node $x$:

$$\text{key}(\text{left subtree of } x) \;\leq\; \text{key}(x) \;\leq\; \text{key}(\text{right subtree of } x)$$

This invariant enables **$O(h)$** search, insertion, and deletion, where $h$ is the tree height.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Constraint / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Max nodes at level $i$ | $2^{i}$ | Root at level 0 |
| 2 | Max total nodes for height $h$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | All levels full |
| 3 | Min total nodes for height $h$ | $h + 1$ | Degenerate tree |
| 4 | Universal identity | $n_0 = n_2 + 1$ | **Holds for ANY binary tree** |
| 5 | Strict binary tree leaves | $L = I + 1$ | $n_1 = 0$ |
| 6 | Total nodes (strict) | $n = 2I + 1$ | Always odd |
| 7 | Height lower bound | $h \geq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Height upper bound | $h \leq n - 1$ | Degenerate |
| 9 | BST search cost | $T(h) = T(h-1) + O(1)$ | $O(h)$ |
| 10 | Number of null pointers | $n + 1$ | $2n$ child slots $-$ $n-1$ used edges |
| 11 | Array index of children (heap) | $\text{left} = 2i, \; \text{right} = 2i+1$ | 1-indexed |
| 12 | Array index of parent (heap) | $\text{parent} = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | 1-indexed |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Binary trees are not an academic exercise — they power:
> - **Compiler Design** — Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) use traversals for code generation; **expression trees** are evaluated via postorder (postfix) and inorder (infix) traversals.
> - **Databases** — B-Trees and B+ Trees (generalizations of balanced binary trees) provide $O(\log n)$ disk-based indexing.
> - **Network Routing** — Decision trees and Huffman coding trees (built via greedy priority queues) compress data in ZIP, JPEG, MP3.
> - **Operating Systems** — Process scheduling with priority heaps (complete binary trees) gives $O(\log n)$ insert/extract-min.
> - **AI / Machine Learning** — Decision tree learning (ID3, C4.5, CART) for classification; binary space partitioning in computer graphics.
> - **File Systems** — Directory structures and version control (git) use tree-based data models.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Nodes in a Binary Tree of Height $h$

**Given:** A binary tree $T$ of height $h$ (root at level 0, deepest leaf at level $h$).

**To Prove:** $N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$.

**Step 1 — Establish the level-wise upper bound.**

A binary tree can have at most **2 children** per node. Hence the number of nodes at any level is **at most double** that of the previous level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(0) &= 1 \quad \text{(the root alone)} \\
N(1) &\leq 2 \cdot N(0) = 2 \\
N(2) &\leq 2 \cdot N(1) = 4 \\
\vdots \\
N(i) &\leq 2 \cdot N(i-1) \leq 2^{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

By **strong induction**, $N(i) \leq 2^{i}$ for all $i \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Sum over all levels from $0$ to $h$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\max}(h) &= \sum_{i=0}^{h} N(i) \;\leq\; \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

We use the closed form for a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$, ratio $r = 2$, and $n = h+1$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= a \cdot \frac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1} = 1 \cdot \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{h+1} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Show the bound is tight.**

A **perfect binary tree** of height $h$ has $2^i$ nodes at level $i$ (no slack), so the bound is **achieved**:

$$\boxed{N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The Universal Identity $n_0 = n_2 + 1$

**Given:** A binary tree with $n$ nodes, $n_0$ leaves, $n_1$ single-child nodes, $n_2$ two-child nodes.

**Step 1 — Count all nodes.**

$$
n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2
$$

**Step 2 — Count edges by definition.**

Every node except the root has exactly **one incoming edge** (its parent pointer). So:

$$E = n - 1$$

**Step 3 — Count edges by the children's contribution.**

Each node with 1 child contributes 1 edge; each node with 2 children contributes 2 edges; leaves contribute 0:

$$E = 0 \cdot n_0 + 1 \cdot n_1 + 2 \cdot n_2 = n_1 + 2n_2$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $E$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - 1 &= n_1 + 2n_2 \\
(n_0 + n_1 + n_2) - 1 &= n_1 + 2n_2 \\
n_0 - 1 &= n_2 \\
n_0 &= n_2 + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{n_0 = n_2 + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity holds for **every** binary tree, even those with nodes having exactly 1 child. The $n_1$ term cancels out completely — which is why the formula is so universally useful.

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of NULL Pointers in a Binary Tree

**Given:** A binary tree stored with explicit `left` and `right` pointers in each of the $n$ nodes.

**Step 1 — Count total pointer slots.**

Each node reserves 2 pointer slots. Total slots: $2n$.

**Step 2 — Count used pointer slots.**

Each non-root node is pointed to by exactly one edge, so exactly $n - 1$ slots are filled.

**Step 3 — Count null slots.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nulls} &= \text{Total slots} - \text{Used slots} \\
&= 2n - (n - 1) \\
&= n + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{Number of NULL pointers} = n + 1}$$

This result is the cornerstone of the **Threaded Binary Tree** optimization, where these null slots are repurposed as inorder predecessor/successor threads.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example Problem

**Problem:** A binary tree has $n_1 = 19$ nodes of degree 1, and the total number of nodes is $n = 52$. Find the number of leaf nodes $n_0$ and the number of degree-2 nodes $n_2$.

**Step 1 — Set up the system of equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= n_0 + n_1 + n_2 \\
n_0 &= n_2 + 1 \quad \text{(universal identity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n_1 = 19$ and $n = 52$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
52 &= n_0 + 19 + n_2 \\
33 &= n_0 + n_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $n_0 = n_2 + 1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
33 &= (n_2 + 1) + n_2 \\
33 - 1 &= 2 n_2 \\
n_2 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n_0$.**

$$
n_0 = n_2 + 1 = 16 + 1 = 17
$$

**Verification:** $n_0 + n_1 + n_2 = 17 + 19 + 16 = 52$ ✓

$$\boxed{n_0 = 17, \quad n_2 = 16}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Traversal Reconstruction

**Problem:** Given the preorder sequence `[A, B, D, E, C, F]` and inorder sequence `[D, B, E, A, F, C]`, reconstruct the binary tree.

**Step 1 — Identify the root from preorder.**

The **first** element of preorder is always the root:

$$\text{Root} = A$$

**Step 2 — Locate $A$ in the inorder sequence.**

Inorder splits the tree into **left subtree elements** (those appearing before $A$) and **right subtree elements** (those appearing after $A$):

$$\underbrace{[D, B, E]}_{\text{left subtree of }A} \;\; A \;\; \underbrace{[F, C]}_{\text{right subtree of }A}$$

**Step 3 — Recurse on the left subtree.**

Preorder of left: `[B, D, E]`, Inorder of left: `[D, B, E]`. Root of left = $B$.

In inorder of left, $B$ splits: $[D] \; B \; [E]$.

**Step 4 — Recurse on the right subtree.**

Preorder of right: `[C, F]`, Inorder of right: `[F, C]$. Root of right = $C$.

In inorder of right, $C$ splits: $[F] \; C \; []$. So $F$ is the **left child** of $C$.

**Step 5 — Reconstructed tree.**

```
            A
          /   \
         B     C
        / \   /
       D   E F
```

**Step 5 — Verification via postorder.**

Postorder = left → right → root = `D, E, B, F, C, A` ✓ (matches the expected postorder traversal of this tree).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Binary Tree Structure (Sample BST)

The following Mermaid block renders a sample **Binary Search Tree** with integer keys inserted in the order `[50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80]`. Each node shows the value, and the structure captures the **left/right child invariant**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeR((50))
    nodeL((30))
    nodeRR((70))
    nodeLL((20))
    nodeLR((40))
    nodeRL((60))
    nodeRRR((80))

    nodeR --- nodeL
    nodeR --- nodeRR
    nodeL --- nodeLL
    nodeL --- nodeLR
    nodeRR --- nodeRL
    nodeRR --- nodeRRR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root `50` has left child `30` and right child `70` (the BST property: $30 \leq 50 \leq 70$). The full BST satisfies *inorder* = `20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80` (sorted ascending).

## 4.2 Recursive Traversal Flow — Function Call Stack

The diagram below visualizes how the **call stack** behaves during a recursive `inorder` traversal. Each frame represents a function call waiting for its left subtree to complete.

```mermaid
graph TD
    callR["CALL inorder 50"]
    callL["CALL inorder 30"]
    callLL["CALL inorder 20"]
    visitD["VISIT 20 PRINT 20"]
    returnL["RETURN from inorder 20"]
    visitB["VISIT 30 PRINT 30"]
    callLR["CALL inorder 40"]
    visitE["VISIT 40 PRINT 40"]
    returnR["RETURN from inorder 40"]
    callRR["CALL inorder 70"]
    callRL["CALL inorder 60"]
    visitF["VISIT 60 PRINT 60"]
    callRRR["CALL inorder 80"]
    visitG["VISIT 80 PRINT 80"]
    visitA["VISIT 50 PRINT 50"]

    callR --> callL
    callL --> callLL
    callLL --> visitD
    visitD --> returnL
    returnL --> visitB
    visitB --> callLR
    callLR --> visitE
    visitE --> returnR
    returnR --> callRR
    callRR --> callRL
    callRL --> visitF
    visitF --> callRRR
    callRRR --> visitG
    visitG --> visitA
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** When tracing traversals in the exam, mentally simulate the recursion. The **visit** order follows the **L → N → R** sequence: as soon as a `VISIT` node is reached, that node is printed. The output of this traversal is `20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80` — which is the **sorted sequence**, confirming the BST inorder property.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BST Operation Pipeline

The following block diagram represents the **engineering pipeline** for BST operations (Search / Insert / Delete). It maps how data flows through the algorithmic layers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp["INPUT KEY X"]
    cmp{"COMPARE X WITH CURRENT NODE"}
    lt["GO LEFT SUBTREE"]
    rt["GO RIGHT SUBTREE"]
    fnd["KEY FOUND RETURN NODE"]
    nf["KEY NOT FOUND RETURN NULL"]
    ins["INSERT X AS NEW LEAF"]
    del["DELETE NODE INVOKE CASES"]
    c1["CASE 1 LEAF REMOVE NODE"]
    c2["CASE 2 ONE CHILD BYPASS"]
    c3["CASE 3 TWO CHILDREN INORDER SUCCESSOR"]
    out["OUTPUT RESULT"]

    inp --> cmp
    cmp --> lt
    cmp --> rt
    lt --> cmp
    rt --> cmp
    cmp --> fnd
    cmp --> nf
    cmp --> ins
    cmp --> del
    del --> c1
    del --> c2
    del --> c3
    fnd --> out
    nf --> out
    ins --> out
    c1 --> out
    c2 --> out
    c3 --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in exams:** The **three deletion cases** are the single most-tested BST operation in KTU. Memorize them as:
> - **Case 1 (Leaf):** Simply remove the node and set the parent's pointer to `NULL`.
> - **Case 2 (One child):** Replace the deleted node with its only child.
> - **Case 3 (Two children):** Replace the node's data with its **inorder successor** (or predecessor), then delete that successor from the subtree.

## 4.4 Iteration Order Visualization — Pre, In, Post, Level

A single subtree is used to demonstrate all four traversals on the same tree:

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \
    D   E
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    na((A))
    nb((B))
    nc((C))
    nd((D))
    ne((E))

    na --- nb
    na --- nc
    nb --- nd
    nb --- ne
```

**Traverse this tree:**

| Traversal | Order | Sequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preorder** (NLR) | A → B → D → E → C | `A, B, D, E, C` |
| **Inorder** (LNR) | D → B → E → A → C | `D, B, E, A, C` |
| **Postorder** (LRN) | D → E → B → C → A | `D, E, B, C, A` |
| **Level-Order** (BFS) | A → B → C → D → E | `A, B, C, D, E` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Shortcut — The "Step-Out" Rule:**
> - **Preorder:** Print on the way **down** (when you first arrive at a node).
> - **Inorder:** Print on the way **out of the left subtree, before the right**.
> - **Postorder:** Print on the way **up** (after both subtrees are done).
> - **Level-Order:** Use a **queue**; print, then enqueue left child, then right child.
