# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Full vs Complete Trees:** A "full" binary tree means every node has 0 or 2 children. A "complete" binary tree means levels are filled left-to-right. These are NOT synonymous. Marks are lost when students interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the Base Case in Recursive Algorithms:** When writing recursive functions for traversals, height, or node count, students forget the `if (root == NULL) return 0;` or equivalent base case. This produces stack-overflow logical errors.
> 3. **Off-by-One in Height Computation:** The root alone has height 0 (KTU convention) or height 1 (some textbooks). Always **state your convention** in the answer to avoid ambiguity.
> 4. **Mixing Up BST Inorder with "Sorted" Claim:** The inorder traversal of a BST is sorted **only** if all keys are distinct and the BST property strictly holds. If duplicates are allowed with a specific policy, the inorder may not be sorted.
> 5. **Deletion Case 3 Mistakes:** When deleting a node with two children, students often forget to **also delete the inorder successor** from its original position, leading to duplicated data in the tree.
> 6. **Skipping Edge Cases:** Empty tree, single-node tree, and skewed tree are the three most important edge cases. Examiners love to test these.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a **binary tree**. State and prove the relation $n_0 = n_2 + 1$ for any non-empty binary tree, where $n_0$ is the number of leaf nodes and $n_2$ is the number of nodes with two children.

**Model Answer:**

A **binary tree** is a finite set of nodes which is either empty or consists of a root node and two disjoint subtrees called the **left subtree** and **right subtree**, each of which is itself a binary tree.

**Proof of $n_0 = n_2 + 1$:**

Let the binary tree have $n$ total nodes, with $n_0$ leaves, $n_1$ nodes of degree 1, and $n_2$ nodes of degree 2.

**(i)** Total nodes: $\quad n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2 \quad \text{...(1)}$

**(ii)** Counting edges: Every non-root node has exactly one parent, hence the number of edges $E = n - 1$. Also, each degree-1 node contributes 1 edge and each degree-2 node contributes 2 edges:

$$E = n_1 + 2n_2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**(iii)** Equating (i) and (ii) via $E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - 1 &= n_1 + 2n_2 \\
(n_0 + n_1 + n_2) - 1 &= n_1 + 2n_2 \\
n_0 - 1 &= n_2 \\
\boxed{n_0 &= n_2 + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**? Show the BST obtained by inserting the keys `[45, 25, 65, 15, 35, 55, 75, 5]` in that order. State the inorder traversal of the resulting tree.

**Model Answer:**

A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree in which for every node $x$, all keys in the left subtree of $x$ are **less than or equal to** the key of $x$, and all keys in the right subtree of $x$ are **greater than or equal to** the key of $x$.

**Insertions (in order):**

| Step | Inserted Key | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 45 | Becomes root |
| 2 | 25 | $25 < 45$ → left child of 45 |
| 3 | 65 | $65 > 45$ → right child of 45 |
| 4 | 15 | $15 < 45$ → left; $15 < 25$ → left child of 25 |
| 5 | 35 | $35 < 45$ → left; $35 > 25$ → right child of 25 |
| 6 | 55 | $55 > 45$ → right; $55 < 65$ → left child of 65 |
| 7 | 75 | $75 > 45$ → right; $75 > 65$ → right child of 65 |
| 8 | 5 | $5 < 45$ → left; $5 < 25$ → left; $5 < 15$ → left child of 15 |

**Resulting BST:**

```
              45
            /    \
          25      65
         /  \    /  \
       15   35 55   75
       /
      5
```

**Inorder Traversal (LNR):** Visit left subtree, then node, then right subtree. Following the BST, inorder always yields **sorted order**:

$$\text{Inorder: } 5, \; 15, \; 25, \; 35, \; 45, \; 55, \; 65, \; 75$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **four standard tree traversals** (preorder, inorder, postorder, level-order) with example. For the tree below, write all four traversals:

```
            10
          /    \
         5      20
        / \    /  \
       3   7  15   25
                \
                18
```

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four standard traversals visit every node of the tree exactly once but in different orders:

**1. Preorder (NLR):** Visit **N**ode first, then **L**eft subtree, then **R**ight subtree. Used for: copying a tree, prefix expression evaluation.

**2. Inorder (LNR):** Visit **L**eft subtree, then **N**ode, then **R**ight subtree. Used for: BST → produces sorted sequence; inorder expression tree gives infix form.

**3. Postorder (LRN):** Visit **L**eft, then **R**ight, then **N**ode. Used for: deleting a tree (children first), postfix expression evaluation.

**4. Level-Order (BFS):** Visit nodes **level by level**, left to right. Implemented using a **queue**. Used for: breadth-first processing, shortest path in unweighted trees.

**Applying to the given tree:**

**(i) Preorder (NLR):**

```
10, 5, 3, 7, 20, 15, 18, 25
```

Trace: Visit 10 → go left to 5 → visit 5 → go left to 3 → visit 3 → return → go right to 7 → visit 7 → return to 10 → go right to 20 → visit 20 → go left to 15 → visit 15 → go right to 18 → visit 18 → return to 20 → go right to 25 → visit 25.

**(ii) Inorder (LNR):**

```
3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 18, 20, 25
```

Trace: Process 3 (leftmost leaf) → visit 5 → process 7 → visit 10 → process 15 → process 18 → visit 20 → visit 25.

**(iii) Postorder (LRN):**

```
3, 7, 5, 18, 15, 25, 20, 10
```

Trace: Process 3 → process 7 → visit 5 → process 18 → visit 15 → visit 25 → visit 20 → visit 10.

**(iv) Level-Order (BFS):**

```
10, 5, 20, 3, 7, 15, 25, 18
```

Trace: Level 0: {10}; Level 1: {5, 20}; Level 2: {3, 7, 15, 25}; Level 3: {18}.

---

**Q (b)** Write a **recursive algorithm** to compute the **height of a binary tree**. Analyze its time complexity. Also write a non-recursive (iterative) algorithm for the same.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Recursive Algorithm:**

```text
Algorithm: HEIGHT(T)
Input  : Binary tree T rooted at node root
Output : Height of T (number of edges on longest path from root to leaf)

Function recursiveHeight(node):
    if node = NULL then
        return -1                              // convention: empty tree has height -1
    else
        leftH  ← recursiveHeight(node.left)
        rightH ← recursiveHeight(node.right)
        return 1 + MAX(leftH, rightH)

End Function
```

**Tracing the algorithm:**

- For an **empty** tree, height = $-1$.
- For a **single node** (root only), height = $0$.
- For a **balanced tree** of $n$ nodes, height $\approx \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

Let $T(n)$ be the time to compute the height of a tree with $n$ nodes. The algorithm makes **two recursive calls** on subtrees of total size $n - 1$, plus $O(1)$ work at the current node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2 \cdot T(k) + T(n-1-k) + O(1) \\
T(n) &= T(n-1) + O(1) \quad \text{(worst case: skewed tree, one subtree empty)} \\
T(n) &= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a balanced tree, both subtrees have $\leq n/2$ nodes:

$$
T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + O(1) \implies T(n) = O(n)
$$

In **all cases**, $T(n) = O(n)$ because we visit every node exactly once.

**Space Complexity:** $O(h)$ due to the recursion call stack, where $h$ is the height. Worst case: $O(n)$ (skewed tree). Best case: $O(\log n)$ (balanced).

**Iterative Algorithm (using level-order BFS):**

```text
Algorithm: HEIGHT_ITERATIVE(T)
Input  : Binary tree T
Output : Height of T

1.  if T.root = NULL then return -1
2.  Initialize an empty queue Q
3.  Enqueue(T.root)
4.  height ← -1
5.  while Q is not empty do
6.      levelSize ← SIZE(Q)
7.      for i ← 1 to levelSize do
8.          node ← DEQUEUE(Q)
9.          if node.left  ≠ NULL then ENQUEUE(node.left)
10.         if node.right ≠ NULL then ENQUEUE(node.right)
11.     end for
12.     height ← height + 1
13. end while
14. return height
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Recursive | Iterative (BFS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Space complexity | $O(h)$ call stack | $O(w)$ queue, $w$ = max width |
| Code length | Short, elegant | Longer, explicit |
| Risk of stack overflow | Yes (deep trees) | No |
| Best for | Balanced trees | Any tree, especially deep ones |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** Award 2 marks for the correct base case, 3 marks for the recursive logic, and 2 marks for the complexity analysis. The iterative version (if asked) carries 3-4 marks separately.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a)** Discuss the **different types of binary trees** (strict, complete, perfect, balanced, degenerate) with diagrams. For a binary tree of height $h$:
   (i) Derive the **maximum number of nodes**.
   (ii) Derive the **minimum number of nodes**.
   (iii) Show that $n_0 = n_2 + 1$ using the same height notation.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Binary Trees (with ASCII diagrams):**

**(1) Strict (Full / Proper) Binary Tree:** Every node has either **0 or 2** children.

```
     A              A
    / \            / \
   B   C          B   C
      / \        / \   \
     D   E      F   G   H
```

**(2) Complete Binary Tree:** All levels full **except possibly the last**, which is filled **strictly left to right**.

```
       A
      / \
     B   C
    / \  /
   D  E F
```

**(3) Perfect Binary Tree:** All internal nodes have 2 children **and** all leaves are at the **same depth**.

```
        A
      /   \
     B     C
    / \   / \
   D   E F   G
```

**(4) Balanced Binary Tree:** Height $h = O(\log n)$.

```
       A
      / \
     B   C
    / \
   D   E
```

**(5) Degenerate (Skewed) Binary Tree:** Each internal node has exactly **1** child. The tree behaves like a linked list.

```
  A
   \
    B
     \
      C
       \
        D
```

**(i) Derivation of Maximum Number of Nodes:**

The number of nodes **at level $i$** is at most $2^i$ (since each node can have at most 2 children, and level 0 has 1 node).

Total nodes for height $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\max}(h) &= \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i} = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{h+1} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

**(ii) Derivation of Minimum Number of Nodes:**

The minimum is achieved by a **degenerate (skewed) tree** of height $h$:

$$\boxed{N_{\min}(h) = h + 1}$$

For example, height 0: 1 node; height 1: 2 nodes; height 2: 3 nodes; … ; height $h$: $h+1$ nodes.

**(iii) Universal Identity in terms of height:**

Recall the derivation:

- $n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2$ — count all nodes.
- $E = n - 1$ — count edges by parent pointers.
- $E = n_1 + 2 n_2$ — count edges by children.
- Equating: $n_0 = n_2 + 1$.

In terms of height bounds:

$$
h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor - 1, \quad h_{\max} = n - 1
$$

$$\boxed{n_0 = n_2 + 1 \text{ holds for any binary tree, independent of height}}$$

---

**Q (b)** Explain the **three cases of deletion in a Binary Search Tree (BST)**. Delete the node with key **50** from the BST given below, showing each step:

```
            50
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \    /  \
      20  40  60   80
```

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Three Cases of BST Deletion:**

**Case 1 — Deleting a Leaf Node (No Children):**
- Simply remove the node.
- Set the parent's corresponding child pointer to `NULL`.
- No restructuring required.
- Example: Deleting node `20` from the above tree — just set `30.left = NULL`.

**Case 2 — Deleting a Node with One Child:**
- Replace the deleted node with its **only child**.
- The child takes the deleted node's position in the tree.
- The BST property is automatically preserved.
- Example: If we had a node `35` as the only child of `30`, deleting `30` would mean `35` takes its place.

**Case 3 — Deleting a Node with Two Children (Most Complex):**
- Find the node's **inorder successor** (the smallest node in the right subtree) — or alternatively, the inorder predecessor.
- **Copy** the successor's data into the node being deleted.
- **Recursively delete** the successor from the right subtree (which will always be a Case 1 or Case 2 deletion since the successor has no left child).
- This preserves the BST property.

**Worked Example — Deleting 50 from the Given Tree:**

Node `50` has **two children** → **Case 3** applies.

**Step 1 — Find the Inorder Successor of 50:**
The inorder successor is the **leftmost node in the right subtree** of 50. Starting at `70`, go left to `60`. `60` has no left child, so `60` is the inorder successor.

**Step 2 — Copy the successor's data into node 50:**

```
            60          ← was 50, now 60 (successor's value)
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \       \
      20  40      80
```

**Step 3 — Delete the original successor node `60` from the right subtree:**

Node `60` is a **leaf** (no children) → **Case 1** applies. Set `70.left = NULL`.

**Step 4 — Final Tree After Deletion of 50:**

```
            60
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \       \
      20  40      80
```

**Verification (Inorder Traversal of New Tree):** 20, 30, 40, 60, 70, 80 — **sorted** ✓

**Complexity Analysis of BST Deletion:**

| Step | Cost |
| :--- | :--- |
| Search for the node to delete | $O(h)$ |
| Find inorder successor (Case 3 only) | $O(h)$ |
| Restructure pointers | $O(1)$ |
| **Total** | $O(h)$ |

For a balanced BST, $h = O(\log n)$, so deletion is $O(\log n)$. For a skewed BST, $h = O(n)$, so deletion degrades to $O(n)$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid — "LCD Rule":**
> - **L**ook up the node to be deleted.
> - **C**ount its children: 0, 1, or 2.
> - **D**ecide the deletion case accordingly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Binary Trees & Tree Operations:**

- [ ] A binary tree is **recursive** by definition: empty, or root + left subtree + right subtree.
- [ ] The **maximum number of nodes** at level $i$ is $2^i$ (root at level 0).
- [ ] The **maximum total nodes** for height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$ — derived from a geometric series.
- [ ] The **minimum total nodes** for height $h$ is $h + 1$ (degenerate tree).
- [ ] The **universal identity** $n_0 = n_2 + 1$ holds for **every** binary tree — derived by double-counting edges.
- [ ] A **strict (full) binary tree** has $n_1 = 0$; leaves = internal nodes + 1; total nodes = $2I + 1$ (always odd).
- [ ] A **complete binary tree** fills levels left-to-right; a **perfect** tree has all leaves at the same depth.
- [ ] **Preorder (NLR):** Process node first → used for tree copy, prefix expressions.
- [ ] **Inorder (LNR):** Left → node → right → BST inorder is **sorted** (ascending).
- [ ] **Postorder (LRN):** Children first → used for tree deletion, postfix expressions.
- [ ] **Level-order (BFS):** Uses a **queue**; processes level by level.
- [ ] A binary tree stored with explicit pointers has exactly $n + 1$ **NULL pointers**.
- [ ] BST search, insert, delete all run in $O(h)$ time — $O(\log n)$ balanced, $O(n)$ skewed.
- [ ] BST deletion has **3 cases**: leaf (Case 1), one child (Case 2), two children (Case 3, use inorder successor/predecessor).
- [ ] For a **perfect** binary tree of height $h$: nodes = $2^{h+1} - 1$, leaves = $2^h$.
- [ ] The **height lower bound** for $n$ nodes: $h \geq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ for $n \geq 1$.
- [ ] The **height upper bound** for $n$ nodes: $h \leq n - 1$ (pathological/degenerate).
- [ ] Recursive traversal complexity: **time $O(n)$**, **space $O(h)$**.
- [ ] The **inorder successor** of a node is the leftmost node in its right subtree.
- [ ] The **inorder predecessor** of a node is the rightmost node in its left subtree.
- [ ] **Threaded binary trees** repurpose the $n+1$ NULL pointers as inorder predecessor/successor threads.
- [ ] Array representation of a complete binary tree: children of node $i$ are at $2i$ and $2i+1$ (1-indexed); parent at $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary Tree Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Binary Tree Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Binary Tree** is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, conventionally referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**. Formally, it is defined as a finite set of nodes that is either **empty** or consists of a distinguished node called the **root** together with two disjoint binary trees called the **left subtree** and the **right subtree**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every binary tree in the KTU 2024 curriculum is assumed to be a **strictly ordered rooted tree**, where the left and right child positions carry distinct meaning. A node with zero children is termed a **leaf node**, and the **height** of an empty tree is conventionally defined as **−1**, while a single-node tree has a height of **0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive Definition (KTU Standard):** A binary tree $T$ is a structure defined as:
> - $T = \emptyset$ (empty tree), **OR**
> - $T = (\text{Root}, T_L, T_R)$ where $T_L$ and $T_R$ are themselves binary trees (the left and right subtrees).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **family genealogy chart** in which every ancestor has at most two direct descendants (an elder and a younger branch). The most senior ancestor sits at the **top (root)**, and the family line branches downward into left and right sub-branches. The people with no further descendants are the **leaves**, while the people in between are **internal nodes**.

Another everyday analogy is the **knock-out tournament bracket** of a sports tournament:
- Each match is a **node**.
- The winner advances to become the parent of the next round.
- The two players entering a match are the **left child** and **right child**.

**Why “Binary”?** Because the structure restricts every node to a maximum of two branches — this restriction is what makes binary trees computationally tractable, enabling efficient search, sorting, and hierarchical indexing (BSTs, Heaps, Expression Trees, Huffman Trees, etc.).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Definitions)

| Term | Formal Definition | Standard Value/Note |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Root** | Topmost node with no parent | Unique for a non-empty tree |
| **Leaf / External Node** | Node with both children absent | Has degree **0** |
| **Internal Node** | Node with at least one child | Degree **1** or **2** |
| **Edge** | Connection between parent and child | A tree with $n$ nodes has exactly **$n−1$ edges** |
| **Depth of node $x$** | Number of edges from root to $x$ | Root depth = **0** |
| **Height of node $x$** | Number of edges on longest path from $x$ to a leaf | Leaf height = **0** |
| **Height of Tree $T$** | Height of the root node | Empty tree height = **−1** |
| **Level** | All nodes at the same depth | Root is at **Level 0** |
| **Degree of node $x$** | Number of children of $x$ | For a binary tree: degree $\in \{0, 1, 2\}$ |
| **Size of Tree $T$** | Total number of nodes | Denoted $n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Note:** In KTU examinations, a binary tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n-1$ edges. This identity is frequently asked as a **2-mark short question** and serves as the foundation for several traversal proofs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Standard Representation Strategies

A binary tree, being an **abstract data type (ADT)**, can be physically mapped into computer memory using **two canonical methods**, both of which are explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus:

1. **Sequential (Array-based) Representation**
2. **Linked (Pointer-based) Representation**

Each strategy presents a unique trade-off between **memory efficiency, access time, and structural flexibility**.

---

## 2.2 Strategy 1: Array (Sequential) Representation

In this scheme, the nodes of the binary tree are stored in a **one-dimensional array** in a *level-order* (breadth-first) traversal sequence. The root is conventionally placed at **index 1** (the KTU standard), although the 0-indexed variant is also occasionally used.

### 2.2.1 Indexing Formulas (1-Indexed — KTU Standard)

For a node stored at array index $i$:

$$L(i) = 2i \quad \text{(Index of Left Child)}$$

$$R(i) = 2i + 1 \quad \text{(Index of Right Child)}$$

$$P(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{2} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(Index of Parent, valid for } i \geq 2 \text{)}$$

### 2.2.2 Indexing Formulas (0-Indexed Variant)

$$L(i) = 2i + 1$$

$$R(i) = 2i + 2$$

$$P(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i - 1}{2} \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** The formulas arise directly from the fact that at each level $\ell$, the maximum number of nodes is $2^{\ell}$, and these nodes occupy a contiguous block of indices. The recurrence $L(i) = 2i$ simply states that the left child of node $i$ begins the next “block” of $2^{\ell}$ nodes.

### 2.2.3 Memory Requirement for a Tree of Height $h$

The maximum number of nodes a binary tree of height $h$ can hold is:

$$n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

Therefore, the array must be allocated with at least $2^{h+1} - 1$ slots. The **wasted memory** for a **skewed (degenerate) tree** with $n$ nodes is:

$$W = (2^{h+1} - 1) - n = 2^{n} - n - 1$$

This quadratic blow-up is the **chief disadvantage** of array representation for unbalanced trees.

---

## 2.3 Strategy 2: Linked (Pointer-based) Representation

Each node is a **struct/object** of three fields:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `data` | Element type | Stores the payload value |
| `lchild` / `left` | Pointer to Node | Points to the left subtree |
| `rchild` / `right` | Pointer to Node | Points to the right subtree |

A `NULL` pointer (or `None` in Python) in `lchild` or `rchild` indicates the **absence** of that child. A global pointer `root` points to the topmost node.

### Memory Calculation for Linked Representation

For a tree of $n$ nodes:
- **Total nodes** = $n$
- **Total pointer fields** = $2n$ (each node has 2 child pointers)
- **NULL pointer count** = $n + 1$ (proved below in §3.1)
- **Used pointers** = $n - 1$ (one parent pointer per non-root node)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Left child (1-indexed) | $L(i) = 2i$ | Index |
| 2 | Right child (1-indexed) | $R(i) = 2i + 1$ | Index |
| 3 | Parent (1-indexed) | $P(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | Valid for $i \geq 2$ |
| 4 | Max nodes of height $h$ | $n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Nodes |
| 5 | Min height for $n$ nodes | $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Edges |
| 6 | Edges in a tree | $E = n - 1$ | Edges |
| 7 | NULL pointers (linked) | $N_{\text{NULL}} = n + 1$ | Pointers |
| 8 | Array waste (skewed tree) | $W = 2^{n} - n - 1$ | Cells |
| 9 | Nodes at level $\ell$ (max) | $N_\ell = 2^{\ell}$ | Nodes |
| 10 | Total nodes level 0 to $h$ | $\sum_{\ell=0}^{h} 2^{\ell} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical bars in formulas:** All absolute values and floor functions in this sheet use `\lfloor` and `\rfloor` (or `\vert`, `\mid`) — never a raw `|` character — to preserve KTU markdown rendering safety.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Binary tree representation underpins several mission-critical engineering systems:

- **Compiler Design:** Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) and Expression Trees use binary representations to evaluate arithmetic expressions.
- **Database Systems:** B-Trees and B+ Trees (generalized binary trees) power indexing in MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle.
- **Network Routing:** Huffman coding trees enable lossless data compression in JPEG, MP3, and ZIP formats.
- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling heaps are complete binary trees stored in arrays.
- **AI / Machine Learning:** Binary Decision Trees form the basis of Random Forests and Gradient Boosted classifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation: NULL Pointer Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** *In a linked representation of a binary tree with $n$ nodes, the total number of NULL pointers is exactly $n+1$.*

### Proof (KTU Board Style — Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Total Pointer Fields Available**

Each node in a binary tree has **exactly two pointer fields** (left and right). Therefore, the total number of pointer fields across all $n$ nodes is:

$$\text{Total pointer fields} = 2n$$

**Step 2 — Partition of Pointers**

Each of these $2n$ pointers falls into one of two categories:
- **Non-NULL pointers** ($P_{\text{used}}$): These point to an actual child node.
- **NULL pointers** ($P_{\text{NULL}}$): These represent the absence of a child.

Therefore:
$$2n = P_{\text{used}} + P_{\text{NULL}}$$

**Step 3 — Counting Non-NULL Pointers**

Every non-root node has **exactly one parent**, and that parent uses **one of its two pointer fields** to point to this child. Since the tree has $n - 1$ non-root nodes (because the root has no parent):

$$P_{\text{used}} = n - 1$$

**Step 4 — Solving for NULL Pointers**

Substituting into the equation from Step 2:

$$2n = (n - 1) + P_{\text{NULL}}$$

$$P_{\text{NULL}} = 2n - (n - 1) = 2n - n + 1$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{NULL}} = n + 1}$$

**Q.E.D.** ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Nodes in a Tree of Height $h$

### Step 1 — Recognize the Geometric Series

A binary tree of height $h$ has levels $0, 1, 2, \ldots, h$. The **maximum number of nodes at level $\ell$** is $2^{\ell}$, since each node at level $\ell$ can have at most 2 children, contributing $2 \cdot 2^{\ell} = 2^{\ell+1}$ nodes to the next level.

### Step 2 — Sum Across All Levels

$$n_{\max} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{h} 2^{\ell}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Series Formula

The sum of a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = 2$, and $h+1$ terms is:

$$S = a \cdot \frac{r^{h+1} - 1}{r - 1} = 1 \cdot \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1}$$

$$\boxed{n_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

### Step 4 — Inversion: Minimum Height for $n$ Nodes

Setting $2^{h+1} - 1 \geq n$ and solving for $h$:

$$2^{h+1} \geq n + 1$$

$$h + 1 \geq \log_2(n+1)$$

$$\boxed{h_{\min} = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Array vs. Linked Representation

| Property | Array Representation | Linked Representation |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Memory allocation** | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (run-time) |
| **Space efficiency (full tree)** | Optimal (no waste) | Optimal ($3n$ fields) |
| **Space efficiency (skewed tree)** | Poor — wastes $\sim 2^{n}$ cells | Optimal — uses only $2n$ pointers |
| **Access to children** | $O(1)$ via formula | $O(1)$ via pointer dereference |
| **Access to parent** | $O(1)$ via formula | $O(h)$ — must traverse up |
| **Insertion / Deletion** | Expensive — needs shifting | $O(1)$ — pointer manipulation |
| **Cache performance** | Excellent (contiguous memory) | Poor (scattered allocations) |
| **Tree height limit** | Fixed at allocation time | Unbounded — grows as needed |
| **Best suited for** | Complete / Full binary trees | Sparse / Dynamic binary trees |
| **NULL representation** | Empty array slot | NULL pointer |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** Consider the binary tree below with nodes labelled A (root), B, C, D, E, F, G in level order.
> - A is the root.
> - B and C are children of A (B = left, C = right).
> - D and E are children of B (D = left, E = right).
> - F and G are children of C (F = left, G = right).

### Step 1 — Draw the Tree Structure

The tree is a **perfect binary tree of height 2** with $n = 7$ nodes.

### Step 2 — Store in Array (1-Indexed)

| Index $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |

### Step 3 — Verify Indexing Formulas

For node **B** at $i = 2$:
- Left child: $L(2) = 2(2) = 4$ → **D** ✓
- Right child: $R(2) = 2(2)+1 = 5$ → **E** ✓
- Parent: $P(2) = \lfloor 2/2 \rfloor = 1$ → **A** ✓

For node **C** at $i = 3$:
- Left child: $L(3) = 2(3) = 6$ → **F** ✓
- Right child: $R(3) = 2(3)+1 = 7$ → **G** ✓
- Parent: $P(3) = \lfloor 3/2 \rfloor = 1$ → **A** ✓

### Step 4 — Memory Analysis

Using the linked representation, the total number of NULL pointers should be:

$$P_{\text{NULL}} = n + 1 = 7 + 1 = 8$$

**Verification:** Of the $2n = 14$ pointer fields, exactly $n - 1 = 6$ are used (one for each non-root node), leaving $14 - 6 = 8$ NULL pointers. ✓

### Step 5 — Compute Height & Levels

- Height of tree: $h = 2$
- Max possible nodes: $2^{h+1} - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 7$ (matches our $n = 7$ — confirms the tree is **perfect**)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Binary Tree → Array Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["A index 1"] --> B0["B index 2"]
    A0 --> C0["C index 3"]
    B0 --> D0["D index 4"]
    B0 --> E0["E index 5"]
    C0 --> F0["F index 6"]
    C0 --> G0["G index 7"]

    ARR["ARRAY: idx1=A idx2=B idx3=C idx4=D idx5=E idx6=F idx7=G"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Linked Node Structure (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph N1["NODE B"]
        L1["lchild ptr"]
        D1["data B"]
        R1["rchild ptr"]
    end

    subgraph N2["NODE C"]
        L2["lchild ptr"]
        D2["data C"]
        R2["rchild ptr"]
    end

    subgraph N3["NODE A (root)"]
        L3["lchild ptr"]
        D3["data A"]
        R3["rchild ptr"]
    end

    L3 --> N1
    R3 --> N2

    subgraph N4["NODE D"]
        L4["NULL"]
        D4["data D"]
        R4["NULL"]
    end

    subgraph N5["NODE E"]
        L5["NULL"]
        D5["data E"]
        R5["NULL"]
    end

    L1 --> N4
    R1 --> N5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Representation Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Binary Tree ADT"]) --> Q1{"Is the tree\ncomplete or\nfull?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> ARR["Array Representation\nCompact memory\nO(1) child access\nNo pointer overhead"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Does the tree\nchange size\nfrequently?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> LINK["Linked Representation\nDynamic memory\nO(1) insertion\nWastes 2n pointers"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Is parent\naccess\nrequired?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> TTHREAD["Threaded Tree\n(Special linked form)"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> LINK
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Skewed (Degenerate) Tree — Wasted Array Cells

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["A idx1"] --> B["B idx2"] --> C["C idx4"] --> D["D idx8"]
    ARRSLOT["Array slots 1,2,4,8 used\nSlots 3,5,6,7,9-15 are WASTED"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1.** Define a binary tree. State the two standard methods of representing a binary tree in memory.
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **binary tree** is a finite set of nodes which is either empty or consists of a root node together with two disjoint binary subtrees called the **left subtree** and the **right subtree**. Each node has **at most two children** referred to as the left child and the right child.

The two standard methods of representation are:
1. **Array (Sequential) Representation** — nodes are stored level-order in a 1D array.
2. **Linked (Pointer-based) Representation** — each node is a structure with `data`, `lchild`, and `rchild` fields.

---

### **Q2.** In a binary tree with $n$ nodes, what is the maximum number of nodes that can be present at level $\ell$? Justify.
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The maximum number of nodes at level $\ell$ of a binary tree is:

$$N_{\max}(\ell) = 2^{\ell}$$

**Justification:** At level $0$, there is exactly $1 = 2^0$ node (the root). Since each node can have at most 2 children, the number of nodes at level $\ell+1$ is at most twice that at level $\ell$. By induction, $N_{\max}(\ell) = 2^{\ell}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer **any one** of the two alternatives. Each Part B carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the formulas for finding the **left child, right child, and parent** of a node stored at index $i$ in the **array representation of a binary tree** (using 1-based indexing). Apply these formulas to a complete binary tree with 10 nodes and verify parent-child relations for three sample nodes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a binary tree with $n$ nodes, prove that the number of **NULL pointers** in its linked representation is exactly $n+1$. State any assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Array Indexing Formulas

**Derivation:**

Consider a binary tree stored in an array with 1-based indexing where the root is at index 1.

At any level $\ell$, the maximum number of nodes is $2^{\ell}$. The nodes at level $\ell$ occupy indices:

$$\text{Indices at level } \ell = 2^{\ell} \text{ to } 2^{\ell+1} - 1$$

Let node $X$ be at index $i$, residing at some level $\ell$. The **leftmost node** at level $\ell$ is at index $2^{\ell}$, and the **rightmost** at $2^{\ell+1} - 1$.

The left child of $X$ resides at level $\ell + 1$, occupying the position directly **below** $X$. The left child of the leftmost node at level $\ell$ (at index $2^{\ell}$) is at index $2^{\ell+1}$. Since $X$ is at index $i$, and the leftmost node at level $\ell$ is at index $2^{\ell}$, the offset is:

$$\text{offset} = i - 2^{\ell}$$

The left child of $X$ is therefore at index $2^{\ell+1} + \text{offset}$:

$$L(i) = 2^{\ell+1} + (i - 2^{\ell}) = 2^{\ell+1} - 2^{\ell} + i = 2^{\ell} + i = 2 \cdot 2^{\ell-1} \cdot 2 + \ldots$$

The simpler direct proof uses the recurrence on a single parent–child relationship. For root ($i=1$): left child is at index 2. For any node at index $i$, doubling the offset from the root's left child gives:

$$L(i) = 2i$$

By similar reasoning (left child + 1):

$$R(i) = 2i + 1$$

For the parent, invert: if $L(p) = i$, then $2p = i \Rightarrow p = i/2$. For odd $i$ (a right child), $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ gives the parent.

$$P(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{2} \right\rfloor$$

**Verification on a 10-Node Complete Binary Tree (level-order: 1, 2, 3, …, 10):**

| Node | Index $i$ | $L(i) = 2i$ | $R(i) = 2i+1$ | $P(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| 1 (root) | 1 | 2 | 3 | — |
| 4 | 4 | 8 | 9 | 2 |
| 5 | 5 | 10 | — (out of range) | 2 |
| 7 | 7 | — (out of range) | — (out of range) | 3 |

For node **5** at $i = 5$: $R(5) = 11$ which exceeds array size $n = 10$, so the right child is **absent** ✓ (correct, since 5 is a right child of 2 with no children in a 10-node tree).

#### Part (b) — NULL Pointer Theorem

*(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full proof. Key steps summarized here:)*

**Assumption:** The tree is a finite, rooted, ordered binary tree with $n$ nodes, represented using a linked structure where each node has exactly two pointer fields (`lchild` and `rchild`).

**Proof Sketch:**

**Step 1:** Total pointer fields $= 2n$.

**Step 2:** These split into used (non-NULL) and NULL pointers: $2n = P_{\text{used}} + P_{\text{NULL}}$.

**Step 3:** Each non-root node has exactly one incoming pointer from its parent, so $P_{\text{used}} = n - 1$.

**Step 4:** $P_{\text{NULL}} = 2n - (n-1) = n + 1$. **Q.E.D.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **linked representation** of a binary tree. Draw the linked node structure for a binary tree where the root is `A`, with left child `B` and right child `C`, and `B` has a right child `D`. Show all pointer values (NULL and non-NULL). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **array representation** and **linked representation** of a binary tree across **five key parameters**. State one scenario where each is preferred. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Linked Representation

**Definition:** In the linked representation, each node of the binary tree is dynamically allocated as a structure (or class) containing three fields: a `data` field, a pointer to the **left child** (`lchild`), and a pointer to the **right child** (`rchild`). A global pointer `root` references the topmost node.

**Node Structure:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph NODE["struct Node"]
        LC["lchild: Node*"]
        DT["data: ElementType"]
        RC["rchild: Node*"]
    end
```

**Tree Structure for the Given Problem:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["A\nL→B R→C"] --> B1["B\nL=NULL R→D"]
    A1 --> C1["C\nL=NULL R=NULL"]
    B1 --> D1["D\nL=NULL R=NULL"]
```

**Pointer Inventory:**

| Node | lchild | rchild | data |
|------|--------|--------|------|
| A (root) | → B | → C | `A` |
| B | NULL | → D | `B` |
| C | NULL | NULL | `C` |
| D | NULL | NULL | `D` |

**Count Verification:** $n = 4$ nodes. Using the NULL pointer theorem: $P_{\text{NULL}} = n + 1 = 5$. Counting from the table: B has 1 NULL, C has 2 NULLs, D has 2 NULLs → Total = **5 NULLs** ✓.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Array Representation | Linked Representation |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Memory allocation** | Static, contiguous | Dynamic, scattered |
| **Space for skewed tree** | Wasteful: $2^n$ cells for $n$ nodes | Efficient: $2n$ pointers |
| **Accessing child of node $i$** | $O(1)$ via formula $2i, 2i+1$ | $O(1)$ via pointer |
| **Accessing parent of node $i$** | $O(1)$ via $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | $O(h)$ — requires traversal |
| **Insertion / Deletion** | $O(n)$ — needs shifting | $O(1)$ — pointer rewiring |

**Preferred Scenarios:**
- **Array** is preferred for **complete or full binary trees** (e.g., binary heaps used in priority queues, Heapsort).
- **Linked** is preferred for **sparse, dynamic, or unbalanced trees** (e.g., general BSTs where insertions and deletions are frequent).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks for):**
>
> 1. **Confusing 0-indexed and 1-indexed formulas** — Writing $L(i) = 2i+1$ when the question clearly specifies root at index 1. **Always state which indexing convention you are using.**
> 2. **Forgetting the NULL pointer theorem** — In linked representation questions, examiners often allocate **2 marks** specifically for computing the NULL pointer count as $n+1$. Skipping this loses easy marks.
> 3. **Misidentifying "height" vs. "depth"** — KTU strictly follows: **depth** of root = 0, **height** of leaf = 0. Mixing these up costs **1–2 marks** in Part A.
> 4. **Not drawing NULL pointers in linked diagrams** — The examiner expects all `NULL` fields to be shown explicitly in linked structure diagrams.
> 5. **Wrong memory waste calculation for skewed trees** — Students often compute $2^n$ instead of $2^{n+1} - 1 - n$. Remember the array is sized for a **complete** tree, not just the actual nodes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **binary tree** is a hierarchical structure where each node has **at most 2 children** (left and right).
- ✅ A tree with $n$ nodes has exactly **$n - 1$ edges**.
- ✅ The **maximum number of nodes** at level $\ell$ is $2^{\ell}$.
- ✅ The **maximum number of nodes** in a tree of height $h$ is $2^{h+1} - 1$.
- ✅ **Array indexing formulas (1-indexed):** $L(i) = 2i$, $R(i) = 2i+1$, $P(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$.
- ✅ **Array indexing formulas (0-indexed):** $L(i) = 2i+1$, $R(i) = 2i+2$, $P(i) = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$.
- ✅ In **linked representation**, each node has 3 fields: `data`, `lchild`, `rchild`.
- ✅ **NULL pointer theorem:** A binary tree with $n$ nodes in linked form has exactly $n+1$ NULL pointers.
- ✅ The root has **no parent** and is conventionally at array index $1$ (KTU standard).
- ✅ An **empty tree** has height $-1$; a **single-node tree** has height $0$.
- ✅ **Array representation** is best for **complete/full binary trees** (e.g., heaps).
- ✅ **Linked representation** is best for **dynamic, sparse, or unbalanced trees** (e.g., general BSTs).
- ✅ For a **skewed tree** with $n$ nodes, array representation wastes $2^{n+1} - 1 - n$ slots.
- ✅ **In-order to Array** and **Array to In-order traversal** conversion questions are **frequently asked** in KTU Module 4 exams — practice both.
- ✅ Always **state the indexing convention** (0-indexed or 1-indexed) when answering KTU questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tree Traversals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET414 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tree Traversals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tree Traversal** is the process of visiting every node in a tree data structure exactly once in a systematic order, so that the nodes are linearised into a sequence for processing, display, evaluation, or storage. The order of visitation is deterministic and depends on the chosen traversal strategy, which in KTU 2024 Scheme is classified into two broad families:
> 1. **Depth-First Traversal (DFT)** — Inorder, Preorder, Postorder (recursion/stack-based)
> 2. **Breadth-First Traversal (BFT)** — Level Order (queue-based)

A **Binary Tree Traversal** specifically refers to the four canonical orders on a binary tree:

- **Preorder (Root → Left → Right)** — used for **copying / serialising** a tree.
- **Inorder (Left → Root → Right)** — used for **sorted output from a Binary Search Tree (BST)**.
- **Postorder (Left → Right → Root)** — used for **deletion / postfix evaluation**.
- **Level Order (Level by level, left to right)** — also called **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** on a tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student must be able to *manually traverse* a given binary tree and *write recursive procedures* to generate the traversal sequence. Construction of the tree *from* its traversals (e.g., Inorder + Preorder) is also part of the expected learning outcome.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Photo Album"

Imagine a multi-generation **family photo album** where every photo has a stamp showing *Grandparent → Parent → Child*. You are asked to print the names in a list, but you only have three rules to choose from:

- **Preorder — "Me first, then my descendants":** You record your name, then walk down the *first* branch completely (recording each ancestor's descendants first), and only then the second branch. *(Like writing a Table of Contents before chapters.)*
- **Inorder — "My left-side relatives, then me, then my right-side relatives":** You go all the way to the *leftmost* junior member, take his photo, return, take your own photo, then descend to the right. *(This is why a Binary Search Tree gives a sorted list — left = small, me = middle, right = large.)*
- **Postorder — "My descendants first, then me":** You wait until all the younger generation photos are taken, *then* your own. *(Like an HR system computing every employee's salary first before issuing the manager's payslip.)*
- **Level Order — "Row by row, like a school photograph":** Topmost row (great-grandparents), then the next row, and so on. *(Queue-based, BFS.)*

---

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Engineering

- **Compilers:** Syntax trees, expression trees and Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) are traversed to generate intermediate code, evaluate expressions, and emit register assignments.
- **Databases:** B-Tree and B+ Tree indexes are traversed (in-order and level-order) to satisfy range and equality queries.
- **Operating Systems:** Directory (folder) trees and process trees are walked using traversal algorithms.
- **AI / Search:** Game trees and decision trees use DFS/BFS variants such as minimax and alpha-beta pruning.
- **Networking:** Spanning Tree Protocol and routing trees are validated via level-order checks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Remember:**
> - Time complexity of every tree traversal on a tree with **n** nodes = **O(n)**
> - Auxiliary space = **O(h)** for recursive DFS (where *h* = height of the tree)
> - Auxiliary space = **O(w)** for Level Order (where *w* = maximum width of any level)

---

## 1.4 Binary Tree Node Convention Used in KTU Papers

Throughout the rest of this module, the **node** is taken as a structure with three fields:

$$
\text{Node} = \langle \text{Data},\ \text{LeftPtr},\ \text{RightPtr} \rangle
$$

where **LeftPtr** and **RightPtr** point to the left and right subtrees respectively, and `null` (or `NULL`) represents an empty subtree.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Recursive Depth-First Traversals

All three DFS traversals share the same skeleton: *recurse on the left subtree, then on the right subtree.* The **only** difference is **when the root is visited**.

### 2.1.1 Preorder Traversal — *Root → Left → Right*

**Operative Steps:**
1. Visit the **root node** and record/process it.
2. Recursively traverse the **left subtree** in preorder.
3. Recursively traverse the **right subtree** in preorder.

**Why this order?** The root is *pre*-recorded so that the original tree can be **reconstructed** uniquely when paired with inorder (because the root disambiguates the split point).

**Recursive Procedure (KTU 2024 Pseudocode Style):**

```
PROCEDURE Preorder(root)
    IF root ≠ NULL THEN
        VISIT(root)
        Preorder(root.left)
        Preorder(root.right)
    END IF
END PROCEDURE
```

**Applications:**
- Copying / cloning a binary tree.
- Serialising a tree to a flat file.
- Prefix expression generation from an expression tree.
- Creating a directory listing where a folder appears *before* its contents.

---

### 2.1.2 Inorder Traversal — *Left → Root → Right*

**Operative Steps:**
1. Recursively traverse the **left subtree** in inorder.
2. Visit the **root node**.
3. Recursively traverse the **right subtree** in inorder.

**Why this order?** On a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, inorder traversal produces nodes in **ascending sorted order** because the BST invariant guarantees that every left descendant is smaller and every right descendant is larger than the root.

**Recursive Procedure:**

```
PROCEDURE Inorder(root)
    IF root ≠ NULL THEN
        Inorder(root.left)
        VISIT(root)
        Inorder(root.right)
    END IF
END PROCEDURE
```

**Applications:**
- Sorted output from a BST.
- Infix expression generation (inserting parentheses as required).
- Validation that a given binary tree is a valid BST.

---

### 2.1.3 Postorder Traversal — *Left → Right → Root*

**Operative Steps:**
1. Recursively traverse the **left subtree** in postorder.
2. Recursively traverse the **right subtree** in postorder.
3. Visit the **root node**.

**Why this order?** The root is visited *last*, which is essential whenever a parent node **depends on its children's values** (e.g., computing directory sizes, evaluating postfix expressions, deleting a tree safely).

**Recursive Procedure:**

```
PROCEDURE Postorder(root)
    IF root ≠ NULL THEN
        Postorder(root.left)
        Postorder(root.right)
        VISIT(root)
    END IF
END PROCEDURE
```

**Applications:**
- Deleting a binary tree (children must be freed before the parent).
- Evaluating postfix expressions on an expression tree.
- Computing aggregate values (size, height, sum) bottom-up.

---

### 2.1.4 Level Order Traversal — *Breadth-First*

**Operative Steps:**
1. Enqueue the **root** into a queue `Q`.
2. **While** `Q` is not empty:
    - Dequeue a node, **visit** it.
    - Enqueue its **left child** (if not `NULL`).
    - Enqueue its **right child** (if not `NULL`).

**Iterative Procedure:**

```
PROCEDURE LevelOrder(root)
    IF root = NULL THEN RETURN
    Q ← empty queue
    Enqueue(Q, root)
    WHILE Q is not empty DO
        node ← Dequeue(Q)
        VISIT(node)
        IF node.left  ≠ NULL THEN Enqueue(Q, node.left)  END IF
        IF node.right ≠ NULL THEN Enqueue(Q, node.right) END IF
    END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
```

**Applications:**
- Printing a tree level by level (good for human reading).
- Shortest path computations on unweighted trees.
- Computing the maximum width of a tree.
- BFS-based search in game trees and web crawlers.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of equations and metrics a KTU 2024 examiner expects you to reproduce.**

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Value** | **Symbol Note** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of nodes in a full binary tree of height h | $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | h = height, root at level 0 |
| Maximum nodes at level L | $n_L = 2^{L}$ | L ≥ 0 |
| Time complexity of any traversal (DFS or BFS) | $T(n) = O(n)$ | n = number of nodes |
| Recursive stack depth (DFS) | $S(n) = O(h)$ | h = tree height |
| Queue memory (Level Order) | $S(n) = O(w)$ | w = max width of a level |
| Inorder of BST | Yields **ascending** sorted sequence | BST property |
| Traversal length | Exactly **n** visits | Each node visited once |
| Total recursive calls in Pre/In/Post order | $2n + 1$ calls | n data calls + (n+1) null calls |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Tree traversals are the *heartbeat* of compiler design — the front-end of GCC, Clang and the JVM all use DFS traversals on the AST to perform semantic analysis, constant folding, and code generation. Mastery of traversal order thus translates directly to *real* software-engineering value.

---

## 2.3 Constructing a Tree from Traversals (KTU Favourite Question)

A binary tree can be **uniquely reconstructed** only from a specific pair of traversals:

| **Given Traversal Pair** | **Tree is Unique?** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Inorder + Preorder | **Yes** | Preorder gives root, Inorder splits left/right |
| Inorder + Postorder | **Yes** | Postorder gives root, Inorder splits left/right |
| Preorder + Postorder | **No** | Cannot disambiguate subtree split |
| Preorder + Inorder | **Yes** | Same as Inorder+Preorder |
| Only one traversal | **No** | Multiple trees share the same single traversal |

**Algorithm Outline (Inorder + Preorder):**
1. Pick the **first node of Preorder** → this is the root.
2. Locate the root in **Inorder** → everything to its left is the left subtree, right is the right subtree.
3. Count the elements on each side → use those counts to slice the preorder into left and right halves.
4. **Recurse** on the two halves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Binary Tree Used Throughout

Consider the following binary tree, which is the *standard sample* the KTU board uses for traversal problems. It contains **7 nodes** labelled with their data values:

```
              50           <- Level 0 (Root)
            /    \
          30      70       <- Level 1
         /  \    /  \
       20   40  60   80    <- Level 2 (Leaves)
```

The tree is also a **valid Binary Search Tree (BST)** because the left descendants are smaller and right descendants are larger than every ancestor.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace — Preorder (Root → Left → Right)

We apply the recursive procedure `Preorder(root)` and follow the call stack manually.

**Step-by-step call expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Preorder}(50) &= 50,\ \text{Preorder}(30),\ \text{Preorder}(70) \\
\text{Preorder}(30) &= 30,\ \text{Preorder}(20),\ \text{Preorder}(40) \\
\text{Preorder}(20) &= 20,\ \text{Preorder}(\text{NULL}),\ \text{Preorder}(\text{NULL}) \\
\text{Preorder}(40) &= 40,\ \text{Preorder}(\text{NULL}),\ \text{Preorder}(\text{NULL}) \\
\text{Preorder}(70) &= 70,\ \text{Preorder}(60),\ \text{Preorder}(80) \\
\text{Preorder}(60) &= 60 \\
\text{Preorder}(80) &= 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Preorder Sequence (collecting the visited roots in order):**

$$
\boxed{\ 50 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 70 \rightarrow 60 \rightarrow 80\ }
$$

**Conversion Logic — Why this order?**
At each recursive call, the root is visited *before* recursing left and right. Hence the *first* element of the traversal is always the root of the current subtree, and the entire left subtree's elements appear before any of the right subtree's elements.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace — Inorder (Left → Root → Right)

**Step-by-step call expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder}(50) &= \text{Inorder}(30),\ 50,\ \text{Inorder}(70) \\
\text{Inorder}(30) &= \text{Inorder}(20),\ 30,\ \text{Inorder}(40) \\
\text{Inorder}(20) &= \text{Inorder}(\text{NULL}),\ 20,\ \text{Inorder}(\text{NULL}) \Rightarrow 20 \\
\text{Inorder}(40) &= 40 \\
\text{Inorder}(70) &= \text{Inorder}(60),\ 70,\ \text{Inorder}(80) \\
\text{Inorder}(60) &= 60 \\
\text{Inorder}(80) &= 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Inorder Sequence:**

$$
\boxed{\ 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow 60 \rightarrow 70 \rightarrow 80\ }
$$

**Conversion Logic — Why this order?**
Because the sample tree is a BST, the inorder output is **ascending-sorted**. The rule is identical for any binary tree: the inorder visits the *leftmost* node first, works back up, and finishes with the *rightmost* node. This is the canonical way to validate a BST in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Trace — Postorder (Left → Right → Root)

**Step-by-step call expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Postorder}(50) &= \text{Postorder}(30),\ \text{Postorder}(70),\ 50 \\
\text{Postorder}(30) &= \text{Postorder}(20),\ \text{Postorder}(40),\ 30 \\
\text{Postorder}(20) &= 20 \\
\text{Postorder}(40) &= 40 \\
\text{Postorder}(70) &= \text{Postorder}(60),\ \text{Postorder}(80),\ 70 \\
\text{Postorder}(60) &= 60 \\
\text{Postorder}(80) &= 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Postorder Sequence:**

$$
\boxed{\ 20 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 60 \rightarrow 80 \rightarrow 70 \rightarrow 50\ }
$$

**Conversion Logic — Why this order?**
The root (50) appears at the *end* of the sequence, and the *last* element of any postorder is always the root of the current subtree. This is what enables safe **bottom-up deletion** of the tree.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Trace — Level Order (BFS with Queue)

We trace the **queue contents** at every step.

**Initial state:** Queue = [50], Output = (empty)

| **Step** | **Dequeue** | **Visit** | **Enqueue** | **Queue after enqueue** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50 | 30, 70 | [30, 70] |
| 2 | 30 | 30 | 20, 40 | [70, 20, 40] |
| 3 | 70 | 70 | 60, 80 | [20, 40, 60, 80] |
| 4 | 20 | 20 | — | [40, 60, 80] |
| 5 | 40 | 40 | — | [60, 80] |
| 6 | 60 | 60 | — | [80] |
| 7 | 80 | 80 | — | [ ] |

**Final Level Order Sequence:**

$$
\boxed{\ 50 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 70 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 60 \rightarrow 80\ }
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
Level Order visits nodes in increasing **depth** (level). The queue is a **FIFO** structure, ensuring that a node added to the queue is processed *after* all nodes already in front of it — so older (shallower) levels are processed before newer (deeper) levels.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Four Traversals

The table below consolidates the result for our sample tree (n = 7):

| **Traversal** | **Output Sequence** | **First Node** | **Last Node** | **Root Position** | **Recursion / Data Structure** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Preorder** | 50 30 20 40 70 60 80 | Root (50) | Rightmost leaf (80) | First | Recursion / Stack |
| **Inorder** | 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 | Leftmost leaf (20) | Rightmost leaf (80) | Middle | Recursion / Stack |
| **Postorder** | 20 40 30 60 80 70 50 | Leftmost leaf (20) | Root (50) | Last | Recursion / Stack |
| **Level Order** | 50 30 70 20 40 60 80 | Root (50) | Rightmost leaf (80) | First | Iteration / Queue |

---

## 3.7 Time-Complexity Derivation (General Case)

For a tree with **n** nodes, let $T(n)$ be the time taken by any traversal.

**Recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = T(L) + T(R) + \Theta(1)
$$

where $L$ and $R$ are the number of nodes in the left and right subtrees, with $L + R = n - 1$.

**Solving by unfolding:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(L) + T(R) + c \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} c \quad \text{(each node contributes a constant)} \\
&= cn
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, in Big-O notation:**

$$
T(n) = O(n)
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
Every node is visited *exactly once* and the *constant work per node* is the visit + the two recursive calls (or two enqueue operations). Hence the cost is **linear in n**, which is provably optimal — you cannot traverse without at least *seeing* every node.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reference Binary Tree (Structural View)

The Mermaid diagram below renders the sample tree used throughout Section 3. Node IDs are alphanumeric (KTU-PREMIER safeguard) and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["50  ROOT  LVL 0"]
    nodeB["30  LVL 1"]
    nodeC["70  LVL 1"]
    nodeD["20  LEAF  LVL 2"]
    nodeE["40  LEAF  LVL 2"]
    nodeF["60  LEAF  LVL 2"]
    nodeG["80  LEAF  LVL 2"]

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeC --- nodeG
```

**Reading the diagram:** The root `50` is at the top (Level 0), the two Level-1 nodes are `30` and `70`, and the four Level-2 leaves are `20`, `40`, `60`, `80`.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Recursive Traversal

When a physical hand-drawn call-tree is required but the Mermaid engine limits a sprawling recursion tree, the **block architecture** below maps the call-and-return interactions cleanly.

```mermaid
graph TD
    main["MAIN  call Preorder 50"]
    step1["VISIT 50"]
    step2["call Preorder 30"]
    step3["VISIT 30"]
    step4["call Preorder 20"]
    step5["VISIT 20  LEAF"]
    step6["call Preorder NULL  return"]
    step7["call Preorder 40"]
    step8["VISIT 40  LEAF"]
    step9["call Preorder NULL  return"]
    step10["call Preorder 70"]
    step11["VISIT 70"]
    step12["call Preorder 60"]
    step13["VISIT 60  LEAF"]
    step14["call Preorder 80"]
    step15["VISIT 80  LEAF"]
    final["OUTPUT  50 30 20 40 70 60 80"]

    main --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> step11
    step11 --> step12
    step12 --> step13
    step13 --> step14
    step14 --> step15
    step15 --> final
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each `VISIT` block represents a print/record operation.
- Each `call Preorder NULL → return` block represents a base-case hit.
- The `OUTPUT` block at the bottom is the linearised preorder sequence.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Level Order (Queue Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph TD
    enqRoot["ENQUEUE ROOT 50"]
    loopStart{"QUEUE EMPTY ?"}
    deq["DEQUEUE FRONT NODE"]
    visit["VISIT NODE  PRINT"]
    enqL["ENQUEUE LEFT CHILD IF EXISTS"]
    enqR["ENQUEUE RIGHT CHILD IF EXISTS"]
    endFlow["END  ALL NODES VISITED"]

    enqRoot --> loopStart
    loopStart -->|NO| deq
    deq --> visit
    visit --> enqL
    enqL --> enqR
    enqR --> loopStart
    loopStart -->|YES| endFlow
```

**Interpretation:** This is the canonical BFS loop. The `loopStart` decision diamond is the only control point; the pipeline `deq → visit → enqL → enqR` is executed **once per node**, giving an overall O(n) cost.

---

## 4.4 Traversal-Order Decision Flowchart (Exam Aid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ["GIVEN A TREE  WHAT DO YOU WANT ?"]
    wantCopy["COPY  SERIALISE"]
    wantSorted["SORTED OUTPUT FROM BST"]
    wantDelete["DELETE TREE  POSTFIX EVAL"]
    wantLevel["LEVEL BY LEVEL OUTPUT"]
    preAns["USE PREORDER  ROOT L R"]
    inAns["USE INORDER  L ROOT R"]
    postAns["USE POSTORDER  L R ROOT"]
    lvlAns["USE LEVEL ORDER  BFS QUEUE"]

    startQ --> wantCopy
    startQ --> wantSorted
    startQ --> wantDelete
    startQ --> wantLevel
    wantCopy --> preAns
    wantSorted --> inAns
    wantDelete --> postAns
    wantLevel --> lvlAns
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This flowchart is **not** a syllabus item but a *high-yield recall aid*. Students who internalise this map can answer Part A 3-mark questions in under 30 seconds.
