# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). State two classical algorithms used to compute an MST and list the time complexity of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ is a spanning tree (i.e., a subgraph that is a tree containing all $n$ vertices) whose total edge weight is the **minimum possible** among all spanning trees of $G$.

**Two classical MST algorithms:**

1. **Kruskal’s Algorithm** — Time complexity: $\mathbf{O(E \log V)}$
   - Edge-greedy approach; uses Disjoint Set Union (DSU).
2. **Prim’s Algorithm** — Time complexity: $\mathbf{O(E \log V)}$ (with binary heap) or $\mathbf{O(E + V \log V)}$ (with Fibonacci heap).
   - Vertex-greedy approach; uses a min-priority queue.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a topological sort? State its time complexity and explain why it is applicable only to Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs).**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Topological sorting** is a linear ordering of vertices of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** such that for every directed edge $(u \rightarrow v)$, vertex $u$ appears before $v$ in the ordering.

**Time complexity:** $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$.

**Why only DAGs?** If the graph contains a directed cycle, no linear ordering can satisfy the property that *for every edge $(u \rightarrow v)$, $u$ precedes $v$* — because traversing the cycle eventually forces a vertex to appear before itself, a logical contradiction. Therefore, topological sort is **defined exclusively on DAGs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Apply **Dijkstra’s algorithm** to find the shortest path from vertex $A$ to all other vertices in the following graph. Show all intermediate steps.

**Graph:** Vertices = $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$
**Edges with weights:**
- $(A, B) = 5$, $(A, C) = 3$
- $(C, B) = 1$, $(C, D) = 6$, $(C, E) = 4$
- $(B, D) = 2$
- $(D, E) = 2$, $(D, F) = 5$
- $(E, F) = 7$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
$$
\text{dist}[A] = 0,\ \text{dist}[B] = \infty,\ \text{dist}[C] = \infty,\ \text{dist}[D] = \infty,\ \text{dist}[E] = \infty,\ \text{dist}[F] = \infty
$$

**Step 2 — Extract $A$ (dist = 0).** Relax edges:
- $(A, B)$: $\text{dist}[B] = 0 + 5 = 5$
- $(A, C)$: $\text{dist}[C] = 0 + 3 = 3$

State: $\{A:0, B:5, C:3, D:\infty, E:\infty, F:\infty\}$

**Step 3 — Extract $C$ (dist = 3).** Relax edges:
- $(C, B)$: $\text{dist}[B] = \min(5,\ 3+1) = 4$ ✓
- $(C, D)$: $\text{dist}[D] = 3 + 6 = 9$
- $(C, E)$: $\text{dist}[E] = 3 + 4 = 7$

State: $\{A:0, B:4, C:3, D:9, E:7, F:\infty\}$

**Step 4 — Extract $B$ (dist = 4).** Relax edges:
- $(B, D)$: $\text{dist}[D] = \min(9,\ 4+2) = 6$ ✓

State: $\{A:0, B:4, C:3, D:6, E:7, F:\infty\}$

**Step 5 — Extract $D$ (dist = 6).** Relax edges:
- $(D, E)$: $\text{dist}[E] = \min(7,\ 6+2) = 7$ (no improvement)
- $(D, F)$: $\text{dist}[F] = 6 + 5 = 11$

State: $\{A:0, B:4, C:3, D:6, E:7, F:11\}$

**Step 6 — Extract $E$ (dist = 7).** Relax edges:
- $(E, F)$: $\text{dist}[F] = \min(11,\ 7+7) = 11$ (no improvement)

**Step 7 — Extract $F$ (dist = 11).** No outgoing edges.

**Final shortest distances from $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \rightarrow A &= 0 \\
A \rightarrow B &= 4 \\
A \rightarrow C &= 3 \\
A \rightarrow D &= 6 \\
A \rightarrow E &= 7 \\
A \rightarrow F &= 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shortest Path Tree:** $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$ and $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F$.

---

**Q: (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** in detail. How does it differ from Dijkstra’s algorithm? Why is it useful when edge weights can be negative?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman-Ford Algorithm** is a single-source shortest-path algorithm that works on **directed or undirected graphs** with **negative edge weights** (but no negative cycles reachable from the source).

**Operational Steps:**

1. Initialize: $\text{dist}[s] = 0$, all others = $\infty$.
2. **Relax all edges** $(u, v)$ with weight $w$: if $\text{dist}[u] + w < \text{dist}[v]$, update $\text{dist}[v]$.
3. Repeat Step 2 for a total of $n - 1$ iterations.
4. **Negative cycle detection pass:** Perform one more iteration. If any edge can still be relaxed, the graph contains a **negative-weight cycle** reachable from the source.

**Time Complexity:** $\mathbf{O(V \cdot E)}$

**Comparison with Dijkstra:**

| Feature | Dijkstra | Bellman-Ford |
|---|---|---|
| Negative edge weights | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** |
| Negative cycle detection | Not possible | Possible |
| Time complexity | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | $O(V \cdot E)$ |
| Strategy | Greedy (min-heap) | Dynamic Programming (relax all edges) |
| Use case | Real-time GPS, OSPF | Currency arbitrage detection, RIP routing |

**Why Bellman-Ford handles negative weights:** Dijkstra assumes that once a vertex is extracted from the priority queue, its shortest distance is *final* (greedy correctness). This fails with negative weights because a longer path through a negative edge may later become the shortest. Bellman-Ford avoids this by relaxing *all* edges repeatedly, allowing distance estimates to propagate.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Apply **Prim’s algorithm** starting from vertex $A$ to find the MST of the graph with the following edges:

- $(A, B) = 2$, $(A, C) = 3$, $(A, D) = 1$
- $(B, C) = 4$, $(B, D) = 5$
- $(C, D) = 6$, $(C, E) = 7$
- $(D, E) = 8$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with $A$.** Available edges from $A$: $(A,B)=2$, $(A,C)=3$, $(A,D)=1$.
- Choose $(A, D) = 1$ (minimum).
- **MST edges:** $\{(A, D)\}$, vertices in MST: $\{A, D\}$.

**Step 2 — Edges from $\{A, D\}$:** $(A,B)=2$, $(A,C)=3$, $(B,D)=5$, $(C,D)=6$, $(D,E)=8$.
- Choose $(A, B) = 2$.
- **MST edges:** $\{(A, D), (A, B)\}$, vertices in MST: $\{A, B, D\}$.

**Step 3 — Edges from $\{A, B, D\}$:** $(A,C)=3$, $(B,C)=4$, $(C,D)=6$, $(D,E)=8$.
- Choose $(A, C) = 3$.
- **MST edges:** $\{(A,D), (A,B), (A,C)\}$, vertices in MST: $\{A, B, C, D\}$.

**Step 4 — Edges from $\{A, B, C, D\}$:** $(B,C)=4$, $(C,D)=6$, $(C,E)=7$, $(D,E)=8$.
- Choose $(B, C) = 4$ — but $B$ and $C$ are already in the MST, so adding it would form a cycle. **Reject.**
- Choose $(C, E) = 7$.
- **MST edges:** $\{(A,D), (A,B), (A,C), (C,E)\}$.

**Total edges: 4 = $n - 1$ (where $n = 5$). MST complete.**

**Total MST weight:**

$$
W_{\text{MST}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 7 = 13
$$

**Q: (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **applications of BFS and DFS** in solving real-world problems. Provide a comparison between the two traversal strategies.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BFS Applications:**
> 1. **Shortest path in unweighted graphs** — Finds minimum number of edges between two vertices.
> 2. **Level-order traversal** of trees.
> 3. **Web crawlers** — Discover all URLs reachable from a starting page (level by level).
> 4. **Social network friend recommendations** — *“People You May Know”* feature (2-hop/3-hop BFS).
> 5. **GPS navigation** for finding the *minimum number of turns*.
> 6. **Broadcasting in networks** — Spreading a message along minimum hops.
> 7. **Bipartite graph testing** — 2-colouring during BFS.
> 8. **Connected components** identification.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFS Applications:**
> 1. **Topological sorting** of DAGs (using a stack).
> 2. **Cycle detection** in directed and undirected graphs.
> 3. **Strongly connected components** (Tarjan’s/Kosaraju’s algorithm).
> 4. **Path finding in mazes / puzzles** (sudoku, N-queens).
> 5. **Solving mazes and backtracking** problems.
> 6. **Detecting bridges and articulation points** in a graph.
> 7. **Tree edge classification** (forward, back, cross edges in directed graphs).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure used | **Queue (FIFO)** | **Stack / Recursion (LIFO)** |
| Traversal order | **Level by level** | **Branch by branch** |
| Backtracking needed? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Shortest path (unweighted) | **Yes — guaranteed** | Not guaranteed |
| Memory consumption | Higher (stores all level nodes) | Lower (stores path stack) |
| Completeness | Complete | Complete (in finite graphs) |
| Optimality | Optimal for unweighted graphs | Not optimal for shortest path |
| Use case | Shortest path, broadcasting | Cycle detection, topological sort, backtracking |
| Time complexity | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V + E)$ |
| Space complexity | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MST with Shortest Path:** The MST is the minimum-cost set of edges that *connect all vertices*; it does **not** give the shortest path between two specific vertices. Many students compute Dijkstra and call it an MST — **deducted 2 to 3 marks** in Part B.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to update the `dist[]` correctly during relaxation:** The condition is $\text{dist}[u] + w(u, v) < \text{dist}[v]$. Writing `dist[u] < dist[v]` instead of `dist[u] + w < dist[v]` is a **classic 2-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Applying Dijkstra on negative-weight graphs:** Dijkstra assumes non-negative weights. If the examiner gives a graph with a negative edge, students who still apply Dijkstra will receive **zero credit** for correctness, even if the trace is shown.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mark vertices as visited in BFS/DFS:** This causes infinite loops / stack overflows in trace, leading to **1 to 2 mark deductions**.
>
> 5. **Kruskal’s — Failing to use DSU/Union-Find:** When asked to *implement* Kruskal, students who simply pick the smallest edge and reject on cycle detection manually (without DSU) will lose **1 to 2 marks** for methodology.
>
> 6. **Topological sort on a graph with a cycle:** If the input contains a cycle, students who still produce a topological order will be **fully penalized (3 marks)**.
>
> 7. **Floyd-Warshall — Wrong $k$ loop placement:** The $k$ loop must be the **outermost** loop (intermediate vertex). Placing it innermost is a **subtle but fatal mistake** (deducted up to 3 marks).
>
> 8. **Bellman-Ford — Iterating $n$ times instead of $n-1$:** The standard is $n-1$ relaxation passes; one extra pass is reserved for negative-cycle detection only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bullet Summary (Must Memorize):**
>
> - **Graph $G(V, E)$:** $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges. Use $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$.
> - **BFS** uses a **queue**; **DFS** uses a **stack/recursion**.
> - **BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs; DFS does not.**
> - **MST** has **exactly $n - 1$ edges** and **no cycles**.
> - **Kruskal** is edge-greedy with **DSU**, time $O(E \log V)$.
> - **Prim** is vertex-greedy with a **min-heap**, time $O(E \log V)$.
> - **Dijkstra** fails on **negative weights**; use **Bellman-Ford** instead.
> - **Bellman-Ford** runs $n-1$ iterations and detects **negative cycles** with an extra pass.
> - **Floyd-Warshall** solves **all-pairs shortest paths** in $O(V^3)$ using **dynamic programming**.
> - **Topological sort** is defined **only on DAGs** (Directed Acyclic Graphs).
> - **Cycle detection** in undirected DFS: visited neighbour ≠ parent ⇒ cycle.
> - **Bipartite check**: 2-colour using BFS/DFS; conflict ⇒ not bipartite.
> - **Bipartite theorem:** A graph is bipartite **iff** it has **no odd-length cycles**.
> - **Real-world anchors:** Dijkstra → GPS; MST → LAN cables; Topological sort → build systems; Bipartite → job assignment.
> - **Always restate the algorithm name, the data structure used, and the time complexity** in your exam answer — this alone can fetch **1–2 grace marks** in borderline cases.
